title: The Scaling Laws of Skills in LLM Agent Systems
summary: As agent systems scale, skills accumulate into large reusable libraries, yet their scaling laws remain poorly understood. Across 15 frontier LLMs, 1,141 real-world skills, and over 3M routing or execution decisions, we identify two coupled laws. Routing law: single-step routing accuracy decays logarithmically with library size ($R^2{>}0.97$ for all models), with errors progressing from local skill competition to cross-family drift and capture by overly general "black-hole skills". Execution law: before state realization, joint routing is approximately multiplicative, whereas correct execution can improve difficult downstream decisions by about $4{\times}$. A single parameter, the routing logarithmic decay slope $b$, couples the two laws: routing-side fits predict execution-side rescue across models, showing that the same library property controls both pre-execution collapse and downstream recoverability. The laws are actionable: law-guided optimization raises held-out routing accuracy from 71.3% to 91.7%, reduces hijack from 22.4% to 4.1%, and transfers directionally to downstream ClawBench and ClawMark execution settings, improving mean pass rate from 49.3% to 61.6% on ClawBench and from 28.4% to 34.5% on ClawMark. These results show that agent performance depends not only on model capability, but also on the structure, granularity, and exposure policy of the skill library.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically discovers and characterizes scaling laws for large skill libraries in LLM-based agent systems, relating library size to routing accuracy and execution performance, and shows these laws can guide practical optimization of skill libraries.
AI.motivation: LLM agents increasingly rely on large libraries of reusable skills or tools, but how performance scales as these libraries grow is not well understood. The authors want to understand the systematic failure modes in skill routing and execution as libraries become large, and to derive quantitative laws that can predict and improve agent performance.
AI.method: The authors evaluate 15 advanced LLMs on 1,141 real-world skills across more than 3 million routing and execution decisions. They empirically measure how single-step routing accuracy changes as the size of the skill library increases, and analyze joint routing and execution behavior before and after state realization. They identify patterns such as local skill competition, cross-family drift, and 'black-hole skills.' They fit a logarithmic decay model to routing accuracy and study how a single parameter, the decay slope b, predicts both routing collapse and execution-side rescue. They then use these laws to optimize the structure and exposure of the skill library and validate performance on benchmarks ClawBench and ClawMark.
AI.result: They find a robust routing law: single-step routing accuracy decays logarithmically with library size, with very high goodness of fit (R^2>0.97) across all 15 models, and failure modes evolve from local competition to cross-family drift to capture by overly general 'black-hole skills.' They also find an execution law: before state realization, joint routing is approximately multiplicative, but correct execution can rescue later decisions, improving difficult downstream decisions by roughly 4x. The same parameter, the routing logarithmic decay slope b, couples these laws, so routing-side fits can predict execution-side rescue. Using these insights, they design law-guided optimizations that increase held-out routing accuracy from 71.3% to 91.7%, cut hijack from 22.4% to 4.1%, and improve downstream benchmark performance: mean pass rate from 49.3% to 61.6% on ClawBench and from 28.4% to 34.5% on ClawMark.
AI.conclusion: Agent performance in LLM-based systems is governed not only by the base model's capability, but also by quantitative scaling laws that relate the size and structure of the skill library to routing and execution performance. A single library parameter (the routing decay slope) explains both pre-execution collapse and downstream recoverability, enabling principled design and optimization of skill libraries. Properly structuring, sizing, and exposing skills—guided by these laws—can substantially improve routing accuracy, reduce hijacking by overly general skills, and boost end-to-end benchmark performance.

title: PQR: A Framework to Generate Diverse and Realistic User Queries that Elicit QA Agent Failures
summary: Evaluating LLM-based agents remains challenging because identifying meaningful failure cases often requires substantial human effort to design realistic test scenarios. Prior works primarily focus on automatically discovering agent failures induced by adversarial users, while overlooking queries with real user intents that also trigger agent failures. We introduce PQR, a framework that not only surfaces agent failures with respect to specific objectives (e.g., helpfulness, safety, etc.) but also resembles real users' intents. PQR operates through an iterative interaction between two complementary modules. The query refinement module performs rewrites to explore diverse query variations, while the prompt refinement module uses prior feedback to derive new objective-violating strategies and realism policies for refining prompts, which in turn generate failure-triggering yet realistic queries. We evaluate PQR on detecting an e-commerce QA agent's unhelpful responses. Our method uncovers 23% - 78% more unhelpful responses, and our generated queries are more diverse and realistic compared to previous methods.

AI.tldr: PQR is a framework to automatically find realistic, objective-violating failures in LLM-based agents by iteratively refining both user queries and prompts, uncovering more diverse and realistic failure cases than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Evaluating LLM-based agents is difficult because it requires significant human effort to design realistic test scenarios, and existing automatic methods mostly focus on adversarial or unnatural queries. There is a need for a systematic way to uncover failures that both violate target objectives (e.g., helpfulness, safety) and reflect real users’ intents.
AI.method: PQR consists of two interacting modules that iteratively generate and refine test queries. The query refinement module rewrites existing queries to explore a broad, diverse space of possible user inputs. The prompt refinement module takes feedback from previously found failures and uses it to derive new strategies that more effectively violate the target objective, as well as realism policies that enforce user-like intent. These refined prompts are then used to generate new, realistic, failure-triggering queries, closing the loop.
AI.result: Applied to detecting unhelpful responses from an e-commerce QA agent, PQR uncovers 23%–78% more unhelpful agent responses than prior methods. The generated queries are also empirically more diverse and more realistic (i.e., closer to real user behavior) compared to baselines in the literature.
AI.conclusion: PQR provides a more effective and realistic way to test LLM-based agents by jointly optimizing for objective-violating failures and user-intent realism. Its iterative, dual-refinement design leads to significantly better coverage of failure cases and more lifelike test queries than existing adversarial-focused approaches.

title: Scaling Accessible Mathematics on arXiv: HTML Conversion and MathML 4

AI.tldr: The paper reports progress on arXiv’s experimental HTML Papers system, focusing on improved HTML quality, large-scale conversion, accessibility via MathML Intent, and performance gains through a Rust port of LaTeXML.
AI.motivation: arXiv wants to deliver high-quality, accessible, and efficient HTML versions of TeX/LaTeX submissions to better serve readers, leverage modern web and accessibility standards, and reduce computational costs compared to traditional PDF-centric workflows.
AI.method: The project iteratively improves the HTML conversion pipeline through community feedback and bug reports, large-scale corpus conversion and error analysis, integration of MathML 4 Intent for semantic math and screen-reader output, and an in-progress reimplementation (port) of LaTeXML in Rust to optimize performance and resource use.
AI.result: About half of 6,000 user reports have been resolved, HTML conversion quality has risen to 75% error-free with a target of 90%, initial MathML 4 Intent annotations have been introduced, and the Rust port of LaTeXML is underway and already demonstrating reduced compute costs and faster previews.
AI.conclusion: Although still experimental, the HTML Papers project is steadily becoming more robust and useful, guided by user needs and enabled by modern standards, languages, and AI techniques; continued development is expected to further improve fidelity, accessibility, and performance of arXiv’s HTML delivery.

title: Beyond Sentiment Classification: A Generative Framework for Emotion Intensity Evaluation in Text
summary:
We introduce a novel approach to emotion modeling that shifts the focus from
  identification to evaluation, addressing the limitations of discrete classification in
  applied domains such as finance. By constructing a dataset of emotional intensity
  scores and fine-tuning open-weight generative language models to output continuous
  values from 0-100, we demonstrate a more expressive, generalizable framework for
  sentiment and emotion analysis. Our findings not only outperform classification
  baselines but also reveal surprising generalization capabilities and transfer effects
  to related constructs such as sentiment and arousal. This work contributes to the
  interdisciplinary recontextualization of NLP by introducing emotion intensity
  evaluation as an alternative to classification, arguing that this shift better aligns
  with the needs of domains--such as finance--where the degree of emotional content is
  central to interpretation and decision-making.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes modeling emotions as continuous intensity scores instead of discrete classes, using fine-tuned generative LMs to output 0–100 values, yielding better performance and generalization for applications like finance.
AI.motivation: Discrete emotion/sentiment classification (e.g., positive/negative, emotion labels) is too coarse for domains where the strength of emotion matters, such as finance. The authors want a more expressive and practically useful representation that captures degrees of emotional content and can transfer across related affective constructs.
AI.method: They construct a dataset with emotion intensity scores (0–100) and fine-tune open-weight generative language models to directly output these continuous scores. They then compare these models against standard classification baselines and test for generalization and transfer to related constructs like sentiment and arousal.
AI.result: The continuous-intensity models outperform classification baselines on relevant tasks. They additionally show strong generalization and transfer effects, meaning that training on emotion intensity also helps in related areas such as sentiment and arousal estimation.
AI.conclusion: Evaluating emotion as a continuous intensity rather than as discrete categories offers a more expressive, generalizable, and practically aligned framework, especially in finance where the degree of emotion matters. The work positions emotion intensity evaluation as an alternative paradigm to standard classification in NLP emotion modeling.

title: AgentWall: A Runtime Safety Layer for Local AI Agents
comment: 16 pages, 2 figures, open-source implementation at https://github.com/agentwall/Agentwall
summary: The safety of autonomous AI agents is increasingly recognized as a critical open problem. As agents transition from passive text generators to active actors capable of executing shell commands, modifying files, calling APIs, and browsing the web, the consequences of unsafe or adversarially manipulated behavior become immediate and tangible. Existing AI safety work has focused primarily on model alignment and input filtering, but these approaches do not address what happens at the moment an agent's intent becomes a real action on a real machine. This gap is especially acute in local environments, where developers run agents against their own filesystems, credentials, and infrastructure with little runtime control. This paper introduces AgentWall, a runtime safety and observability layer for local AI agents. AgentWall intercepts every proposed agent action before it reaches the host environment, evaluates it against an explicit declarative policy, requires human approval for sensitive operations, and records a complete execution trail for audit and replay. It is implemented as a policy-enforcing MCP proxy and native OpenClaw plugin, working across Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, Claude Code, and OpenClaw with a single install command. We present the design, architecture, threat model, and policy model of AgentWall, and demonstrate 92.9% policy enforcement accuracy with sub-millisecond overhead across 14 benchmark tests. AgentWall is open-source at https://github.com/agentwall/Agentwall.

AI.tldr: AgentWall is a runtime safety and observability layer that mediates all actions of local AI agents via declarative policies, human approvals, and full auditing, achieving high enforcement accuracy with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: As autonomous AI agents gain capabilities to act directly on a user’s machine (shell commands, file edits, API calls, web browsing), existing alignment and input filtering techniques no longer suffice, because they don't control what happens at the precise point where an agent turns intent into concrete system actions. This is particularly risky in local developer environments that expose real filesystems, credentials, and infrastructure without strong runtime controls. The paper aims to close this gap by enforcing safety at the action-execution boundary.
AI.method: The authors design and implement AgentWall as a runtime safety and observability layer that sits between AI agents and the host environment. It intercepts every proposed action, evaluates it against a declarative policy model, can require explicit human approval for sensitive operations, and logs a complete execution trace for audit and replay. Technically, it is realized as a policy-enforcing MCP proxy and a native plugin for OpenClaw, integrated with tools such as Claude Desktop, Cursor, Windsurf, Claude Code, and OpenClaw, deployable via a simple installation process. They evaluate it on 14 benchmark tests, measuring policy enforcement accuracy and runtime overhead.
AI.result: AgentWall enforces policies correctly in 92.9% of benchmarked cases while adding sub-millisecond runtime overhead per action across 14 test scenarios. It successfully mediates potentially dangerous actions and provides comprehensive logging without significantly impacting performance.
AI.conclusion: Runtime, policy-based mediation of agent actions is both practical and effective for improving the safety of local AI agents. By inserting a lightweight, declarative control layer at the point where agents interact with the system, developers can gain fine-grained control, mandatory approvals for risky operations, and strong observability, all with minimal performance cost. AgentWall demonstrates that such an approach can be implemented in a tool- and IDE-agnostic manner and is made available as open-source infrastructure for the community.

title: SKG-Eval: Stateful Evaluation of Multi-Turn Dialogue via Incremental Semantic Knowledge Graphs
summary: Evaluating multi-turn dialogue systems remains challenging because response quality depends not only on the current prompt, but also on previously established entities, claims, and conversational commitments. Existing automatic evaluators, including LLM-as-a-judge frameworks and embedding-based metrics, largely rely on flat or turn-isolated representations, making them less effective at detecting long-range issues such as contradiction, topic drift, and entity inconsistency. To address this, we propose SKG-Eval, a quasi-deterministic and interpretable framework that models dialogue as an evolving Semantic Knowledge Graph (SKG) of entities, relations, and commitments across turns. The framework incrementally updates the graph through structured triple extraction and computes three complementary signals: (i) local relevance, measuring alignment with the current prompt and optional reference; (ii) historical consistency, evaluating how newly introduced information connects to prior conversational context using graph-based and embedding-driven signals; and (iii) logical coherence, assessed by a geometric contradiction engine that detects cross-turn conflicts without relying on NLI models or LLM judges. These signals are adaptively fused and aggregated into a length-invariant session score via recency-weighted trend analysis. Across multiple benchmarks, SKG-Eval achieves higher correlation with human judgments and substantially improves detection of long-range inconsistencies in extended conversations. In addition, the framework produces explicit contradiction certificates and deterministic scores for fixed inputs, enabling reproducible and auditable evaluation. Overall, our results suggest that structured externalized state tracking through semantic knowledge graphs provides a scalable alternative to implicit reasoning in LLM-based dialogue evaluators.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SKG-Eval, a dialogue evaluation framework that tracks entities and commitments in a semantic knowledge graph to better detect long-range issues like contradictions and topic drift, achieving higher alignment with human judgments than existing LLM- or embedding-based evaluators.
AI.motivation: Evaluating multi-turn dialogue is hard because a response must be consistent not only with the immediate prompt but with the entire prior conversation. Existing automatic metrics treat turns in isolation or as flat text, making them poor at catching long-range problems such as contradictions, topic drift, and inconsistent entities. There is a need for a more structured, interpretable, and reproducible evaluation method that can explicitly model conversational state over time.
AI.method: The authors propose SKG-Eval, which represents the dialogue as an evolving Semantic Knowledge Graph (SKG) over entities, relations, and conversational commitments. At each turn, they extract structured triples and update the graph. Based on this representation they compute three signals: (i) local relevance to the current prompt and optional reference; (ii) historical consistency, using graph-based and embedding-based measures to see how new information connects to prior context; and (iii) logical coherence, via a geometric contradiction engine that detects cross-turn conflicts without NLI or LLM judges. These signals are adaptively fused and aggregated into a length-invariant session score using recency-weighted trend analysis. The system also outputs explicit contradiction certificates and deterministic scores.
AI.result: On multiple dialogue evaluation benchmarks, SKG-Eval shows higher correlation with human judgments than standard LLM-as-a-judge and embedding-based metrics, especially in detecting long-range inconsistencies such as contradictions and entity drift in extended conversations. The framework also demonstrates deterministic behavior and can provide interpretable evidence (contradiction certificates) for its scores.
AI.conclusion: Structured, externalized state tracking via semantic knowledge graphs offers a scalable and interpretable alternative to implicit reasoning in LLM-based dialogue evaluators. By modeling entities, relations, and commitments over turns and combining local relevance, historical consistency, and logical coherence, SKG-Eval yields more reliable, reproducible, and human-aligned evaluation of multi-turn dialogue systems, particularly for long-range consistency issues.

title: ANNEAL: Adapting LLM Agents via Governed Symbolic Patch Learning
summary: LLM-based agents can recover from individual execution errors, yet they repeatedly fail on the same fault when the underlying process knowledge--operator schemas, preconditions, and constraints--remains unrepaired. Existing self-evolving approaches address this gap by updating prompts, memory, or model weights, but none directly repair the symbolic structures that encode how tasks are executed, and few provide the governance guarantees required for safe deployment. We introduce ANNEAL, a neuro-symbolic agent that converts recurring failures into governed symbolic edits of a process knowledge graph without modifying foundation model weights. Its core mechanism, Failure-Driven Knowledge Acquisition (FDKA), localizes the responsible operator, synthesizes a typed patch through constrained LLM generation, and validates the proposal via multi-dimensional scoring, symbolic guardrails, and canary testing before commit. Every accepted edit carries full provenance and deterministic rollback capability. Across four domains and 27 multi-seed runs, ANNEAL is the only evaluated system that commits persistent structural repairs--strong baselines such as ReAct and Reflexion achieve high episodic recovery yet retain 72-100% holdout failure rates on recurring faults, whereas ANNEAL reduces these to 0% in the tested recurring-failure settings. Ablation confirms that removing FDKA eliminates all structural repairs and drops success rate by up to 26.7 percentage points. These results suggest that governed symbolic repair offers a complementary paradigm to weight-level and prompt-level adaptation for persistent fault elimination.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ANNEAL, a neuro-symbolic agent that performs governed, persistent repair of process knowledge graphs to eliminate recurring failures in LLM-based agents, achieving 0% holdout failure on tested recurring-fault settings.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents can often recover from single-step errors but fail repeatedly on the same underlying process faults because the symbolic process knowledge (operator schemas, preconditions, constraints) is never repaired. Existing self-evolving methods mainly update prompts, memory, or model weights and lack mechanisms to directly repair symbolic task structures or provide governance guarantees (provenance, rollback, validation) needed for safe deployment. The paper aims to fill this gap by enabling persistent, governed repair of the symbolic process knowledge itself.
AI.method: The authors propose ANNEAL, a neuro-symbolic agent that operates over a process knowledge graph. Its core mechanism, Failure-Driven Knowledge Acquisition (FDKA), detects recurring failures and: (1) localizes the responsible operator in the process knowledge graph; (2) uses constrained LLM generation to synthesize a typed patch (a symbolic edit) to that operator; (3) validates the candidate patch via multi-dimensional scoring, symbolic guardrails, and canary testing; and (4) commits accepted edits with full provenance and deterministic rollback capability, without modifying foundation model weights. The system is evaluated against strong baselines like ReAct and Reflexion across four domains with multiple random seeds, plus ablation where FDKA is removed.
AI.result: Across four domains and 27 multi-seed runs, ANNEAL is the only evaluated system that produces persistent structural repairs to the process knowledge graph. While baselines like ReAct and Reflexion achieve high episodic recovery, they leave 72–100% of holdout failures unresolved when the same faults recur. ANNEAL, in contrast, drives these recurring-failure rates down to 0% in the tested settings. In ablation studies, removing the FDKA mechanism eliminates all structural repairs and reduces success rate by up to 26.7 percentage points, demonstrating the centrality of FDKA to the method’s effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Governed symbolic repair of process knowledge graphs—via mechanisms like ANNEAL’s FDKA—offers a powerful, complementary paradigm to prompt-level and weight-level adaptation for eliminating persistent faults in LLM-based agents. By turning recurring failures into validated, provenance-tracked symbolic edits with rollback guarantees, ANNEAL can achieve durable improvements in task execution that existing self-evolving approaches do not provide.

title: A Scalable Tool for Measuring Manner and Result Verbs in Developmental Language Research
summary: Manner and result verbs encode different aspects of event structure and have been discussed in developmental work as a potentially informative distinction for studying early verb learning. However, this distinction remains difficult to measure at scale because large annotated resources for manner and result classification are not currently available. We present a computational approach for identifying manner and result verbs in sentence context. Using linguistically informed prompts, we generate sentence-level annotations with large language models over data drawn from MASC and InterCorp, extending coverage from previously annotated portions of VerbNet to 436 classes. We then train a RoBERTa-based classifier on these annotations and evaluate it on three held-out gold-standard datasets, including previously annotated items and a new expert-annotated set. Across these evaluations, the model shows promising performance, with average accuracy up to 89.6%. We present this work as a scalable measurement tool that can support future research on verb semantics in developmental and other language datasets, while noting that further validation is needed for borderline cases, mixed manner/result verbs, and downstream developmental applications.

AI.tldr: They build and validate an automatic method to label verbs in context as manner vs. result, enabling large-scale research on verb semantics and development.
AI.motivation: Manner vs. result is an important theoretical distinction in event semantics and child language acquisition, but it has been hard to study at scale because there are no large annotated datasets labeling verbs this way. Manual annotation is costly and existing resources (e.g., portions of VerbNet) are limited in coverage, which constrains empirical and developmental research on verb learning.
AI.method: They use linguistically informed prompts with large language models to automatically annotate verbs in context as manner or result over sentences from MASC and InterCorp, greatly expanding beyond previously annotated VerbNet classes to a total of 436 classes. Using these LLM-generated, sentence-level labels as training data, they train a RoBERTa-based classifier. They then evaluate this classifier on three held-out gold-standard datasets: earlier human-annotated items and a new set of expert-annotated verbs, measuring classification accuracy.
AI.result: The RoBERTa classifier achieves strong performance, with average accuracy up to 89.6% across the three gold-standard evaluation sets. This demonstrates that manner/result classification in context can be learned effectively from LLM-generated annotations and generalized to new data and to a wide range of VerbNet classes.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that their model provides a scalable tool for measuring manner vs. result verb semantics in sentence context, opening the door to larger-scale quantitative work in developmental and other language datasets. However, they caution that additional validation is needed, particularly for borderline or mixed manner/result verbs and for using the tool in downstream developmental research, where subtle distinctions and edge cases may matter a great deal.

title: From Prompts to Protocols: An AI Agent for Laboratory Automation
summary: Automating science laboratories enables faster, safer, more accurate, and more reproducible execution of protocols, accelerating the discovery and testing of new materials, drugs, and more. However, setting up and running autonomous labs requires coordinating numerous instruments and robots, forcing scientists to write code, manage configuration files, and navigate complex software infrastructure. We present an AI agent architecture that integrates large language models with laboratory orchestration, enabling scientists to interactively create and monitor automated lab protocols using natural language. Integrated into the Experiment Orchestration System (EOS), the AI agent operates under an agentic loop with automated validation and error correction, and supports the complete experimental lifecycle: creating protocols, running and monitoring both protocols and closed-loop optimization campaigns, and analyzing results. A visual graph editor renders protocols as interactive node-based diagrams synchronized with the AI agent's protocol representation, enabling seamless alternation between AI-assisted and manual protocol construction. Evaluated on three simulated automated labs spanning chemistry, biology, and materials science, the AI agent achieves a 97% first-attempt protocol generation success rate and an order of magnitude reduction in required interface actions.

AI.tldr: AI-driven system that lets scientists design, run, and monitor automated lab experiments in natural language, tightly integrated with a lab orchestration platform.
AI.motivation: Autonomous laboratories speed up and improve scientific experiments, but currently require scientists to handle low-level coding, configuration, and complex software, which is a major barrier to adoption and productivity.
AI.method: Design an AI agent architecture that couples large language models with the Experiment Orchestration System (EOS). The agent runs in an agentic loop with automated validation and error correction, supports end-to-end experimental workflows, and is paired with a visual graph editor that keeps a synchronized, node-based representation of protocols for both AI-assisted and manual editing. Performance is evaluated in simulation across three domain-specific automated labs (chemistry, biology, and materials science).
AI.result: In simulated chemistry, biology, and materials science labs, the AI agent correctly generates executable lab protocols on the first attempt 97% of the time, while reducing the number of user interface actions needed by about an order of magnitude compared with non‑agentic tooling.
AI.conclusion: Integrating LLM-based agents with lab orchestration systems and synchronized visual editors can make autonomous laboratories far more accessible and efficient, enabling scientists to specify complex automated experiments in natural language while maintaining robustness through validation and error-correction loops.

title: CHI-Bench: Can AI Agents Automate End-to-End, Long-Horizon, Policy-Rich Healthcare Workflows?
summary: End-to-end automation of realistic healthcare operations stresses three capabilities underrepresented in current benchmarks: policy density, decisions must be grounded in a large library of medical, insurance, and operational rules; Multi-role composition: a single task requires the agent to play multiple roles with handoffs; and multilateral interaction: intermediate workflow steps are multi-turn dialogs, such as peer-to-peer review and patient outreach. We introduce $χ$-Bench, a benchmark of long-horizon healthcare workflows across three domains: provider prior authorization, payer utilization management, and care management. Each task hands the agent a clinical case in a high-fidelity simulator of 20 healthcare apps exposed via 87 MCP tools, which it must drive to a terminal status through tool calls and writing the role's artifacts, guided by a 1,290+ document managed-care operations handbook skill. Across 30 agent harness/models configurations, the best agent resolves only 28.0% of tasks, no agent clears 20% on strict pass^3, and executing all tasks in a single session slumps the performance to 3.8%. These results raise the hypothesis that similar gaps are likely to surface in other policy-dense, role-composed, irreversible enterprise domains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces χ-Bench, a benchmark for end-to-end, tool-using healthcare agents, showing that current LLM agents perform poorly on realistic, policy-dense workflows.
AI.motivation: Existing AI and LLM benchmarks do not adequately capture the complexity of real healthcare operations, which involve dense policy constraints, multiple organizational roles, and multi-party, multi-turn interactions. The authors want to evaluate whether current agentic LLMs can actually execute realistic healthcare workflows that are constrained by extensive rules and require coordination across systems and roles.
AI.method: They design χ-Bench, a benchmark composed of long-horizon healthcare workflows in three key domains: provider prior authorization, payer utilization management, and care management. Each task is instantiated as a high-fidelity simulation environment that exposes 20 realistic healthcare applications via 87 MCP tools. The agent receives a clinical case and must progress it to a terminal state by calling tools and generating the written artifacts appropriate to each role, following a large, 1,290+ document managed-care operations handbook. They then evaluate 30 different agent harness/model configurations on these tasks, including single-task and full-session execution conditions, and measure various success metrics such as task resolution and strict multi-criteria pass rates (pass^3).
AI.result: The best-performing evaluated agent configuration successfully resolves only 28.0% of tasks. Under a stricter pass^3 metric, no agent configuration achieves a 20% success rate. When all tasks are run in a single, long session—thereby stressing memory, long-horizon planning, and stability—performance collapses to 3.8% task resolution. These results quantitatively demonstrate that current LLM-based agents struggle significantly with realistic, policy-dense, multi-role healthcare workflows, even when they have access to powerful toolsets and detailed policy documents.
AI.conclusion: χ-Bench exposes substantial gaps between current LLM-agent capabilities and the requirements of real-world, complex healthcare operations: dense policy grounding, multi-role coordination, and multilateral, irreversible workflows. The poor performance indicates that state-of-the-art agents are far from ready for autonomous deployment in such enterprise settings. The authors further hypothesize that similar performance gaps will appear in other domains that share these characteristics—policy density, role composition, and irreversible actions—highlighting the need for new methods, architectures, and training strategies for robust enterprise-grade agents.

title: Skim: Speculative Execution for Fast and Efficient Web Agents
summary: Skim is a speculative execution framework for web agents that exploits the predictable structure of purpose-built websites. Today's web-agent expense is not intrinsic to the tasks but a property of how agents are composed: frontier-model inference, browser rendering, and ReAct-style planning are applied to every step of every task regardless of complexity. Skim's key observation is that websites enforce stable URL patterns, answer formats, and task-to-trajectory mappings across queries of the same type, so most queries can bypass these heavyweight components entirely. An offline profiler captures these patterns once per site. At runtime, Skim matches each query to a template, synthesizes the destination URL, and extracts the answer with a small model. A lightweight verifier gates each fast-path output against the query and schema; rare misspeculations cascade to the full agent, warm-started by the fast path's final URL to preserve upstream trajectory progress. Across standard web-agent benchmarks paired with three backboneagents (WebVoyager, AgentOccam, BrowserUse), Skim reduces median per-task cost by 1.9x and latency by 33.4% with no accuracy loss.

AI.tldr: Skim is a speculative-execution framework for web agents that uses site-specific templates and small models to skip expensive steps for most queries, cutting cost and latency without hurting accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current web agents are expensive and slow because they repeatedly run large models, full browser rendering, and complex planning for every step of every task, even when interacting with simple, highly-structured, purpose-built websites. Many sites have predictable URL patterns, answer formats, and consistent trajectories for similar query types, but existing agents do not exploit this regularity, leading to unnecessary overhead. The paper aims to reduce cost and latency by rethinking how agents are composed for such predictable environments.
AI.method: The authors propose Skim, a speculative execution framework for web agents. First, an offline profiler analyzes each target website to learn stable patterns: URL templates, response schemas, and mappings from task types to navigation trajectories. At runtime, for a new query, Skim (1) classifies the query into a template; (2) directly synthesizes the destination URL using learned patterns; (3) extracts the answer using a small model rather than a large backbone agent; and (4) passes the result through a lightweight verifier that checks alignment with the query and schema. If verification fails (misspeculation), Skim falls back to a full web agent (e.g., WebVoyager, AgentOccam, BrowserUse), warm-started using the final URL from the speculative path to preserve progress. This creates a fast path for predictable cases and a safe slow path for difficult ones.
AI.result: On standard web-agent benchmarks and using three backbone agents (WebVoyager, AgentOccam, BrowserUse), Skim achieves a 1.9x reduction in median per-task cost and a 33.4% reduction in latency, all while maintaining the same task accuracy as the underlying agents. Misspeculations are rare due to the stability of site patterns and the verifier effectively filters incorrect fast-path outputs.
AI.conclusion: Speculative execution via site-specific templates can substantially reduce the computational and time cost of web agents without sacrificing accuracy, by exploiting the predictable structure of many purpose-built websites. Separating an offline profiling phase from a lightweight, template-driven runtime path allows most queries to bypass heavy components like large models, full browser rendering, and complex planning. Skim demonstrates that web-agent design should explicitly leverage website regularities, and its framework can be overlaid on existing agents to yield significant efficiency gains.

title: Language Acquisition Device in Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) remain substantially less data-efficient than humans. Pre-pretraining (PPT) on synthetic languages has been proposed to close this gap, with prior work emphasizing highly expressive formal languages such as $k$-Shuffle Dyck. Inspired by the Language Acquisition Device (LAD) hypothesis, which posits that innate constraints preemptively restrict the learner's hypothesis space to natural-language-like structure, we propose LAD-inspired PPT: pre-pretraining on MP-STRUCT, a formal language whose strings encode hierarchical composition, feature-based dependencies, and long-distance displacement via MERGE, AGREE, and MOVE. A brief 500-step PPT with MP-STRUCT matches strong formal-language baselines in token efficiency while additionally imparting a human-like resistance to structurally implausible languages (e.g., REVERSE). Analyzing simplified variants, we find that MP-STRUCT CORE outperforms $k$-Shuffle Dyck despite not being definable in C-RASP (a formal bound on transformer expressivity), challenging the prior hypothesis that effective PPT languages must be both hierarchically expressive and circuit-theoretically learnable. We show that functional landmarks, which reduce dependency resolution ambiguity, are a key driver, suggesting that effective PPT design depends not only on expressivity but also on the accessibility of dependency resolution.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LAD-inspired pre-pretraining (PPT) using a synthetic formal language, MP-STRUCT, to improve the data efficiency and inductive biases of LLMs.
AI.motivation: LLMs are far less data-efficient than humans. Existing synthetic PPT languages like k-Shuffle Dyck help but are motivated mainly by formal expressivity and circuit-theoretic learnability, not by linguistically motivated inductive biases. The authors are motivated by the LAD hypothesis from linguistics: that humans come with innate constraints restricting the hypothesis space to natural-language-like structures. They aim to design a PPT language that encodes such constraints and to test whether this improves efficiency and yields more human-like generalization, including resistance to unnatural languages like REVERSE.
AI.method: They design MP-STRUCT, a formal language whose strings explicitly encode core generative-syntax operations: MERGE (hierarchical composition), AGREE (feature-based dependencies), and MOVE (long-distance displacement). They run short pre-pretraining (about 500 steps) of transformer LLMs on MP-STRUCT, then continue with normal pretraining. They compare this to strong formal PPT baselines including k-Shuffle Dyck and others, testing token efficiency and generalization, including to structurally implausible languages. They also create simplified variants like MP-STRUCT CORE to ablate components (e.g., functional landmarks, types of dependencies) and analyze their learnability and performance. They connect these variants to formal notions like C-RASP, a bound on transformer expressivity, to test prior hypotheses about what makes a PPT language effective.
AI.result: A brief PPT phase on MP-STRUCT achieves token efficiency comparable to strong formal-language PPT baselines while inducing a human-like resistance to structurally implausible languages such as REVERSE. MP-STRUCT CORE, a simpler variant, outperforms k-Shuffle Dyck despite not being definable in C-RASP, contradicting earlier beliefs that PPT languages must be both hierarchically rich and C-RASP-learnable. Through ablations, they identify the presence of functional landmarks that reduce ambiguity in resolving dependencies as a crucial factor driving performance.
AI.conclusion: Effective PPT for LLMs does not depend solely on formal expressivity or circuit-theoretic learnability of the synthetic language. Instead, linguistically inspired design choices—particularly functional landmarks that make hierarchical and dependency structures easier to resolve—play a key role. LAD-inspired PPT with MP-STRUCT offers a more human-like inductive bias, improving token efficiency and robustness to unnatural structures, and challenges existing assumptions about how synthetic languages for PPT should be constructed.

title: Scalable Uncertainty Reasoning in Knowledge Graphs
comment: 14 pages. Preprint of a paper accepted at the ESWC 2026 PhD Symposium
summary: Knowledge Graphs are pivotal for semantic data integration. The real-world data they model is often inherently uncertain. Within knowledge graphs, uncertainty manifests in three distinct levels: imprecise attribute values, probabilistic triple existence, and incomplete schema knowledge. However, current Semantic Web standards lack native support for reasoning over such uncertainty, and naïve extensions often incur computational intractability. In this thesis, I aim to develop a modular framework that addresses each level through tailored techniques: (1) defining probabilistic literals and a corresponding query algebra for continuous attributes; (2) a compilation-based framework transforming SPARQL provenance into tractable probabilistic circuits for uncertain triples; and (3) topology-aware geometric embeddings for statistical schema reasoning. The central hypothesis is that specialized reasoning mechanisms, namely algebraic, logical, and geometric approaches, can reconcile semantic precision with computational tractability.

AI.tldr: The thesis proposes a modular framework to handle different kinds of uncertainty in knowledge graphs while keeping reasoning computationally tractable.
AI.motivation: Real-world data is inherently uncertain, but current Semantic Web standards and reasoning mechanisms do not natively support multiple forms of uncertainty in knowledge graphs, and naïve extensions can become computationally intractable. There is a need for principled methods that can handle uncertainty at different levels without sacrificing tractability.
AI.method: The work decomposes uncertainty in knowledge graphs into three levels and tackles each with a specialized technique: (1) introduce probabilistic literals and a query algebra for reasoning about continuous-valued, imprecise attributes; (2) design a compilation-based framework that converts SPARQL query provenance into tractable probabilistic circuits to reason over uncertain triple existence; and (3) develop topology-aware geometric embedding methods to support statistical reasoning about incomplete or uncertain schema information.
AI.result: The expected results are a set of coherent, modular reasoning components—algebraic for probabilistic literals, logical/compilation-based for uncertain triples, and geometric/embedding-based for schema uncertainty—that together allow expressive yet tractable uncertainty-aware reasoning over knowledge graphs.
AI.conclusion: Specialized, level-specific reasoning mechanisms (algebraic, logical, geometric) can be integrated into a modular framework that reconciles semantic precision with computational efficiency for uncertain knowledge graphs, overcoming limitations of current Semantic Web standards.

title: Retrieval-Based Multi-Label Legal Annotation: Extensible, Data-Efficient and Hallucination-Free
summary: Multi-label legal annotation requires assigning multiple labels from large, evolving taxonomies to long, fact-intensive documents, often under limited supervision. Parametric encoders typically require task-specific training and retraining when the label set changes, while prompting generative large language models becomes costly and degrades as the label space grows. We cast legal annotation as retrieval: we embed documents and label descriptions with a frozen retrieval model and predict labels via k-nearest neighbors in the embedding space, enabling updates by re-embedding and re-indexing rather than gradient-based backpropagation. Across three legal datasets (ECtHR-A, ECtHR-B, and Eurlex with 100 labels), retrieval achieves competitive accuracy and strong data efficiency; on Eurlex, Qwen-8B retrieval improves Macro-F1 from 40.41 (GPT-5.2, zero-shot) to 49.12 while reducing estimated compute by 20-30 times compared to fine-tuning. With only (N=100) training samples, retrieval nearly doubles Micro-F1 over hierarchical Legal-BERT on ECtHR-A (48.29 vs. 27.87). We also quantify a reliability failure mode of generative inference: GPT-5.2 hallucinates labels outside the provided taxonomy in 0.12-0.9% of test samples under deterministic decoding. In contrast, retrieval strictly respects defined label sets, eliminating hallucination by design. These results suggest retrieval-model-based annotators are a practical, deployable alternative for high-cardinality and rapidly changing legal label spaces.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a retrieval-based approach for multi-label legal annotation that embeds documents and label descriptions using a frozen model and predicts labels via k-NN, achieving competitive or better performance and much lower compute than fine-tuned or prompted LLMs, while eliminating label hallucination.
AI.motivation: Legal documents are long, complex, and must be annotated with multiple labels from large and evolving taxonomies. Existing approaches either require expensive task-specific training and retraining when labels change (parametric encoders) or rely on prompting large generative models, which becomes costly and unreliable as the label space grows, including problems like label hallucination. A more scalable, data-efficient, and reliable method is needed, especially when label taxonomies change frequently and supervision is limited.
AI.method: The authors reframe multi-label legal annotation as a retrieval problem. They use a frozen retrieval model to embed both legal documents and textual descriptions of labels into a shared embedding space. At inference, labels are predicted via k-nearest neighbors between a document’s embedding and the label embeddings, without gradient-based training on the classifier. Updating the label set only requires re-embedding the labels and re-indexing, not retraining the model. They evaluate this with a Qwen-8B-based retrieval setup on three legal datasets (ECtHR-A, ECtHR-B, and Eurlex-100) and compare against prompted GPT-5.2 and fine-tuned/hierarchical Legal-BERT baselines, also measuring hallucination in generative models.
AI.result: On Eurlex with 100 labels, the Qwen-8B retrieval approach improves Macro-F1 from 40.41 (zero-shot GPT-5.2 baseline) to 49.12 while using an estimated 20–30× less compute than fine-tuning approaches. On ECtHR-A with only 100 labeled training samples, retrieval nearly doubles Micro-F1 performance compared to hierarchical Legal-BERT (48.29 vs. 27.87), demonstrating strong data efficiency. The authors also find that GPT-5.2, even with deterministic decoding and an explicit taxonomy, hallucinates labels not in the label set for 0.12–0.9% of test samples, whereas retrieval never predicts out-of-taxonomy labels by construction.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval-based multi-label annotation with frozen encoders offers a practical alternative to fine-tuned or prompted generative models for legal text classification, especially in scenarios with many labels and frequently changing taxonomies. It yields competitive or better accuracy, is far more data- and compute-efficient, and completely avoids label hallucinations because predictions are constrained to the indexed label set. This makes retrieval-style annotators particularly suitable for real-world legal AI systems that must adapt quickly while maintaining reliability and clear adherence to predefined legal taxonomies.

title: Counterparty Modeling is Not Strategy: The Limits of LLM Negotiators
summary: Negotiation requires more than inferring what the other side wants: it requires using that information to make advantageous offers and counteroffers over multiple turns. We study whether large language model (LLM) agents do this in a controlled multi-attribute bargaining environment. We find that current LLM agents can model a counterparty's preferences, but do not reliably turn that knowledge into strategic bargaining. When given negotiating partner preference information, agents model it accurately and early in their reasoning traces, yet this does not reliably improve outcomes for the informed side. Turn-level analyses show why: agents often respond to what they believe the counterparty values, but do not consistently pair those moves with gains on their own high-value attributes. Sellers are more accommodating overall, and in asymmetric-information conditions, the informed side often makes the more weakly compensated concessions. Because agents fail to leverage this underlying utility structure for strategic advantage, their final agreements are heavily dictated by surface-level opening anchors rather than actual utility weights. Finally, requiring agents to explicitly state concession-for-reciprocity trades before making an offer makes individual turns look more strategic, but ultimately fails to improve the efficiency of the final agreements.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether large language model agents can use inferred partner preferences to negotiate strategically in a multi-attribute bargaining task and finds they generally cannot.
AI.motivation: While LLMs can infer and reason about other agents’ preferences, it is unclear whether they can exploit this knowledge in multi-turn bargaining to achieve better outcomes, especially under asymmetric information. The paper aims to test whether preference modeling translates into strategic negotiation behavior and improved payoffs.
AI.method: The authors use a controlled multi-attribute bargaining environment where LLM agents play buyer–seller negotiations across multiple turns. Conditions vary whether one or both sides have information about the other’s preferences. They analyze agents’ internal reasoning traces to see how and when they model counterpart preferences, evaluate turn-level behavior (offers, concessions, and trade-offs across attributes), compare informed vs uninformed conditions, and test an intervention where agents must explicitly state concession-for-reciprocity trades before making offers.
AI.result: LLM agents accurately infer counterpart preferences and do so early in their reasoning. However, this knowledge only weakly and inconsistently improves negotiation outcomes for the informed party. Agents’ offers often cater to what they believe the counterpart values, but they fail to secure corresponding gains on their own high-value attributes. Sellers tend to be more accommodating, and in asymmetric-information settings, the side with more information paradoxically makes larger, poorly compensated concessions. Final deals are strongly influenced by initial opening anchors instead of underlying utility weights. The explicit-trade articulation intervention makes turns appear more strategic but does not meaningfully increase allocative efficiency or informed-side advantage.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM agents can perform preference modeling but struggle to convert that into effective, utility-maximizing bargaining strategies. Their negotiations are driven more by surface-level patterns such as opening anchors than by systematic exploitation of utility structures, and simple prompting interventions that encourage explicit concession-for-reciprocity reasoning are insufficient to correct this. This highlights a gap between theory-of-mind style reasoning and robust strategic behavior in LLM-based agents, suggesting that additional mechanisms or training are needed for genuinely strategic negotiation performance.

title: Exploring Lightweight Large Language Models for Court View Generation
summary: Criminal Court View Generation (CVG) is a critical task in Legal Artificial Intelligence (Legal AI), involving the generation of court view based on case facts. In this work, we systematically explore the capabilities of lightweight (smaller than 2B) large language models (LLMs) in CVG and their impact on charge prediction. Our study addresses four key questions: (1) how does different architecture of LLMs affect the CVG quality and charge prediction. (2) how does LLMs size contribute to the performance, (3) how do lightweight LLMs compare with Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) in these tasks, and (4) how does predicting charge by court view generation first compare with predicting it directly. Additionally, we also develop CVGEvalKit, an evaluation framework including three public available datasets for CVG tasks, as well as predicting their charges. Comprehensive experiments are conducted on this framework, where models are trained on a mixed training set and evaluated on each dataset's test set. Experimental results provide new insights into the trade-offs between model architecture, model size, and the influence between different tasks, highlighting the potential of lightweight LLMs in judicial AI applications. The source code is anonymously available at \url{https://github.com/ZhitianHou/CVGEvalKit}

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how small (<2B) language models perform on generating criminal court views (reasoned judgments) and predicting charges, introducing an evaluation toolkit and offering insights into architecture, size, and task-design trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Legal AI has focused largely on larger models and direct charge prediction, with less attention to how well small, lightweight LLMs can generate structured legal reasoning (criminal court views) and how this reasoning affects downstream tasks like charge prediction. There is also a lack of standardized benchmarks and evaluation frameworks specifically tailored to Criminal Court View Generation (CVG).
AI.method: The authors define Criminal Court View Generation (CVG) as generating court views from case facts and set up four research questions around architecture, model size, comparison to classic DNNs, and direct vs. via-CVG charge prediction. They build CVGEvalKit, an evaluation framework comprising three public datasets for CVG and charge prediction, train various lightweight (<2B) LLMs on a mixed training set, and evaluate them on each dataset’s test set for both CVG quality and charge prediction performance. They compare results across architectures, sizes, and task setups (direct prediction vs. via generated court views).
AI.result: Experiments across three datasets show systematic differences in CVG and charge prediction performance depending on model architecture and size. Lightweight LLMs can be competitive with or superior to traditional DNNs for CVG and related charge prediction, and the design choice of predicting charges via generated court views vs. directly has measurable effects. The results reveal nuanced trade-offs among architecture, parameter count, and task formulation.
AI.conclusion: Lightweight (<2B) LLMs have strong potential for use in judicial AI, especially for criminal court view generation and associated charge prediction. The new CVGEvalKit framework enables standardized evaluation on multiple datasets, and empirical findings clarify how architecture, size, and indirect (via CVG) vs. direct prediction strategies impact performance. These insights can guide the design of efficient, interpretable legal AI systems in resource-constrained or deployment-critical settings.

title: AgentKernelArena: Generalization-Aware Benchmarking of GPU Kernel Optimization Agents
summary: GPU kernel optimization is increasingly critical for efficient deep learning systems, but writing high-performance kernels still requires substantial low-level expertise. Recent AI coding agents can iteratively read code, invoke compilers and profilers, and refine implementations, yet existing kernel benchmarks evaluate single LLM calls rather than full agent workflows, and none include both kernel-to-kernel optimization and unseen-configuration generalization testing. We present AgentKernelArena, an open-source benchmark for measuring AI coding agents on GPU kernel optimization. The benchmark contains 196 tasks spanning HIP-to-HIP optimization, Triton-to-Triton optimization, and PyTorch-to-HIP translation, and evaluates complete agent workflows in isolated workspaces using gated compilation, correctness, and performance checks, centralized scoring and an unseen-configuration generalization protocol that tests whether optimizations transfer to input configurations the agent never observed. Across production agents including Cursor Agent, Claude Code, and Codex Agent, we find near-perfect compilation and high correctness rates on most task categories, with the strongest configurations achieving mean speedups of up to 6.89x on PyTorch-to-HIP, 6.69x on HIP-to-HIP, and 2.13x on Triton-to-Triton tasks. Our unseen-configuration evaluation shows that HIP-to-HIP and Triton-to-Triton optimizations largely transfer to unseen input shapes, while PyTorch-to-HIP exhibits substantial correctness drops, indicating that agents generating kernels from scratch frequently hardcode shape-specific assumptions. AgentKernelArena is designed as a modular, extensible framework for rigorous evaluation of agentic GPU kernel optimization across agents, tasks, and hardware targets.

AI.tldr: AgentKernelArena is a benchmark to evaluate full AI coding agent workflows for GPU kernel optimization, including generalization to unseen configurations.
AI.motivation: Existing GPU kernel benchmarks focus on single LLM calls and do not assess full agentic workflows or whether optimizations generalize to unseen input configurations. There is a need for a standardized, extensible benchmark to rigorously evaluate AI coding agents on realistic GPU kernel optimization tasks.
AI.method: They design AgentKernelArena, an open-source benchmark with 196 tasks across HIP-to-HIP optimization, Triton-to-Triton optimization, and PyTorch-to-HIP translation. The framework runs agents in isolated workspaces with gated compilation, correctness, and performance checks, centralized scoring, and a specific protocol that evaluates whether optimizations learned on some input configurations transfer to unseen input shapes.
AI.result: On this benchmark, production AI coding agents (Cursor Agent, Claude Code, Codex Agent) achieve near-perfect compilation and high correctness for most categories. Strong configurations reach mean speedups up to 6.89x for PyTorch-to-HIP, 6.69x for HIP-to-HIP, and 2.13x for Triton-to-Triton tasks. Unseen-configuration tests show good generalization for HIP-to-HIP and Triton-to-Triton, but large correctness drops for PyTorch-to-HIP, revealing shape-specific assumptions in kernels generated from scratch.
AI.conclusion: AgentKernelArena provides a modular, extensible framework to rigorously evaluate agentic GPU kernel optimization across different agents, task types, and hardware. Results highlight both the promise of current agents in delivering substantial speedups and key weaknesses in generalization, especially when translating from high-level frameworks like PyTorch to low-level kernels.

title: TTE-Flash: Accelerating Reasoning-based Multimodal Representations via Think-Then-Embed Tokens
summary: Recent research has demonstrated that Universal Multimodal Embedding (UME) benefits significantly from Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. In this paradigm, a generative model produces explicit reasoning traces for a multimodal query, with the final representation extracted from an <eos> embedding token attending to both the query and the reasoning. Despite its effectiveness, the computational overhead of generating explicit CoT traces is often prohibitive. In this work, we propose replacing explicit CoT with latent think tokens, which are interpreted as latent variables that can produce explicit CoT traces as observed variables. By optimizing think tokens using CoT generation loss and subsequent embedding tokens using contrastive loss, we produce high-performance, reasoning-aware representations at a constant inference cost. Our study investigates two key architectural designs: 1) how think and embeddings tokens should be extracted from the same LLM backbone. 2) how the tokens should be trained as two dependent tasks. We introduce TTE-Flash-2B, a reasoning-aware multimodal representation model that outperforms its explicit-CoT counterpart on the MMEB-v2 benchmark, while producing latent think tokens that are interpretable both textually and visually. Furthermore, zero-shot evaluation across 15 video datasets reveals scaling behavior as the number of think tokens increases, and motivating a pilot study of adaptive think budget allocation based on task requirements.

AI.tldr: They replace expensive explicit Chain-of-Thought generation in universal multimodal embeddings with cheap latent “think tokens” that are trained to approximate CoT, achieving strong reasoning-aware representations at constant inference cost.
AI.motivation: Explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) greatly improves multimodal embeddings but requires generating long reasoning traces, which is computationally expensive and slows inference. The authors want embeddings that retain the reasoning benefits of CoT while keeping inference cost low and scalable, especially for large-scale multimodal understanding across images and video.
AI.method: They introduce latent “think tokens” inside a shared LLM backbone. These tokens are treated as latent variables that could generate explicit CoT traces. During training, they jointly optimize: (1) the think tokens using a CoT generation loss, forcing them to contain reasoning information, and (2) downstream embedding tokens with a contrastive loss for universal multimodal embedding. They explore how to architecturally place and extract think vs. embedding tokens from the same backbone, and how to train them as coupled tasks. The final model, TTE-Flash-2B, uses these latent think tokens to produce reasoning-aware multimodal representations without explicitly generating CoT at inference.
AI.result: TTE-Flash-2B achieves better performance than a baseline that uses explicit CoT on the MMEB-v2 multimodal benchmark, despite having constant inference cost. The learned think tokens are shown to be interpretable at both the text and visual levels. In zero-shot tests across 15 video datasets, performance scales as the number of think tokens increases, indicating that more latent reasoning capacity improves results and enabling an initial exploration of adaptively choosing how many think tokens to use per task.
AI.conclusion: Latent think tokens can effectively replace explicit Chain-of-Thought in universal multimodal embedding, preserving or even improving reasoning-aware performance while avoiding the high inference cost of generating reasoning traces. Jointly training think and embedding tokens in a shared backbone yields a compact, efficient model with interpretable latent reasoning and a controllable reasoning budget, as demonstrated by TTE-Flash-2B’s strong results on image and video benchmarks.

title: Constrained Code Generation with Discrete Diffusion
summary: Discrete diffusion models are a powerful, emerging paradigm for code generation. They construct programs through iterative refinement of partially corrupted token sequences and enable parallel token refinement. Importantly, this paradigm exposes a global program state at each denoising step, which provides a natural intervention point for enforcing program-level functionality and security constraints, guiding the generation before the final code is committed. Building on this observation, the paper introduces Constrained Diffusion for Code (CDC), a training-free neurosymbolic inference framework that integrates constraint satisfaction directly into the reverse denoising process. CDC augments the base discrete diffusion sampler with constraint-aware denoising operators that combine mathematical optimization with program analysis to identify constraint-relevant regions of the intermediate program state and locally adjust the denoising trajectory, steering generation toward feasible programs while remaining close to the base model. Across code generation benchmarks, CDC consistently improves constraint satisfaction in functional correctness, security, and even syntax, outperforming discrete diffusion and autoregressive baselines with less corrective computation and more localized edits.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CDC, a training‑free neurosymbolic framework that embeds constraint satisfaction into discrete diffusion code generation to better enforce functional, security, and syntactic constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing code generation models, including discrete diffusion models, struggle to reliably enforce high‑level constraints such as functional correctness, security properties, and syntactic validity, and typical constraint‑handling methods rely on post‑hoc correction or expensive search. The authors observe that discrete diffusion exposes a full intermediate program state at every denoising step, creating a natural opportunity to intervene during generation rather than after the fact.
AI.method: They introduce Constrained Diffusion for Code (CDC), which modifies the reverse denoising process of a discrete diffusion model via constraint‑aware denoising operators. These operators use program analysis and mathematical optimization to (1) detect which parts of the intermediate program state are relevant to the constraints and (2) locally adjust token distributions in those regions, redirecting the denoising trajectory toward constraint‑satisfying programs while keeping it close to the base model’s behavior. The approach is training‑free and operates purely at inference time as an augmented sampler.
AI.result: On standard code generation benchmarks, CDC yields higher rates of constraint satisfaction for functional correctness, security properties, and syntactic validity compared to both vanilla discrete diffusion models and strong autoregressive baselines. It achieves these gains with fewer corrective steps and more localized code edits than competing methods.
AI.conclusion: Integrating constraint satisfaction directly into the discrete diffusion denoising process, rather than relying on post‑generation fixes or retraining, is an effective way to guide code generation toward programs that satisfy functional, security, and syntactic constraints. CDC demonstrates that neurosymbolic, optimization‑guided interventions at intermediate diffusion steps can significantly improve reliability while preserving the strengths of the underlying diffusion model.

title: Sustainable Intelligence for the Wild: Democratizing Ecological Monitoring via Knowledge-Adaptive Edge Expert Agents
summary: Rapid biodiversity loss underscore the urgency of effective monitoring, yet manual surveys remain resource-intensive. While on-device AI offers a scalable alternative, its performance in the wild is often challenged by environmental variability. Current methods rely heavily on cloud resource, which requires continuous uploading of field data for model retraining. This approach is unsuitable for remote deployments because it consumes limited power and network connectivity. To address these constraints, this research proposes a shift from model adaptation to knowledge adaptation. We introduce an architecture that separates visual perception from reasoning, combining a visual encoder with a dynamic knowledge base. We uses an explicit knowledge base to replace implicitly encoding expert knowledge into model parameters. This method also supports knowledge sustainability by preserving expert insights in a structured form. Through cross-disciplinary collaboration with biologists and Indigenous communities, this work advances ethical AI co-development, fostering responsible and culturally informed ecosystem management.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a low-power, field-deployable biodiversity monitoring system that decouples perception from reasoning by using a visual encoder plus an explicit, updatable knowledge base instead of constant cloud retraining.
AI.motivation: Manual biodiversity surveys are costly and slow, and current AI-based monitoring solutions depend heavily on cloud connectivity for model updates, which is impractical for remote locations with limited power and network access. There is also a need for ethically grounded, cross-cultural knowledge integration in ecological monitoring.
AI.method: Design an on-device AI architecture that separates visual perception from high-level reasoning: a lightweight visual encoder runs on edge devices to extract features from field data, while an explicit, structured knowledge base encodes expert and community knowledge for downstream reasoning and decision-making. Knowledge, rather than model weights, is updated and maintained, enabling adaptation without continuous cloud retraining.
AI.result: The abstract implies that the architecture can operate in resource-constrained remote environments while remaining adaptable, and that expert and Indigenous knowledge can be preserved and integrated in a structured, sustainable way. Specific empirical results are not provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Shifting from model adaptation to knowledge adaptation with a separated perception–reasoning architecture offers a more sustainable, scalable, and ethically grounded approach to biodiversity monitoring in remote settings, enabling long-term maintenance of expert insights and responsible ecosystem management.

title: CompactAttention: Accelerating Chunked Prefill with Block-Union KV Selection
summary: Chunked prefill has become a widely adopted serving strategy for long-context large language models, but efficient attention computation in this regime remains challenging. Existing sparse attention methods are primarily designed for one-shot prefill and do not translate efficiently to chunked prefill: block-sparse kernels lose efficiency when the query length is limited by the chunk size, while fine-grained pattern search becomes costly when repeated over the accumulated KV cache at every chunk. QUOKA, a recent method that directly targets chunked prefill, avoids sparse-kernel overhead but relies on query-subsampled, token-level KV selection, which can miss query-specific KV entries and introduce explicit KV-copy overhead. To address these limitations, we propose CompactAttention, a chunked-prefill attention mechanism based on Block-Union KV Selection. CompactAttention treats 2D block-sparse masks as KV-selection signals rather than direct sparse-kernel execution plans, and converts them into GQA-aware per-group KV block tables through Q-block union and intra-group union. This construction produces the minimal block tables that preserve all KV blocks selected by the input masks under paged execution constraints, enabling selected KV blocks to be accessed in place without explicit KV compaction. On LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct, CompactAttention maintains accuracy close to dense attention on the RULER benchmark while delivering up to 2.72$\times$ attention speedup at 128K context length under chunked prefill.

AI.tldr: CompactAttention is a new attention mechanism for long-context LLMs that makes chunked prefill both fast and accurate by turning block-sparse masks into minimal KV-block lookup tables instead of executing them directly as sparse kernels.
AI.motivation: Chunked prefill is widely used to serve long-context LLMs, but existing sparse attention methods are inefficient in this setting. Block-sparse kernels underperform when each chunk has a short query length, and fine-grained token-level sparsity requires repeated expensive pattern searches over growing KV caches. QUOKA targets chunked prefill but may miss important keys/values and needs explicit KV copying, hurting performance. A method is needed that preserves accuracy like dense attention while gaining speed in chunked prefill without costly KV movement or search.
AI.method: The paper introduces CompactAttention, built on Block-Union KV Selection. Instead of using 2D block-sparse attention masks as direct execution plans for sparse kernels, CompactAttention interprets them as signals indicating which KV blocks to keep. It then constructs GQA-aware per-group KV block tables via two unions: Q-block union (aggregating KV blocks needed across queries in a block) and intra-group union (merging selections within each GQA group). The resulting minimal block tables satisfy paged execution constraints so that all selected KV blocks can be accessed in place without explicitly compacting or copying KV memory.
AI.result: On LLaMA-3.1-8B-Instruct evaluated with the RULER long-context benchmark, CompactAttention achieves accuracy close to dense attention while offering up to 2.72× attention computation speedup at 128K context length in the chunked prefill regime.
AI.conclusion: CompactAttention effectively adapts sparse-style attention to chunked prefill by separating selection from execution: block-sparse masks guide which KV blocks to retain, which are then organized into minimal, GQA-aware block tables that enable in-place access under paged execution. This design avoids the inefficiencies of traditional sparse kernels and the overheads or accuracy losses of token-level selection, yielding substantial speedups with minimal accuracy degradation for long-context LLM serving.

title: LinAlg-Bench: A Forensic Benchmark Revealing Structural Failure Modes in LLM Mathematical Reasoning
comment: 42 pages, 3 figures, 12 tables. NeurIPS 2026 Evaluations and Datasets Track submission. Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/LinAlgBench/linalg-bench
summary: We introduce LinAlg-Bench, a diagnostic benchmark evaluating 10 frontier large language models on structured linear algebra computation across a strict dimensional gradient of 3x3, 4x4, and 5x5 matrices. Spanning 9 task types and 660 SymPy-certified problems, the benchmark exhaustively evaluates 6,600 model outputs. Beyond binary accuracy, LinAlg-Bench introduces a three-stage automated forensic pipeline classifying 1,156 failures into ten primary error tags with fine-grained subtypes, revealing that LLM mathematical failure is not random but structurally constrained by algorithm type and matrix dimension. Our central finding is a sharp behavioral threshold at 4x4 scale: below it, models fail through execution errors -- sign tracking failures, arithmetic drift, and parity errors; above it, failure transitions to computational abandonment, with models fabricating responses through tool roleplay, constraint-consistent confabulation, and structured hallucination rather than attempting computation. This fabrication-to-abandonment transition is near-universal across all model tiers and architectures, suggesting a working memory limit rather than a knowledge gap, supported by three scale-emergent error types absent at 3x3 but present at 4x4 and 5x5. We further show that solution strategy rigidity is a near-perfect predictor of 5x5 determinant accuracy, document constraint-aware confabulation as a novel structured hallucination failure mode, and release all data, model outputs, error labels, and judge pipeline publicly.

AI.tldr: LinAlg-Bench evaluates how frontier LLMs perform on structured linear algebra tasks with increasing matrix sizes and reveals systematic, scale-dependent failure patterns rather than random mistakes.
AI.motivation: To rigorously diagnose and understand how and why large language models fail at concrete, small-scale linear algebra computations, especially as task dimensionality grows from 3x3 to 5x5 matrices.
AI.method: The authors build a benchmark of 660 SymPy-verified linear algebra problems over 3x3, 4x4, and 5x5 matrices, covering 9 task types. They run 10 leading LLMs, yielding 6,600 outputs, and then analyze not just accuracy but also error types via an automated three-stage forensic pipeline that tags failures into ten major categories with subtypes, focusing on structural patterns related to algorithm type and dimension.
AI.result: They find a strong scale threshold at 4x4 matrices: for 3x3 tasks, errors are mostly execution-level arithmetic and sign mistakes, while for 4x4 and 5x5, models increasingly abandon genuine computation and instead fabricate answers via tool roleplay, constraint-consistent confabulation, and other structured hallucinations. This pattern is consistent across architectures and model tiers, suggesting a working memory bottleneck rather than missing knowledge, and they identify new error types that emerge only at higher dimensions. They also show that the rigidity of a model’s solution strategy is highly predictive of whether it will correctly compute 5x5 determinants.
