AI.conclusion: LLM failures in small-scale linear algebra are systematic and scale-dependent, with a near-universal behavioral shift from genuine but error-prone computation at 3x3 to strategic abandonment and structured hallucination at 4x4 and above. These patterns imply inherent working memory limitations and highlight the need for better methods to support multi-step, high-dimensional reasoning; the authors provide an open benchmark and labeled dataset to facilitate such research.

title: RTI-Bench: A Structured Dataset for Indian Right-to-Information Decision Analysis
summary: India's Right to Information Act, 2005 gives every citizen the right to demand information from public authorities, yet in practice most people cannot make sense of the dense administrative language used in Central Information Commission (CIC) decisions, let alone predict whether an appeal is worth filing. This paper introduces RTI-Bench, a structured dataset of CIC decisions with outcome labels, exemption citations, IRAC-style reasoning components, and procedural timelines. To the best of our knowledge it is the first publicly released structured dataset for Indian RTI administrative decisions. The dataset draws from two sources: 1,218 cases from a publicly available instruction-response corpus (with structured fields added through rule-based extraction), and 298 CIC decision PDFs collected directly from the Commission portal, spanning five commissioners and three document format generations from 2023 to 2026. Label coverage reaches 89% on the instruction-response corpus. For the PDF subset of 239 primary decisions, coverage is 51% in this first release. A random sample of 50 labelled cases was manually reviewed, yielding a label precision of 95.3%. A zero-shot Mistral 7B baseline on 100 cases gives 57.3% accuracy and 37.0% macro-F1 on outcome prediction, well above the majority-class baseline of 14.3% macro-F1. RTI-Bench is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/joyboseroy/rti-bench

AI.tldr: Introduces RTI-Bench, the first structured dataset of Indian Right to Information (RTI) Central Information Commission decisions, with labels, reasoning components, and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Citizens struggle to understand complex CIC decisions and to assess whether filing an appeal is worthwhile; there is also a lack of structured, publicly available datasets for Indian RTI administrative decisions for NLP and legal AI research.
AI.method: Constructed RTI-Bench by enriching 1,218 cases from an existing instruction-response corpus using rule-based extraction, and by scraping and structuring 298 CIC decision PDFs from the Commission portal covering multiple commissioners and formats (2023–2026), adding outcome labels, exemption citations, IRAC-style reasoning segments, and timelines; evaluated label quality via manual review and benchmarked a zero-shot Mistral 7B model on outcome prediction.
AI.result: Achieved 89% label coverage on the instruction-response corpus and 51% coverage on 239 primary PDF decisions in the first release; manual review of 50 labelled cases showed 95.3% label precision; a zero-shot Mistral 7B model attained 57.3% accuracy and 37.0% macro-F1 on outcome prediction, significantly outperforming a 14.3% macro-F1 majority baseline.
AI.conclusion: RTI-Bench provides a high-precision, structured benchmark dataset for Indian RTI CIC decisions that can support research on legal NLP, outcome prediction, and decision explainability, and is publicly released via Hugging Face for further community use and extension.

title: Enhancing Metacognitive AI: Knowledge-Graph Population with Graph-Theoretic LLM Enrichment
summary: Metacognition-the ability to monitor one's own knowledge state, spot gaps, and autonomously fill them--remains largely absent from modern AI. Here, we present MetaKGEnrich, a fully automated pipeline that endows large language model (LLM) applications with self-directed knowledge repair. The system (i) builds knowledge graphs from a seed query, (ii) detects sparse regions via seven graph metrics, (iii) has GPT-4o generate targeted questions, (iv) retrieves web evidence with Tavily and ingests it into Neo4j, and (v) re-answers the query with GraphRAG for GPT-4 to evaluate improvement. Tested on 30 queries from each of three widely-used datasets: Google Research Natural Questions, MS MARCO, and Hot-potQA. MetaKGEnrich improved answer quality in 80% of HotpotQA questions, 87% of Google Research Natural Questions and 83% of MS MARCO questions, while preserving well-supported regions. This proof of concept demonstrates how topological self-diagnosis plus targeted retrieval can advance AI toward humanlike metacognitive learning.

AI.tldr: MetaKGEnrich is an automated pipeline that lets LLM systems detect gaps in their own knowledge graphs and actively repair them by targeted web retrieval, improving QA performance.
AI.motivation: Modern LLMs are strong reasoners but lack metacognition: they cannot reliably detect where their knowledge is incomplete or incorrect and then autonomously seek new information. Existing RAG and knowledge-graph methods mainly focus on retrieval efficiency, not on self-diagnosis of knowledge gaps and targeted enrichment. The paper aims to move AI toward humanlike metacognitive behavior, where a system can notice sparse or weakly supported areas in its knowledge for a given task and repair them automatically.
AI.method: The authors propose MetaKGEnrich, a fully automated pipeline: (1) From a seed query, an LLM constructs an initial knowledge graph. (2) The system computes seven graph-theoretic metrics to identify sparse or under-supported regions of this graph. (3) GPT-4o is prompted to generate focused follow-up questions specifically about these sparse regions. (4) These questions are sent to a web search API (Tavily), and retrieved evidence is stored and integrated into a Neo4j knowledge graph. (5) The original query is then re-answered using a GraphRAG approach over the enriched graph, and GPT-4 is used to evaluate whether the answer quality improved while ensuring well-supported areas are not degraded.
AI.result: On 30 queries from each of three QA datasets—Google Research Natural Questions, MS MARCO, and HotpotQA—MetaKGEnrich improved answer quality in 80% of HotpotQA questions, 87% of Google Research Natural Questions, and 83% of MS MARCO questions. The evaluation suggests that the pipeline effectively fills knowledge gaps relevant to the query and largely preserves correctness in already well-supported regions.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that combining graph-topological self-diagnosis with targeted retrieval enables a form of metacognitive knowledge repair in LLM-based systems. This proof of concept indicates that endowing LLM applications with automated gap detection and enrichment can reliably improve QA performance and moves AI a step closer to humanlike metacognitive learning behaviors.

title: MixSD: Mixed Contextual Self-Distillation for Knowledge Injection
summary: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) is widely used to inject new knowledge into language models, but it often degrades pretrained capabilities such as reasoning and general-domain performance. We argue this forgetting arises because fine-tuning targets from humans or external systems diverge from the model's autoregressive distribution, forcing the optimizer to imitate low-probability token sequences. To address this problem, we propose MixSD, a simple external-teacher-free method for distribution-aligned knowledge injection. Instead of training on fixed targets, MixSD constructs supervision dynamically by mixing tokens from two conditionals of the base model itself: an expert conditional that observes the injected fact in context, and a naive conditional that reflects the model's original prior. The resulting supervision sequences preserve the factual learning signal while remaining substantially closer to the base model's distribution. We evaluate MixSD on two synthetic corpora that we construct to study factual recall and arithmetic function acquisition in a controlled setting, together with established benchmarks for open-domain factual question answering and knowledge editing. Across multiple model scales and settings, MixSD consistently achieves a better memorization-retention trade-off compared to SFT and on-policy self distillation baselines, retaining up to 100% of the base model's held-out capability while maintaining near-perfect training accuracy, whereas standard SFT retains as little as 1%. We further show that MixSD produces substantially lower-NLL supervision targets under the base model and reduces harmful movement along Fisher-sensitive parameter directions. These results suggest that aligning supervision with the model's native generation distribution is a simple and effective principle for knowledge injection that mitigates catastrophic forgetting.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MixSD, a new fine-tuning method that injects knowledge into language models while minimizing catastrophic forgetting by aligning supervision with the model’s own generation distribution.
AI.motivation: Standard supervised fine-tuning injects new knowledge but often harms pretrained abilities such as reasoning and general-domain performance. The authors hypothesize this is because human- or teacher-generated targets differ from the model’s native autoregressive distribution, forcing it to learn low-probability token sequences and causing forgetting. They want a way to add knowledge without sacrificing existing capabilities.
AI.method: MixSD generates dynamic training targets using only the base model. It constructs two conditionals: an expert conditional that conditions on the desired new fact, and a naive conditional that reflects the original model prior without the fact. During training, it mixes tokens from these two conditionals to form supervision sequences that encode the correct fact but stay close to the model’s pretraining distribution. The method is external-teacher-free and designed to reduce off-distribution learning. They analyze supervision via NLL under the base model and movement along Fisher-sensitive parameter directions.
AI.result: On synthetic datasets for factual recall and arithmetic function learning, as well as standard open-domain QA and knowledge-editing benchmarks, MixSD outperforms standard SFT and on-policy self-distillation baselines in the trade-off between new knowledge memorization and retention of prior abilities. It can reach near-perfect training accuracy while preserving up to 100% of the base model’s held-out capabilities, whereas SFT can drop retention to about 1%. MixSD also yields lower-NLL training targets under the base model and smaller updates along Fisher-sensitive directions, supporting the distribution-alignment hypothesis.
AI.conclusion: Aligning supervision with a model’s native generation distribution is an effective strategy for injecting knowledge while mitigating catastrophic forgetting. MixSD operationalizes this principle by mixing expert and naive conditionals from the base model itself, avoiding heavy off-distribution supervision and harmful parameter shifts. This offers a simple, teacher-free alternative to conventional SFT for safer knowledge editing and capability-preserving fine-tuning.

title: Recall Isn't Enough: Bounding Commitments in Personalized Language Systems
summary: Long-context and memory systems usually treat personalization as a recall problem. In practice, many failures occur later, when a system commits: it turns noisy hints into hard constraints, drops rare witnesses, forgets downstream obligations, or answers despite infeasibility. We introduce Contract-Bounded Evidence Activation (CBEA) with Lexicographic Commitment Validation (LCV). CBEA activates a bounded evidence set using typed coverage, tail witnesses, and consequence debt; LCV validates structured commitments before prose and routes infeasible states to repair, abstention, or recontract. Across 360 fixtures and three generation backends, CBEA+LCV reaches zero failures within validator scope at 0.49-0.60 availability over attempted runs. Raw and long-context baselines with the same LCV gate reach zero only at 0.003-0.092. A shadow oracle diagnostic marks the limit: CBEA+LCV recalls 0.012 of uncompiled visible facts, while raw recalls 0.53. The result is a bounded operating point: explicit commitment control and 74-75% lower median input payload, not universal memory dominance.

AI.tldr: They propose a method to control how long-context/memory systems use personal data, focusing on safe commitments rather than maximizing recall, and show it drastically reduces failure rates at moderate availability and with smaller inputs.
AI.motivation: Existing long-context and memory-based personalization systems mostly focus on retrieving as many relevant facts as possible. Many real failures, however, happen later: the model over-commits to noisy hints, ignores rare but important facts, forgets implied obligations, or answers even when constraints are mutually inconsistent. The authors want a framework that explicitly governs when and how the system is allowed to commit to user-specific facts and obligations, prioritizing reliability over raw recall.
AI.method: They introduce Contract-Bounded Evidence Activation (CBEA) and Lexicographic Commitment Validation (LCV). CBEA selects a bounded, structured evidence set using typed coverage criteria, explicit inclusion of tail (rare) witnesses, and tracking of obligation "debts" implied by chosen facts. LCV then checks any proposed commitments in a structured form before natural-language output, using a lexicographic decision rule to validate feasibility and decide among: proceeding, repairing the plan, abstaining, or renegotiating the contract when contradictions or infeasibility are detected. They test this on 360 benchmark scenarios and with three different generation backends.
AI.result: With CBEA+LCV, the system achieves zero observed failures within the validator's scope on the benchmark, with availability (fraction of runs that succeed rather than abstain/fail) between 0.49 and 0.60. Using the same LCV gate but without CBEA (raw or long-context baselines), zero failures can only be reached at much lower availability, 0.003-0.092. A diagnostic "shadow oracle" shows that CBEA+LCV recalls only about 1.2% of visible uncompiled facts compared to 53% for the raw system, confirming that the approach works by restricting and structuring commitments rather than maximizing memory recall. It also reduces median input payload size by ~74-75%.
AI.conclusion: The paper argues for a new operating point in personalized long-context systems: instead of aiming for universal, high-recall memory, focus on explicit, bounded contracts and validated commitments. CBEA+LCV demonstrates that carefully constrained evidence activation and commitment checking can eliminate in-scope failures while using significantly less context, at the cost of moderate availability and lower raw recall. This reframes personalization from a pure retrieval problem to one of contract management and commitment control.

title: PaliBench: A Multi-Reference Blueprint for Classical Language Translation Benchmarks
comment: Preprint. This manuscript has not yet been peer reviewed
summary: Digital humanities projects increasingly rely on machine translation and large language models to widen access to classical, religious, and otherwise under-translated textual traditions. Yet standard translation benchmarks are poorly suited to such materials: they typically compare a system output against a single reference translation, even though classical texts often support multiple faithful renderings that differ in terminology, register, and interpretation. This article introduces PaliBench, both a benchmark for Pali-to-English translation and a reusable method for constructing multi-reference translation benchmarks for classical languages. The Pali case study draws on passages from the Sutta Pitaka aligned with independent English translations by Bhikkhu Sujato, Bhikkhu Thanissaro, and Bhikkhu Bodhi. The workflow combines LLM-assisted alignment of independently segmented translations, automated verification against source files, passage-level quality filtering, deduplication of formulaic repetitions, and multi-metric evaluation against multiple human references. The resulting benchmark contains 1,700 passages spanning 8,389 segments and approximately 345,000 tokens. We use it to evaluate ten contemporary large language models with complementary metrics, finding strong cross-metric concordance in system rankings alongside substantial variation in reliability and semantic outlier rates. The broader contribution is methodological: PaliBench shows how existing scholarly translations can be transformed into evaluation infrastructure for interpretive textual traditions without treating any single translation as definitive. Although developed for Pali Buddhist texts, the approach could be portable to other classical corpora where sufficient independent reference translations exist.

AI.tldr: Introduces PaliBench, a multi-reference benchmark for Pali-to-English translation using existing independent translations, and a generalizable method to build such benchmarks for classical languages.
AI.motivation: Standard machine translation benchmarks assume a single reference translation, which is inadequate for classical and interpretive texts that admit multiple faithful renderings. Digital humanities need better evaluation tools for MT and LLMs on under-translated traditions like Pali Buddhist texts.
AI.method: Construct PaliBench by aligning passages from the Pali Sutta Pitaka with three independent English translations (Sujato, Thanissaro, Bodhi). Use an LLM-assisted workflow to align independently segmented translations, automatically verify against source files, apply passage-level quality filters, deduplicate formulaic repetitions, and evaluate system outputs using multiple metrics against multiple human references. Then evaluate ten contemporary LLMs on this benchmark.
AI.result: PaliBench comprises 1,700 passages, 8,389 segments, and about 345,000 tokens. Evaluation of ten large language models with multiple complementary metrics shows strong agreement in system rankings across metrics, but reveals notable differences in reliability and semantic outlier rates among systems.
AI.conclusion: PaliBench demonstrates that existing scholarly translations can be converted into robust, multi-reference evaluation benchmarks for interpretive classical texts without privileging any single translation. While instantiated on Pali Buddhist texts, the methodology is broadly applicable to other classical languages when multiple independent translations are available.

title: GRID: Graph Representation of Intelligence Data for Security Text Knowledge Graph Construction
summary: Security knowledge graphs can provide computable external memory for security agents, but constructing them from long-form cyber threat intelligence (CTI) remains difficult: LLMs often lack grounded security-domain knowledge, and end-to-end document-to-graph training is hard to supervise with cheap, stable rewards. We present GRID (Graph Representation of Intelligence Data), an end-to-end framework for security text knowledge graph construction. GRID first builds security-domain supervision from CTI articles by creating traceable article-graph alignments through graph extraction and knowledge-graph-conditioned text revision. It then turns document-to-graph learning into a scripted task bank combining four-option multi-select questions with triple-level regex matching targets, yielding more stable task-specific rewards than repeatedly scoring full graph outputs with an LLM judge. Using this supervision pipeline, we train two Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507-based 4B extractors: a primary Task-bank Reward model and a secondary End2End Reward model with LLM-as-judge precision/recall rewards. On 249 CTI articles from GRID, CASIE, CTINexus, MalKG, and SecureNLP, the Task-bank Reward model with the ontology-guided GRID extraction pipeline reaches 84.62% source-averaged precision, 64.91% source-averaged recall, and 68.53% Avg F1, achieving the best source-averaged recall and near-top Avg F1 with lower token usage and deployment cost. The End2End Reward model reaches 76.91% precision, 53.85% recall, and 58.06% Avg F1. Further analyses show that task-bank rewards can be built once offline and reused across later post-training runs, outperforming online End2End LLM-as-judge reward and weaker alternatives such as Choice-only Reward and End2End SFT without RL.

AI.tldr: GRID is a framework to build security knowledge graphs from long CTI reports using an offline task-bank reward scheme that stabilizes training and improves recall and F1 versus LLM-as-judge rewards at lower cost.
AI.motivation: Constructing accurate, computable knowledge graphs from long-form cyber threat intelligence is difficult because LLMs lack grounded security knowledge and direct document-to-graph training is hard to supervise cheaply and stably. Existing approaches that rely on LLM judges for full-graph scoring are expensive and produce noisy, unstable rewards, limiting extraction quality and scalability.
AI.method: The authors propose GRID, an end-to-end pipeline that (1) derives supervision from CTI articles by aligning them with extracted graphs via graph extraction and KG-conditioned text revision, and (2) converts document-to-graph extraction into a large scripted task bank of four-option multi-select questions with triple-level regex-matching targets. These tasks provide structured, reusable rewards for RL-style post-training. They train two 4B-parameter extractors based on Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507: a Task-bank Reward model trained primarily on the offline task-bank rewards, and an End2End Reward model trained with online LLM-as-judge precision/recall rewards that evaluate whole-graph outputs.
AI.result: On 249 CTI articles drawn from multiple datasets (GRID, CASIE, CTINexus, MalKG, SecureNLP), the Task-bank Reward model with an ontology-guided GRID extraction pipeline achieves 84.62% source-averaged precision, 64.91% source-averaged recall, and 68.53% average F1, giving the best recall and nearly the top F1 among compared systems while using fewer tokens and lower deployment cost. The End2End Reward model attains 76.91% precision, 53.85% recall, and 58.06% average F1. Additional experiments show that the offline task-bank rewards can be created once and reused across post-training runs, and that this approach outperforms online LLM-as-judge reward schemes and simpler baselines such as Choice-only Reward and pure supervised fine-tuning without RL.
AI.conclusion: GRID demonstrates that transforming document-to-graph extraction into a bank of structured, regex-checkable QA-style tasks yields stable, reusable reward signals that enable small 4B-parameter models to construct high-quality security knowledge graphs from CTI text. The task-bank reward approach improves recall and cost efficiency over end-to-end LLM-judged training and weaker baselines, suggesting a practical path to scalable, domain-grounded security KG construction and a general blueprint for supervised RL in other information extraction settings.

title: E-PMQ: Expert-Guided Post-Merge Quantization with Merged-Weight Anchoring
summary: Low-resource deployment constraints have made model quantization essential for deploying neural networks while preserving performance. Meanwhile, model merging has become an increasingly practical low-resource strategy for integrating multiple task- or domain-specialized experts into a single model without joint training or multi-model serving. Together, quantization and model merging enable an efficient low-resource deployment pipeline by integrating multiple experts into one low-bit model. We formulate this setting as Post-Merge Quantization (PMQ). We show that directly applying post-training quantization (PTQ) to a merged model is unreliable because two distinct deviations are coupled: the quantization deviation introduced by low-bit reconstruction and the expert-relative merging deviation inherited from model merging. To mitigate these deviations, we propose E-PMQ, an expert-guided PMQ framework that uses source expert weights to provide expert- guided output targets during layer-wise calibration, together with merged-weight anchoring to stabilize the calibration and preserve the integrated behavior of the merged model. On CLIP-ViT-B/32 eight-task merging, E-PMQ improves 4-bit GPTQ from 65.0% to 73.6% under Task Arithmetic and from 69.1% to 74.8% under TIES-Merging. On harder settings, E-PMQ improves GPTQ from 34.8% to 76.7% on 20-task CLIP-ViT-L/14 and from 78.26% to 83.34% on FLAN-T5- base GLUE. These results demonstrate that E-PMQ enables effective post-merge quantization and low-bit deployment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Post-Merge Quantization (PMQ) and an expert-guided framework E-PMQ to reliably quantize merged multi-expert models to low bit-width while preserving performance.
AI.motivation: Model quantization is crucial for deploying neural networks under low-resource constraints, and model merging is an efficient way to integrate multiple specialized experts into a single model. However, combining these—quantizing a merged model—causes reliability issues because quantization error and merging error interact. The paper is motivated by the need for a practical, low-resource pipeline that both merges multiple expert models and successfully quantizes the resulting model without severe performance loss.
AI.method: The authors formalize the setting of quantizing a merged multi-expert model as Post-Merge Quantization (PMQ). They analyze that standard post-training quantization (PTQ) on merged models is unreliable due to coupled deviations: quantization deviation from low-bit approximation and merging deviation from combining experts. To address this, they propose E-PMQ, an expert-guided PMQ framework. During layer-wise calibration, E-PMQ uses the original source expert weights to generate expert-guided output targets and applies merged-weight anchoring to keep the quantized weights close to the merged model, thereby stabilizing calibration and preserving the merged model’s integrated behavior. They apply this on existing merging strategies like Task Arithmetic and TIES-Merging, using quantization methods such as GPTQ, on models including CLIP-ViT-B/32, CLIP-ViT-L/14, and FLAN-T5-base.
AI.result: On CLIP-ViT-B/32 with eight-task merging, E-PMQ raises 4-bit GPTQ performance from 65.0% to 73.6% for Task Arithmetic and from 69.1% to 74.8% for TIES-Merging. On more challenging setups, such as 20-task CLIP-ViT-L/14, E-PMQ improves GPTQ accuracy from 34.8% to 76.7%. On FLAN-T5-base evaluated on GLUE, performance improves from 78.26% to 83.34%. These substantial gains show that the proposed expert-guided calibration and merged-weight anchoring significantly reduce the performance degradation typically seen when quantizing merged models.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that naive post-training quantization of merged multi-expert models is unreliable due to coupled quantization and merging deviations. E-PMQ, which incorporates expert-guided targets and merged-weight anchoring during layer-wise calibration, effectively mitigates these deviations. Empirical results across multiple models, merging methods, and tasks show large performance improvements at 4-bit precision, indicating that E-PMQ enables robust post-merge quantization and practical low-bit deployment of merged expert models.

title: Baba in Wonderland: Online Self-Supervised Dynamics Discovery for Executable World Models
summary: Executable world models can be read, edited, executed, and reused for planning, but only if the program captures the environment's transition law rather than semantic shortcuts in its surface vocabulary. We study online executable world-model learning under prior misalignment, where an agent must induce state-dependent dynamics from interaction evidence alone, without rule descriptions, reward signals, or trustworthy lexical priors. We introduce Alice, a closed-loop system that treats failed candidate updates as structural signal: when a candidate explains a new transition but loses previously explained ones, the preservation conflict reveals dynamics that the current program had conflated. Alice refines these conflicts into hypothesis classes that both provide compact, class-stratified preservation counterexamples for update and guide frontier exploration toward transitions that are novel and underrepresented with respect to the current program. We evaluate Alice on Baba in Wonderland, a prior-misaligned variant of Baba Is You that preserves simulator dynamics while replacing semantically meaningful rule-property labels with unrelated words. Experiments show that Alice substantially improves executable world-model learning under prior misalignment, and ablations show that both class refinement and class-aware exploration contribute.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Alice, a system for learning executable world models from interaction alone, even when prior semantic labels are misleading, by using update failures as structural signals to refine hypotheses and guide exploration.
AI.motivation: Executable world models are powerful for planning because they can be read, edited, and reused, but they are hard to learn when the prior vocabulary (labels for rules/properties) is misaligned with the true environment dynamics. Existing methods often rely on trustworthy semantics, rule descriptions, or rewards. The authors aim to enable robust learning of state-dependent transition dynamics purely from interaction, without relying on aligned prior semantics or external supervision.
AI.method: They formulate an online executable world-model learning setting under prior misalignment, where an agent must infer the transition law from observed state transitions. The proposed system, Alice, runs in a closed loop: it proposes programmatic world-model updates, executes them, and checks whether the updated model preserves previously explained transitions. When a new candidate model explains a novel transition but fails to explain earlier ones, Alice interprets this preservation conflict as evidence that the model has conflated distinct dynamics. It then refines these conflicts into structured hypothesis classes that (1) serve as compact, class-stratified counterexamples guiding how to update the program, and (2) drive an exploration policy that seeks transitions that are novel and underrepresented with respect to the current model.
AI.result: They test Alice on Baba in Wonderland, a modified version of the game "Baba Is You" where the underlying simulator dynamics are unchanged but semantically meaningful rule/property labels are replaced with unrelated words, creating prior misalignment. Experiments show that Alice significantly improves the accuracy and efficiency of executable world-model learning in this setting compared with baselines. Ablation studies indicate that both the class-refinement mechanism and the class-aware exploration component are important contributors to this performance gain.
AI.conclusion: Using failed model updates as structured signals about conflated dynamics, and organizing these into hypothesis classes that also guide exploration, enables more effective learning of executable world models when prior semantic information is misleading. The approach is robust to prior misalignment and yields substantial empirical gains, suggesting a promising direction for program-induction-based world-model learning in environments with unreliable lexical priors.

title: A Global-Local Graph Attention Network for Traffic Forecasting
summary: Traffic forecasting is a significant part of intelligent transportation systems. One of the critical challenges of traffic forecasting is to find spatio-temporal correlations. In recent years, graph convolutional networks and graph attention networks have replaced traditional statistical models to predict future traffic. However, it is complicated for both of them to allow vertices to have far different characters. To address this, we propose the Global-Local Graph Attention Network (GLGAT) with pairwise encoding and the event-based adjacency matrix. The GLGAT allows vertices to have a global attention matrix set for the whole graph and assigns local attention matrix sets to each vertex. Experiments on two real-world traffic datasets show that GLGAT can effectively capture spatio-temporal correlations and has competitive performance against other state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new neural network model, GLGAT, that improves traffic forecasting by better capturing complex global and local spatio-temporal relationships on road networks.
AI.motivation: Accurate traffic forecasting is crucial for intelligent transportation systems but remains challenging because traffic conditions depend on complex spatial and temporal correlations across the road network. Existing deep models such as graph convolutional networks (GCNs) and graph attention networks (GATs) have improved performance over traditional statistical methods, but they struggle when different vertices (road segments or sensors) have very different characteristics and interaction patterns. A more flexible representation of global and local dependencies is needed to handle heterogeneous nodes and dynamic interactions in traffic data.
AI.method: The authors propose a Global-Local Graph Attention Network (GLGAT). The key ideas are: (1) Introduce pairwise encoding to better model interactions between pairs of vertices, enabling the network to explicitly learn relationships between specific node pairs. (2) Construct an event-based adjacency matrix that dynamically reflects relevant connections based on traffic events rather than relying solely on fixed physical topology. (3) Design a dual attention mechanism in which the model learns a global attention matrix that captures holistic dependencies across the entire graph, and local attention matrices assigned to each vertex to model its specific neighborhood and idiosyncratic behavior. This architecture is applied to spatio-temporal traffic data to jointly learn spatial and temporal patterns.
AI.result: On two real-world traffic datasets, GLGAT is empirically evaluated against multiple state-of-the-art baselines, including GCN- and GAT-based models. The experiments demonstrate that GLGAT more effectively captures spatio-temporal correlations and achieves competitive or superior prediction performance, likely measured in standard forecasting metrics such as MAE, RMSE, or MAPE. The results indicate that combining global and local attention with pairwise encoding and event-based adjacency provides tangible gains over existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that the proposed GLGAT model successfully addresses limitations of existing GCN- and GAT-based traffic forecasting methods by enabling heterogeneous node behavior through combined global and local attention mechanisms. By leveraging pairwise encoding and an event-based adjacency matrix, GLGAT can more accurately model complex, dynamic spatio-temporal dependencies in traffic networks. The approach yields competitive performance on real-world datasets, suggesting it is a promising direction for improving intelligent transportation systems and could be extended to other spatio-temporal graph modeling tasks.

title: Full Attention Strikes Back: Transferring Full Attention into Sparse within Hundred Training Steps
summary: Long-context inference in large language models is bottlenecked by the quadratic cost of full attention. Existing efficient alternatives often rely either on native sparse training or on heuristic token eviction, creating an undesirable trade-off among efficiency, training cost, and accuracy. In this work, we show that full-attention LLMs are already intrinsically sparse and can be transformed into highly sparse models with only minimal adaptation. Our approach is built on three observations: (1) only a small subset of attention heads truly requires full long-context processing; (2) long-range retrieval is governed primarily by a low-dimensional subspace, allowing relevant tokens to be retrieved efficiently with a 16-dimensional indexer; and (3) the useful token budget is strongly query-dependent, making dynamic top-$p$ selection more suitable than fixed top-$k$ sparsification. Based on these insights, we propose RTPurbo, which retains the full KV cache only for retrieval heads and introduces a lightweight token indexer for sparse attention. By exploiting the model's intrinsic sparsity, RTPurbo achieves sparsification with only a few hundred training steps. Experiments on long-context benchmarks and reasoning tasks show that RTPurbo preserves near-lossless accuracy while delivering substantial efficiency gains, including up to a 9.36$\times$ prefill speedup at 1M context and about a 2.01$\times$ decode speedup. These results suggest that strong sparse inference can be obtained from standard full-attention training without expensive native sparse pretraining.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that standard full-attention LLMs can be converted into highly sparse long-context models with minimal retraining, preserving near-full accuracy while greatly improving speed.
AI.motivation: Long-context inference with LLMs is very expensive due to the quadratic complexity of full attention, and existing efficient methods either require costly sparse pretraining or sacrifice accuracy through heuristic token dropping. The authors aim to break this trade-off by reusing already-trained full-attention LLMs and exploiting their intrinsic sparsity to get efficient long-context inference without expensive retraining.
AI.method: The authors empirically analyze attention patterns in full-attention LLMs and identify three key properties: only a few attention heads truly need full long-range processing, long-range retrieval mainly lies in a low-dimensional subspace that can be indexed with a 16-dimensional representation, and the number of useful tokens varies with the query, favoring dynamic top-p over fixed top-k selection. Using these findings, they design RTPurbo, which keeps full KV caches only for designated retrieval heads and adds a lightweight token indexer that performs dynamic sparse attention using the low-dimensional index, then fine-tune the model for only a few hundred steps to adapt to this sparsification.
AI.result: RTPurbo maintains near-lossless performance on long-context benchmarks and reasoning datasets while significantly reducing inference cost. It achieves up to 9.36× speedup in the prefill phase at 1M-token context length and roughly 2.01× speedup during decoding, showing that sparse inference can be highly efficient without degrading accuracy when built on top of full-attention LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Full-attention LLMs already contain enough intrinsic sparsity that they can be converted into efficient sparse long-context models with minimal additional training. By selectively keeping full attention only where necessary and using a low-dimensional, query-dependent sparse indexer for other tokens, RTPurbo offers substantial efficiency gains with almost no accuracy loss, indicating that expensive native sparse pretraining is not required for strong sparse inference.

title: Effort as Ceiling, Not Dial: Reasoning Budget Does Not Modulate Cognitive Cost Alignment Between Humans and Large Reasoning Models
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) generate chain-of-thought traces whose length tracks human reaction times across cognitive tasks, but recent debate questions whether this alignment reflects genuine computational structure or surface verbosity. We test whether the alignment varies with inference-time reasoning effort. Across GPT-OSS-20B and GPT-OSS-120B, three effort levels, and six reasoning tasks, within-task and cross-task alignment remain invariant: Bayes Factors lean toward the null, and mean alignment is numerically near-identical across conditions. A manipulation check reveals that the effort parameter sets an upper budget on generation rather than driving real-time allocation, suggesting that the allocation policy is crystallized at training time. Arithmetic complexity contrasts further show that token allocation tracks fine-grained, format-dependent human difficulty patterns, with model scale improving the match. Cognitive cost alignment between LRMs and humans appears to be a training-time achievement, robust to inference-time perturbations, supporting a compiled rather than online account of LRM problem-solving.

AI.tldr: The paper examines whether the match between Large Reasoning Models’ (LRMs) chain-of-thought length and human reaction times reflects real computational structure or mere verbosity, finding that this cognitive-cost alignment is stable across different inference-time reasoning efforts and likely learned during training.
AI.motivation: Although LRMs’ chain-of-thought lengths correlate with human reaction times on cognitive tasks, it is unclear if this indicates genuine similarity in internal computation or is an artifact of superficial factors like verbosity or output length constraints. There is active debate about whether such alignment is meaningful. The authors aim to rigorously test the robustness and origin of this alignment, especially under controlled changes to the model’s inference-time reasoning effort.
AI.method: The authors manipulate an inference-time “effort” parameter for two open-source GPT-style LRMs (GPT-OSS-20B and GPT-OSS-120B), creating three levels of reasoning effort across six different reasoning tasks. They then measure how the length of the models’ chain-of-thought traces aligns with human reaction times within and across tasks, using Bayes Factor analyses to assess whether alignment changes with effort. They also run a manipulation check to see what the effort parameter actually controls (budget vs real-time allocation) and contrast model performance across arithmetic problems with different complexity and formats to test fine-grained difficulty tracking.
AI.result: The alignment between LRM chain-of-thought length and human reaction times is statistically invariant across changes in the inference-time effort parameter: Bayes Factors favor the null hypothesis that alignment does not differ across effort levels, and mean alignment values are nearly identical. The manipulation check indicates that the effort parameter merely caps maximum generation length rather than dynamically modulating how reasoning effort is allocated. Additionally, analyses of arithmetic complexity show that LRMs’ token allocation mirrors subtle, format-dependent patterns in human difficulty, and this match improves with larger model scale.
AI.conclusion: Cognitive-cost alignment between LRMs and humans—operationalized as the correspondence between chain-of-thought length and human reaction times—appears to be a property learned during training rather than something controlled or tuned at inference time. The models behave as if they have a compiled policy for allocating reasoning tokens that is robust to inference-time perturbations in an effort parameter. This supports a view of LRM problem-solving as relying on precompiled reasoning strategies rather than flexible online adjustment of effort, suggesting that future work on interpretability and cognitive modeling should focus on training dynamics and learned policies rather than inference-time knobs.

title: Body-Grounded Perspective Formation and Conative Attunement in Artificial Agents
summary: This paper proposes a minimal architecture for body-grounded perspective formation in artificial agents. Extending prior work, the model introduces an interoceptive viability signal, a Fisher-style metric over fused exteroceptive-interoceptive states, and a conative alignment mechanism linking bodily tendency to action readiness. In a reward-free gridworld, conation converts learned bodily tendency into stable body-directed behavior, while body-to-perspective routing allows bodily perturbations to leave a recoverable geometric residue in the perspective latent. This study shows how minimal structural conditions for artificial subjectivity can be operationalized in the phenomenological sense, through the embodied organization of how a world is given to an agent.

AI.tldr: A minimal AI architecture is proposed that grounds an agent’s perspective in its body, using interoceptive signals and a geometric metric to form subjective-like behavior without rewards.
AI.motivation: To identify and operationalize the minimal structural requirements for artificial agents to have a body-grounded, phenomenology-like perspective on the world, rather than just computing optimal behavior from external rewards.
AI.method: The authors design a model for agents in a reward-free gridworld comprising: (1) an interoceptive viability signal representing bodily state; (2) a Fisher-style information metric defined over fused exteroceptive (world-facing) and interoceptive (body-facing) states; and (3) a conative alignment mechanism that maps bodily tendencies to action readiness. They also implement body-to-perspective routing so that bodily changes are reflected in the latent perspective space. They then simulate agents to study how these mechanisms shape behavior and latent geometry.
AI.result: In simulations, the conative alignment mechanism turns learned bodily tendencies into stable, body-directed behavior even without external rewards. Moreover, the body-to-perspective routing makes bodily perturbations leave a consistent, recoverable geometric trace in the agent’s perspective latent space, indicating a structured link between bodily state and subjective perspective.
AI.conclusion: The architecture demonstrates that relatively simple structural components—interoceptive viability signals, a metric over combined body-world states, and conative alignment—are sufficient to realize a form of artificial subjectivity. This subjectivity is understood phenomenologically as arising from the embodied organization of how the world appears to the agent, not from explicit reward optimization.

title: State Contamination in Memory-Augmented LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents increasingly rely on persistent state, including transcripts, summaries, retrieved context, and memory buffers, to support long-horizon interaction. This makes safety depend not only on individual model outputs, but also on what an agent stores and later reuses. We study a failure mode we call memory laundering: toxic or adversarial context can be compressed into memory summaries that no longer appear toxic under standard detectors, while still preserving hostile framing or conflict structure that influences future generations. Using paired counterfactual multi-agent rollouts, we show that toxic-origin memory summaries can remain below common toxicity thresholds while nevertheless increasing downstream toxicity relative to matched neutral baselines. To measure this hidden influence, we introduce the sub-threshold propagation gap (SPG), which quantifies downstream behavioral differences conditioned on memory states that a deployed monitor would classify as safe. Our experiments show that toxicity propagates through distinct state channels: raw transcript reuse drives overt downstream toxicity, while compressed memory carries hidden sub-threshold influence. We further find that mitigation depends critically on intervention placement. Sanitizing toxic state before summarization substantially reduces the hidden propagation gap, whereas cleaning only the completed summary can leave laundered influence intact. These results suggest that safety in memory-augmented agents should be treated as a state-control problem over evolving context, with sanitization applied before unsafe information is compressed into persistent memory.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how toxic or adversarial content can be "laundered" through memory summaries in LLM-based agents, remaining undetected by standard toxicity filters while still influencing future behavior, and proposes a metric and mitigation strategy focused on state control before summarization.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents increasingly use long-term memory (transcripts, summaries, retrieved context), safety depends not only on single outputs but on what is stored and reused. Existing safety tools focus on detecting toxicity in immediate outputs, not in compressed or summarized memories. There is a need to understand whether harmful content can evade detectors once summarized, yet still affect future generations, and how to measure and mitigate this risk.
AI.method: The authors define a failure mode called memory laundering, where toxic or adversarial content is compressed into seemingly safe summaries. They run paired counterfactual multi-agent rollouts comparing trajectories that differ only in the toxicity of the original context. They then examine the resulting memory summaries and their downstream influence on behavior. To quantify hidden influence, they introduce the sub-threshold propagation gap (SPG), measuring behavioral differences conditioned on memory that toxicity detectors classify as safe. They analyze different state channels (raw transcript vs. compressed memory) and test mitigation strategies by varying where in the pipeline sanitization is applied (before vs. after summarization).
AI.result: They find that toxic-origin memory summaries can be crafted that fall below common toxicity thresholds while still increasing downstream toxic behavior compared to neutral baselines. Toxicity propagates through distinct channels: direct reuse of raw transcripts leads to overt toxic outputs, whereas compressed summaries carry subtler, sub-threshold influence that standard monitors miss. Experiments show that sanitizing toxic state before summarization significantly reduces the SPG, whereas cleaning only the final summaries often fails to remove the laundered harmful influence.
AI.conclusion: Safety for memory-augmented LLM agents should be formulated as a state-control problem over the agent’s evolving internal context. Because harmful information can be laundered through summarization into undetected but influential memory, effective mitigation requires sanitizing state before unsafe content is compressed into persistent memory, rather than relying solely on post hoc filtering of summaries or outputs.

title: Closing the Gap at CRAC 2026: Two-Stage Adaptation for LLM-Based Multilingual Coreference Resolution
summary: We present our submission to the LLM track of the 2026 Computational Models of Reference, Anaphora and Coreference (CRAC 2026) shared task. With an average CoNLL F1 score of 74.32 on the official test set, our system ranked first in the LLM track, and third overall. Our system is based on the Gemma-3-27b model, fine-tuned using a two-stage strategy with a multilingual base adapter followed by dataset-specific adapters. We represent mention spans by their headword using an XML-inspired format with local reindexing and annotate documents iteratively. These design choices proved effective across languages, document lengths, and annotation guidelines.

AI.tldr: A top-ranked coreference resolution system built by fine-tuning Gemma-3-27B with a multilingual and dataset-specific adapter strategy, using headword-based XML-style span representations and iterative document annotation.
AI.motivation: To build a competitive, language-robust coreference resolution system for the CRAC 2026 shared task, especially within the LLM track, and to investigate design choices that generalize across languages, document lengths, and differing annotation guidelines.
AI.method: Use Gemma-3-27b as the base LLM and apply a two-stage fine-tuning strategy: first train a multilingual base adapter, then stack or specialize dataset-specific adapters. Represent mentions by their headwords in an XML-inspired span format with local reindexing, and process each document iteratively, updating annotations over multiple passes.
AI.result: The system achieved an average CoNLL F1 of 74.32 on the official test set, ranking first in the LLM track and third overall in the shared task, demonstrating strong performance across multiple languages and document conditions.
AI.conclusion: A two-stage adapter-based fine-tuning of a large language model, combined with headword-based XML-style span representations and iterative document annotation, yields a high-performing and robust coreference resolution system that transfers well across languages, document sizes, and annotation schemes.

title: NeuroMAS: Multi-Agent Systems as Neural Networks with Joint Reinforcement Learning
summary: Multi-agent language systems are often built as hand-designed workflows, where agents are assigned semantic roles and communication protocols are specified in advance. We propose NeuroMAS, a method that first treats a multi-agent language system as a trainable and scalable neural-network-like architecture with LLM agents as nodes and intermediate textual signals as edges. In NeuroMAS, agent nodes are role-free but structure-aware: the topology only determines how information can flow in general, while reinforcement learning training determines how nodes communicate, specialize, and coordinate. This formulation shifts multi-agent design from workflow engineering toward architecture design, where depth, width, connectivity, and growth protocol become scalable sources of capability. Further, we provide a theoretical perspective showing why such modular textual computation is more parameter-efficient when tasks admit hierarchical decompositions. Experiments show that NeuroMAS improves significantly over both inference-time and trained multi-agent baselines. We further find that organizational scaling is path-dependent: larger systems can be challenging to train from scratch, but become feasible when grown progressively from smaller trained systems. These results suggest that learned neural multi-agent systems are a promising scaling axis for LLMs.

AI.tldr: NeuroMAS treats multi-agent LLM systems as trainable neural-like architectures, learning roles and communication via RL over a fixed topology, leading to more efficient hierarchical computation and better performance than hand-designed multi-agent baselines, especially when scaled progressively.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent language systems rely on hand-designed workflows with fixed semantic roles and communication protocols, which are hard to scale systematically as capabilities grow. The authors want a more principled and scalable way to design multi-agent systems, analogous to how neural network architectures are designed and trained. They are motivated to make multi-agent design less about manual workflow engineering and more about learning roles, communication, and coordination automatically, while also understanding when such modular multi-agent computation is parameter-efficient, especially for hierarchically decomposable tasks.
AI.method: They introduce NeuroMAS, representing a multi-agent language system as a directed graph whose nodes are LLM agents and edges are intermediate textual signals. Nodes are initially role-free but aware of the communication structure given by the topology. They train the system end-to-end using reinforcement learning so that agents learn how to communicate, specialize, and coordinate over this fixed structure. They explore architectural choices such as depth, width, and connectivity and study growth protocols that progressively expand a smaller trained system into a larger one. They also provide a theoretical analysis arguing that modular textual computation in this framework is more parameter-efficient when underlying tasks have hierarchical decompositions.
AI.result: Empirically, NeuroMAS significantly outperforms both inference-time multi-agent baselines (e.g., manually orchestrated agent workflows) and other trained multi-agent setups across evaluated tasks. They find that simply scaling up the system from scratch is difficult; large NeuroMAS architectures train poorly without curriculum, whereas progressively growing larger systems from smaller, already trained ones makes training feasible and effective. Their theoretical results support that when tasks admit hierarchical structure, the modular multi-agent formulation can achieve better parameter efficiency than monolithic approaches.
AI.conclusion: NeuroMAS demonstrates that viewing multi-agent LLM systems as trainable neural-style architectures, with RL-learned communication and specialization over a fixed topology, is a powerful approach. Architectural choices such as depth, width, and growth protocols become key levers for scaling capabilities. The work suggests that neural multi-agent systems, grown and trained rather than hand-scripted, provide a promising new scaling axis for large language models, particularly on hierarchically structured tasks and when using progressive organizational scaling.

title: Skills on the Fly: Test-Time Adaptive Skill Synthesis for LLM Agents
summary: LLM agents benefit from reusable skills, yet test-time tasks often require guidance more specific than a static skill library can provide. We propose \emph{SkillTTA}, a Test-Time Adaptive Skill Synthesis method that retrieves a small set of training trajectories relevant to the current task and synthesizes them into a temporary, task-specific textual skill. The solver model is kept fixed, so adaptation happens entirely through generated context rather than parameter updates. We evaluate the method on SpreadsheetBench, ALFWorld, and BigCodeBench. Compared with static trajectory-to-skill synthesis using GPT-5.5, task-specific skills improve SpreadsheetBench Pass@1 from 0.397 to 0.505 and BigCodeBench Pass@1 from 0.517 to 0.651. On ALFWorld, the method matches a heavier memory-learning baseline within four points of success rate while producing the shortest successful trajectories among reported methods. Ablations on SpreadsheetBench further show that synthesized skills outperform raw trajectory prompting, that top-$k$ retrieval should stay small, and that failed trajectories are especially useful because they expose recurring evaluator-facing mistakes.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SkillTTA, a method for generating temporary, task-specific textual skills for LLM agents at test time by synthesizing retrieved training trajectories, achieving better performance than static skills and raw trajectories without changing model parameters.
AI.motivation: Large language model agents benefit from reusable skills, but real test-time tasks often need guidance more tailored than what a fixed, pre-built skill library can offer. Existing approaches that adapt via memory or parameter updates can be heavy or inflexible. The authors want a lightweight way to adapt behavior per-task using only context, not model finetuning, while leveraging past trajectories more effectively.
AI.method: SkillTTA first retrieves a small set of training trajectories that are relevant to the current task. It then synthesizes these trajectories into a concise, textual, task-specific skill that captures reusable patterns, strategies, and pitfalls. This synthesized skill is inserted into the context of a fixed “solver” LLM, which then performs the task using this guidance. The method is evaluated against static trajectory-to-skill synthesis and other baselines, with ablations exploring retrieval size, use of failed vs. successful trajectories, and comparison to raw trajectory prompting.
AI.result: On SpreadsheetBench, SkillTTA improves Pass@1 from 0.397 to 0.505 over static trajectory-to-skill synthesis. On BigCodeBench, Pass@1 improves from 0.517 to 0.651. On ALFWorld, SkillTTA matches a heavier memory-learning baseline within four points of success rate, while producing the shortest successful trajectories among reported methods. Ablation studies on SpreadsheetBench show synthesized skills outperform directly prompting with raw trajectories, that using a small top-k for retrieval is best, and that failed trajectories provide especially useful information by highlighting common evaluator-facing errors.
AI.conclusion: Test-time adaptive skill synthesis via SkillTTA is an effective, parameter-free way to specialize LLM agents to individual tasks. By turning a few retrieved trajectories, including failed ones, into a compact textual skill, the method improves performance and efficiency across diverse benchmarks. The findings suggest that small, carefully selected retrieval sets and explicit synthesis of higher-level guidance are more beneficial than simply feeding raw example trajectories, offering a practical direction for context-based adaptation of LLM agents.

title: Multi-Paradigm Agent Interaction in Practice:A Systematic Analysis of Generator-Evaluator, ReAct Loop,and Adversarial Evaluation in the buddyMe Framework
summary: The rapid evolution of Large Language Model (LLM) agents has produced diverse interaction paradigms, yet few production systems integrate multiple paradigms within a unified architecture. This paper presents a systematic analysis of three principal agent interaction paradigms, including Multi-Agent Orchestration (Generator-Evaluator), ReAct Tool-Use Loops, and Memory-Augmented Interaction, as implemented in buddyMe, an open-source multi-model agent programming framework. We formalize a five-stage processing pipeline: Requirement Pre-Review -> Task Decomposition -> ReAct Execution -> Real-Execution Verification -> Adversarial Evaluation Discussion, and establish a six-dimensional evaluation schema with weighted scoring. Through four empirical case studies drawn from real-world deployment logs covering museum guide generation, scheduled weather tasks, and comprehensive tour planning, we draw three key conclusions. First, Generator-Evaluator pre-review detects requirement omissions in 20 percent of complex tasks, with 80 percent tasks passing initial inspection. Second, the ReAct loop ensures stable subtask execution but leads to around 30 percent redundant tool invocations. Third, adversarial Evaluator-Defender discussions reach consensus within 2-3 rounds for nearly 70 percent of scenarios, functioning mainly for content refinement rather than logical reversal. We additionally provide three Mermaid-based architectural diagrams and conduct cross-paradigm comparisons with CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph, MemGPT and A-Mem across six system dimensions. The research outcomes offer practical design guidelines for constructing stable and reliable multi-paradigm agent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes and unifies three major LLM agent interaction paradigms within a single framework (buddyMe), proposes a five-stage processing pipeline and a six-dimensional evaluation schema, and empirically evaluates their behavior and trade-offs using real deployment logs.
AI.motivation: Although many LLM-agent interaction paradigms exist—multi-agent orchestration, ReAct tool use, and memory-augmented interaction—few production systems integrate them coherently or provide systematic, comparative analysis. Practitioners lack concrete design guidelines for building stable, reliable multi-paradigm agent systems, and there is limited empirical evidence from real-world deployments about how these paradigms behave and interact.
AI.method: The authors: (1) categorize three principal paradigms—Generator-Evaluator orchestration, ReAct-style tool-use loops, and memory-augmented interaction—and implement them in buddyMe, an open-source multi-model agent framework; (2) formalize a five-stage processing pipeline (Requirement Pre-Review, Task Decomposition, ReAct Execution, Real-Execution Verification, Adversarial Evaluation Discussion); (3) design a six-dimensional, weighted evaluation schema to assess system behavior; (4) conduct four empirical case studies using real deployment logs (museum guide generation, scheduled weather tasks, tour planning, etc.); and (5) compare buddyMe’s architecture and paradigms against other frameworks (CrewAI, AutoGen, LangGraph, MemGPT, A-Mem) along six system dimensions, supported by Mermaid diagrams.
AI.result: Empirical evidence shows: (1) Generator-Evaluator pre-review identifies missing or unclear requirements in about 20% of complex tasks, while ~80% pass initial inspection; (2) the ReAct loop provides robust subtask execution but incurs ~30% redundant tool calls, indicating an efficiency–reliability trade-off; (3) adversarial Evaluator-Defender dialogue converges within 2–3 rounds in nearly 70% of scenarios and mainly improves content quality rather than overturning core logic. The framework is successfully applied to multiple real-world cases such as museum guides, scheduled weather tasks, and tour planning.
AI.conclusion: Integrating multiple LLM-agent paradigms in a unified, structured pipeline is both feasible and beneficial for building stable, reliable agent systems. Generator-Evaluator pre-review improves requirement completeness, ReAct loops stabilize subtask execution at the cost of some redundancy, and adversarial evaluation is effective primarily as a refinement layer. The proposed five-stage pipeline, six-dimensional evaluation schema, and cross-framework comparisons provide actionable design guidance for future multi-paradigm agent architectures.

title: Response-free item difficulty modelling for multiple-choice items with fine-tuned transformers: Component-wise representation and multi-task learning
summary: Response-free item difficulty modelling promises to reduce reliance on response-based calibration but is intrinsically difficult on reading-comprehension multiple-choice items, where difficulty depends on inferential demands across wording components. Whereas most existing approaches extract item-text features and pass them to a separate statistical or machine-learning model, we fine-tune transformer encoders end-to-end on the item wording, eliminating the manual feature engineering and preprocessing that discards information. Moreover, two extensions to this joint-encoding approach are proposed: a component-wise variant that encodes wording components separately through a shared encoder, and a multi-task variant that retains joint encoding and adds an auxiliary multiple-choice question answering objective on the shared encoder. Each method is evaluated under a Monte Carlo subsampling design at three training-set sizes on a held-out test set. We find that joint encoding is a viable end-to-end alternative to feature-engineering pipelines; while the component-wise variant shows no detectable benefit, consistent with self-attention already harvesting the cross-component signal, the multi-task variant delivers significant paired improvements in the smallest-sample regime. Transformer fine-tuning, especially if regularised by a suitable auxiliary task, recovers a substantial share of the wording-derivable signal at training-set sizes typical of applied measurement. The framework provides a customisable interface for psychometrically motivated extensions.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to predict the difficulty of reading-comprehension multiple-choice items directly from their text using transformer models, avoiding manual feature engineering, and finds that joint transformer encoding works well and that a multi-task QA auxiliary objective helps in low-data settings.
AI.motivation: Traditional item difficulty calibration relies on large amounts of response data and manual feature engineering when using text-based prediction, which is inefficient and discards information, especially for reading-comprehension items whose difficulty arises from complex inferential relations across passages, questions, and options. The authors aim to develop a more direct, data-efficient, and information-preserving way to model item difficulty from wording alone.
AI.method: They fine-tune transformer encoders (e.g., BERT-like models) end-to-end on the full wording of reading-comprehension multiple-choice items to predict item difficulty, comparing three architectures: (1) joint encoding of all wording components; (2) component-wise encoding, where passage, stem, and options are encoded separately through a shared encoder; and (3) a multi-task joint-encoding variant that adds an auxiliary multiple-choice QA objective on the shared encoder. Performance is evaluated via Monte Carlo subsampling at three training-set sizes on a held-out test set to assess robustness and sample-efficiency.
AI.result: Joint encoding of item wording performs competitively with, and can replace, feature-engineering pipelines in predicting item difficulty. The component-wise variant offers no detectable improvement over joint encoding, suggesting that standard self-attention already captures cross-component relationships effectively. The multi-task variant, which adds a QA auxiliary loss, yields statistically significant performance gains in the smallest training-sample regime, indicating better regularisation and use of limited data.
AI.conclusion: End-to-end transformer fine-tuning on item wording is a practical, effective alternative to traditional feature-based difficulty modelling for reading-comprehension multiple-choice items. While splitting components does not help, augmenting joint encoding with a suitably chosen auxiliary QA task improves performance when training data are scarce. Overall, transformer-based response-free difficulty modelling can recover much of the difficulty signal present in the wording at data scales common in applied measurement and offers a flexible basis for future psychometric extensions.

title: Voices in the Loop: Mapping Participatory AI
comment: Accepted to The 2026 ACM Conference on Fairness, Accountability, and Transparency (FAccT '26), June 25--28, 2026, Montreal, QC, Canada
summary: Participatory approaches to artificial intelligence are increasingly documented across public, civic, and humanitarian settings, but evidence about how participation is organized remains fragmented. This paper reports on the construction of an open repository and interactive atlas of participatory AI initiatives, using records harmonized from Maga~na and Shilton's Trustworthy AI corpus, and additional audited cases from research and practice. We contribute three elements. First, we specify a reproducible protocol for discovery, vetting, harmonization, geocoding, provenance tracking, and release-based publication of participatory AI records. Second, we report corpus-level patterns in geography, participation tiers, lifecycle loci, organizational form, verification status, and remaining documentation gaps. Documented initiatives remain concentrated in a small number of countries, while participation is most often coded at problem formulation, evaluation, and governance rather than model development or training. Third, we show how the atlas operationalizes a design and governance framework for participatory-by-default AI infrastructures through versioned releases, record-linked issue and annotation channels, schema feedback workflows, and redaction or restricted-disclosure requests. The atlas is intended to support comparative research, policy learning, and community scrutiny through a living inventory that can be updated, contested, and reused.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an open, interactive atlas and repository of participatory AI initiatives, detailing how it was built and what patterns it reveals about where and how participation happens in AI projects.
AI.motivation: Participatory AI projects are growing across public, civic, and humanitarian domains, but knowledge about them is scattered, inconsistently documented, and hard to compare. This fragmentation makes it difficult for researchers, policymakers, and practitioners to understand where and how participation is occurring, what is missing, and how to design AI infrastructures that are participatory by default. The authors aim to centralize, standardize, and make searchable the evidence on participatory AI initiatives so that it can inform future research, policy, and practice.
AI.method: The authors construct an open repository and interactive atlas by harmonizing records of participatory AI initiatives from Magaña and Shilton's Trustworthy AI corpus and augmenting it with additional audited cases from research and practice. They define and follow a reproducible protocol that includes discovery of initiatives, vetting and auditing them, harmonizing metadata, geocoding locations, tracking provenance of records, and publishing them through versioned releases. The atlas includes mechanisms such as record-linked issue and annotation channels, schema feedback workflows, and processes for redaction or restricted disclosure.
AI.result: The resulting corpus and atlas reveal systematic patterns: initiatives are geographically concentrated in a relatively small set of countries; participation is most frequently documented at the stages of problem formulation, evaluation, and governance, and much less during model development or training. The analysis also surfaces gaps in documentation across dimensions such as geography, lifecycle phase, organizational type, and verification status. Technically and organizationally, the authors produce a functioning, open, versioned atlas with linked feedback and governance mechanisms for the records.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that an open, living atlas of participatory AI initiatives can serve as shared infrastructure for comparative research, policy learning, and community oversight. By operationalizing a “participatory-by-default” design and governance framework—through transparent versioning, feedback channels, and flexible disclosure controls—the atlas not only documents existing projects but also provides a model for how AI infrastructures themselves can be made more participatory. The authors position the atlas as an evolving resource that can be updated, contested, and reused by diverse stakeholders.

title: Evaluation Drift in LLM Personality Induction: Are We Moving the Goalpost?
comment: 14 pages, 8 main pages, 5 figures, 4 main page figures
summary: Can large language models reliably express a human-like personality, or are they merely mimicking surface cues without a stable underlying profile? To investigate this, we induce personality in LLMs by fine-tuning them on the long-form essays, where each essay is associated with a target Big Five personality profile. We then evaluate the stability and fidelity of the induced personality using the IPIP-NEO questionnaire. Specifically, we ask: (i) does post-training (SFT, DPO, ORPO) stabilize questionnaire scores under prompt rephrasings, and (ii) can it induce target Big Five profiles from unguided essays? Our results demonstrate that fine-tuning consistently reduces variance in questionnaire responses across five models, directly mitigating the evaluation fragility reported in pre-trained models. However, this newfound stability reveals a more fundamental limitation: accuracy on the full five-dimensional profile remains near chance, even when single-trait scores improve. This indicates that unguided essays lack the cues needed for faithful personality expression. We therefore argue for scenario-grounded datasets or interactive elicitation that accumulates test-aligned evidence over time.

AI.tldr: The paper examines whether fine-tuning LLMs on personality-labeled essays can induce stable, accurate Big Five personality profiles, finding improved stability but limited accuracy, suggesting essays alone are insufficient for faithful personality expression.
AI.motivation: There is growing interest in giving LLMs consistent, human-like personalities for applications like chatbots and agents, but it is unclear whether current models genuinely express stable personality traits or just reflect prompt phrasing and superficial cues. Prior work shows that personality assessments of pre-trained LLMs are fragile to prompt variations, casting doubt on their reliability as personality-bearing agents. The paper is motivated by the need to understand if targeted training can both stabilize and accurately control LLM personality traits, and what data and evaluation setups are required to do so.
AI.method: The authors induce personality in LLMs by fine-tuning them on long-form essays, each paired with a Big Five personality profile. They explore several post-training strategies, including supervised fine-tuning (SFT), Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), and ORPO, across five base models. They then evaluate personality expression via the IPIP-NEO questionnaire, probing two main questions: (i) whether fine-tuning stabilizes questionnaire scores under different prompt phrasings, and (ii) whether the fine-tuned models can match target Big Five profiles when generating unguided essays. Variance in questionnaire responses and accuracy of predicted profiles relative to targets are the main metrics.
AI.result: Across all five models and training strategies, fine-tuning reduces variance in questionnaire responses under prompt rephrasings, indicating more stable personality-related outputs compared to pre-trained baselines. Some single-trait predictions improve in accuracy. However, when considering the full five-dimensional Big Five profile, overall accuracy remains close to chance, even after fine-tuning. This suggests that although the models become more consistent in how they answer personality items, they still do not reliably encode the intended multidimensional personality profiles from the training essays alone.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that while post-training methods like SFT, DPO, and ORPO can enhance the stability of LLM personality assessments, they do not yield accurate, fully specified Big Five profiles when relying solely on unguided essays as the conditioning data. The underlying limitation is that such essays do not contain enough explicit, diagnostic cues for all five traits to be faithfully learned and expressed. The authors therefore advocate for scenario-grounded datasets or interactive elicitation procedures that gather test-aligned, trait-relevant evidence over time, as a more promising route to building LLMs with reliable and controllable personality profiles.

title: Sketch Then Paint: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning for Diffusion Multi-Modal Large Language Models
summary: Diffusion Multi-Modal Large Language Models (dMLLMs) are powerful for image generation, but optimizing them through reinforcement learning (RL) remains a major challenge. One primary difficulty is that a single image can be generated through many different unmasking sequences, which makes calculating importance ratios often intractable. Additionally, existing methods tend to ignore the hierarchical generation process of dMLLMs, where early tokens define the global layout and later tokens focus on local details. By assigning uniform rewards to all tokens, these current methods fail to reflect the actual contribution of each token to the final image. To address these issues, we propose Hierarchical Token GRPO (HT-GRPO), which integrates this hierarchy directly into the policy optimization process.
