AI.motivation: As social media moves toward pluralistic, community-governed spaces, moderation depends heavily on local, community-specific rules rather than universal standards. Existing moderation approaches and benchmarks mostly assume uniform policies and do not capture the complexity where the same content can be allowed in one community and banned in another. The authors want to rigorously evaluate whether current AI models can help moderators by accurately identifying which specific community rule, if any, a post or comment violates.
AI.method: They formalize community moderation as a multiple-choice prediction problem: given a comment and its context from a specific community, the model must select which rule (from that community’s rule set), if any, has been violated. They build PluRule, a large-scale, multimodal (text and images) and multilingual benchmark derived from 1,989 Reddit communities, covering 2,885 unique rules in 9 languages and 13,371 labeled rule-violation instances. They then evaluate a range of state-of-the-art vision-language models, including a strong model referred to as GPT-5.2 with enhanced reasoning, under varying model sizes and context lengths.
AI.result: Across the benchmark, even the strongest evaluated model (GPT-5.2 with high reasoning capabilities) performs only slightly better than a trivial baseline, indicating that the task is very challenging for current systems. Scaling model size and providing more context yield only marginal performance improvements. The analysis further shows that rule categories which are more universal or broadly interpretable across communities, such as civility and self-promotion, are relatively easier for models to detect compared to more idiosyncratic or community-specific norms.
AI.conclusion: The findings suggest that current state-of-the-art language and vision-language models are poorly equipped to handle the nuanced, community-specific moderation required in pluralistic social media environments. Automated moderation that reliably maps content to particular community rules remains an open, fundamental challenge. The PluRule benchmark and released code are intended to catalyze research toward models that better understand and operationalize diverse, localized governance norms.

title: A Conflict-aware Evidential Framework for Reliable Sleep Stage Classification
summary: Multi-view learning has been widely applied for sleep stage classification using multi-modal data. However, existing methods typically assume that different modalities are well-aligned, which is often unattainable in real-world scenarios, thereby compromising the reliability of the staging results. In this paper, we propose ConfSleepNet, a conflict-aware evidential framework that dynamically resolves inter-view conflicts. The framework consists of multi-view evidence extraction and conflict-aware aggregation. In the first phase, it learns category-related evidence from different modalities, which represents the degree of support for individual sleep stages. Considering the inherent characteristics of varying modalities, we propose hybrid category structures for different modalities to promote more reasonable evidence learning. In the second phase, view-specific opinions, including prediction results and uncertainty, are constructed from the learned evidence. Notably, we propose a novel conflict-aware aggregation method that integrates these view-specific opinions into a reliable joint decision. This mechanism can effectively resolve conflicts among opinions and synthesize them into a reliable joint decision. Both theoretical analysis and experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of ConfSleepNet in sleep staging tasks. The code is available at https://github.com/By4te/ConfSleepNet_ICML2026/.

AI.tldr: They propose ConfSleepNet, a conflict-aware evidential multi-view learning framework that handles misaligned multimodal signals for sleep stage classification by extracting modality-specific evidential support and then aggregating them through a conflict-aware decision mechanism.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-view / multimodal sleep stage classifiers assume that modalities (e.g., EEG, EOG, EMG, etc.) are well-aligned in time and quality. In practice, signals can be misaligned, noisy, or partially missing, which causes conflicting predictions between modalities and reduces reliability. There is a need for a principled way to model uncertainty and resolve inter-modality conflicts when making sleep stage decisions.
AI.method: They design ConfSleepNet, an evidential deep-learning framework with two phases. (1) Multi-view evidence extraction: for each modality, the network learns category-related evidential quantities that represent how strongly that modality supports each sleep stage. To respect differences between modalities, they use hybrid category structures tailored to each modality to obtain more reasonable evidence representations. (2) Conflict-aware aggregation: from the per-modality evidence, they construct view-specific opinions containing both predicted classes and associated uncertainty, based on evidential theory. They then propose a novel conflict-aware aggregation rule that combines these conflicting opinions into a single joint decision while explicitly accounting for and resolving inter-view conflicts. The framework is supported by theoretical analysis of the aggregation mechanism.
AI.result: On sleep staging benchmarks, ConfSleepNet achieves better or more reliable performance than existing multi-view methods, particularly in cases with conflicting or misaligned modalities. Theoretical analysis shows that the conflict-aware evidential aggregation behaves desirably under conflicts, and empirical experiments confirm improved accuracy and robustness.
AI.conclusion: ConfSleepNet provides a principled evidential framework for multi-view sleep staging under real-world conditions with misaligned or conflicting modalities. By learning modality-specific evidential support with hybrid label structures and aggregating them through a conflict-aware opinion fusion rule, the method yields more reliable and robust sleep stage decisions than traditional multi-view approaches.

title: LLMs for automatic annotation of Mandarin narrative transcripts
summary: Linguistic annotation of transcribed speech is essential for research in language acquisition, language disorders, and sociolinguistics, yet remains labor-intensive and time-consuming. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise in automating annotation tasks, their ability to handle complex discourse-level annotation in non-English languages remains understudied. This study evaluates whether LLMs can reliably annotate narrative macrostructure-the hierarchical organization of story grammar elements-in spoken Mandarin, using the Multilingual Assessment Instrument for Narratives (MAIN) as a testbed. We compared four LLMs against trained human annotators on narratives produced by children, young adults, and older adults. The best-performing model achieved agreement with human raters (k=.794) approaching human-human reliability levels (k=.872) while reducing annotation time by 65%, whereas the locally deployable lightweight model performed substantially worse. Annotation difficulty varied systematically by macrostructure element type, with categories requiring subtle semantic differentiation posing persistent challenges. Furthermore, model reliability decreased on young adult narratives, which exhibited greater lexical variation, semantic ambiguity, and multi-element integration within single utterances. These findings suggest that LLMs can effectively support discourse-level annotation in non-English spoken corpora, while highlighting the continued need for human oversight in semantically complex tasks. Our prompt templates are open sourced for future use.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether large language models can automatically annotate the macrostructure of spoken Mandarin narratives and compares their performance to human annotators.
AI.motivation: Linguistic annotation of spoken language is critical for research but remains slow and labor-intensive, especially for complex discourse-level features and non-English languages. While LLMs have been promising for simpler annotation tasks, their effectiveness for detailed narrative macrostructure annotation in languages like Mandarin has not been systematically evaluated.
AI.method: The authors use narratives in Mandarin elicited with the Multilingual Assessment Instrument for Narratives (MAIN) from children, young adults, and older adults. Four different LLMs are prompted to perform narrative macrostructure annotation (story grammar elements). Their annotations are compared with those of trained human annotators using inter-rater agreement (Cohen’s kappa). They also analyze which macrostructure categories and which speaker groups are most challenging for the models, and compare performance of a best-performing large model with a lightweight, locally deployable model.
AI.result: The best LLM achieves high agreement with human annotators (kappa = 0.794), close to human-human agreement (kappa = 0.872), and reduces annotation time by 65%. A lightweight local model performs substantially worse. Annotation accuracy varies by type of macrostructure element, with elements requiring fine-grained semantic distinctions remaining difficult. Reliability is lower on young adult narratives, which show more lexical diversity, semantic ambiguity, and multiple macrostructure elements packed into single utterances.
AI.conclusion: LLMs can substantially support discourse-level annotation of non-English spoken narratives and approach human reliability while offering large time savings. However, they struggle with semantically subtle categories and more complex, variable narratives, so human oversight and validation remain necessary. The authors release their prompt templates to facilitate future work and replication.

title: Reliability and Effectiveness of Autonomous AI Agents in Supply Chain Management
summary: This paper studies autonomous generative AI agents in multi-echelon supply chains using the MIT Beer Game. We identify four inference-time levers that shape performance: model selection, policies and guardrails, centralized data sharing, and prompt engineering. Model capability is the dominant factor: an out-of-the-box reasoning model exceeds human-level performance, and optimized reasoning models reduce costs by up to 67% relative to human teams. However, strong average performance masks substantial reliability risks. We introduce the agent bullwhip effect, the amplification of decision unreliability across echelons, manifesting along two dimensions: decision variance increases both across facilities at the same point in time and within the same facility across time. We develop a mathematical framework showing that this phenomenon is inherent to multi-agent systems that involve coordination and information delays, and we demonstrate that repeated sampling fails to meaningfully reduce it. To address this limitation, we propose a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO)-based reinforcement-learning post-training framework that trains a shared base LLM using system-level supply-chain rewards. GRPO post-training substantially reduces tail events, curtails agent bullwhip, and improves the reliability of autonomous supply-chain agents.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates autonomous generative AI agents in a multi-echelon supply chain (MIT Beer Game), identifies four key configuration levers, reveals a new “agent bullwhip effect” in decision unreliability, proves it is inherent in coordinated multi-agent systems with delays, shows that naive resampling doesn’t fix it, and introduces a GRPO-based RL post-training method that improves reliability and reduces extreme negative outcomes.
AI.motivation: To understand whether and how autonomous generative AI agents can reliably manage complex multi-echelon supply chains, where coordination, information delays, and demand uncertainty already create the classic bullwhip effect. The authors want to know which deployment choices matter most (model choice, guardrails, data sharing, prompts), what new risks arise (reliability and instability of decisions), and how to train agents to be not only performant on average but also reliable in the tails.
AI.method: They instantiate an autonomous multi-agent supply-chain environment via the MIT Beer Game, where each echelon (retailer, wholesaler, distributor, factory) is controlled by a generative AI agent based on large language models. They systematically vary four inference-time levers: model selection, policies and guardrails, centralized versus siloed data, and prompt engineering. They benchmark against human teams and compute costs and variability. They formalize and analyze the “agent bullwhip effect” mathematically, showing how decision noise and coordination with delays produce amplified unreliability across time and echelons, and they test repeated sampling as a mitigation. Finally, they propose and implement a Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) reinforcement-learning post-training framework that uses system-level rewards to fine-tune a shared base LLM, evaluating its impact on performance, tail risk, and reliability.
AI.result: Out-of-the-box reasoning models already outperform average human teams in the Beer Game; with optimized reasoning models, total supply-chain costs decrease by up to 67% compared with humans. Nonetheless, decisions from autonomous agents exhibit high unreliability that grows across echelons and over time—the agent bullwhip effect. Mathematical analysis indicates this effect is an inherent feature of multi-agent systems with coordination and information delays. Repeated sampling of LLM decisions (e.g., taking multiple draws) does not significantly mitigate this unreliability. Applying the proposed GRPO-based post-training leads to substantial reductions in extreme cost events, dampens the agent bullwhip effect, and improves consistency and reliability of the agents’ decisions while preserving or improving average performance.
AI.conclusion: While powerful generative AI agents can deliver large efficiency gains in multi-echelon supply chains, they introduce a distinct reliability risk: the agent bullwhip effect, where decision noise is amplified across agents and time. This phenomenon is structurally tied to coordinated, delayed-information environments and is not solved by naive sampling strategies. A GRPO-based reinforcement-learning post-training approach that uses system-level rewards can train shared LLM agents to coordinate more reliably, reducing tail risks and making autonomous supply-chain management more robust in practice.

title: Artificial Intolerance: Stigmatizing Language in Clinical Documentation Skews Large Language Model Decision-Making
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed in high-stakes domains such as clinical decision support and medical documentation. However, the robustness of these models against subtle linguistic variations, specifically stigmatizing language (SL) commonly found in human-authored clinical notes, remains critically under-explored. In this work, we investigate whether frontier LLMs inherit and propagate this human bias when processing clinical text. We systematically evaluate nine frontier LLMs across four stigmatized medical conditions, utilizing clinical vignettes injected with varying intensities and phenotypes of SL (doubt, blame, and maligning). Our results demonstrate that all evaluated models exhibit substantial bias, with clinical decision-making significantly skewed towards less aggressive patient management. Notably, we observe a high sensitivity to linguistic framing, where a single SL sentence is sufficient to alter model outputs, revealing a clear dose-response relationship. Furthermore, we evaluate standard prompt-based mitigation strategies, including Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning and model self-debiasing. These approaches show limited efficacy; models struggle to explicitly identify SL while remaining implicitly influenced by it. Our findings expose a critical vulnerability in current LLMs regarding fairness and robustness in clinical NLP, underscoring the need for rigorous algorithmic guardrails to prevent the automation of health disparities.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that leading large language models become less aggressive in clinical decision-making when exposed to stigmatizing language in notes, even at low levels, and that simple prompt-based debiasing offers little protection.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in sensitive medical tasks like decision support and documentation, it is crucial to understand whether they inherit and amplify human biases present in clinical notes, especially stigmatizing language that may already contribute to health disparities.
AI.method: The authors construct clinical vignettes covering four medical conditions and systematically inject different phenotypes (doubt, blame, maligning) and intensities of stigmatizing language. They then evaluate nine frontier LLMs on these vignettes, measuring how management recommendations change with linguistic framing, and test standard prompt-based mitigation strategies such as Chain-of-Thought reasoning and model self-debiasing to assess whether models can detect and resist stigmatizing language.
AI.result: All nine LLMs displayed substantial bias: exposure to stigmatizing language consistently shifted recommendations toward less aggressive patient management. A single stigmatizing sentence could reliably alter outputs, with stronger language leading to stronger effects, indicating a dose–response relationship. Mitigation strategies like CoT and self-debiasing only weakly reduced this effect; models rarely labeled stigmatizing language correctly yet their decisions remained influenced by it.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are highly sensitive to stigmatizing language in clinical text and standard prompting-based mitigations are insufficient, revealing a serious fairness and robustness weakness in clinical applications. The paper argues that stronger algorithmic guardrails and more rigorous evaluation are needed to prevent automated systems from perpetuating or exacerbating existing health disparities.

title: Evidential Information Fusion on Possibilistic Structure
summary: Dempster's rule is a fundamental tool for combining belief functions from distinct and reliable sources. However, its intersection-based semantics imposes strong structural restrictions, which limits its flexibility in handling complex source states and diverse information fusion scenarios. To overcome this limitation, we propose a reversible transformation, derived from the isopignistic principle, between belief functions and a possibilistic structure defined on the power set. In this transformation, the relationships among subsets are explicitly characterized by a belief evolution network, which provides a more flexible representation of evidential information beyond the conventional mass function structure. On this basis, we further introduce the triangular norm family to develop a general and adaptive evidential information fusion framework. Unlike fusion methods rooted in Dempster semantics, the proposed framework supports more flexible combination behaviors and exhibits advantages in non-distinct source fusion, conflict management, parametric combination design, and heterogeneous information fusion.

AI.tldr: They propose a new, reversible way to transform belief functions into a possibilistic structure on the power set, and then use t-norms to build a more flexible framework for evidential information fusion than Dempster’s rule, especially under conflict and non-distinct sources.
AI.motivation: Dempster’s rule, while fundamental in belief function theory, is based on intersection semantics that enforce strong structural constraints. These constraints make it hard to model complex source states, non-distinct sources, and heterogeneous information, and to flexibly manage conflict or design parametric fusion rules. The authors want a more general and adaptive fusion mechanism that relaxes these constraints while remaining principled.
AI.method: 1) Define a reversible transformation between belief functions and a possibilistic structure on the power set, guided by the isopignistic principle (preserving pignistic probabilities). 2) Represent relationships between subsets via a belief evolution network, which extends the conventional mass function representation. 3) Introduce the triangular norm (t-norm) family into this possibilistic setting to construct a general information fusion framework whose behavior can be tuned by the choice of t-norm and parameters. 4) Use this framework to design flexible combination rules for different fusion scenarios.
AI.result: They obtain a unified, t-norm-based fusion framework for belief functions that generalizes Dempster’s rule by working in a possibilistic representation and using belief evolution networks. This framework can emulate or extend existing fusion rules, allows parametric tuning of fusion behavior, and better handles non-distinct sources, conflicts, and heterogeneous information compared to traditional Dempster-based methods.
AI.conclusion: By transforming belief functions into a richer possibilistic structure and using t-norms for combination, evidential information fusion becomes more flexible and adaptive. The framework relaxes the rigid intersection-based semantics of Dempster’s rule, improves conflict management and non-distinct-source fusion, and supports the design of tailored fusion behaviors for diverse application scenarios, while remaining reversible back to standard belief functions.

title: OProver: A Unified Framework for Agentic Formal Theorem Proving
summary: Recent progress in formal theorem proving has benefited from large-scale proof generation and verifier-aware training, but agentic proving is rarely integrated into prover training, appearing only at inference time. We present OProver, a unified framework for agentic formal theorem proving in Lean 4, in which failed proof attempts are iteratively revised using retrieved compiler verified proofs and Lean compiler feedback. OProver is trained through continued pretraining followed by iterative post-training: each iteration runs agentic proving, indexes newly verified proofs into OProofs and the retrieval memory, uses repair trajectories as SFT data, and uses unresolved hard cases for RL. OProofs is built from public Lean resources, large-scale proof synthesis, and agentic proving traces, containing 1.77M Lean statements, 6.86M compiler-verified proofs, and serialized trajectories with retrieved context, failed attempts, feedback, and repairs. Across five benchmarks, OProver-32B attains the best Pass@32 on MiniF2F (93.3%), ProverBench (58.2%), and PutnamBench (11.3%), and ranks second on MathOlympiad (22.8%) and ProofNet (33.2%) more top placements than any prior open-weight whole-proof prover.

AI.tldr: OProver is a Lean 4 formal theorem prover that learns from its own agentic proof attempts, using retrieved verified proofs and compiler feedback to iteratively improve.
AI.motivation: Existing formal theorem provers often rely on large-scale proof data and verifier-aware training but only use agentic, search-based proving at inference time. There is a lack of a unified framework where the prover’s exploratory behavior, including its failures and repairs, is systematically integrated back into training to improve future performance.
AI.method: They introduce OProver, which performs agentic theorem proving in Lean 4 by iteratively revising failed proof attempts using retrieved, compiler-verified proofs and Lean feedback. Training proceeds via continued pretraining and then iterative post-training cycles. In each cycle, OProver runs agentic proving, stores newly found proofs in a large corpus called OProofs, logs trajectories (retrieved context, failed attempts, feedback, repairs), uses these repair trajectories as supervised fine-tuning data, and applies reinforcement learning to still-unresolved hard problems. OProofs aggregates proofs from public Lean sources, large-scale proof synthesis, and traces from agentic proving.
AI.result: They build OProofs with 1.77M Lean statements and 6.86M compiler-verified proofs plus serialized trajectories. On five theorem-proving benchmarks, the 32B-parameter OProver achieves state-of-the-art or near state-of-the-art Pass@32 performance, ranking first on MiniF2F, ProverBench, and PutnamBench, and second on MathOlympiad and ProofNet among open-weight whole-proof provers.
AI.conclusion: Integrating agentic proving tightly into the training loop—by learning from repair trajectories, retrieved proofs, and compiler feedback—substantially improves the capabilities of large Lean-based provers. OProver demonstrates that a unified, iterative framework for proof search, data generation, and training yields state-of-the-art performance across diverse formal theorem-proving benchmarks.

title: PersonaArena: Dynamic Simulation for Evaluating and Enhancing Persona-Level Role-Playing in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) increasingly serve as interactive social agents, yet their ability to maintain coherent and authentic persona-level role-playing remains limited, particularly in realistic social scenarios. Existing research predominantly focuses on character-level settings and relies on static evaluation formats, failing to capture the complexity of everyday social interactions. In this work, we present PersonaArena, a dynamic simulation framework for evaluating and improving persona-level role-playing in LLMs. PersonaArena leverages a large, filtered corpus of user-generated social content to construct a nuanced persona bank, and elicits multi-turn, context-rich interactions within simulated social environments. Our framework features a multi-agent debating judge for holistic and unbiased assessment. Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that PersonaArena enables rigorous evaluation and enhancement of LLMs' role-playing capabilities, advancing the development of more authentic and socially adept AI agents.

AI.tldr: PersonaArena is a dynamic framework to evaluate and improve how LLMs role-play consistent personas in realistic social interactions.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs struggle to sustain coherent, authentic personas in realistic, multi-turn social settings. Prior work mainly tests character role-play in static, simplified scenarios, which misses the complexity of everyday social interaction and gives limited insight into social competence.
AI.method: The authors build PersonaArena, which (1) mines and filters a large corpus of user-generated social content to construct a rich persona bank; (2) simulates context-rich, multi-turn social environments where LLMs perform persona-level role-playing; and (3) employs a multi-agent debating judge system to holistically and more fairly assess role-playing quality.
AI.result: Experiments using PersonaArena show it can systematically evaluate and improve LLMs’ persona role-playing performance, providing finer-grained and more realistic measures of social behavior than prior static benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PersonaArena offers a practical, dynamic simulation and evaluation framework that advances the development of LLMs with more authentic, coherent, and socially competent persona-level role-playing abilities.

title: ConflictRAG: Detecting and Resolving Knowledge Conflicts in Retrieval Augmented Generation
comment: 6 pages, 6 figures, submitted to IEEE SMC 2026
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems implicitly assume mutual consistency among retrieved documents -- an assumption that frequently fails in practice. We present ConflictRAG, a conflict-aware RAG framework that detects, classifies, and resolves knowledge conflicts prior to answer generation. The framework introduces three contributions: (1) a two-stage conflict detection module combining a lightweight embedding-based MLP classifier with selective LLM refinement, reducing API costs by 62% while maintaining 90.8% detection accuracy; (2) an Entropy-TOPSIS framework for data-driven source credibility assessment, improving selection accuracy by 7.1% over manual heuristics; and (3) a Conflict-Aware RAG Score (CARS) for diagnostic evaluation of conflict-handling capabilities. Experiments on three benchmarks against six baselines demonstrate 88.7% conflict-detection F1 and consistent 5.3--6.1% correctness gains over the strongest conflict-aware baseline, with the pipeline transferring effectively across backbone LLMs.

AI.tldr: ConflictRAG is a conflict-aware Retrieval-Augmented Generation framework that detects and resolves inconsistencies among retrieved documents before answer generation, improving accuracy and reliability while reducing API cost.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG pipelines assume that all retrieved documents are mutually consistent, but in real-world settings retrieved sources often contain conflicting or erroneous information. This leads to unreliable answers, especially when the model lacks an explicit mechanism to recognize, assess, and resolve conflicts among sources. The paper aims to make RAG more robust by explicitly handling knowledge conflicts instead of ignoring or implicitly averaging them.
AI.method: The authors propose ConflictRAG, which operates before answer generation. (1) It uses a two-stage conflict detection module: first, a lightweight embedding-based MLP classifier identifies potential conflicts cheaply; second, a selective LLM-based refinement step reassesses only ambiguous cases, cutting API costs while retaining high detection accuracy. (2) It introduces an Entropy-TOPSIS-based framework to estimate source credibility in a data-driven way, replacing ad-hoc manual heuristics; this framework ranks sources by aggregating multiple indicators with entropy-based weighting and a TOPSIS ranking scheme. (3) It defines a Conflict-Aware RAG Score (CARS) as a diagnostic metric to quantify how well a system detects and resolves conflicts in RAG pipelines. These components are integrated into a RAG pipeline that first detects conflicts, then classifies and resolves them using credibility scores before generating final answers.
AI.result: On three benchmarks and against six baseline methods, ConflictRAG achieves an 88.7% F1 score on conflict detection and yields consistent 5.3–6.1% gains in answer correctness compared to the strongest existing conflict-aware baseline. The two-stage detection design reduces LLM API costs by 62% while maintaining 90.8% detection accuracy, and the Entropy-TOPSIS credibility model improves source selection accuracy by 7.1% over manual heuristic-based approaches. The approach transfers well across different backbone LLMs, indicating generality of the framework.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling and handling conflicts among retrieved documents significantly improves the robustness and accuracy of RAG systems. ConflictRAG shows that combining lightweight classifiers with selective LLM refinement, along with a principled, data-driven source credibility assessment and a dedicated evaluation metric (CARS), leads to better conflict detection and resolution at substantially lower cost. The framework is effective across benchmarks and backbone LLMs, suggesting it can serve as a practical, general solution for conflict-aware RAG in real-world applications.

title: Towards Human-Level Book-Writing Capability
summary: Large language models optimized for instruction following and agentic tasks remain poorly aligned with the requirements of high-quality creative writing. Fiction frequently depends on behaviors that assistant-tuned models are explicitly trained to avoid, particularly deception, moral ambiguity, and unreliable narration. As a result, generated stories often appear structurally correct while remaining stylistically generic, overly explanatory, or weakly grounded in human literary behavior. We present a dataset construction and training framework for book-scale creative writing that reframes supervised fine-tuning as a prompt-to-book generation task grounded in human-authored fiction. Starting from public-domain novels, we derive a multi-resolution planning scaffold by summarizing each book at progressively finer levels, from a high-level premise to chapter- and scene-level structure. We then invert this hierarchy during training: the model learns to expand a prompt into increasingly detailed plans and finally into the original human-authored book text. This formulation preserves human prose as the final supervised target while using intermediate summaries to make book-scale generation learnable. We train a long-context language model on these prompt-to-book trajectories and study whether this objective shifts generation away from assistant-style prose and toward human literary writing.

AI.tldr: They build and train a long-context model that learns to turn short prompts into full books by expanding through multi-level outlines derived from real novels, in order to make its writing more like human literary fiction and less like assistant-style text.
AI.motivation: Instruction- and agent-optimized large language models are misaligned with the needs of high-quality fiction: they avoid deception and moral ambiguity, over-explain, and produce generic, non-literary prose. There is a gap between assistant-style outputs and the complex behaviors of human-authored novels, particularly at book length. The authors want a training setup that teaches models to write book-scale, stylistically rich fiction grounded in real literary works.
AI.method: They start from public-domain novels and summarize each book at multiple resolutions: a high-level premise, then chapter-level, then scene-level outlines. This forms a top-down planning hierarchy. For training, they invert this hierarchy, so the model conditions on a relatively short prompt and learns to generate successively more detailed plans, finally producing the original book text. The intermediate summaries serve as scaffolding that makes the very long generation task learnable, while the final supervision remains the true human prose. They implement this as a prompt-to-book trajectory objective for a long-context language model.
AI.result: They successfully train a long-context model on these prompt-to-book trajectories. The paper analyzes how this training objective affects generation, specifically whether it shifts outputs away from assistant-like, generic, over-explanatory prose and toward stylistically richer, human-like literary writing at book scale. The abstract implies that they observe such a shift, though detailed metrics are not provided in the abstract itself.
AI.conclusion: Reframing supervised fine-tuning as a prompt-to-book generation task with hierarchical planning scaffolds is a viable way to align long-context language models with human literary fiction. By grounding training in public-domain novels and preserving original human prose as the final target, their framework helps models learn book-scale creative writing that better reflects human literary behavior instead of assistant-style text.

title: Weak-to-Strong Elicitation via Mismatched Wrong Drafts
summary: We consider whether off-policy experience from a smaller, weaker model can elicit capability in a stronger learner that on-policy RL fine-tuning (e.g., GRPO) does not reach. We find that injecting mathematically wrong drafts from a smaller but more domain-trained model -- mismatched to the current problem -- into a stronger learner's GRPO context consistently outperforms standard on-policy GRPO on held-out MATH-500 and out-of-distribution AIME 2025/2026. Concretely, we use Mathstral-7B as the learner, Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B as the draft model, 8.8K Level 3--5 MATH problems (with MATH-500 held out), and train with Dr. GRPO. Mismatch is an active ingredient: shuffling drafts to mismatched problems while holding everything else constant yields $+1.62$pp on MATH-500 (greedy pass@1) over the matched-wrong variant ($n=10$ seeds, $p=0.0015$, Welch's $t$). In fact, the mismatched-wrong variant leads all other variants we tested on MATH-500 across both greedy pass@1 and sampling pass@$k$. On out-of-distribution AIME 2025 and 2026, the mismatched-wrong variant uniquely lifts pass@$k$ above both Mathstral-7B (in its native [INST] format) and the Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B draft model at every sample budget from $k=1$ to $k=1024$ across 2 seeds ($+14.2$pp on 2025 and $+9.0$pp on 2026 at pass@1024 over Mathstral-7B), and at pass@1024 also leads no-draft, matched-wrong, and mismatched-correct variants on both years. All variants use the same prompt with no draft injection at test time. The recipe -- trained on a single GPU with no SFT, no reward models, no synthesized data, and no produce-critique-revise inner loop -- reaches 71.98% MATH-500 on Mathstral-7B-v0.1, the highest published result on this model to our knowledge, surpassing the heavier WizardMath pipeline at 70.9% on full MATH (SFT + PPO with process/instruction reward models).

AI.tldr: They show that feeding a stronger math model with *mismatched, wrong* solution drafts from a smaller model during GRPO training improves its final math performance more than standard on‑policy GRPO or using matched drafts.
AI.motivation: Understand whether and how off‑policy experience from a weaker model can unlock capabilities in a stronger model that normal on‑policy RL fine‑tuning fails to reach, especially in math reasoning tasks. Explore if imperfect external guidance (wrong drafts) can be harnessed effectively and whether mismatch between drafts and current problems matters.
AI.method: Use Mathstral-7B as the main learner and Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B as a draft generator. During Dr. GRPO training on 8.8K level 3–5 MATH problems, they inject solution drafts into the context in different ways: no drafts, matched wrong drafts, mismatched wrong drafts (draft for a different problem), and mismatched correct drafts. They keep prompts and training setup fixed and compare variants on held‑out MATH‑500 and out‑of‑distribution AIME 2025/2026 benchmarks, measuring greedy pass@1 and sampling pass@k. They run multiple seeds and use statistical tests (Welch’s t‑test) to assess significance.
AI.result: The mismatched-wrong draft condition consistently outperforms standard on‑policy GRPO and all other draft variants on MATH‑500 in both greedy pass@1 and sampling pass@k, with a statistically significant +1.62 percentage point gain in pass@1 over matched-wrong drafts. On AIME 2025/2026, mismatched-wrong uniquely improves pass@k over both the base Mathstral‑7B and the draft model Qwen2.5-Math‑1.5B for all k from 1 to 1024, with substantial gains (e.g., +14.2pp on AIME 2025 and +9.0pp on AIME 2026 at pass@1024 over Mathstral‑7B). The final recipe, trained on a single GPU without SFT, reward models, synthetic data, or iterative critique loops, achieves 71.98% on MATH‑500 with Mathstral‑7B‑v0.1, surpassing the prior best WizardMath pipeline (70.9% on full MATH).
AI.conclusion: Off‑policy drafts from a smaller model, especially when they are deliberately mismatched and mathematically incorrect, can be an effective training signal for a stronger learner under GRPO-style RL, producing better math reasoning performance than standard on‑policy RL or matched drafts. Mismatch appears to be a crucial ingredient, suggesting that forcing the model to contrast, reject, or correct irrelevant wrong reasoning may build more robust problem‑solving skills. A simple, resource‑light GRPO setup can therefore reach or exceed state‑of‑the‑art performance on Mathstral‑7B without complex pipelines or extra supervision.

title: AnchorDiff: Topology-Aware Masked Diffusion with Confidence-based Rewriting for Radiology Report Generation
summary: Radiology report generation (RRG) aims to automatically produce clinically accurate textual reports from medical images. Existing methods predominantly rely on autoregressive (AR) language models, whose causal dependency structure restricts generation to a unidirectional left-to-right process. This paradigm can induce sequence bias, where models tend to follow stereotypical token orders and high-frequency report templates rather than fully grounding generation in image-specific evidence. In this paper, we propose AnchorDiff, the first masked-diffusion framework for RRG that integrates knowledge-graph-derived clinical anchors into diffusion language modeling. By leveraging bidirectional context and iterative refinement, AnchorDiff mitigates the limitations of fixed-order autoregressive decoding. Specifically, we introduce a topology-aware training strategy that uses RadGraph-derived entity hierarchies to assign clinically important tokens differentiated masking protection and loss weights. We further design an inference-time rewriting strategy that detects unstable committed tokens through perturbation-based testing and selectively revises them during denoising. Extensive experiments on the MIMIC-CXR and MIMIC-RG4 benchmarks demonstrate that AnchorDiff achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance, showing the effectiveness of clinically anchored masked diffusion for radiology report generation.

AI.tldr: AnchorDiff is a masked-diffusion language model for radiology report generation that uses knowledge-graph clinical anchors to improve image-grounded, clinically accurate reports and achieves SOTA on MIMIC benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive language models for radiology report generation generate text only left-to-right, which leads to sequence bias: they overfit to common token orders and generic report templates instead of faithfully reflecting image-specific clinical findings. There is a need for a generation paradigm that can better leverage bidirectional context, allow iterative refinement, and explicitly emphasize clinically important information so that generated reports are more accurate and less template-driven.
AI.method: The paper proposes AnchorDiff, a masked-diffusion framework for radiology report generation. Instead of left-to-right autoregression, it performs iterative denoising over text with bidirectional context. Clinical anchors are extracted from a knowledge graph (RadGraph), providing entity hierarchies that identify clinically important tokens. A topology-aware training scheme differentially masks and reweights losses for these tokens according to their clinical importance. During inference, a rewriting strategy tests token stability via perturbations; tokens deemed unstable are selectively revised during the denoising process, allowing correction of less confident content while preserving stable, clinically crucial tokens.
AI.result: On the MIMIC-CXR and MIMIC-RG4 benchmarks, AnchorDiff achieves state-of-the-art performance for radiology report generation, surpassing previous autoregressive-based methods on standard automatic metrics, indicating better clinical accuracy and image grounding.
AI.conclusion: Clinically anchored masked diffusion is an effective alternative to autoregressive decoding for radiology report generation. By integrating knowledge-graph-derived clinical anchors, topology-aware training, and inference-time rewriting, AnchorDiff reduces sequence bias and improves the quality and clinical fidelity of generated radiology reports, establishing new SOTA results on major benchmarks.

title: RAGA: Reading-And-Graph-building-Agent for Autonomous Knowledge Graph Construction and Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary:
Existing LLM-driven knowledge graph (KG) construction methods predominantly employ stateless batch processing pipelines, exhibiting structural deficiencies in cross-chunk semantic relation capture, entity disambiguation, and construction process interpretability. These limitations undermine KG quality, retrieval precision, and deployment trust in high-stakes domains.
  We propose RAGA (Reading And Graph-building Agent), an LLM-based autonomous KG construction and retrieval fusion framework. RAGA provides an atomic toolset supporting full KG lifecycle CRUD operations and embeds a Read-Search-Verify-Construct cognitive constraint into a ReAct tool loop. A KG-vector synchronization mechanism enables hybrid symbolic-vector retrieval, while evidence-anchored verification links every knowledge entry to its source text for auditable provenance.
  Preliminary experiments on a subset of the QASPER scientific QA dataset indicate that RAGA's fusion retrieval outperforms zero-shot baselines, with KG integration providing measurable gains in both answer and evidence quality. The framework design and experimental baseline serve as a reference for agent-driven autonomous KG construction.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RAGA, an LLM-based autonomous framework that jointly builds and queries knowledge graphs, overcoming limitations of traditional stateless batch KG construction and improving retrieval quality.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-driven KG construction pipelines are mostly offline, stateless, and batch-based. They struggle to capture semantic relations across text chunks, to reliably disambiguate entities, and to provide transparent, interpretable construction processes. These issues reduce the quality of the resulting KGs, harm retrieval precision, and weaken trust in high-stakes applications where provenance and explainability are critical.
AI.method: The authors design RAGA (Reading And Graph-building Agent), an autonomous agent framework powered by LLMs. RAGA offers atomic tools that cover the full CRUD lifecycle of KG management and operates under a Read-Search-Verify-Construct cognitive loop implemented in a ReAct-style tool-using framework. It synchronizes a symbolic knowledge graph with a vector store to support hybrid retrieval (graph-based and embedding-based). Each KG triple or node is linked to its originating text spans via evidence-anchored verification, ensuring traceable provenance.
AI.result: On a subset of the QASPER scientific QA dataset, RAGA’s fusion of KG-based and vector-based retrieval surpasses zero-shot baseline methods. Integrating the KG into the retrieval pipeline yields measurable improvements in both answer correctness and the quality/relevance of supporting evidence passages.
AI.conclusion: RAGA demonstrates that an agentic, tool-using LLM framework can autonomously construct and maintain a knowledge graph while simultaneously leveraging it for improved retrieval. The system’s hybrid symbolic-vector retrieval and evidence-linked KG entries enhance QA performance and interpretability. The proposed architecture and initial experimental results provide a baseline and reference design for future work on agent-based autonomous KG construction and retrieval systems.

title: Taming "Zombie'' Agents: A Markov State-Aware Framework for Resilient Multi-Agent Evolution
summary: Recent advancements in LLM-based multi-agent systems have demonstrated remarkable collaborative capabilities across complex tasks. To improve overall efficiency, existing methods often rely on aggressive graph evolution among agents (e.g., node or edge pruning), which risks prematurely discarding valuable agents due to transient issues such as hallucinations or temporary knowledge gaps. However, such hard pruning overlooks the potential for ``zombie'' agents to recover and contribute in subsequent discussion rounds. In this paper, we propose AgentRevive, a Markov state-aware framework for resilient multi-agent evolution. Our approach dynamically manages agent collaboration through soft state transitions, implemented via two key components: (1) State-Aware Policy Learning: Agent states are divided into ``Active'', ``Standby'', and ``Terminated'' states, selectively propagating messages based on agent memory. The policy employs a risk estimator to optimize agent state transitions by assessing hallucination risk, minimizing the influence of unreliable nodes while safeguarding valuable ones. (2) State-Aware Edge Optimization: Subgraph edges are pruned according to states learned from the policy, permanently removing ``Terminated'' nodes and retaining ``Standby'' nodes for subsequent rounds to assess their potential future contributions. Extensive experiments on general reasoning, domain-specific, and hallucination challenge tasks show that our method consistently outperforms strong baselines and significantly reduces token consumption through state-aware agent scheduling.

AI.tldr: AgentRevive is a state-aware Markov framework that manages LLM-based multi-agent systems via soft agent state transitions (Active, Standby, Terminated) and state-aware edge pruning, improving performance and reducing token cost compared to hard pruning methods.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based multi-agent systems often use aggressive graph evolution, such as hard pruning of nodes or edges, to reduce computation and improve efficiency. However, this can prematurely remove agents that are temporarily unreliable due to hallucinations or knowledge gaps, losing their potential future contributions. The authors aim to design a more resilient evolution mechanism that preserves possibly useful agents while still mitigating the impact of unreliable ones and controlling token consumption.
AI.method: The paper introduces AgentRevive, a Markov state-aware framework for multi-agent collaboration. Agents are assigned states (Active, Standby, Terminated) and messages are propagated selectively based on these states and agent memory. A state-aware policy learning module uses a risk estimator to evaluate each agent’s hallucination risk and decide state transitions, thus reducing the influence of unreliable agents while protecting potentially valuable ones. Additionally, a state-aware edge optimization component prunes edges in the agent interaction graph according to learned states: edges of Terminated nodes are permanently removed, while Standby nodes and their edges are retained to allow possible reactivation in later rounds.
AI.result: Across experiments on general reasoning tasks, domain-specific problems, and hallucination-focused benchmarks, AgentRevive surpasses strong baseline multi-agent methods in accuracy and robustness. It also notably lowers token usage by more efficiently scheduling which agents are active and which communications occur, without sacrificing (and often improving) performance.
AI.conclusion: Soft, state-aware evolution of multi-agent LLM systems—implemented via Markovian agent states and state-driven edge pruning—can achieve better robustness to hallucinations and transient failures than hard pruning approaches. By allowing potentially useful "zombie" agents to move into a Standby state rather than being immediately discarded, AgentRevive improves both task performance and token efficiency, suggesting that resilient, probabilistic management of agent lifecycles is a promising direction for multi-agent LLM architectures.

title: Scientific Logicality Enriched Methodology for LLM Reasoning: A Practice in Physics
comment: Accepted to the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: With the continuous advancement of reasoning abilities in Large Language Models (LLMs), their application to scientific reasoning tasks has gained significant research attention. Current research primarily emphasizes boosting LLMs' performance on scientific QA benchmarks by training on larger, more comprehensive datasets with extended reasoning chains. However, these approaches neglect the essence of the scientific reasoning process -- logicality, which is the rational foundation to ensure the validity of reasoning steps leading to reliable conclusions. In this work, we make the first systematic investigation into the internal logicality underlying LLM scientific reasoning, and develop a scientific logicality-enriched methodology, including a set of assessment criteria and data sampling methods for logicality-guided training, to improve the logical faithfulness as well as task performance. Further, we take physics, characterized by its diverse logical structures and formalisms, as an exemplar discipline to practise the above methodology. For data construction, we extract scientific problems from academic literature and sample a high-quality dataset exhibiting strong logicality. Experiments based on three different backbone LLMs reveal that: 1) the training data we constructed can effectively improve the scientific logicality in LLM reasoning; and 2) the enriched scientific logicality plays a critical role in solving scientific problems. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/ScienceOne-AI/PhysLogic}{https://github.com/ScienceOne-AI/PhysLogic}.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a methodology to explicitly model and improve the logical coherence of LLMs’ scientific reasoning, showing that logic-enriched training data significantly boosts both reasoning faithfulness and performance on physics problems.
AI.motivation: Existing work on applying LLMs to scientific reasoning mostly focuses on scaling data and making reasoning chains longer, but pays insufficient attention to whether the intermediate reasoning steps are logically valid. Since scientific reasoning crucially depends on rigorous logical structure to guarantee reliable conclusions, the authors aim to systematically study and enhance the internal logicality of LLM reasoning, which they argue has been largely overlooked.
AI.method: The authors (1) define assessment criteria to evaluate the logicality of LLM-generated scientific reasoning; (2) design data sampling methods that preferentially collect examples with strong logical structure for use in training; and (3) instantiate this methodology in physics, extracting problems from academic literature and curating a high-quality physics reasoning dataset. They then fine-tune or train three different backbone LLMs on this logicality-enriched dataset and evaluate changes in both logical faithfulness and task performance.
AI.result: Across three backbone LLMs, training on the constructed physics dataset leads to marked improvements in the logical coherence (scientific logicality) of their reasoning chains and also yields better accuracy on scientific problem-solving benchmarks. The experiments demonstrate that models trained with logicality-aware data are more consistent and rigorous in their step-by-step reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The study provides the first systematic examination of internal logicality in LLM-based scientific reasoning and shows that explicitly enriching training data with logically well-structured examples significantly benefits both reasoning faithfulness and end-task accuracy. Physics serves as a strong testbed, and the results suggest that logicality is not just an aesthetic property but a key factor in effectively solving scientific problems. The authors release their dataset and code to support further research on logic-aware scientific LLMs.

title: AMATA: Adaptive Multi-Agent Trajectory Alignment for Knowledge-Intensive Question Answering
summary: Despite substantial advances in large language models (LLMs), generating factually consistent responses for knowledge-intensive question answering remains challenging. These difficulties are primarily due to hallucinations and the limitations of LLMs in bridging long-tail knowledge gaps. To address this, we propose AMATA, an Adaptive Multi-Agent Trajectory Alignment framework that dynamically integrates external knowledge to improve response interpretability and factual grounding. Our architecture leverages six specialized agents that collaboratively perform structured actions for complex question reasoning. We formalize multi-agent collaboration with external tools as a trajectory preference alignment problem, incorporating question-aware agent customization and inter-agent preference harmonization. AMATA introduces two principal innovations: (1) Intra-Trajectory Preference Learning, which learns objective-oriented preferences to prioritize critical agents, and (2) Inter-Agent Dependency Learning, which captures cross-agent tool dependencies through a novel dependency-aware direct preference optimization technique. Empirical results show that AMATA consistently outperforms baseline approaches, knowledge-augmented frameworks, and LLM-based trajectory systems on five established knowledge-intensive QA benchmarks. Further analysis demonstrates the efficiency of our method in reducing token consumption.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AMATA, an adaptive multi-agent framework that aligns reasoning trajectories with external knowledge to reduce hallucinations and improve factual accuracy in knowledge-intensive QA.
AI.motivation: LLMs still struggle with factual consistency in knowledge-intensive QA because of hallucinations and difficulty handling long-tail knowledge that is not well represented in model parameters. Existing methods for tool use or multi-agent collaboration are not sufficiently aligned with question needs or inter-agent dependencies. The authors aim to provide a principled way to coordinate multiple specialized agents and tools so that reasoning steps are both interpretable and better grounded in external knowledge.
AI.method: The authors design AMATA, an Adaptive Multi-Agent Trajectory Alignment framework with six specialized agents that interact with external tools and each other via structured actions. They cast multi-agent tool-augmented reasoning as a trajectory preference alignment problem: among many possible multi-step interaction trajectories, the system should prefer those that best serve the question’s objective. Two main components are introduced. (1) Intra-Trajectory Preference Learning: learns objective-oriented preferences to focus on the most critical agents and their actions within a trajectory. (2) Inter-Agent Dependency Learning: models and optimizes cross-agent tool dependencies via a dependency-aware variant of direct preference optimization, so that agents’ actions are coordinated rather than isolated. Training aligns trajectories and agent behaviors with ground-truth answers and preferences over reasoning paths.
AI.result: On five standard knowledge-intensive QA benchmarks, AMATA achieves better performance than strong baselines, including general LLMs, knowledge-augmented systems, and prior LLM-based trajectory reasoning approaches. The framework not only improves answer accuracy and factual consistency but also reduces token usage compared to competing methods, indicating more efficient tool use and reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Coordinating multiple specialized agents via trajectory preference alignment, with explicit modeling of intra-trajectory importance and inter-agent dependencies, leads to more accurate, interpretable, and efficient knowledge-intensive QA. AMATA demonstrates that structured multi-agent collaboration with preference-based optimization is an effective way to reduce hallucinations and leverage external knowledge, suggesting a promising direction for future LLM reasoning systems.

title: Capturing LLM Capabilities via Evidence-Calibrated Query Clustering
summary: Query clustering organizes queries into groups that reflect shared latent capability demands, enabling capability-aware LLM evaluation. Existing clustering methods, which primarily rely on semantic taxonomies or embeddings, often fail to capture such latent capability requirements due to a misalignment between surface-level semantics and actual model performance. We propose ECC, an algorithm that calibrates prior semantic embeddings using limited posterior model comparisons to bridge the gap between surface-level semantics and latent capability requirements. ECC characterizes each cluster through a capability profile parameterized by a Bradley-Terry model and uses trainable mixture weights to accommodate queries with mixed capability demands, jointly learning a flexible, capability-aware clustering structure that supports query-specific inference of LLM capabilities. Extensive quantitative and qualitative evaluations demonstrate that ECC significantly improves LLM capability ranking quality, outperforming human-labeled and embedding-based baselines by an average of 17.64 and 18.02 percentage points, respectively, and proves effective in downstream tasks such as query routing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ECC, a capability-aware query clustering algorithm for more accurate LLM evaluation by aligning clusters with latent capability demands rather than surface semantics.
AI.motivation: Existing query clustering for LLM evaluation relies mostly on semantic similarity or human taxonomies, which often misalign with the true underlying capabilities required to answer queries, leading to poor capability-aware evaluation and routing.
AI.method: ECC starts from prior semantic embeddings and calibrates them using a limited number of posterior model performance comparisons. It represents each cluster via a capability profile modeled with a Bradley-Terry framework and employs trainable mixture weights so that each query can express mixed capability demands. The algorithm jointly learns these capability profiles and mixture weights to construct a flexible, capability-aware clustering that supports query-specific capability inference.
AI.result: In experiments with both quantitative metrics and qualitative analysis, ECC significantly improves the quality of LLM capability ranking compared with human-labeled clusters and pure embedding-based clustering, with average gains of 17.64 and 18.02 percentage points, respectively. It also shows strong performance on practical downstream tasks, such as query routing.
AI.conclusion: Calibrating semantic embeddings with limited model comparison data enables clustering that better reflects latent capability demands of queries. ECC yields more accurate, flexible capability-aware clusters, improving LLM capability ranking and downstream applications like query routing.

title: Learning Transferable Topology Priors for Multi-Agent LLM Collaboration Across Domains
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based multi-agent systems have shown strong potential for complex reasoning by coordinating specialized agents through structured communication. However, existing topology-evolution methods typically construct or optimize a collaboration topology for each query from scratch, leading to substantial online search overhead, high inference-time token consumption, and limited scalability in multi-domain settings. We propose TopoPrior, a framework for learning transferable topology priors for multi-agent LLM collaboration across domains. Rather than repeatedly searching for effective collaboration structures online, TopoPrior learns reusable topology priors from reference collaboration graphs collected offline from multiple domains and uses them to generate query-conditioned initial collaboration graphs for downstream refinement. By shifting part of topology search from per-query online optimization to offline prior learning, TopoPrior amortizes search cost while remaining compatible with existing topology-evolution backbones. Technically, TopoPrior contains two key components. First, a transferable topology prior learning module employs a conditional variational graph framework to capture reusable structural regularities across domains in a latent space. Second, a query-conditioned latent adaptation module introduces adversarial alignment to reduce unnecessary domain discrepancy while preserving query-relevant structural variation. Experiments on multi-domain reasoning benchmarks show that TopoPrior consistently improves several heterogeneous topology-evolution backbones while reducing online inference-time token usage, with only modest additional trainable parameters. These results suggest that transferable topology initialization is an effective and lightweight mechanism for improving the efficiency of multi-agent LLM collaboration across domains.

AI.tldr: TopoPrior learns reusable, transferable collaboration topologies for LLM-based multi-agent systems to cut online search cost and token usage while improving reasoning performance across domains.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems need to discover good collaboration structures (who talks to whom, in what pattern) for each query. Current topology-evolution methods search from scratch per query, which is slow, token-expensive, and hard to scale to many domains. There is a need for a way to reuse structural knowledge about effective collaboration so that search is amortized and more efficient across domains.
AI.method: TopoPrior collects reference collaboration graphs offline from multiple domains and learns a transferable topology prior using a conditional variational graph model that encodes structural regularities into a latent space. At inference, given a new query, it samples or predicts a query-conditioned initial collaboration graph from this prior, then lets any existing topology-evolution backbone refine it. A query-conditioned latent adaptation module with adversarial alignment aligns latent spaces across domains to reduce irrelevant domain differences while preserving query-relevant structural variability.
AI.result: On multi-domain reasoning benchmarks, plugging TopoPrior into various heterogeneous topology-evolution backbones leads to consistent performance gains while reducing online inference-time token consumption. The approach achieves these benefits with only a modest number of additional trainable parameters, showing that the prior can be learned efficiently and transferred effectively.
AI.conclusion: Learning a transferable topology prior is an effective and lightweight way to initialize collaboration structures for multi-agent LLM systems. By moving part of the topology search from online, per-query optimization to offline prior learning across domains, TopoPrior improves efficiency and scalability of multi-agent LLM collaboration without major architectural changes to existing topology-evolution methods.

title: F2IND-IT! -- Multimodal Fuzzy Fake Indian News Detection using Images and Text
summary: Biased manipulation of facts across regional and national media outlets complicates misinformation detection in diverse landscapes like India. This paper introduces a novel multimodal framework combining visual and textual modalities for enhanced fake news detection on Indian media. The architecture utilizes a ResNet-50 Convolutional Neural Network to extract visual features from news images, a DistilBERT encoder to obtain textual semantic embeddings, and an Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS) to generate a fuzzy reliability score. A lightweight attention-based fusion module assigns learnable weights to each modality prior to classification. Evaluated on the IFND dataset, the proposed model is validated through an in-depth comparative analysis against previous research. Experimental results demonstrate superior performance across accuracy, precision, recall, and $F_1$-scores, confirming the efficacy of the architecture.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a multimodal fake news detection model for Indian media that fuses image and text features with a neuro-fuzzy reliability score, achieving better performance than prior work on the IFND dataset.
AI.motivation: Misinformation in India is challenging to detect because news narratives and their visual presentations vary widely across regional and national outlets, and existing fake news detectors often rely on a single modality (usually text) and are not tailored to the Indian media context. There is a need for robust methods that can jointly exploit visual and textual signals and handle uncertainty in the reliability of each modality.
AI.method: The method is a multimodal architecture consisting of: (1) a ResNet-50 CNN to extract high-level visual features from accompanying news images; (2) a DistilBERT encoder to obtain compact semantic embeddings from the news text; (3) an Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference System (ANFIS) that computes a fuzzy reliability score, likely modeling uncertainty or trustworthiness of the inputs; and (4) a lightweight attention-based fusion module that assigns learnable importance weights to the different modalities (and possibly the fuzzy score) before a final classification layer predicts whether the news is fake or real. The system is trained and evaluated on the IFND dataset.
AI.result: On the IFND dataset, the proposed framework outperforms previous approaches on standard classification metrics, including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, as shown by comparative experiments against prior research baselines.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that combining visual and textual features with an attention-based fusion mechanism and a neuro-fuzzy reliability component yields a more effective fake news detector for Indian media. The improved metrics demonstrate that modeling multimodal information and uncertainty can significantly enhance misinformation detection performance in complex, diverse media environments like India.

title: NewsLens: A Multi-Agent Framework for Adversarial News Bias Navigation
summary: Media bias detection has predominantly been framed as a classification task: assign a political label to an article or outlet. We argue this framing is too shallow: it identifies that bias exists but not where, how, or crucially, what is structurally omitted. We present NewsLens, a five-agent adversarial pipeline for structured news bias navigation. A Fact Verifier, Progressive Framing Analyst, Conservative Framing Analyst, Propaganda Detector, and Neutral Summarizer collaborate to deconstruct articles into interpretable framing maps, exposing ideological omissions, rhetorical manipulation, and framing boundaries. The system is evaluated on 15 articles across four geopolitical event clusters (India-Pakistan Kashmir, Gaza, Climate Policy, Ukraine) using Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct (4-bit quantised, Google Colab T4), with cross-model validation using Mistral 7B on the Kashmir cluster. Center outlets show the highest mean Perspective Divergence Score (PDS: Qwen 0.907, Mistral 0.729 on Kashmir subset); conservative-framing outlets show the highest mean Manipulation Index (MI: 0.600 across both models). Cross-model comparison shows high consistency for high-propaganda content (Republic World delta-PDS=0.125, MI=0.8 both models) and greater variance for nuanced reporting. Mann-Whitney U tests find no statistically significant between-group differences at n=15, reported honestly as a sample-size limitation confirmed by post-hoc power analysis. A partial ablation removing the Propaganda Detector shows degraded omission precision in the Neutral Summarizer output. The architecture extends prior lexical-geometric bias work to agentic LLM reasoning, and is fully reproducible using open-weight models without API keys.

AI.tldr: Introduces NewsLens, a multi-agent LLM pipeline that maps and explains ideological framing and propaganda in news articles, going beyond simple left/right label classification.
AI.motivation: Most media bias detection work just classifies outlets or articles into coarse political labels, which indicates that bias exists but not where it appears in the text, how it is linguistically realized, or what perspectives are omitted. This coarse framing is inadequate for transparency, critique, or user-facing tools that help readers understand framing structures, omissions, and propagandistic elements. The authors aim to build a more fine-grained, interpretable system that decomposes news articles into explicit framing components and can surface ideological gaps and manipulations.
AI.method: They design NewsLens, a five-agent adversarial pipeline based on open-weight LLMs. The agents are: (1) Fact Verifier to separate factual content from contested claims, (2) Progressive Framing Analyst, (3) Conservative Framing Analyst, (4) Propaganda Detector, and (5) Neutral Summarizer. These agents collaboratively analyze each article to construct “framing maps” that highlight what frames are used, what perspectives are missing, and where rhetorical or propagandistic techniques appear. They run the system on 15 news articles across four geopolitical topic clusters using a quantized Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct model on commodity hardware, and perform cross-model validation on one cluster using Mistral 7B. They define metrics such as Perspective Divergence Score (PDS) to quantify how far progressive vs conservative framings diverge, and a Manipulation Index (MI) to quantify propaganda intensity. They conduct a partial ablation by removing the Propaganda Detector to see how it impacts omission precision in the Neutral Summarizer’s output, and use statistical tests (Mann–Whitney U, power analysis) to assess between-group differences, though with low sample size.
AI.result: Center outlets show the highest mean PDS (Qwen: 0.907; Mistral on Kashmir subset: 0.729), implying that ostensibly centrist reporting still elicits strongly divergent framings from progressive and conservative agents. Outlets associated with conservative framing exhibit the highest mean Manipulation Index (MI = 0.600 across both models), suggesting denser propagandistic or manipulative rhetoric in these sources under the defined metric. Cross-model comparison indicates that high-propaganda content produces stable measurements across Qwen and Mistral (e.g., for Republic World, delta-PDS=0.125 and MI=0.8 on both models), whereas more nuanced reporting yields higher variance across models. Statistical tests (Mann–Whitney U) with n=15 do not find significant between-group differences, which the authors attribute to low power, confirmed via post-hoc power analysis. The ablation removing the Propaganda Detector degrades omission precision in the Neutral Summarizer, indicating that explicit propaganda recognition improves the system’s ability to surface missing perspectives and refine neutral summaries.
AI.conclusion: NewsLens demonstrates that a structured, multi-agent LLM architecture can move media bias analysis beyond simple partisan labels toward interpretable framing maps that reveal omissions and rhetorical manipulation. The system is reproducible with small, open-weight models on modest hardware and shows cross-model consistency for clearly propagandistic content. While the small evaluation set prevents strong statistical claims about differences between outlet groups, the pipeline’s qualitative behavior and ablation results support its design choices. The work extends prior lexical-geometric approaches to bias by integrating agentic reasoning and suggests a path toward practical, transparent tools for navigating ideological framing in news media.

title: Latent Heuristic Search: Continuous Optimization for Automated Algorithm Design
comment: Accepted at LION 2026, The Learning and Intelligent Optimization Conference
summary: The integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) into evolutionary frameworks has established a new paradigm for automated heuristic discovery. Despite their promise, these methods typically search in the discrete space of program syntax, relying on stochastic sampling to navigate a highly non-convex optimization landscape. This work proposes a continuous heuristic discovery framework that shifts optimization to a learned latent manifold. We employ an encoder to map discrete programs into continuous embeddings and train a differentiable surrogate model to predict performance, enabling gradient-based search. To regularize the optimization trajectory, an invertible normalizing flow maps these embeddings to a structured Gaussian prior, where we perform gradient ascent. The resulting optimized latent vectors are projected through a learned mapper into soft prompts, which condition a frozen LLM to synthesize novel executable heuristics. We evaluate the proposed method on the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP), the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP), the Knapsack Problem (KSP), and Online Bin Packing (OBP). Empirical results demonstrate that continuous latent-space optimization achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art discrete evolutionary baselines while offering a complementary methodological alternative for automated algorithm design. The implementation code is available at \url{https://github.com/cheikh025/LHS}.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a continuous latent-space framework for discovering heuristics with LLMs, achieving performance comparable to state-of-the-art discrete evolutionary methods on several combinatorial optimization problems.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based heuristic discovery methods operate in discrete program space, leading to difficult non-convex optimization that relies on stochastic search. The authors aim to overcome limitations of discrete search by moving to a continuous latent space where gradient-based optimization is feasible and potentially more efficient.
AI.method: They encode discrete heuristic programs into continuous embeddings using an encoder, then train a differentiable surrogate model to predict heuristic performance from these embeddings. To regularize and structure the search space, they use an invertible normalizing flow that maps embeddings to a Gaussian prior, where gradient ascent is performed. Optimized latent vectors are then mapped into soft prompts via a learned mapper, which condition a frozen LLM to generate new executable heuristic programs. The framework is evaluated on TSP, CVRP, KSP, and OBP.
AI.result: Across multiple combinatorial optimization benchmarks, the continuous latent-space optimization framework matches or closely approaches the performance of strong discrete evolutionary baselines, demonstrating that gradient-based search in latent space can be competitive for automated heuristic discovery.
AI.conclusion: Continuous latent-space optimization combined with LLM-based program synthesis is a viable and competitive alternative to traditional discrete evolutionary search for automated algorithm and heuristic design, offering a different methodological perspective with comparable empirical performance. The authors release code to facilitate reuse and further research.

title: Learning Faster with Better Tokens: Parameter-Efficient Vocabulary Adaptation for Specialized Text Summarization
comment: 16 pages. Accepted in the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics [ACL (Main) 2026] as a long paper
summary: Large language models pretrained on general-domain corpora often exhibit tokenization inefficiencies when applied to specialized domains. Although continual pretraining for domain adaptation partially alleviate performance degradation, it does not resolve the fundamental vocabulary mismatch. To address this gap, we introduce a targeted parameter-efficient domain adaptation approach that combines vocabulary adaptation with pretraining for LLM-based text summarization. Our unified framework augments pretrained tokenizers with domain-specific tokens while selectively replacing under-trained and unreachable tokens to limit parameter growth. We evaluate our approach on Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen2.5-7B across legal and medical summarization tasks on a challenge-oriented evaluation protocol focused on expert-driven text and summaries which typically has higher concentration of over-fragmented Out-of-Vocabulary (OOV) words.
