The vocabulary adaptation algorithm enhances the overall quality of the summarization model by improving semantic similarity between the generated summaries and their references. In addition, the adapted model produces summaries that incorporate more appropriate novel and domain-specific words, leading to improved coherence, relevance, and faithfulness. We further observe that our proposed approach significantly reduce training time by $35-55\%$ over continual pretraining and reduce parameter counts up to $37\%$ w.r.t expansion-only methods. We make the codebase publicly available at https://github.com/gb-kgp/VocabReplace-Then-Expand.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a parameter-efficient method to adapt large language models’ vocabularies and pretraining to specialized domains (legal, medical) for summarization, yielding better summaries with less training time and fewer extra parameters.
AI.motivation: General-domain LLMs use tokenizers and vocabularies that are inefficient for specialized domains, causing over-fragmentation of domain-specific terms and leading to degraded summarization quality; existing continual pretraining reduces performance gaps but does not fix the underlying vocabulary mismatch or associated computational inefficiencies.
AI.method: They design a unified vocabulary adaptation and pretraining framework that (1) augments an existing tokenizer with domain-specific tokens, and (2) selectively replaces under-trained and unreachable tokens to control vocabulary and parameter growth; they then perform targeted, parameter-efficient domain adaptation pretraining of Llama-3.1-8B and Qwen2.5-7B, and evaluate on legal and medical summarization tasks using a challenge-oriented, expert-driven evaluation protocol emphasizing texts rich in over-fragmented OOV words.
AI.result: Vocabulary adaptation leads to improved semantic similarity between model-generated and reference summaries, and the adapted models generate more suitable novel and domain-specific terms, which enhances coherence, relevance, and faithfulness; computationally, their approach cuts training time by 35–55% compared with standard continual pretraining and reduces additional parameter counts by up to 37% relative to expansion-only vocabulary adaptation baselines.
AI.conclusion: Targeted vocabulary adaptation combined with efficient pretraining offers a more effective and resource-efficient way to adapt general LLMs to specialized-domain summarization than traditional continual pretraining or naive vocabulary expansion, improving both output quality and computational efficiency; the approach is implemented and released as open-source code (VocabReplace-Then-Expand).

title: Dynamics of collective creativity in AI art competitions
summary: Creativity is a fundamental aspect of how culture evolves, yet the mechanisms by which groups produce novelty are notoriously difficult to infer from the historical record. Iterated learning experiments have shown that cultural transmission reliably distorts artifacts toward the inductive biases of learners, but most of this work uses linear chains between human participants, leaving open how these dynamics play out in the networked, human-AI systems that increasingly shape cultural production. In this study, we leverage one such system, Artbreeder, which hosts daily "remix parties" where users iteratively build on each other's work from a single seed image, producing branching lineages of human-AI co-created images. We analyze a dataset of 130,882 images from 368 remix parties over 13 months and find that images become simpler and converge toward common thematic "attractors" (e.g., steampunk scenes, alien architecture). We also find that while more novel "parent" images produce more novel and complex "children" that attract more likes, users paradoxically prefer to remix images that are less novel and complex. Finally, larger remix parties produce more novelty at the cost of lower complexity.

AI.tldr: Study of how creativity and novelty emerge in networked human-AI image remix communities using Artbreeder data.
AI.motivation: To understand how cultural artifacts evolve and how creativity and novelty are generated in modern networked human-AI systems, going beyond traditional linear iterated learning chains in lab settings.
AI.method: Collected and analyzed 130,882 images from 368 Artbreeder "remix parties" over 13 months, examining branching lineages of images derived from a single seed to quantify novelty, complexity, thematic convergence, and user engagement (likes and remix choices).
AI.result: Images tend to become simpler over time and converge toward recurrent thematic attractors (such as steampunk scenes or alien architecture). More novel parent images yield more novel, more complex, and more liked child images, yet users preferentially choose to remix parents that are less novel and complex. Larger remix parties generate greater overall novelty but at the cost of reduced complexity in the resulting images.
AI.conclusion: Cultural evolution in networked human-AI remix systems is characterized by convergence to thematic attractors and a tension between users’ expressed preferences (likes) and their remix behavior, as well as a trade-off between novelty and complexity that is influenced by group size.

title: MiniGPT: Rebuilding GPT from First Principles
summary: This paper presents MiniGPT, a compact from-scratch implementation of GPT-style autoregressive language modeling in PyTorch. The aim is to rebuild the core GPT pipeline from first principles after studying the design of nanoGPT by Andrej Karpathy, while keeping the model and training code independently written in a single notebook. MiniGPT implements token and positional embeddings, causal multi-head self-attention, pre-LayerNorm Transformer blocks, residual connections, feed-forward MLP layers, next-token cross-entropy training (teacher forcing), validation tracking, checkpoint selection, and autoregressive text generation. This paper evaluates the implementation on Tiny Shakespeare dataset using character-level tokenization. A baseline 0.83M-parameter model reaches a validation loss of 1.7236 after 3000 training iterations. A stronger 10.77M-parameter configuration, using a larger context length and improved training settings, reaches a best validation loss of 1.4780 and generates text with recognizable Shakespeare-style dialogue structure. MiniGPT does not introduce a new language-model architecture. Instead, it documents a clear and reproducible implementation path from raw text to trained character-level generation, including design choices, training behavior, generation quality, and practical limitations.

AI.tldr: MiniGPT is a clean, from-scratch PyTorch reimplementation of a GPT-style Transformer, focused on education and reproducibility rather than proposing a new architecture, and validated on the Tiny Shakespeare dataset.
AI.motivation: To provide a compact, fully self-contained, and independently written educational implementation of GPT-style autoregressive language modeling that bridges the gap between theoretical descriptions and practical training code, inspired by but not copying nanoGPT.
AI.method: Implement a GPT-like Transformer in a single PyTorch notebook, including token and positional embeddings, causal multi-head self-attention, pre-LayerNorm Transformer blocks with residual connections, MLP layers, next-token cross-entropy training with teacher forcing, validation tracking and checkpointing, and autoregressive generation; then train and evaluate this model on the Tiny Shakespeare dataset with character-level tokenization using both a small (0.83M parameters) and a larger (10.77M parameters) configuration.
AI.result: On Tiny Shakespeare, the 0.83M-parameter baseline achieves a validation loss of 1.7236 after 3000 iterations, while a 10.77M-parameter model with longer context and improved training settings achieves a best validation loss of 1.4780 and produces text that qualitatively resembles Shakespearean dialogue in structure.
AI.conclusion: MiniGPT successfully demonstrates a transparent, reproducible path from raw text to a trained character-level GPT-style generator, highlighting key design decisions, observed training dynamics, output quality, and constraints, without claiming architectural novelty but offering an educational reference implementation.

title: MADP: A Multi-Agent Pipeline for Sustainable Document Processing with Human-in-the-Loop
summary: Document processing automation remains a critical challenge in enterprise environments, where traditional manual approaches are labor-intensive and error-prone. We present MADP, a multi-agent architecture that addresses the challenge of automating document processing in enterprise settings by combining deep learning-based classification and parsing with large language model extraction, while maintaining accuracy through selective human validation. Our system integrates five specialized agents--Classificator, Splitter, Parser, Extraction, and Validator--with a Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) mechanism and a novel Prompt Fine Tuning with Feedback Inheritance (PFTFI) approach. The operational analysis on a production use-case scenario of 100,000 invoices per year indicates a potential reduction of Full-Time Equivalent (FTE) requirements by approximately 70%. Production deployment on 955 real-world documents processed through January 2026 achieves a 97.0% full-pipeline automation rate, with only 3% requiring non-AI fallback. Ablation evaluation on a stratified 100-document subset (5 documents per each of 20 supplier/document-type categories) demonstrates that the full MADP configuration with Human-in-the-Loop supervision attains 98.5% document-level accuracy. Additionally, we present a comprehensive sustainability analysis showing that our hybrid AI+HITL approach reduces CO2 emissions by 69%, energy consumption by 69%, and water usage by 63% compared to traditional manual processing. Benchmark comparisons of multiple LLM backends (Granite-Docling, Mistral-Small, DeepSeek-OCR) provide practical insights for deployment in production environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MADP, a multi-agent AI system with human-in-the-loop for automating enterprise document processing, achieving high accuracy, major labor savings, and environmental benefits compared to manual processing.
AI.motivation: Enterprise document processing (e.g., invoices) is still largely manual, costly, slow, and error-prone. Existing automated systems either lack accuracy, don’t scale well to diverse document types, or ignore human oversight and sustainability impacts. The authors want a practical, production-ready solution that combines modern AI (deep learning + LLMs) with controlled human supervision to significantly reduce manual effort and environmental footprint while preserving or improving quality.
AI.method: They design MADP, a multi-agent architecture with five specialized agents: (1) Classificator for document type classification, (2) Splitter for segmenting or separating multi-document inputs, (3) Parser for structural parsing, (4) Extraction agent using large language models to extract fields, and (5) Validator agent, together with a Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) component to selectively review uncertain or critical cases. They also introduce a Prompt Fine Tuning with Feedback Inheritance (PFTFI) mechanism, which refines prompts over time based on human feedback, improving the LLM behavior. The system is evaluated in a realistic enterprise scenario at production scale and compared across different LLM backends.
AI.result: Operational analysis suggests MADP can cut required Full-Time Equivalents (FTEs) by about 70% in an environment processing 100,000 invoices annually. In production on 955 real documents, 97% of cases are fully automated end-to-end, with 3% falling back to non-AI methods. Ablation on a stratified 100-document test set shows 98.5% document-level accuracy when using the full MADP configuration with HITL. Sustainability analysis indicates a 69% reduction in CO2 emissions and energy consumption and a 63% reduction in water usage compared to manual processing. Benchmarking multiple LLMs (Granite-Docling, Mistral-Small, DeepSeek-OCR) provides comparative performance data for deployment choices.
AI.conclusion: MADP demonstrates that a multi-agent, hybrid AI plus human-in-the-loop architecture can reliably automate the majority of enterprise document processing while maintaining high accuracy and substantially reducing labor and environmental costs. The PFTFI mechanism improves prompt quality over time, and the empirical results on invoices suggest that such systems are ready for real-world production deployment, with flexibility to choose among different LLM backends depending on constraints and needs.

title: BELIEF: Structured Evidence Modeling and Uncertainty-Aware Fusion for Biomedical Question Answering
summary: Biomedical question answering often requires decisions from retrieved literature whose relevance, quality, and support for candidate answers are uneven. Most retrieval-augmented large language model (LLM) methods feed this literature to the model as flat text, leaving evidence reliability and remaining uncertainty largely implicit. We propose BELIEF, a structured evidence modeling and uncertainty-aware fusion framework for closed-set biomedical question answering. Rather than treating retrieved documents as undifferentiated context, BELIEF converts them into evidence objects that record clinical attributes, source quality, question relevance, support strength, and the associated candidate hypothesis. These evidence objects provide a shared basis for two complementary reasoning paths. The symbolic path constructs reliability-weighted basic probability assignments based on Dempster--Shafer (D-S) theory over a finite answer space and performs uncertainty-aware symbolic evidence fusion to estimate belief and residual uncertainty. The neural path uses the same structured evidence for LLM-based semantic inference, while a reliability-aware arbitration module reconciles the symbolic and neural outputs according to belief strength, uncertainty, evidence reliability, and semantic consistency. Experiments on PubMedQA, MedQA, and MedMCQA with five general-purpose LLM backbones show that BELIEF obtains the best result in 25 of 30 backbone--dataset--metric settings. Comparisons with biomedical-domain models indicate that BELIEF is competitive on MedQA and MedMCQA, while specialized biomedical pretraining remains advantageous on PubMedQA. Ablation, complementarity, uncertainty-stratified, and cost analyses further show that BELIEF improves retrieved-evidence utilization by making evidence structure, path disagreement, and decision uncertainty explicit.

AI.tldr: BELIEF is a retrieval-augmented QA framework that turns biomedical literature into structured evidence objects and fuses symbolic uncertainty modeling with neural LLM reasoning to answer closed-set medical questions more reliably.
AI.motivation: Existing biomedical QA with LLMs typically feeds retrieved documents as flat text, implicitly handling evidence quality, relevance, and uncertainty. This makes it hard to know which evidence is trustworthy, how conflicting findings are reconciled, and how uncertain the final answer is. The authors aim to explicitly model evidence reliability and decision uncertainty so that retrieved information is used more effectively and transparently.
AI.method: BELIEF first retrieves literature for a biomedical question and converts passages into structured evidence objects, each encoding clinical attributes, source quality, question relevance, support strength, and the candidate hypothesis it supports or refutes. It then runs two coordinated reasoning paths: (1) a symbolic path that builds reliability-weighted basic probability assignments under Dempster–Shafer theory, performing uncertainty-aware evidence fusion over the finite answer set to compute belief and residual uncertainty; (2) a neural path where an LLM performs semantic inference over the same structured evidence. A reliability-aware arbitration module combines symbolic and neural outputs by considering belief strength, uncertainty, evidence reliability, and semantic consistency between the paths.
AI.result: On PubMedQA, MedQA, and MedMCQA using five general-purpose LLM backbones, BELIEF achieves the best performance in 25 out of 30 backbone–dataset–metric combinations. Against specialized biomedical LLMs, BELIEF remains competitive on MedQA and MedMCQA, though domain-specific pretraining still has an edge on PubMedQA. Additional ablation, complementarity, uncertainty-stratified, and cost analyses show that structured evidence modeling and dual-path fusion increase the effective use of retrieved evidence and clarify disagreement and uncertainty in decisions.
AI.conclusion: Structuring retrieved biomedical literature into explicit evidence objects and jointly leveraging symbolic uncertainty modeling with neural LLM reasoning leads to more accurate and transparent closed-set biomedical QA. BELIEF improves evidence utilization, exposes uncertainty and path disagreement, and can make general-purpose LLMs competitive with some domain-specialized models, though domain pretraining still matters for certain datasets like PubMedQA.

title: From Imitation to Interaction: Mastering Game of Schnapsen with Shallow Reinforcement Learning
summary: This paper investigates whether shallow neural network agents can master the card game Schnapsen and challenge a strong search-based baseline, RdeepBot, which uses Monte Carlo sampling and lookahead search. Guided by a progressively more complex experimental design, we first evaluate a supervised learning agent (MLPBot) trained on replay data and then a reinforcement learning agent (RLBot) with the same shallow architecture trained through asynchronous Monte Carlo updates and experience replay. The results show that supervised imitation does not generalize well enough to defeat strong RdeepBot opponents, whereas reinforcement learning produces substantially stronger agents. In the setting that focuses on the depth parameter of RdeepBot, the best performance is achieved when the learned value function is combined with deeper lookahead during gameplay, allowing RLBot to achieve statistically significant higher winning rates against the strongest evaluated RdeepBot baseline. In the sample-based setting, the gains are more conditional: the strongest performance appears at a relatively lower training num_samples parameter rather than increasing uniformly with stronger sampling.

AI.tldr: Shallow neural network agents are evaluated for playing Schnapsen against a strong search-based bot; reinforcement learning with the same shallow architecture outperforms supervised imitation and can beat the strong baseline when combined with deeper search.
AI.motivation: To understand whether simple, shallow neural network architectures can effectively learn to play a complex imperfect-information card game and challenge a strong, established search-based Monte Carlo bot, especially in terms of sample use and generalization beyond imitation.
AI.method: They build two agents with the same shallow MLP architecture: (1) MLPBot, trained via supervised learning on replay data to imitate expert-like play, and (2) RLBot, trained with reinforcement learning using asynchronous Monte Carlo updates and experience replay. They evaluate both against RdeepBot under two experimental regimes: varying the search depth parameter of RdeepBot and varying the number of Monte Carlo samples used during search, and they also test hybrid agents that combine the learned value function with deeper lookahead search.
AI.result: Supervised imitation (MLPBot) fails to generalize sufficiently and cannot defeat strong RdeepBot configurations. Reinforcement learning (RLBot), in contrast, yields substantially stronger agents. When varying RdeepBot’s depth, the best results occur when RLBot’s learned value function is coupled with deeper search, producing statistically significant higher win rates than the strongest RdeepBot baseline. When varying Monte Carlo samples, performance improvements are more nuanced: RLBot’s best results arise at a relatively modest training num_samples value instead of monotonically improving with more sampling.
AI.conclusion: Shallow neural networks alone, trained via imitation, are insufficient to beat a strong search-based Schnapsen bot, but reinforcement learning with the same shallow architecture and appropriate integration with search can produce agents that surpass the baseline. Furthermore, more search or more sampling does not always yield better performance; there is a sweet spot in search depth and sampling where the learned value function and lookahead complement each other most effectively.

title: Beyond Catalogue Counts: the Dataset Visibility Asymmetry in Low-Resource Multilingual NLP
comment: Accepted at the 15th edition of the Language Resources and Evaluation Conference (LREC 2026)
summary: Multilingual NLP often relies on dataset counts from centralized catalogues to characterize which languages are resource-rich or resource-poor. However, these catalogues record only one layer of dataset visibility: what has been registered or institutionally distributed. They do not necessarily reflect which datasets are created, cited, or reused in the research literature. To examine this gap, we combine a catalogue-based baseline with literature-backed evidence of dataset circulation. We introduce the Resource Density Index (RDI), defined as the number of catalogued datasets per one million speakers, and compute it for the 200 most widely spoken languages in Ethnologue. Among them, 118 languages (59%) have an average RDI of zero across the LRE Map and the Linguistic Data Consortium (LDC), and another 23 fall below 0.1, corresponding to at most one catalogued dataset per ten million speakers. We then apply an LLM-assisted citation-mining pipeline over the Semantic Scholar corpus to these 141 low-visibility languages. After manual validation and consolidation, we identify 609 unique datasets across 53 languages, of which 356 remain openly accessible through working public links. These results reveal a substantial visibility gap: many large-speaker languages appear data-poor in catalogue records yet show clear evidence of dataset activity in the research literature. Our findings suggest that multilingual data scarcity should be understood not only as a production problem, but also as a question of documentation, discoverability, and long-term accessibility. Code and data are publicly available at (https://github.com/zhiyintan/dataset-visibility-asymmetry).

AI.tldr: The paper shows that standard multilingual NLP catalogues vastly underreport datasets for many high-population languages, and that literature mining uncovers hundreds of overlooked resources, reframing ‘low-resource’ as partly a visibility and accessibility issue.
AI.motivation: Multilingual NLP research often labels languages as ‘resource-rich’ or ‘resource-poor’ based on counts from centralized dataset catalogues. However, these catalogues only cover datasets that are formally registered or institutionally distributed and may miss many datasets used in research papers. This mismatch risks mischaracterizing data scarcity, misdirecting funding and research priorities, and overlooking existing resources for large-speaker languages. The authors aim to measure and understand this gap between catalogue records and actual dataset usage in the literature.
AI.method: 1) Define the Resource Density Index (RDI) as the number of catalogued datasets per one million speakers. Compute RDI for the 200 most widely spoken languages in Ethnologue using two major catalogues: the LRE Map and the Linguistic Data Consortium (LDC). 2) Identify 141 languages with very low catalogue visibility (RDI=0 or <0.1). 3) Build an LLM-assisted citation-mining pipeline over the Semantic Scholar corpus to search research papers for dataset mentions related to these low-visibility languages. 4) Manually validate and consolidate the mined results to deduplicate and verify actual datasets and check their current accessibility via public links.
AI.result: Among the top 200 languages by speaker count, 118 (59%) have an average RDI of zero across the LRE Map and LDC, and another 23 have RDI below 0.1 (≤1 catalogued dataset per 10 million speakers). For these 141 low-visibility languages, the literature-mining pipeline uncovers 609 unique datasets across 53 languages, and 356 of these remain available via functioning public links. This demonstrates that a significant number of large-speaker languages that appear data-poor in catalogues in fact have nontrivial dataset activity in the research literature.
AI.conclusion: Standard multilingual dataset catalogues significantly underrepresent available resources for many widely spoken languages, creating a visibility gap between institutional records and actual research practice. As a result, multilingual data scarcity should not be understood solely as a lack of dataset production; it is also driven by incomplete documentation, limited discoverability, and fragile long-term accessibility of datasets. The paper argues that improving dataset cataloguing, citation practices, and infrastructure is crucial for accurately assessing resource levels and supporting fairer multilingual NLP development.

title: Responsible Agentic AI Requires Explicit Provenance
summary: Agentic AI is rapidly proliferating across diverse real-world domains such as software engineering, yet public trust has not kept pace. The central reason is that responsibility, despite being widely discussed, remains a subjective and unenforced concept, as no current agentic framework produces the quantifiable, traceable, and interventionable provenance needed to assign it when harm emerges from compositions no single party designed. We position that what is missing is not better benchmark-level evaluation but $\textbf{explicit provenance}$ across the full agentic lifecycle, which is the only viable basis for making responsibility computable and actionable. We advance this agenda along four axes: establishing $\textit{why}$ such provenance is a structural necessity by identifying responsibility gaps across sociotechnical dimensions, formalizing $\textit{what}$ it must encode through a causal attribution function and responsibility tensor, discussing $\textit{how}$ it can be made computable across four lifecycle layers with preliminary experiments showing that provenance is estimable and interveneable online before irreversible harm accumulates, and examining $\textit{who}$ bears responsibility through a concrete agentic incident. Explicit provenance is not a discretionary refinement but the necessary condition for responsible agentic AI, and no stakeholder across its ecosystem can afford to treat it as optional.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that responsible agentic AI requires explicit, computable provenance across the entire agent lifecycle to assign and operationalize responsibility when harms occur.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI systems are rapidly being deployed in areas like software engineering, but public trust lags because responsibility for harms is diffuse, subjective, and unenforceable. Existing agent frameworks and benchmarks do not provide the quantifiable, traceable, and interventionable provenance needed to attribute responsibility when harm arises from complex compositions that no single actor directly designed.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize and formalize an explicit provenance framework for agentic AI. They: (1) analyze sociotechnical responsibility gaps to show why such provenance is structurally necessary; (2) define what provenance must encode via a causal attribution function and a responsibility tensor; (3) propose how to compute this provenance across four layers of the agentic lifecycle and conduct preliminary experiments showing that provenance can be estimated and used for online intervention before irreversible harm; and (4) examine a concrete agentic incident to map responsibility across stakeholders.
AI.result: They show, through formalization and early experiments, that provenance signals can be computed online across different lifecycle layers, can be linked to causal attribution and responsibility assignments, and can support interventions to prevent or limit accumulating harm. The incident analysis illustrates how their provenance framework clarifies which actors bear which parts of responsibility.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, lifecycle-wide provenance is a structural prerequisite for making responsibility in agentic AI computable, traceable, and actionable. It should not be treated as an optional enhancement but as a necessary condition for responsible deployment, with all ecosystem stakeholders required to engage with and support such provenance mechanisms.

title: Analyzing Error Propagation in Korean Spoken QA with ASR-LLM Cascades
summary: We analyze how automatic speech recognition (ASR) errors propagate through ASR-LLM cascades in Korean spoken question answering (SQA), focusing on downstream semantic failures that conventional ASR metrics cannot fully capture. Our analysis shows that the relative downstream degradation caused by ASR errors is consistent across LLMs with different absolute performance, suggesting that cascade degradation largely tracks ASR-stage information loss. We further identify single-character Korean ASR errors as a distinct semantic-failure channel, where the gold answer becomes entirely absent from the downstream prediction despite only a minimal transcription difference. Finally, an auxiliary comparison shows that a large audio language model outperforms an ASR-LLM pipeline with a matched language backbone in noisy Korean SQA, indicating the potential of direct audio input to mitigate transcript-induced information loss.

AI.tldr: Study of how ASR errors affect LLM performance in Korean spoken QA and comparison with direct audio-language models.
AI.motivation: Conventional ASR metrics like WER do not capture how transcription errors impact downstream semantic tasks, especially in languages like Korean. Understanding this error propagation is crucial for building robust spoken QA systems and for deciding between cascaded ASR-LLM architectures and end-to-end audio language models.
AI.method: Analyze Korean spoken QA pipelines where ASR outputs feed different LLMs; measure downstream performance degradation due to ASR errors and compare it across LLMs. Perform fine-grained error analysis, focusing on single-character Korean transcription errors and their semantic impact. Conduct auxiliary experiments comparing an ASR-LLM cascade against a large audio language model that shares the same language backbone, under noisy conditions.
AI.result: The relative downstream degradation from ASR errors is similar across LLMs with different base accuracy, indicating that the main factor is information loss at the ASR stage. Single-character Korean ASR errors are identified as a key semantic failure mode where the correct answer disappears from the prediction despite minimal textual differences. The audio language model achieves better performance than the matched ASR-LLM cascade on noisy Korean SQA tasks.
AI.conclusion: ASR errors, particularly subtle single-character mistakes in Korean, can cause severe semantic failures in spoken QA that are not adequately reflected by standard ASR metrics. Degradation patterns are largely determined by ASR information loss rather than the specific LLM used. Direct audio language models can mitigate transcript-induced information loss and outperform cascaded ASR-LLM pipelines in noisy conditions, highlighting the promise of end-to-end audio understanding for robust SQA.

title: CAREBench: Evaluating LLMs' Emotion Understanding by Assessing Cognitive Appraisal Reasoning
summary: Emotion understanding is a core capability for LLMs to interact effectively with humans, yet existing evaluation paradigms rely on discrete emotion label prediction and fail to capture the cognitive processes underlying emotion generation. Grounded in appraisal theory, we introduce CAREBench, the first benchmark with complete inferential chain annotations from both first- and third-person perspectives on real-world narratives, spanning appraisal reasoning, appraisal ratings, and multi-label emotion annotation. We propose a process-level evaluation framework and conduct systematic experiments across six LLMs organized around four research questions. We find that stronger models match or surpass human observers on certain tasks, yet fall short on appraisal reasoning and positive emotion recognition; performance across chain steps and sensitivity to appraisal interventions exhibit dissociations across models; and current models have not internalized the mechanisms needed to capture human subjective heterogeneity. These findings suggest that downstream emotion prediction metrics may overestimate LLMs' true emotion understanding, and CAREBench provides a foundation for more diagnostically informative evaluation of LLMs' affective cognitive capabilities.

AI.tldr: Introduces CAREBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLMs’ emotion understanding via appraisal-based reasoning chains instead of only discrete labels.
AI.motivation: Current emotion evaluation for LLMs typically uses simple discrete emotion label prediction, which does not reflect the underlying cognitive and inferential processes by which humans appraise situations and generate emotions. The authors aim to create a more process-level, theory-grounded way to assess whether LLMs genuinely understand emotions, not just match labels.
AI.method: They build CAREBench, a benchmark grounded in appraisal theory, using real-world narratives annotated with full inferential chains from both first- and third-person perspectives. The chains include: (1) appraisal reasoning (textual explanations of why an emotion arises), (2) appraisal ratings (structured assessments of appraisal dimensions), and (3) multi-label emotion annotations. They then design a process-level evaluation framework and run systematic experiments on six different LLMs, structured around four research questions about their appraisal reasoning ability, emotion prediction, sensitivity to appraisal interventions, and capacity to capture subjective heterogeneity.
AI.result: Stronger LLMs can match or exceed human observers on some benchmark tasks, indicating impressive surface performance, but they notably underperform in detailed appraisal reasoning and in recognizing positive emotions. Performance is not uniform across the steps of the appraisal chain, and different models show different patterns of sensitivity to manipulations of appraisals. None of the evaluated models appears to have internalized the mechanisms required to reflect diverse, subjective human emotional experiences reliably.
AI.conclusion: Standard downstream emotion prediction metrics (e.g., label accuracy) likely overstate LLMs’ real emotion understanding, because they miss the underlying cognitive processes involved. CAREBench offers a more diagnostic, process-oriented benchmark that can reveal gaps and dissociations in LLMs’ affective cognition, helping guide future model development and evaluation toward more genuine emotion understanding.

title: VerifyMAS: Hypothesis Verification for Failure Attribution in LLM Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Large language model-driven multi-agent systems (LLM-MAS) excel at complex tasks, yet unreliable agents remain a key bottleneck to system-level reliability. Automatic failure attribution is therefore critical, but existing approaches, such as direct prediction of agent-error pairs and agent-first failure attribution, rely on local logs of agents and miss global failures that only manifest over full interaction trajectories, such as cross-step inconsistencies and inter-agent coordination errors. Moreover, directly predicting failures induces a large combinatorial search space, hindering fine-grained attribution. To address these challenges, we propose VerifyMAS, a hypothesis verification framework for agent failure attribution. Instead of directly predicting faulty agents and error types, VerifyMAS formulates and verifies failure hypotheses against full trajectories. This verification-based approach decomposes attribution into trajectory-level error validation and fine-grained agent localization, providing an error-first attribution approach that captures global failure patterns while substantially reducing the search space. We further introduce a hypothesis-based data construction strategy grounded in a structured error taxonomy and fine-tune a specialized LLM verifier model for trajectory-level failure verification and agent attribution. Experiments on Aegis-Bench and Who&When show that VerifyMAS consistently improves diverse backbone models, including open-source Qwen and API-based GPT models, outperforming prior methods without sacrificing inference efficiency for long multi-agent trajectories.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes VerifyMAS, a verification-based framework to attribute failures in large language model multi-agent systems by testing structured error hypotheses over full interaction trajectories, enabling accurate, fine-grained identification of faulty agents and error types.
AI.motivation: LLM-based multi-agent systems can handle complex tasks, but their overall reliability is limited by individual agents that behave unreliably. Existing failure attribution methods mostly examine local agent logs or predict agent-error pairs directly, which (1) miss global failures that only appear when looking at the entire multi-step, multi-agent trajectory, and (2) suffer from a large combinatorial search space when trying to label which agent made which type of error. The field needs a way to systematically detect and localize failures, especially trajectory-level inconsistencies and coordination issues, without exploding complexity or sacrificing efficiency.
AI.method: The authors introduce VerifyMAS, a hypothesis verification framework for failure attribution in LLM multi-agent systems. Instead of directly predicting which agents and error types occurred, they define structured failure hypotheses, grounded in an error taxonomy, and verify these hypotheses against complete interaction trajectories. The process decomposes attribution into two stages: (1) trajectory-level error validation, where the system checks whether a hypothesized failure pattern occurs anywhere along the trajectory, and (2) fine-grained agent localization, where it pinpoints which specific agents and steps are responsible once an error is confirmed. They also design a hypothesis-based data construction strategy using the error taxonomy to generate training data and fine-tune a dedicated LLM verifier that specializes in trajectory-level failure verification and agent attribution.
AI.result: On benchmarks Aegis-Bench and Who&When, VerifyMAS is evaluated with various backbone LLMs, including open-source Qwen and closed-source GPT models. Across these settings, VerifyMAS consistently improves failure attribution performance over prior methods. It better captures global failure patterns in long multi-agent trajectories and achieves higher accuracy in identifying faulty agents and errors, while maintaining comparable inference efficiency and scalability for long trajectories.
AI.conclusion: VerifyMAS shows that recasting failure attribution in LLM-based multi-agent systems as a hypothesis verification task over full trajectories leads to more accurate and efficient identification of errors and responsible agents. By reducing the combinatorial search space and enabling error-first, trajectory-level reasoning, the framework outperforms previous local-log and direct prediction methods without incurring extra inference costs, suggesting a promising direction for building more reliable multi-agent LLM systems.

title: Hybrid Feature Combinations with CNN for Bangla Fake News Classification
comment: Already accepted and presented in the 3rd International Conference on Big Data, IoT and Machine Learning (BIM 2025)
summary: Nowadays, people in Bangladesh frequently rely on the internet and social media for daily news instead of traditional newspapers. However, the spread of false Bangla news through these platforms poses risks and challenges to the credibility of authentic media. Although several studies have been conducted on detecting Bangla fake news, there is still significant room for improvement in this area. To assist people, this research explores the effectiveness of feature selection approaches in identifying appropriate features, such as semantic, statistical, and character-level features, or their combinations, on the BanFakeNews-2.0 dataset for detecting Bangla fake news using a CNN model. In this paper, key findings reveal that combining multiple features significantly improves recall and F1-scores compared to using individual features alone. The code for this research can be availed here, https://github.com/gulzar09/Bn\_FNews\_H.Feature.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates which feature types or combinations (semantic, statistical, character-level) work best with a CNN to detect Bangla fake news on the BanFakeNews-2.0 dataset, finding that combining features notably improves recall and F1-score.
AI.motivation: With the rise of internet and social media usage in Bangladesh, many people get news online, but the proliferation of fake Bangla news undermines trust in legitimate media. Existing Bangla fake news detection work still leaves room for performance improvement, especially in choosing effective input features. The paper aims to systematically study how different feature types and their combinations affect fake news detection performance.
AI.method: The authors use the BanFakeNews-2.0 dataset as the benchmark for Bangla fake news detection and build a CNN-based classifier. They engineer multiple categories of features: semantic, statistical, and character-level representations. They then experiment with individual feature types and various combinations of them as inputs to the CNN, comparing performance metrics such as recall and F1-score to assess which feature sets are most effective.
AI.result: Experiments show that using combinations of feature types yields better performance than using any single feature category alone. In particular, combined features substantially improve recall and F1-scores in detecting Bangla fake news on the BanFakeNews-2.0 dataset.
AI.conclusion: Effective feature selection and integration are crucial for Bangla fake news detection. Combining semantic, statistical, and character-level features provides a more informative representation for a CNN model than any individual feature type, leading to improved recall and F1. This suggests that future Bangla fake news detection systems should leverage multi-feature fusion to enhance robustness and accuracy.

title: Towards Robust Argumentative Essay Understanding via TIDE: An Interactive Framework with Trial and Debate
summary: Argumentative essays serve as a vital medium for assessing critical thinking and reasoning skills, yet there is limited works on accurately understanding and evaluating such texts via prompt. In this work, we propose TIDE, a novel framework designed to improve criteria-based prompt optimization for argument-related tasks by integrating TrIal and DEbate mechanism. Our method addresses key limitations of criteria-based prompt optimizing by mitigating the influence of noisy training data and enhancing optimization stability. We evaluate TIDE on three core tasks: Automated Essay Scoring, Argument Component Detection, and Argument Relation Identification. Results demonstrate that our framework improves performance across tasks. These findings underscore the potential of combining prompt-based methods for advanced argument understanding.

AI.tldr: A framework (TIDE) to better optimize prompts for argument-related NLP tasks using a Trial-and-Debate mechanism, improving robustness and performance on essay scoring and argument mining.
AI.motivation: Argumentative essays reflect critical thinking, but existing prompt-based approaches struggle to accurately and reliably understand and evaluate them, partly due to noisy supervision and unstable criteria-based prompt optimization. There is a need for a method that can make prompt optimization for argument-focused tasks more robust and effective.
AI.method: Introduce TIDE, a criteria-based prompt optimization framework that incorporates a Trial-and-Debate mechanism. Prompts are iteratively refined using evaluation criteria, while the mechanism reduces the influence of noisy training data and stabilizes optimization. The framework is applied to multiple argument-related tasks, including essay scoring and argument mining, to systematically improve prompt quality.
AI.result: Across three tasks—Automated Essay Scoring, Argument Component Detection, and Argument Relation Identification—TIDE yields better performance than baselines, indicating more accurate argument understanding and evaluation under prompt-based setups.
AI.conclusion: By integrating a Trial-and-Debate mechanism into criteria-based prompt optimization, TIDE makes prompt-based models more robust to noise and more stable, leading to consistent gains on several argument-related NLP tasks and showing that structured prompt optimization can advance automatic understanding of argumentative writing.

title: Residual Semantic Decomposition of Word Embeddings
comment: Short paper; includes appendix. Code and data are not included in the arXiv source package
summary: We introduce Residual Semantic Decomposition (RSD), a neural additive decomposition of word embeddings that balances embedding reconstruction with relational structure preservation. RSD supports recursive binary decomposition: each $K=2$ fit extracts a local semantic axis, while residuals expose information not absorbed by that axis. In manually specified paired-context diagnostics over ambiguous words, RSD separates supplied context anchors above shuffled-label controls, but entropy diagnostics show that ambiguous targets are not uniformly high-entropy boundary points in static GloVe. We therefore treat residual neighborhoods as qualitative diagnostics rather than benchmark sense predictions.

AI.tldr: Proposes Residual Semantic Decomposition (RSD), a method to decompose word embeddings into additive components that capture semantic axes while preserving relational structure, and uses residuals as qualitative diagnostics for word sense ambiguity.
AI.motivation: To better understand and dissect what information word embeddings contain, particularly for ambiguous words, by finding interpretable semantic directions without destroying the original embedding’s relational properties.
AI.method: Introduce RSD, a neural model that performs recursive binary decomposition of word embeddings (K=2). Each fit identifies a semantic axis that reconstructs embeddings while preserving relational structure (e.g., similarities), and the leftover residual vectors at each step capture unmodeled information. The authors evaluate RSD using manually specified paired-context diagnostics for ambiguous words and compare against shuffled-label baselines, and analyze entropy of predicted senses in GloVe space.
AI.result: RSD effectively identifies local semantic axes; in paired-context diagnostics, it separates context anchors for ambiguous words better than shuffled-label controls. However, entropy analysis reveals that ambiguous word embeddings are not simply high-entropy boundary points in static GloVe space. Residual neighborhoods around embeddings show structured information but are not directly usable as definitive sense partitions.
AI.conclusion: RSD can decompose word embeddings into meaningful semantic axes while preserving relational structure, and residuals provide useful qualitative insight into ambiguity. But static embeddings like GloVe do not represent ambiguity as simple high-entropy boundaries, so residuals are better viewed as exploratory diagnostics than as precise word-sense predictions.

title: CatalyticMLLM: A Graph-Text Multimodal Large Language Model for Catalytic Materials
summary:
Property prediction and inverse structural design of catalytic materials are typically modeled as two independent tasks: the former predicts target properties from given structures, whereas the latter generates candidate structures according to desired properties. Although the decoupled paradigm facilitates the implementation of a ``generation--evaluation--screening'' workflow, the inconsistency between the generative model and the property prediction model in terms of representation spaces and training objectives can readily introduce data distribution shifts and evaluator bias, thereby limiting the stability of closed-loop optimization.
  In this work, we propose QE-Catalytic-V2, a unified graph--text multimodal large language model for catalytic materials, which integrates property prediction and inverse design within the same model and shared representation space. Under this unified framework, QE-Catalytic-V2 can not only perform reliable property prediction by leveraging three-dimensional structures and textual information, but also generate and screen physically feasible CIF candidates conditioned on target properties, thereby forming a closed-loop optimization workflow of ``inverse design--prediction--screening--redesign.'' Experimental results demonstrate that this unified paradigm outperforms decoupled baselines on both catalytic relaxed-energy prediction and inverse design tasks, validating the effectiveness of jointly modeling property prediction and structure generation within a single multimodal model.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces QE-Catalytic-V2, a unified multimodal large language model that jointly performs catalytic property prediction and inverse structural design in a single shared representation space, improving stability and performance over traditional decoupled workflows.
AI.motivation: Conventional approaches treat catalytic property prediction and inverse structural design as two separate tasks, using different models and representation spaces. This decoupling creates inconsistencies between generators and predictors, causing data distribution shifts, evaluator bias, and instability in closed-loop optimization workflows for catalytic materials discovery. The authors aim to overcome these limitations by integrating both tasks into a single, coherent framework.
AI.method: The authors design QE-Catalytic-V2 as a unified graph–text multimodal large language model tailored to catalytic materials. The model consumes three-dimensional structural information (e.g., crystal graphs/CIF-based representations) and textual descriptions as inputs, and learns a shared representation space that supports both forward property prediction and inverse structural generation. Using this shared space, the model is trained to (1) predict catalytic properties such as relaxed energies from given 3D structures and associated text, and (2) generate physically plausible CIF candidates conditioned on desired target properties and/or textual prompts. The unified architecture enables a closed-loop workflow where generated candidates are immediately evaluated and iteratively refined within the same model.
AI.result: QE-Catalytic-V2 demonstrates superior performance compared with decoupled baseline approaches on two key tasks: (1) catalytic relaxed-energy prediction, where it yields higher accuracy and reliability by leveraging multimodal (3D + text) inputs; and (2) inverse design of catalytic materials, where it produces more physically feasible candidate CIF structures aligned with target properties. The integration of generation and prediction in one model reduces distribution shift and evaluator bias, resulting in more stable and effective closed-loop optimization.
AI.conclusion: By unifying property prediction and inverse structural design in a single graph–text multimodal large language model, QE-Catalytic-V2 provides a more consistent and stable framework for catalytic materials discovery. The shared representation space and closed-loop workflow (“inverse design–prediction–screening–redesign”) outperform traditional decoupled paradigms on both prediction and inverse design benchmarks, highlighting the advantages of jointly modeling structure generation and property estimation for accelerating the design of catalytic materials.

title: Mixture of Experts for Low-Resource LLMs
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures enable efficient model scaling, yet expert routing behavior across underrepresented languages remains poorly understood. We analyze routing dynamics in two architecturally distinct MoE models -- a pure Transformer (Qwen3-30B-A3B) and a hybrid Mamba-Transformer (Nemotron-3-Nano-30B-A3B) -- using Hebrew as a morphologically rich, low-resource testbed. Both pre-trained models exhibit \emph{deep-layer routing collapse}: usage entropy drops sharply in final layers and tokens concentrate on a narrow expert subset, a pattern largely absent for English. Continual pre-training (CPT) on balanced bilingual data substantially corrects this imbalance, increasing entropy and shifting routing toward shared, language-agnostic experts; supervised fine-tuning (SFT) alone achieves less complete correction. Extending the analysis to Japanese reveals quantitatively consistent collapse signatures, providing cross-linguistic evidence that the phenomenon is a systematic consequence of pre-training underrepresentation rather than any language-intrinsic property. Routing improvements correlate with consistent downstream benchmark gains, positioning routing entropy and expert specialization as principled diagnostics for multilingual capacity in MoE systems.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models route tokens for underrepresented languages, finding a collapse to a few experts in deep layers that hurts low-resource languages and showing that continued pre-training on balanced data can fix this and improve performance.
AI.motivation: While MoE models scale efficiently by routing tokens to subsets of experts, it is unclear how well this routing works for low-resource languages, which may be poorly served by expert allocation learned during pre-training. Understanding whether routing patterns are biased or collapsed for such languages is important for diagnosing and improving multilingual capability in MoE systems.
AI.method: The authors analyze expert routing in two large MoE models with different architectures (a pure Transformer and a hybrid Mamba-Transformer), using Hebrew as a morphologically rich, low-resource case. They measure routing entropy and expert usage across layers and compare patterns between Hebrew and English. They then apply continual pre-training on balanced bilingual data and supervised fine-tuning, examining how these interventions change routing behavior. They extend the same analysis to Japanese to see if similar patterns emerge, and relate routing metrics to downstream benchmark performance.
AI.result: Both models show deep-layer routing collapse for Hebrew: in later layers, routing entropy drops and tokens are funneled into a small subset of experts, unlike English, which maintains more diverse expert usage. Continual pre-training on balanced bilingual data significantly increases routing entropy, shifts usage toward more shared, language-agnostic experts, and yields more balanced expert specialization, whereas supervised fine-tuning alone only partially alleviates the collapse. Similar collapse patterns appear for Japanese, supporting the idea that the phenomenon is due to underrepresentation in pre-training rather than language-intrinsic factors. Improvements in routing metrics correlate with better downstream task performance.
AI.conclusion: Expert routing in MoE models is systematically biased against underrepresented languages, manifesting as deep-layer routing collapse into a few experts. This collapse is not tied to specific linguistic properties but to data imbalance during pre-training. Continual pre-training on balanced multilingual data is an effective way to restore higher-entropy, shared expert usage and improve multilingual performance, whereas SFT alone is less effective. Routing entropy and expert specialization emerge as useful diagnostic tools for assessing and guiding multilingual capacity in MoE architectures.

title: CAM-Bench: A Benchmark for Computational and Applied Mathematics in Lean
summary: Formal theorem-proving benchmarks enable mechanically verifiable evaluation of mathematical reasoning in large language models. However, existing benchmarks mainly focus on Olympiad-style problems and algebraic domains, leaving computational and applied mathematics underrepresented. We introduce CAM-Bench, a Lean 4 theorem-proving benchmark of 1,000 Lean proof targets in computational and applied mathematics, with coverage spanning optimization, numerical linear algebra, and numerical analysis. These problems are adapted from textbook exercises and often depend on locally introduced definitions, notation, algorithms, and elementary results. To construct CAM-Bench, we develop a dependency-recovery pipeline that reconstructs the local textbook context needed to state each problem faithfully. It then normalizes each problem into a standalone informal theorem and translates it into a Lean target. We validate the resulting formal problems through Lean compilation and semantic review, checking both formal correctness and semantic alignment with the original exercises. For each problem, we release the raw exercise, recovered context, normalized informal theorem, and final Lean target. CAM-Bench complements existing formal mathematics benchmarks by targeting applied mathematics problems that rely on textbook concepts and elementary theorems, many of which are not directly available as standard Mathlib4 lemmas. We evaluate widely used large language models and formalization agents on CAM-Bench, and analyze common failure modes in tracking local assumptions, applying elementary results, decomposing proofs, and maintaining long-horizon control in Lean.

AI.tldr: Introduces CAM-Bench, a Lean 4 benchmark of 1,000 formalized problems in computational and applied mathematics to assess LLM theorem proving.
AI.motivation: Existing formal theorem-proving benchmarks focus mostly on Olympiad-style and algebraic math, underrepresenting computational and applied mathematics. There is a need for benchmarks grounded in textbook-style problems involving local definitions, notation, and algorithms to better evaluate and advance LLM formal reasoning in practical mathematical domains.
AI.method: The authors curate 1,000 problems from computational and applied mathematics textbooks (optimization, numerical linear algebra, numerical analysis). They build a dependency-recovery pipeline that reconstructs the local textbook context (definitions, notation, elementary results) required to state each exercise faithfully. This pipeline then normalizes each into a standalone informal theorem and translates it into a Lean 4 proof target. They validate each problem via Lean compilation and semantic review to ensure both formal correctness and alignment with the original exercise. They package for each problem: the raw exercise, recovered context, normalized theorem, and Lean target. They then run widely used LLMs and formalization agents on the benchmark and analyze the failure modes.
AI.result: The result is CAM-Bench, a new Lean 4 benchmark of 1,000 formally validated problems in computational and applied math, each accompanied by multi-level representations (exercise, context, normalized theorem, Lean target). Empirical evaluation of existing LLMs and formal agents on CAM-Bench reveals systematic weaknesses in handling local assumptions, using elementary results, decomposing proofs, and maintaining long-horizon control in Lean scripts.
AI.conclusion: CAM-Bench fills a gap in current formal mathematics benchmarks by focusing on applied, textbook-style problems whose lemmas are often not present in standard libraries like Mathlib4. The benchmark provides a more realistic testbed for LLM theorem proving in computational and applied mathematics and exposes concrete failure patterns that can guide future model and agent design.

title: Temporal Decay of Co-Citation Predictability: A 20-Year Statute Retrieval Benchmark from 396M Ukrainian Court Citations
summary: Co-citation structure is widely assumed to provide stable retrieval signal in legal information systems. We test this assumption longitudinally by constructing UA-StatuteRetrieval, a benchmark that measures co-citation predictability across 20 annual snapshots (2007-2026) of 396 million codex citations from 101 million Ukrainian court decisions. Using a leave-one-out protocol over the full bipartite citation graph, we find that Adamic-Adar MRR declines 33% on a fixed set of articles (from 0.43 to 0.29) and 47% under a train/test temporal split (from 0.51 to 0.27) confirming genuine temporal decay rather than compositional shift or evaluation artifact. The decay is non-uniform: criminal procedure maintains stable co-citation patterns (MRR ~0.40), while civil law degrades from 0.35 to 0.15, coinciding with the 2017 judicial reform. Hub articles (>100K citations) resist decay, but mid-frequency articles (1K-10K) -- the practical retrieval frontier lose half their predictability. A BM25 text baseline decays even faster (31%), and embedding drift analysis with E5-large reveals a 4.3% semantic shift in how articles are cited, providing a mechanistic explanation for the observed decay. The benchmark is released at https://huggingface.co/datasets/overthelex/ua-statute-retrieval.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a large longitudinal benchmark of Ukrainian statute citations and shows that co-citation-based retrieval signals decay substantially over time, in a domain previously assumed to be stable.
AI.motivation: Legal search systems often rely on co-citation structures, assuming that relationships between statutes are stable over time. However, legal systems evolve through reforms, changing practice, and shifting interpretations, which may undermine this assumption. There was a lack of large-scale, long-term empirical evidence quantifying how reliable co-citation patterns remain as a retrieval signal in statutory law over many years.
AI.method: The authors construct UA-StatuteRetrieval, a benchmark dataset based on 396 million codex citations from 101 million Ukrainian court decisions across 20 yearly snapshots from 2007 to 2026. They model the data as a bipartite citation graph between decisions and statutory articles. Using a leave-one-out protocol, they evaluate how well co-citation similarity (specifically Adamic-Adar) predicts held-out citations. They measure mean reciprocal rank (MRR) over time, both on a fixed set of articles and under a train/test temporal split, and analyze decay patterns across legal domains, citation frequency bands, and hub vs. mid-frequency articles. They also compare against a BM25 text retrieval baseline and conduct embedding drift analysis with the E5-large model to quantify semantic shift in how articles are cited.
AI.result: Adamic-Adar co-citation performance decays substantially over time. On a fixed set of articles, MRR falls from 0.43 to 0.29 (33% decline), and with a temporal train/test split, from 0.51 to 0.27 (47% decline), indicating real temporal decay rather than artifacts. Decay is heterogeneous: criminal procedure articles retain relatively stable predictability (MRR about 0.40), while civil law articles degrade sharply from 0.35 to 0.15, temporally aligned with the 2017 Ukrainian judicial reform. Highly cited statutes (>100K citations) are relatively robust, but mid-frequency statutes (1K–10K citations), which are most important for practical retrieval, lose around half of their predictability. BM25 text-based retrieval decays even faster (31%), and E5-large embedding drift shows a 4.3% semantic shift in citation usage patterns, offering a plausible mechanism for the observed decay in co-citation signals.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that co-citation structures in legal corpora are not temporally stable and that their retrieval effectiveness erodes notably over time, especially for mid-frequency statutes and certain legal domains like civil law. Legal information systems cannot safely assume static co-citation signals; they must account for temporal dynamics, reforms, and evolving semantic usage to maintain retrieval quality. The UA-StatuteRetrieval benchmark provides a public resource for studying time-aware legal retrieval and evaluation of temporally robust methods.

title: Is VLA Reasoning Faithful? Probing Safety of Chain-of-Causation
comment: Accept (Poster), CVPR 2026 Workshop DriveX NonArchival Track
summary: We present the first systematic study of faithfulness in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) driving models, analyzing 300 Alpamayo-R1-10B inferences across 100 diverse PhysicalAI-AV scenarios. Our main finding is that output natural-language rationales with trajectories may be significantly unfaithful: (i) overall reasoning fidelity is only 42.5%, with Chain-of-Causation matching scene reality less than half the time; (ii) 94 missed pedestrians in one-third of pedestrian-relevant scenes; (iii) 97.7% trajectory fragility under mild visual perturbations; and (iv) only 48.3% mean reasoning-action consistency, with 53.3% of inferences exhibiting low consistency, including 37.9% of stop-claimed cases where the model continues instead. We formalize faithfulness information-theoretically, define entity and action fidelity with verification criteria, and outline a four-component safety architecture aligned with these results.

AI.tldr: Study of how faithful VLA driving models’ verbal reasoning and trajectories are to actual scene reality and resulting actions.
AI.motivation: VLA models for autonomous driving provide natural-language rationales and trajectories, but it is unclear whether these explanations and planned actions are faithful to what the model truly perceives and how it will behave. Unfaithful reasoning could mislead users and hide safety-critical failures.
AI.method: Evaluate 300 inferences from the Alpamayo-R1-10B VLA model across 100 diverse PhysicalAI-AV driving scenarios. Measure multiple dimensions of faithfulness: (1) overall reasoning fidelity, including whether the Chain-of-Causation in the rationale matches scene reality; (2) missed critical entities like pedestrians; (3) robustness of trajectories under mild visual perturbations; and (4) consistency between stated reasoning (e.g., claims about stopping) and the actual predicted actions. Formalize faithfulness information-theoretically and define entity and action fidelity with explicit verification criteria. Propose a safety architecture with four components based on observed failure patterns.
AI.result: Find that reasoning and trajectories are often unfaithful: only 42.5% overall reasoning fidelity; causation chains match reality less than 50% of the time; 94 pedestrians missed in one-third of pedestrian-relevant scenes; 97.7% of trajectories are fragile under small visual changes; reasoning-action consistency averages 48.3%, with low consistency in 53.3% of inferences, and in 37.9% of cases where the model claims it will stop, it actually continues. Derived formal definitions and metrics of entity and action fidelity and collected empirical evidence across scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Current VLA driving models’ natural-language rationales and planned trajectories cannot be relied upon as faithful explanations or guarantees of behavior. Substantial gaps exist between what the model says, what it perceives, and what it does, raising safety concerns. The paper argues for information-theoretic notions of faithfulness, measurement of entity and action fidelity, and adoption of a dedicated four-component safety architecture to mitigate these risks.

title: Beyond Transcripts: Iterative Peer-Editing with Audio Unlocks High-Quality Human Summaries of Conversational Speech
summary: There are not enough established benchmarks for the task fo speech summarization. Creating new benchmarks demands human annotation, as LLMs could embed systemic errors and bias into datasets. We test ten annotation workflows varying input modality (audio, transcript, or both) and the inclusion of editing (self or peer-editing) to investigate potential quality tradeoffs from using human annotators to summarize audio. We compare human audio-based summaries to human transcript-based summaries to track the impact of the different information modalities on summary quality. We also compare the human outputs against four LLM benchmarks (three text, one audio) to examine whether human-written summaries are less informative than highly fluent automated outputs. We find that audio-based summaries are less informative and more compressed than transcript summaries. However, iterative peer-editing with audio mitigates this difference, enabling audio-based summaries to be as informative as their transcript counterparts and LLM summaries. These findings validate iterative peer-editing among human annotators for the creation of benchmarks informed by both lexical and prosodic information. This enables crucial dataset collection even in setting where transcripts are unavailable.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to best create human-annotated benchmarks for speech summarization, comparing audio- vs transcript-based summaries and different editing workflows, and finds that peer-editing of audio summaries can match the informativeness of transcript and LLM summaries.
AI.motivation: Speech summarization lacks high-quality, standardized benchmarks. While LLMs could help generate labels, relying on them risks encoding systematic model errors and biases into the benchmark. Human annotation from raw audio is more reliable but expensive and may miss information compared to transcript-based annotation. The authors want to understand the tradeoffs between audio vs transcript inputs and between single-pass vs iterative editing workflows, to design efficient, high-quality human annotation pipelines for speech summarization datasets, particularly when transcripts are unavailable.
AI.method: The authors design ten different human annotation workflows that vary along two axes: (1) input modality provided to annotators (audio only, transcript only, or both audio and transcript) and (2) whether summaries undergo additional editing (no editing, self-editing, or peer-editing / iterative editing). Human annotators generate abstractive summaries of speech content according to these workflows. The resulting summaries are evaluated and compared for informativeness, compression, and overall quality. They are also compared against four LLM-based baselines (three that summarize text transcripts and one that summarizes audio directly). The analysis focuses on how modality and editing process affect summary informativeness and length, and how human-written summaries stack up against LLM outputs.
AI.result: Human summaries created from audio alone are generally shorter (more compressed) and less informative than those created from transcripts. However, when audio-based summaries undergo iterative peer-editing, their informativeness improves substantially, closing the gap with transcript-based summaries and with LLM-generated summaries. This indicates that initial information loss from relying on audio-only input can be mitigated through a structured human editing process.
AI.conclusion: To build reliable benchmarks for speech summarization—especially in cases where transcripts are missing—human annotation pipelines should incorporate iterative peer-editing of audio-based summaries. This process yields summaries that are as informative as those derived from transcripts or generated by strong LLMs, while also capturing prosodic (non-lexical) information present in speech. The findings argue against naive use of LLMs as annotators and instead validate carefully designed human workflows, particularly audio-based peer-edited annotation, for creating high-quality, prosody-aware summarization datasets.

title: A2RBench: An Automatic Paradigm for Formally Verifiable Abstract Reasoning Benchmark Generation
summary: Abstract reasoning ability reflects the intelligence and generalization capacity of LLMs to extract and apply abstract rules. However, accurately measuring this ability remains challenging: existing benchmarks either rely on expensive manual annotation, limiting their scale, or risk measuring memorization rather than genuine reasoning. To address this, we introduce an automated pipeline named A2RBench, encompassing generation, expansion, evaluation, and analysis. Specifically, in the generation stage, LLMs create diverse tasks demanding genuine reasoning; in the expansion stage, LLMs reuse validated rules and expand new input spaces to generate task variations, achieving scaling. However, such a process may cause hallucinations. To eliminate it, we further establish a theoretical framework and prove that programmatic verification--testing whether the inverse operation perfectly reverses the forward operation (cycle consistency)--guarantees a unique solution. Through extensive evaluations on mainstream LLMs, we find: (1) Current LLMs exhibit fundamental deficiencies in abstract reasoning, with top models significantly underperforming humans on a representative subset (39.8% vs. 68.5%). (2) Current LLMs fall far short of 2D and 1D in the complexity of generated 3D tasks, revealing their lack of understanding of high-dimensional tasks. (3) Counterintuitively, inputs with higher information complexity can simplify the reasoning process.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces A2RBench, an automated benchmark generation pipeline to rigorously and scalably evaluate LLMs’ abstract reasoning, using programmatic verification to ensure tasks demand genuine reasoning rather than memorization.
AI.motivation: Existing abstract reasoning benchmarks for LLMs either depend on costly human annotation, which limits dataset size and diversity, or are automatically generated but risk testing memorization or superficial pattern-matching instead of genuine rule abstraction. There is a need for a scalable, automated, and theoretically grounded way to construct abstract reasoning tasks that are provably unambiguous and require real reasoning, enabling fair comparison between LLMs and humans.
AI.method: They design A2RBench, a four-stage automated pipeline: (1) Generation: LLMs are prompted to create diverse abstract reasoning tasks that require understanding and applying rules, not recall. (2) Expansion: Validated tasks are expanded by reusing the same underlying rules while varying inputs, thereby scaling the dataset. (3) Programmatic verification: They introduce a formal framework where each task has a forward and an inverse operation; they verify cycle consistency—checking that applying the inverse after the forward recovers the original input—to ensure the task has a unique correct answer and avoid hallucinated or invalid items. (4) Evaluation and analysis: Mainstream LLMs and humans are evaluated on the constructed tasks, and the authors analyze performance by dimensionality and information complexity of tasks.
AI.result: Evaluating multiple mainstream LLMs, they find: (1) Even top LLMs perform poorly on abstract reasoning compared with humans on a representative subset (39.8% accuracy vs. 68.5% for humans), indicating fundamental limitations. (2) LLM-generated 3D reasoning tasks are much less complex than 1D or 2D tasks, revealing poor understanding of truly high-dimensional structures. (3) Surprisingly, tasks whose inputs have higher information complexity can sometimes make reasoning easier for LLMs, suggesting that richer input structure can guide reasoning rather than hinder it.
AI.conclusion: A2RBench provides a scalable and theoretically grounded automated benchmark for abstract reasoning in LLMs by combining LLM-based task synthesis with programmatic verification via cycle consistency. The study reveals significant gaps between current LLMs and humans in abstract reasoning, especially for high-dimensional tasks, and uncovers a non-intuitive relationship between input complexity and reasoning difficulty. This benchmark and framework aim to facilitate more reliable evaluation and future development of LLMs with stronger abstract reasoning capabilities.

title: Stop When Reasoning Converges: Semantic-Preserving Early Exit for Reasoning Models
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) achieve strong performance by generating long chains of thought (CoT), but often overthink, continuing to reason after a solution has already stabilized and thereby wasting tokens and increasing latency. Existing inference-time early-exit methods rely primarily on answer-level signals, such as confidence or trial-answer consistency, to decide when to stop. However, these signals mainly reflect answer readiness rather than reasoning convergence: they may trigger before the model has finished exploring or self-correcting, causing premature exits that can degrade final-answer accuracy and leave the retained reasoning chain semantically incomplete. We identify reasoning-level semantic redundancy as a complementary signal for semantic-preserving early exit: when successive steps no longer add novel progress and instead revisit established conclusions, the reasoning trajectory has likely converged. Building on this insight, we propose PUMA, a plug-and-play framework that combines a lightweight Redundancy Detector with answer-level verification. The detector flags semantically redundant candidate exits, while verification confirms whether stopping is safe, allowing PUMA to remove redundant continuation while preserving both answer accuracy and a coherent reasoning prefix. Across five LRMs and five challenging reasoning benchmarks, PUMA achieves 26.2% average token reduction while preserving accuracy and retained CoT quality. Additional experiments on code generation, zero-shot vision-language reasoning, and learned stopping-policy internalization further demonstrate that reasoning-level redundancy is a robust, transferable, and learnable signal for efficient reasoning. Our code is available at \url{https://github.com/giovanni-vaccarino/PUMA}.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PUMA, a framework that detects when a Large Reasoning Model’s chain-of-thought has become semantically redundant, enabling early stopping of reasoning while preserving answer quality and coherent explanations, achieving about 26% token savings on several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Large Reasoning Models often generate unnecessarily long chains of thought, continuing to reason even after reaching a stable solution. Existing early-exit methods mostly rely on answer-level indicators like confidence or agreement among sampled answers. These indicators can misfire: they may suggest stopping before the reasoning has fully converged or self-corrected, harming accuracy and leaving the explanation incomplete.
