The authors are motivated to find a better, reasoning-aware signal that reflects true convergence of the reasoning process, not just superficial answer readiness, in order to reduce tokens and latency without sacrificing solution quality or explanation coherence.
AI.method: The authors propose PUMA, a plug-and-play early-exit framework centered on reasoning-level semantic redundancy. They design a lightweight Redundancy Detector that monitors successive reasoning steps produced by the LRM and identifies when new steps add little or no novel semantic content, indicating convergence. PUMA then combines this redundancy signal with standard answer-level verification to decide whether it is safe to stop. The Redundancy Detector flags candidate exit points where the current step is semantically redundant with previous reasoning; the verification module checks that stopping at these points does not harm answer correctness. The system is evaluated across multiple LRMs and tasks, and also tested on other domains such as code generation and vision-language reasoning, and for learnability of the stopping policy.
AI.result: On five large reasoning models and five challenging reasoning benchmarks, PUMA reduces generated tokens by an average of 26.2% while maintaining final-answer accuracy and preserving the quality and semantic coherence of the retained chain-of-thought. Further experiments show that redundancy-based stopping generalizes beyond pure text reasoning: it remains effective for code generation tasks, zero-shot vision-language reasoning, and when the stopping behavior is internalized as a learned policy. These results support the claim that semantic redundancy is a robust and transferable signal for efficient reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that reasoning-level semantic redundancy provides a powerful and complementary signal to traditional answer-level metrics for early exiting in large reasoning models. By using a lightweight Redundancy Detector in combination with answer verification, PUMA can safely eliminate redundant reasoning steps, significantly cutting token usage and latency without compromising accuracy or explanation quality. The demonstrated robustness across different models, benchmarks, and modalities suggests that redundancy-aware early stopping is a generally useful approach for making LRM inference more efficient and that the stopping policies themselves can be learned and transferred across settings.

title: MetaCogAgent: A Metacognitive Multi-Agent LLM Framework with Self-Aware Task Delegation
summary: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) systems have shown promise for solving complex tasks through agent collaboration. However, existing frameworks assign tasks based on predefined roles without considering whether an agent can accurately assess its own competence boundaries, leading to overconfident execution on tasks beyond its expertise. Inspired by metacognition theory from cognitive science, we propose MetaCogAgent, a multi-agent LLM framework where each agent is equipped with a Metacognitive Self-Assessment Unit that evaluates task-capability alignment before execution. The framework introduces three contributions: (1) a self-assessment mechanism that estimates per-task confidence by combining verbalized uncertainty with historical capability profiles; (2) an adaptive delegation protocol that routes low-confidence tasks to better-suited agents through cross-agent evaluation; and (3) a capability boundary learning module that iteratively refines each agent's competence model via cybernetic feedback. Experiments on our constructed MetaCog-Eval benchmark (700 tasks across 5 cognitive dimensions) demonstrate that MetaCogAgent achieves 82.4% task accuracy -- 8.7% above the best routing baseline -- while using 5% fewer API calls than AutoGen and 34% fewer than ensemble voting. Ablation studies confirm that each metacognitive component contributes to overall system performance.

AI.tldr: MetaCogAgent is a multi-agent LLM framework where each agent metacognitively self-assesses its capability per task, enabling better task routing, fewer API calls, and higher accuracy than existing multi-agent or ensemble methods.
AI.motivation: Current multi-agent LLM systems assign tasks using static, predefined roles and lack a reliable way for each agent to understand and respect its own competence boundaries. This leads to overconfident attempts on tasks they are not good at, wasting compute and hurting performance. The authors are motivated by metacognition theory in cognitive science, aiming to give LLM agents a notion of self-knowledge about their strengths and weaknesses so that they can decide when to act, when to abstain, and when to delegate.
AI.method: They design MetaCogAgent, a multi-agent framework in which every LLM agent is augmented with a Metacognitive Self-Assessment Unit. This unit estimates confidence for each incoming task by combining (a) verbalized uncertainty from the LLM on that specific query and (b) a learned, historical capability profile describing what the agent has done well or poorly before. Based on these confidence estimates, an adaptive delegation protocol routes low-confidence tasks to other agents, using cross-agent evaluation to find a better match. Over time, a capability boundary learning module updates each agent’s competence model using cybernetic feedback (i.e., performance outcomes are fed back into the capability profile), iteratively sharpening agents’ understanding of what they can and cannot do well.
AI.result: On the MetaCog-Eval benchmark of 700 tasks spanning 5 cognitive dimensions, MetaCogAgent attains 82.4% accuracy. This is an 8.7 percentage point improvement over the strongest existing task-routing baseline. The framework is also more efficient: it uses 5% fewer API calls than AutoGen and 34% fewer than ensemble-voting approaches. Ablation experiments removing each metacognitive component in turn show that all three (self-assessment, adaptive delegation, and capability boundary learning) each make a positive, measurable contribution to performance.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating metacognitive self-assessment into multi-agent LLM systems leads to better task allocation, higher accuracy, and lower API usage than existing role-based or ensemble methods. By explicitly modeling and updating each agent’s competence boundaries, MetaCogAgent reduces overconfident misallocation and demonstrates that ideas from human metacognition can be fruitfully translated into LLM-based multi-agent architectures.

title: Validate Your Authority: Benchmarking LLMs on Multi-Label Precedent Treatment Classification
comment: Accepted for publication at the Natural Legal Language Processing Workshop (NLLP) 2025, co-located with EMNLP
summary: Automating the classification of negative treatment in legal precedent is a critical yet nuanced NLP task where misclassification carries significant risk. To address the shortcomings of standard accuracy, this paper introduces a more robust evaluation framework. We benchmark modern Large Language Models on a new, expert-annotated dataset of 239 real-world legal citations and propose a novel Average Severity Error metric to better measure the practical impact of classification errors. Our experiments reveal a performance split. Google's Gemini 2.5 Flash achieved the highest accuracy on a high-level classification task (79.1%), while OpenAI's GPT-5-mini was the top performer on the more complex fine-grained schema (67.7%). This work establishes a crucial baseline, provides a new context-rich dataset, and introduces an evaluation metric tailored to the demands of this complex legal reasoning task.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates large language models on classifying negative treatment in legal precedents, introducing a new dataset and a severity-aware error metric.
AI.motivation: Standard accuracy metrics are insufficient and risky for classifying negative treatment in legal precedent, where misclassification can have serious practical consequences. There is a need for a better evaluation framework, high-quality annotated data, and metrics that reflect the real-world impact of errors in this nuanced legal NLP task.
AI.method: The authors construct an expert-annotated dataset of 239 real-world legal citations involving negative treatment. They benchmark several modern Large Language Models on two tasks: a high-level classification task and a more detailed fine-grained classification schema. They then introduce and apply a new evaluation metric, Average Severity Error, designed to quantify how severe misclassifications are rather than just whether they are correct or incorrect.
AI.result: The experiments show a split in model performance: Google's Gemini 2.5 Flash attains the best accuracy on the high-level classification task (79.1%), whereas OpenAI's GPT-5-mini achieves the best performance on the more complex fine-grained classification task (67.7%). The new Average Severity Error metric helps differentiate the practical impact of different models' errors beyond simple accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a context-rich, expert-annotated dataset for negative treatment classification in legal precedent and establishes baseline performance of modern LLMs. By proposing the Average Severity Error metric, it offers a more realistic, impact-oriented way to evaluate models on high-stakes legal reasoning tasks, highlighting that different models may excel at different granularity levels of classification.

title: CyberCorrect: A Cybernetic Framework for Closed-Loop Self-Correction in Large Language Models
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure, submitted to IEEE SMC 2026
summary: Large language model (LLM) self-correction -- the ability to detect and fix errors in generated outputs -- remains largely ad hoc, relying on generic prompts such as "please reconsider your answer" without systematic error analysis or convergence guarantees. We propose CyberCorrect, a framework that formalizes LLM self-correction as a closed-loop control system grounded in cybernetic theory. The framework models the LLM generator as the plant and introduces a tri-modal Error Detector (combining self-consistency, verbalized confidence, and logic-chain verification) as the sensor. A type-directed Correction Controller generates targeted repair instructions based on diagnosed error categories, while a Convergence Judge determines iteration termination using stability criteria adapted from control theory. We further introduce three control-theoretic evaluation metrics -- convergence rate, overshoot rate, and oscillation rate -- that capture correction dynamics beyond final accuracy. Experiments on our constructed CyberCorrect-Bench (440 reasoning tasks with annotated error types and correction paths) show that CyberCorrect achieves 79.8% final accuracy, improving upon the best existing self-correction method by 6.2 percentage points, while reducing overshoot (erroneous over-correction) by 41% through its convergence control mechanism.

AI.tldr: CyberCorrect frames LLM self-correction as a cybernetic control loop with explicit error sensing, targeted correction, and convergence judgment, improving both accuracy and stability of iterative reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM self-correction techniques are heuristic, using vague prompts and lacking principled ways to detect specific errors, design targeted repairs, or guarantee that iterative corrections will converge instead of oscillating or over-correcting. The authors want a theory-grounded framework that treats self-correction as a feedback control problem to systematically analyze and improve correction behavior, not just final accuracy.
AI.method: They formalize LLM self-correction as a closed-loop control system. The LLM that produces answers is modeled as the plant. A tri-modal Error Detector acts as the sensor, combining (1) self-consistency across multiple samples, (2) the model’s own verbalized confidence, and (3) logic-chain verification to diagnose error types. Based on these diagnosed error categories, a type-directed Correction Controller generates targeted repair instructions rather than generic “reconsider” prompts. A Convergence Judge monitors the correction trajectory and decides when to stop iterating using stability-inspired criteria from control theory. They also define three control-theoretic metrics—convergence rate, overshoot rate, and oscillation rate—to quantitatively characterize correction dynamics. They construct CyberCorrect-Bench, a benchmark of 440 reasoning tasks annotated with error types and correction paths, and evaluate their framework against existing self-correction methods.
AI.result: On the CyberCorrect-Bench dataset of 440 annotated reasoning tasks, CyberCorrect attains 79.8% final accuracy, which is a 6.2 percentage-point improvement over the strongest prior self-correction baseline. In addition to accuracy gains, the framework significantly improves correction stability: overshoot, defined as erroneous over-correction where a correct answer becomes incorrect, is reduced by 41% thanks to the explicit convergence control. The new metrics show more favorable convergence behavior and fewer unstable correction trajectories compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: By casting LLM self-correction as a cybernetic closed-loop control problem, CyberCorrect provides a principled design for error detection, targeted correction control, and convergence judgment. The framework not only improves final reasoning accuracy but also yields more stable correction dynamics, as shown by reduced overshoot and better convergence metrics on a new, carefully annotated benchmark. This suggests that control-theoretic perspectives and metrics are a promising direction for systematically designing and evaluating self-correcting LLMs.

title: Do LLM Agents Mirror Socio-Cognitive Effects in Power-Asymmetric Conversations?
summary: Power differences shape human communication through well documented socio cognitive effects, including language coordination, pronoun usage, authority bias, and harmful compliance. We examine whether large language models (LLMs) exhibit similar behaviors when assigned high or low status personas. Using personas from diverse professions, we simulate multi turn, power asymmetric dialogues (e.g., principal teacher, justice lawyer) and measure (i) linguistic coordination, (ii) pronoun usage, (iii) persuasion success, and (iv) compliance with unsafe requests. Our results show that LLMs show key socio cognitive effects of power, albeit with nuances and variability, linking simulated interactions to both desirable and unsafe behaviors.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how power dynamics affect the behavior of large language models by assigning them high- and low-status personas and observing socio-cognitive effects in dialogue.
AI.motivation: In human communication, power differences strongly influence language patterns and social behavior, including coordination, pronoun choice, persuasion, and compliance, sometimes leading to harmful outcomes. As LLMs are increasingly integrated into social and decision-making contexts, it is crucial to understand whether they replicate these power-related behaviors, which can have both beneficial and harmful consequences.
AI.method: The authors assign LLMs personas representing different levels of social power across diverse professions (such as principal vs. teacher, justice vs. lawyer). They then simulate multi-turn, power-asymmetric dialogues between these personas. During these simulations, they quantify four aspects: (i) linguistic coordination (how much one speaker adapts to the other’s language), (ii) pronoun usage patterns, (iii) the success rate of persuasion attempts, and (iv) the degree of compliance with unsafe requests.
AI.result: The experiments show that LLMs do exhibit key socio-cognitive effects typically associated with power differences in humans. High- and low-status personas lead to measurable changes in coordination, pronoun use, persuasion outcomes, and willingness to comply with unsafe requests. However, these effects are not uniform; there are nuances and variability across settings and personas.
AI.conclusion: LLMs mirror important power-related socio-cognitive behaviors found in human communication when prompted with status-differentiated personas. This suggests that power cues in prompts can influence not only helpful capabilities (like persuasive effectiveness or adaptive communication) but also riskier behaviors (like unsafe compliance). Understanding and controlling these dynamics is important for the safe and beneficial deployment of LLMs in real-world, hierarchical interactions.

title: Reasoning Before Diagnosis: Physician-Inspired Structured Thinking for ECG Classification
summary: Electrocardiogram (ECG) diagnosis in clinical practice relies on structured reasoning over multiple hierarchical aspects, including cardiac rhythm, conduction properties, waveform morphology, and overall diagnostic impression. However, most existing approaches predict labels directly from ECG signals without explicit clinical reasoning, resulting in opaque decisions that lack clinical alignment. To bridge this gap, we propose CardioThink, a physician-inspired multimodal large language model (MLLM) framework that explicitly models the diagnostic reasoning process through human-interpretable intermediate stages (rhythm, conduction, morphology, and impression) to derive final classification results. Furthermore, we introduce Structured Set Policy Optimization (SSPO) to jointly optimize adherence to this structured reasoning format and the accuracy of variable-size diagnostic sets, without requiring manually annotated reasoning traces. Extensive experiments on diverse ECG benchmarks demonstrate the significant superiority of our approach in diagnostic accuracy, while simultaneously providing interpretable clinical reasoning. Notably, reasoning quality evaluations confirm that SSPO substantially enhances the clinical validity of the generated rationales. These findings reveal that moving beyond direct label prediction toward structured reasoning offers a more clinically aligned direction for future ECG modeling.

AI.tldr: CardioThink is a physician-inspired multimodal LLM framework for ECG diagnosis that introduces explicit, clinically structured reasoning stages and a new training method (SSPO) to jointly optimize reasoning format and diagnostic accuracy, achieving better performance and more interpretable rationales than prior direct label-prediction methods.
AI.motivation: Existing ECG diagnostic models typically map raw ECG signals directly to labels, producing opaque predictions that do not reflect how clinicians reason using hierarchical aspects like rhythm, conduction, morphology, and overall impression. This lack of alignment with real-world diagnostic processes hinders interpretability, trust, and clinical utility. The authors aim to build a model that mirrors physician reasoning, provides human-interpretable intermediate steps, and preserves or improves diagnostic performance without requiring expensive manual annotation of reasoning traces.
AI.method: The authors propose CardioThink, a multimodal large language model framework that takes ECG data as input and generates diagnostic outputs through explicitly defined intermediate reasoning stages corresponding to clinical practice: rhythm analysis, conduction assessment, waveform morphology description, and final diagnostic impression. To train this framework, they introduce Structured Set Policy Optimization (SSPO), an RL-style training objective that: (1) enforces adherence to a structured reasoning format; and (2) optimizes the accuracy of predicted diagnostic label sets of variable size. SSPO leverages supervision from final labels and structural constraints instead of requiring manually labeled reasoning steps. The model is evaluated on multiple ECG benchmarks to compare diagnostic accuracy and reasoning quality against baseline methods.
AI.result: Across diverse ECG benchmark datasets, CardioThink achieves significantly higher diagnostic accuracy than existing direct label-prediction approaches. The generated intermediate reasoning steps are clinically interpretable and aligned with real diagnostic practice. Evaluations of reasoning quality, likely involving expert assessment or quantitative proxies, show that SSPO markedly improves the clinical validity and coherence of the model’s rationales compared with naïve or non-structured training schemes.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling ECG diagnosis as a structured, multi-stage reasoning process—mirroring how clinicians think—yields better accuracy and more interpretable outputs than direct label prediction from ECG signals. The CardioThink framework and SSPO training method demonstrate that aligning model behavior with clinical reasoning hierarchies can improve both performance and transparency, suggesting that future ECG modeling and similar medical AI tasks should move toward structured, reasoning-centered designs rather than purely end-to-end label prediction.

title: Sometin Beta Pass Notin (SBPN): Improving Multilingual ASR for Nigerian Languages via Knowledge Distillation
summary: Although modern multilingual Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems support several Nigerian languages, their performance consistently lags behind high-resource languages like English and French. Nigerian languages present unique modelling hurdles, including acute data scarcity, inconsistent orthography, tonal diacritics, diverse accents, frequent code-switching, and localized named entities. To address these challenges, we developed a multilingual ASR framework utilizing a two-stage distillation process. First, we employ student-teacher knowledge distillation from existing monolingual models, conditioned on robust language-specific N-gram language models. Second, we perform iterative self improvement using pseudo-labelled data to further refine accuracy. Our method significantly bridges the performance gap, achieving on average a relative Word Error Rate (WER) reduction of 29 % over monolingual baselines. Our models also outperform state-of-the-art multilingual models across major benchmarks, including Common Voice and Fleurs. We introduce Sometin Beta Pass Notin (SBPN), a foundational multilingual ASR model covering Yorùbá, Hausa, Igbo, Nigerian Pidgin, and Nigerian English. SBPN is released in two sizes: SBPN-Base (120 M parameters) and SBPN-Large (600 M parameters). By releasing these as open foundation models, we aim to provide ASR resources for further research into the rich phonetic and cultural landscape of the region.

AI.tldr: The paper presents SBPN, a multilingual ASR framework and set of open models tailored for major Nigerian languages, using a two-stage distillation and self-training pipeline to substantially reduce WER versus monolingual and state-of-the-art multilingual baselines.
AI.motivation: Modern multilingual ASR systems underperform on Nigerian languages compared with high-resource languages. This gap is driven by data scarcity, orthographic inconsistency, tones, accent variation, code-switching, and localized entities. The authors are motivated to build stronger, publicly available ASR models that handle these linguistic challenges and improve recognition quality for Yorùbá, Hausa, Igbo, Nigerian Pidgin, and Nigerian English.
AI.method: They design a multilingual ASR framework based on a two-stage distillation process. Stage 1 performs knowledge distillation from existing monolingual teacher models into a multilingual student, using language-specific N-gram language models to guide the process. Stage 2 applies iterative self-training: the multilingual model generates pseudo-labels on additional unlabeled or weakly labeled speech, and is then further fine-tuned on this pseudo-labelled data for accuracy refinement. The resulting system is instantiated as two transformer-based models, SBPN-Base (120M) and SBPN-Large (600M).
AI.result: The proposed approach yields, on average, a 29% relative WER reduction compared to monolingual baselines on Nigerian languages. Additionally, their multilingual SBPN models surpass existing state-of-the-art multilingual ASR systems on standard benchmarks such as Common Voice and FLEURS for the covered languages.
AI.conclusion: A tailored two-stage distillation and self-training framework can substantially close the performance gap between Nigerian languages and high-resource languages in ASR. The released SBPN-Base and SBPN-Large models serve as open, foundational resources to support further research and applications in speech technology for Nigerian languages and their diverse phonetic and cultural characteristics.

title: HyperPersona: A Multi-Level Hypergraph Framework for Text-Based Automatic Personality Prediction
summary: As a modern commodity, language has become a vast repository of socially and psychologically significant traits and concepts, reflecting the ways people encode pattern of thoughts, behaviors, and emotions into words. Text-based Automatic Personality Prediction (APP), seeks to infer personality from linguistic behavior, offering a scalable alternative to traditional psychometric assessments. Although text is inherently hierarchical, with the document-level capturing global features, the sentence-level encoding local semantics, and the word-level providing fine-grained lexical information, most existing approaches rely on shallow, sequential, or single-level representations that ignore the multi-level structure of written language. To address this, we propose HyperPersona, a framework that explicitly models the hierarchical organization of text (document, sentence, and word) through hypergraph structure, where a document and its sentences are represented as hyperedges, and the words are represented as nodes, enabling joint modeling of global, local, and lexical dependencies of text. Followed by a transformer-based graph encoder that learns interactions within and across these linguistic layers, yielding context-sensitive and structurally grounded feature representations for personality prediction. Experiments on the Big Five personality dimensions show that, while relying solely on text, HyperPersona effectively integrates multi-level linguistic cues, achieving superior performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines. These findings underscore the critical role of textual hierarchy in advancing human-like personality inference from natural language.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HyperPersona, a hypergraph and transformer-based framework that models the hierarchical structure of text (document, sentence, word) to improve automatic personality prediction from language.
AI.motivation: Existing text-based automatic personality prediction models often use shallow, sequential, or single-level text representations, failing to capture the inherent hierarchical organization of language. This limits their ability to jointly model global document-level context, local sentence-level semantics, and fine-grained word-level cues that are important for inferring personality traits from text. The authors aim to build a model that can explicitly leverage this multi-level structure to enhance prediction accuracy and better approximate human-like personality inference.
AI.method: The authors propose HyperPersona, which represents text as a hypergraph capturing multiple linguistic levels. Words are modeled as nodes, while sentences and the full document are modeled as hyperedges connecting relevant word nodes. This structure allows the model to encode relationships within and across document, sentence, and word levels. A transformer-based graph encoder is then applied over the hypergraph to learn context-sensitive, structurally grounded feature representations. These representations are used to predict the Big Five personality traits from text, relying solely on linguistic input.
AI.result: On datasets annotated with the Big Five personality dimensions, HyperPersona outperforms state-of-the-art baseline models that do not explicitly model the hierarchical structure of text. The results show that integrating document-level, sentence-level, and word-level cues via a hypergraph and transformer-based encoder leads to superior personality prediction performance using only textual data.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that explicitly modeling the hierarchical organization of text is crucial for effective automatic personality prediction from language. By leveraging a hypergraph representation and transformer-based graph encoder, HyperPersona successfully integrates global, local, and lexical information, yielding better performance than existing methods. The findings highlight the importance of capturing textual hierarchy to move toward more human-like personality inference from natural language alone.

title: Bridging the Version Gap: Multi-version Training Improves ICD Code Prediction, Especially for Rare Codes
summary: Clinical coding maps clinical documentation to standardized medical codes, an essential yet time-consuming administrative task that could benefit from automation. Current models on ICD coding are typically optimized for codes from a specific ICD version. However, in reality, ICD systems evolve continuously, and different versions are adopted across time periods and regions. Moreover, ICD coding suffers from the long-tail problem, and rare code performance can be a bottleneck for developing implementable models. We examine whether it is viable to train version-independent models by combining data annotated in different ICD versions, which may help address these challenges. We add ICD-9 data to the training of a modified label-wise attention model for ICD-10 prediction, and find that despite the version mismatch, adding ICD-9 yields a 27% increase in micro F1 for 18K rare ICD codes compared to training on ICD-10 alone. On 8K frequent ICD-10 codes, the multi-version training also substantially improves macro metrics, with far fewer model parameters.

AI.tldr: They investigate whether combining datasets annotated with different ICD versions can improve automated clinical coding, especially for rare codes.
AI.motivation: Clinical coding is essential but time-consuming. Existing ICD coding models are tuned to specific ICD versions, while in practice different and evolving versions are used. Additionally, performance on rare (long-tail) codes is poor and hinders real-world deployment. The authors want to see if version-independent models trained on multiple ICD versions can alleviate these problems.
AI.method: They modify a label-wise attention model for ICD-10 prediction and augment its training data with clinical notes annotated using ICD-9 codes, effectively training on multiple ICD versions simultaneously. They then evaluate performance separately on rare and frequent ICD-10 codes and compare to a baseline trained only on ICD-10 data. They also consider model parameter efficiency.
AI.result: Adding ICD-9 data to the training, despite the version mismatch, leads to a 27% increase in micro F1 for 18,000 rare ICD-10 codes relative to the ICD-10-only baseline. For 8,000 frequent ICD-10 codes, multi-version training also substantially improves macro-level metrics, and does so while requiring far fewer model parameters than alternative approaches.
AI.conclusion: Training clinical coding models jointly on data labeled with different ICD versions is not only feasible but beneficial. Multi-version training significantly boosts performance, especially for rare ICD codes, and improves overall macro metrics for frequent codes with improved parameter efficiency. This suggests that version-independent, multi-version models are a promising direction for more practical and robust automated clinical coding systems.

title: ADR: An Agentic Detection System for Enterprise Agentic AI Security
summary: We present the Agentic AI Detection and Response (ADR) system, the first large-scale, production-proven enterprise framework for securing AI agents operating through the Model Context Protocol (MCP). We identify three persistent challenges in this domain: (1) limited observability -- existing Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) tools see file writes but not the agent reasoning, prompts, or causal chains linking intent to execution; (2) insufficient robustness -- static defenses constrained by pre-defined rules fail to generalize across diverse attack techniques and enterprise contexts; and (3) high detection costs -- LLM-based inference is prohibitively expensive at scale. ADR addresses these challenges via three components: the ADR Sensor for high-fidelity agentic telemetry, the ADR Explorer for systematic pre-deployment red teaming and hard-example generation, and the ADR Detector for scalable, two-tier online detection combining fast triage with context-aware reasoning. Deployed at Uber for over ten months, ADR has sustained reliable detection in production with growing adoption reaching over 7,200 unique hosts and processing over 10,000 agent sessions daily, uncovering hundreds of credential exposures across 26 categories and enabling a shift-left prevention layer (97.2% precision, 206 detected credentials). To validate the approach and enable community adoption, we introduce ADR-Bench (302 tasks, 17 techniques, 133 MCP servers), where ADR achieves zero false positives while detecting 67% of attacks -- outperforming three state-of-the-art baselines (ALRPHFS, GuardAgent, LlamaFirewall) by 2--4x in F1-score. On AgentDojo (public prompt injection benchmark), ADR detects all attacks with only three false alarms out of 93 tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces ADR, a large-scale security framework for monitoring and defending AI agents using Model Context Protocol (MCP), validated in Uber production and new benchmarks.
AI.motivation: As AI agents increasingly act on enterprise systems via MCP, traditional Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) lacks visibility into prompts, reasoning, and causal chains, is brittle against diverse attacks, and makes large-scale LLM-based detection too costly. There is a need for a robust, observable, and cost-effective security layer purpose-built for agentic AI.
AI.method: Design a three-part Agentic AI Detection and Response (ADR) system: (1) ADR Sensor to capture rich telemetry about agent behavior and context; (2) ADR Explorer to support systematic pre-deployment red teaming and generate hard attack examples; and (3) ADR Detector, a scalable two-tier online detection pipeline that combines fast triage with deeper, context-aware reasoning using LLMs. Deploy ADR at Uber at scale and create ADR-Bench, a benchmark of 302 tasks, 17 attack techniques, and 133 MCP servers, plus evaluation on the public AgentDojo benchmark. Compare ADR against prior methods ALRPHFS, GuardAgent, and LlamaFirewall.
AI.result: In production at Uber for over 10 months, ADR runs on 7,200+ hosts, processing 10,000+ agent sessions daily, detecting hundreds of credential exposures across 26 categories. A shift-left prevention layer achieves 97.2% precision with 206 detected credentials. On ADR-Bench, ADR detects 67% of attacks with zero false positives, obtaining 2–4x higher F1 than ALRPHFS, GuardAgent, and LlamaFirewall. On AgentDojo, ADR detects all attacks with only 3 false alarms over 93 tasks.
AI.conclusion: ADR provides a practical, scalable security framework for agentic AI in enterprises, significantly improving observability, robustness, and cost-efficiency over prior work. Its validated deployment at Uber and superior performance on ADR-Bench and AgentDojo show that targeted sensor design, systematic red teaming, and tiered detection can effectively secure MCP-based AI agents and offer a foundation for community adoption and future research.

title: Internalizing Tool Knowledge in Small Language Models via QLoRA Fine-Tuning
summary: Large language models are increasingly used as planning components in agentic systems, but current tool-use pipelines often require full tool schemas to be included in every prompt, creating substantial token overhead and limiting the practicality of smaller models. This paper investigates whether tool-use knowledge can be internalized into small language models through parameter-efficient fine-tuning, enabling structured planning without explicit tool descriptions at inference time. Using AssetOpsBench as the primary benchmark, we fine-tune Gemma 4 E4B and Qwen3-4B with 8-bit QLoRA on approximately 1,700 tool-use examples spanning tool knowledge, question-to-plan mappings, and execution-style traces. We evaluate the resulting models under description-free inference, where the prompt omits the tool catalog entirely. The fine-tuned models outperform an informed unfine-tuned baseline that receives full tool descriptions, reducing input length by 82.6\% while improving structural and LLM-judge planning scores. In the best Gemma run, the model achieves an AT-F1 of 0.65 and an overall judge score of 3.88, compared with 0.47 and 2.88 for the informed baseline. Qwen3-4B achieves a strong overall judge score of 3.78 while using 62\% less memory and running 2.5$\times$ faster than Gemma, though it also exhibits greater catastrophic forgetting on general multiple-choice benchmarks. Additional ablations show that LoRA rank controls a quality--retention trade-off, with $r=32$ maximizing planning quality and smaller ranks preserving more general knowledge. These results suggest that, for fixed tool catalogs, QLoRA fine-tuning can shift tool knowledge from prompt context into model weights, substantially reducing inference overhead while maintaining or improving tool-planning quality.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that small language models can internalize tool-use knowledge through QLoRA fine-tuning, enabling effective tool-based planning without including tool schemas in the prompt, which cuts token and memory costs while improving planning quality.
AI.motivation: Current agentic LLM tool-use setups require passing full tool schemas in every prompt, which is token-intensive and makes small models less practical. The authors want to know whether the knowledge of how to use a fixed tool catalog can instead be stored directly in model weights, so that models can plan structured tool calls without seeing explicit tool descriptions at inference time.
AI.method: They use AssetOpsBench as the main benchmark and collect about 1,700 examples covering: (1) tool semantics, (2) mappings from natural language questions to tool-based plans, and (3) execution-style traces. They fine-tune Gemma 4E4B and Qwen3-4B with 8-bit QLoRA, varying LoRA rank to study quality–retention trade-offs. They then evaluate under a “description-free inference” setting where no tool catalog is included in the prompt, comparing to a baseline model that is not fine-tuned but is given the full tool schema. Metrics include structural planning scores (e.g., AT-F1), LLM-judge scores, memory usage, speed, and performance on general multiple-choice benchmarks to measure catastrophic forgetting.
AI.result: Fine-tuned models, even without seeing any tool descriptions at inference, outperform a baseline that does receive full tool schemas. Input length is reduced by 82.6%. The best Gemma run achieves AT-F1 of 0.65 and judge score 3.88 vs. 0.47 and 2.88 for the informed baseline. Qwen3-4B gets a judge score of 3.78 with 62% less memory usage and 2.5× faster inference than Gemma, but suffers more catastrophic forgetting on general MC benchmarks. Ablations show that higher LoRA rank (r=32) maximizes planning quality, while lower ranks better preserve general knowledge, revealing a controllable trade-off between specialization and retention.
AI.conclusion: For fixed tool catalogs, parameter-efficient QLoRA fine-tuning can move tool-use knowledge from the prompt into the model’s parameters, enabling description-free tool planning that both improves planning quality and substantially reduces context length and resource usage. However, this specialization can cause catastrophic forgetting, which can be modulated via the LoRA rank to balance planning performance against general knowledge retention.

title: Systematic Evaluation of the Quality of Synthetic Clinical Notes Rephrased by LLMs at Million-Note Scale
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can generate or synthesize clinical text for a wide range of applications, from improving clinical documentation to augmenting clinical text analytics. Yet evaluations typically focus on a narrow aspect -- such as similarity or utility comparisons -- even though these aspects are complementary and best viewed in parallel. In this study, we aim to conduct a systematic evaluation of LLM-generated clinical text, which includes intrinsic, extrinsic, and factuality evaluations of synthetic clinical notes rephrased from MIMIC databases at million-note scale. Our analysis demonstrates that synthetic notes preserve core clinical information and predictive utility for coarse-grained tasks despite substantial linguistic changes, but lose fine-grained details for task like ICD coding. We show this loss of detail can be substantially mitigated by rephrasing notes by chunks rather than by the whole note, but at the cost of reduced factual precision under incomplete context. Through fact-checking and error analysis, we further find that synthesis errors are dominated by misinterpretation of clinical context, alongside temporal confusion, measurement errors, and fabricated claims. Finally, we show that the synthetic notes -- despite their task-agnostic nature -- can effectively augment task-specific training for rare ICD codes.

AI.tldr: Systematic evaluation of LLM-generated synthetic clinical notes (from MIMIC) shows they preserve coarse clinical info and predictive utility but lose fine-grained details; chunk-based rephrasing helps detail but can reduce factual precision, and synthetic notes can still augment rare ICD code training.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used to generate clinical text for documentation and analytics, but existing evaluations target only narrow aspects like similarity or downstream task performance. A unified, large-scale assessment is needed to understand how well synthetic clinical notes retain information, utility, and factual correctness, and how they might safely and effectively be used in clinical ML pipelines.
AI.method: Rephrase MIMIC clinical notes at million-note scale using LLMs to create synthetic notes. Perform a systematic evaluation along three axes: intrinsic (linguistic properties and similarity to originals), extrinsic (downstream predictive utility for tasks such as risk prediction and ICD coding), and factuality (fact-checking and error analysis of clinical content). Compare whole-note vs chunk-level rephrasing strategies and analyze error types, such as misinterpretation, temporal confusion, and fabrication.
AI.result: Synthetic clinical notes preserve core clinical information and maintain predictive performance for coarse-grained downstream tasks (e.g., broad risk prediction), despite substantial surface-level linguistic changes. However, they lose fine-grained details needed for tasks like ICD coding. Chunk-based rephrasing recovers much of the lost detail but, due to limited context, leads to more factual inaccuracies. Error analysis shows that most synthesis errors stem from misinterpreting clinical context, with additional issues from temporal mixing of events, measurement mistakes, and hallucinated statements. Synthetic notes, even though generated in a task-agnostic manner, are still useful for augmenting training data for rare ICD codes, improving performance on these underrepresented labels.
AI.conclusion: LLM-generated synthetic clinical notes can reliably preserve coarse clinical signals and are valuable for many downstream tasks and data augmentation, especially for rare labels, but they are less reliable for fine-grained clinical coding and detailed factual use. Chunk-based rephrasing offers a trade-off: better detail and coding-related utility at the expense of increased factual risk due to incomplete context. Any deployment of synthetic clinical text should therefore consider these trade-offs and include explicit factuality checks and context-sensitive safeguards.

title: Heterogeneous Information-Bottleneck Coordination Graphs for Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Coordination graphs are a central abstraction in cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), yet existing sparse-graph learners lack a theoretically grounded mechanism to decide which edges should exist and how much information each edge should carry. Current methods rely on heuristic criteria that offer no formal guarantee on the learned topology, and no principled way to allocate different communication capacities to structurally different agent relationships. To address this, we propose Heterogeneous Information-Bottleneck Coordination Graphs (HIBCG), which learns a group-aware sparse graph in which both edge existence and message capacity are theoretically justified. With the graph information bottleneck (GIB) serving as the underlying tool, HIBCG first constructs a group-aligned block-diagonal prior that provides a closed-form criterion for edge retention -- determining which edges should exist and at what density per group block -- and then controls per-agent feature bandwidth on the resulting topology, compressing messages to retain only task-relevant content. We prove that the group-aligned prior strictly tightens the variational bound on topology learning, that the objective decomposes per group block, enabling differential edge control, and that capacity allocation follows a water-filling principle.

AI.tldr: They propose HIBCG, a MARL coordination-graph method that learns which edges to keep and how much information each edge can carry, with theoretical guarantees from a graph information bottleneck formulation.
AI.motivation: Existing coordination-graph methods in cooperative MARL use heuristic rules to decide graph structure and communication capacity, lacking formal guarantees and principled, heterogeneous capacity allocation across different agent relationships.
AI.method: Use the graph information bottleneck (GIB) to formulate topology and communication learning. Introduce a group-aligned block-diagonal prior over graphs, which yields a closed-form criterion for keeping or pruning edges and determining density within each group. Then, on the learned sparse topology, apply information bottleneck-style compression to control per-agent feature bandwidth, keeping only task-relevant message content.
AI.result: Theoretically, they show: (1) the group-aligned block-diagonal prior tightens the variational bound for topology learning; (2) the learning objective decomposes over group blocks, enabling different edge densities and structures per group; and (3) optimal capacity allocation across agents/edges follows a water-filling principle. Empirical results are not described in the abstract but are implied to validate the method.
AI.conclusion: HIBCG provides a theoretically grounded way to learn sparse, heterogeneous coordination graphs and communication capacities in cooperative MARL, improving over heuristic approaches by yielding provable structure selection, decomposable objectives, and principled capacity allocation.

title: Computational Challenges in Token Economics: Bridging Economic Theory and AI System Design
summary: Token economics has emerged as a useful lens for understanding resource allocation, value creation, and pricing in large language model systems. While recent work has increasingly treated tokens as economic primitives, there remains a substantial gap between high-level economic theory and the computational realities of modern AI infrastructure. This paper identifies and analyzes the key computational challenges that arise when token-economic principles are implemented in real-time inference systems. We argue that computational feasibility is not merely one dimension of token economics, but its governing constraint: these challenges are driven by fundamental tensions among fine-grained valuation, low-latency execution, and allocation optimality under uncertainty. To structure this problem space, we introduce the notion of \textbf{Computational Token Economics} and propose the \textbf{Token Economics Trilemma} -- a conditional no-free-lunch principle that captures the inherent trade-offs among granularity, real-time performance, and optimality. We further categorize the main technical challenges into three areas: real-time value accounting, constrained resource allocation, and economic-aware system architecture. Rather than presenting a complete solution, this paper aims to define a research agenda for bridging token economics and AI system design, highlighting open problems at the intersection of computational economics, machine learning systems, and AI infrastructure.

AI.tldr: The paper defines a new area, Computational Token Economics, and introduces the Token Economics Trilemma, arguing that real-time token-based economic mechanisms in LLM systems face inherent trade-offs among valuation granularity, latency, and optimality, and sets a research agenda rather than full solutions.
AI.motivation: Token economics is increasingly used to reason about value and resource allocation in LLM systems, but existing work is mostly theoretical or coarse-grained and does not align well with the hard computational and systems constraints of real-time AI inference. There is a need to understand how economic principles interact with the low-latency, uncertain, and resource-constrained environment of modern AI infrastructure.
AI.method: The paper is conceptual and analytical. It defines the notion of Computational Token Economics, formulates the Token Economics Trilemma as a high-level no-free-lunch principle, and organizes the problem space into three core technical challenge areas: real-time value accounting, constrained resource allocation, and economic-aware system architecture. It then uses this structure to outline open questions and research directions rather than executing experiments.
AI.result: The main results are conceptual frameworks: (1) the definition of Computational Token Economics as a field; (2) the Token Economics Trilemma describing trade-offs among fine-grained valuation, real-time performance, and allocation optimality under uncertainty; and (3) a taxonomy of technical challenges into three categories (value accounting, resource allocation, and system architecture) that together form a roadmap for future research.
AI.conclusion: Computational constraints are not peripheral but central to token economics in LLM systems: real-time inference imposes fundamental trade-offs that prevent simultaneously achieving highly granular valuation, low latency, and globally optimal allocation under uncertainty. Recognizing and formalizing these tensions via Computational Token Economics and the Token Economics Trilemma provides a foundation for a systematic research agenda connecting economic design with AI system engineering.

title: Generating Pretraining Tokens from Organic Data for Data-Bound Scaling
summary: LLM pretraining is shifting from a compute-bound to a data-bound regime, where available human (organic) text falls far short of scaling demands. However, reaching the data-bound regime does not mean the model has fully utilized its organic corpus. In this paper, we introduce SynPro, a synthetic data generation framework that helps LLMs more thoroughly learn from limited organic data. SynPro applies two operations, rephrasing and reformat, that present the same organic source in diverse forms to facilitate deeper learning without introducing external information. Both generators are optimized via reinforcement learning with quality, faithfulness, and data influence rewards, and are continuously updated as pretraining plateaus to target content the model has yet to absorb. We pretrain 400M and 1.1B models with 10% of their Chinchilla-optimal tokens (0.8B and 2.2B) from DCLM-Baseline, reflecting a realistic data-bound regime in frontier pretraining. Our results reveal that organic data is significantly underutilized by standard repetition: SynPro unlocks 3.7-5.2x the effective tokens of repetition, even surpassing the non-data-bound oracle that trains on equivalent unique data at the 1.1B scale. Analyses confirm that faithful, model-aware synthesis sustains data-bound scaling without causing distribution collapse. We open-source our code at https://github.com/cxcscmu/SynPro.

AI.tldr: SynPro is a synthetic data framework that repeatedly rephrases and reformats the same human-written text so LLMs can learn more from limited data, greatly increasing the effective training signal without adding new external content.
AI.motivation: As LLMs scale, training is becoming limited by the amount of high-quality human (organic) text rather than compute. Simply repeating the same data underutilizes it and cannot fully meet scaling laws. The authors want a way to get more effective learning signal out of a fixed, limited organic corpus, staying faithful to its content while avoiding distribution collapse and still obeying data-bound scaling behavior.
AI.method: They propose SynPro, which generates synthetic variants of an existing organic corpus using two operations: (1) rephrasing (different wordings of the same content) and (2) reformatting (e.g., changing structure or style) while not introducing any new external information beyond the original text. Two generator models are trained with reinforcement learning, optimized jointly on three reward dimensions: quality, faithfulness to the source, and data influence (how much the synthetic sample helps the target model learn). During pretraining of a target LLM, these generators are periodically updated when performance plateaus, focusing generation on content areas the model has not yet fully absorbed. Experiments are done on 400M and 1.1B models trained with only 10% of the Chinchilla-optimal token budget using the DCLM-Baseline corpus, to simulate a realistic data-bound regime.
AI.result: Under this data-limited setting, SynPro produces much more useful training signal from the same organic corpus than standard data repetition. It effectively multiplies the usable token budget: synthetic variants unlock 3.7–5.2 times the effective tokens compared with naive repetition. At 1.1B scale, SynPro-trained models even outperform an "oracle" setting where the model has access to equivalent amounts of unique, non-repeated data (i.e., a non-data-bound regime). Empirical analyses show that the synthetic data remains faithful to the original text and tailored to model weaknesses, avoiding distribution collapse while preserving data-bound scaling trends.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current pretraining pipelines severely underutilize available organic text when they rely on simple repetition. Faithful, model-aware synthetic transformations—specifically rephrasing and reformatting optimized with RL—can substantially extend the effective size of a fixed corpus, sustaining data-bound scaling for LLMs. This offers a practical path to improve model performance in regimes where additional high-quality human data is scarce, without depending on new external text sources. The authors release SynPro to facilitate further research on synthetic data for efficient LLM pretraining.

title: Multi-Party Multi-Objective Optimization as Consensus Search: Runtime Analysis of Cross-Party Recombination
summary: Multi-party multi-objective optimization problems (MPMOPs) require consensus among autonomous decision makers and therefore differ from flattened many-objective formulations. Existing runtime theory for multi-objective evolutionary algorithms is largely tailored to single-party Pareto-front approximation and does not directly explain common-solution search in MPMOPs. We investigate cross-party recombination in two representative settings. On MP-JCG, a pseudo-Boolean benchmark with an explicit gap region, we prove that a payoff-guided mutation baseline faces a gap-crossing bottleneck requiring \(Θ(n^2)\) expected fitness evaluations. In contrast, an analytical CPR-NSGA-II variant discovers both common Pareto-optimal solutions in \(O(n\log n)\) expected evaluations by directly assembling complementary prefix and suffix templates distributed across party populations. Comparing this with the flattened four-objective formulation F-JCG, our full-front coverage analysis illustrates the additional coverage burden introduced by flattening. For BPBOMST, the bi-party, two-objective-per-party specialization of the multi-party multi-objective minimum spanning tree problem, we develop a layered support-cover analysis. For each common Pareto objective vector, the symmetric average projection induces an auxiliary bi-objective MST instance, and suitable support representatives yield a \(2λ\)-common approximation cover with \(λ\in[1,2]\). We further derive an instance-parameterized expected runtime bound for a representative-pool CPR-NSGA-II variant using edge-union recombination and uniform repair. This bound separates the effects of local auxiliary-front filling, cross-party recombination shortcuts, and edge-union repair ambiguity.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how evolutionary algorithms can efficiently find solutions agreed upon by multiple independent parties in multi-objective optimization, and provides runtime theory and approximation guarantees for specialized algorithms using cross-party recombination.
AI.motivation: Standard multi-objective evolutionary algorithm theory focuses on a single decision maker approximating a Pareto front. However, in many real applications, multiple autonomous parties must jointly agree on common solutions, which leads to different search dynamics and challenges, especially when naive flattening to a many-objective problem increases complexity. There is a lack of runtime theory explaining how to efficiently search for such common solutions and how cross-party recombination can help.
AI.method: The authors analyze cross-party recombination in multi-party multi-objective optimization using two benchmark families. For MP-JCG, a pseudo-Boolean instance with an explicit gap, they compare a payoff-guided mutation baseline with a CPR-NSGA-II variant, proving separate expected runtime bounds based on how each crosses the gap and assembles templates from different parties. They also compare this multi-party formulation with a flattened four-objective version, F-JCG, via full-front coverage analysis. For BPBOMST, a structured bi-party multi-objective MST problem, they introduce a layered support-cover analysis that maps each common Pareto objective vector to an auxiliary bi-objective MST instance via symmetric average projection, and use support representatives to construct a 2λ-common approximation cover. They further derive an instance-dependent expected runtime bound for a representative-pool CPR-NSGA-II with edge-union recombination and uniform repair, decomposing contributions from local auxiliary-front filling, recombination shortcuts, and repair ambiguity.
AI.result: For MP-JCG, the payoff-guided mutation baseline is shown to suffer from a gap-crossing bottleneck that requires Θ(n^2) expected evaluations, whereas an analytical CPR-NSGA-II variant exploits cross-party recombination to find both common Pareto-optimal solutions in O(n log n) expected evaluations by recombining complementary solution templates. The comparison with the flattened F-JCG formulation demonstrates that flattening introduces extra coverage requirements, making complete front coverage harder. For BPBOMST, the layered support-cover analysis shows that for each common Pareto objective vector, the induced auxiliary bi-objective MST instance admits support representatives that yield a 2λ-common approximation cover with λ between 1 and 2. The runtime bound for the representative-pool CPR-NSGA-II variant isolates and quantifies the impact of local auxiliary-front optimization, beneficial cross-party recombination, and uncertainty caused by edge-union repair.
AI.conclusion: Cross-party recombination can dramatically improve the efficiency of evolutionary search for common solutions in multi-party multi-objective optimization compared to mutation-only baselines, especially in the presence of structural gaps. Flattening multi-party problems into higher-dimensional single-party formulations increases the difficulty of achieving comprehensive front coverage. The proposed theoretical frameworks—full-front coverage analysis for MP-JCG and layered support-cover analysis for BPBOMST—clarify how multi-party structure and algorithm design interact, and yield both runtime bounds and approximation guarantees that are specific to the multi-party setting.

title: PAREDA: A Multi-Accent Speech Dataset of Natural Language Processing Research Discussions
comment: Accepted and presented at SPEAKABLE 2026 workshop at LREC 2026
summary: While modern Automatic Speech Recognition (ASR) systems achieve high accuracy on benchmark corpora, their performance often degrades when there is real-world variability. This work focuses on variability arising due to accented, spontaneous, and domain-specific speech. In particular, we introduce PAper REading DAtaset (PAREDA), a first-of-its-kind multi-accent speech dataset consisting of discussions on academic Natural Language Processing (NLP) papers between speakers with Australian, Indian-English, and Chinese English accents. Each session elicits a spontaneous monologue (a summary of a paper's abstract) and a non-monologue (a question-and-answer session between participants), resulting in a corpus rich with technical jargon and conversational phenomena. We evaluate the performance of SOTA ASR models on PAREDA, analysing the impact of accent mixing and increased speech rate. Our results show that, in the zero-shot setting, models perform worse, confirming the dataset's challenging nature. However, fine-tuning on PAREDA significantly reduces the Word Error Rate (WER), demonstrating that our dataset captures linguistic characteristics often missing from existing corpora. PAREDA serves as a valuable new resource for building and evaluating more robust and inclusive ASR systems for specialised, real-world applications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PAREDA, a multi-accent, NLP-paper-focused speech dataset and shows that fine-tuning ASR models on it greatly improves recognition accuracy over zero-shot use, highlighting its value for robust, real-world ASR.
AI.motivation: Existing ASR systems perform well on standard benchmarks but struggle with real-world variability, especially accented, spontaneous, and domain-specific speech that includes technical jargon and conversational phenomena. There is a lack of datasets that jointly capture multiple accents, spontaneous dialogue, and specialised vocabulary in realistic settings, which limits the development and evaluation of robust ASR for such conditions.
AI.method: The authors construct PAREDA, a multi-accent speech dataset formed from discussions about academic NLP papers. Sessions involve speakers with Australian, Indian-English, and Chinese English accents. Each session contains two types of speech: (1) a spontaneous monologue where a participant summarizes a paper’s abstract, and (2) a non-monologue question–answer discussion among participants. This yields speech with high speech rates, accent mixing, technical terms, and conversational features. They then benchmark several state-of-the-art ASR models on PAREDA under zero-shot conditions and after fine-tuning on the dataset. They analyze factors such as accent mixing and increased speaking rate on ASR performance, using metrics like Word Error Rate (WER).
AI.result: State-of-the-art ASR models, when used in a zero-shot manner on PAREDA, show degraded performance, confirming that the dataset is challenging and not well-covered by existing training data. After fine-tuning these models on PAREDA, their WER drops significantly, indicating that the models can adapt effectively and that PAREDA provides useful, previously underrepresented linguistic variability. The analysis also highlights negative impacts from accent mixing and higher speech rates on recognition accuracy.
AI.conclusion: PAREDA is a novel and challenging multi-accent dataset centered on technical, NLP-related speech in both monologic and dialogic settings. It exposes weaknesses in current ASR models when dealing with accented, fast, and domain-specific speech, but also shows that targeted fine-tuning can substantially mitigate these issues. The dataset thus offers an important resource for creating and evaluating more robust, inclusive, and domain-aware ASR systems suited for specialized, real-world use cases.

title: The Capability Paradox: How Smarter Auditors Make Multi-Agent Systems Less Secure
summary: Multi-agent systems extend large language models (LLMs) by decomposing tasks among specialized agents, but their distributed decision process creates new attack surfaces. We identify \emph{semantic hijacking}, an attack in which harmful requests are concealed within domain-specific narratives and propagated to a Manager through Worker reports, without any syntactic injection primitives. Across 42,000 adversarial trials over 12 Manager models and 7 Worker configurations, we uncover a \emph{capability paradox}: as Worker capability increases, the mean system-level Attack Success Rate (ASR) increases from 18.4% to 63.9%, peaking at 94.4%. To explain this effect, we conduct multi-level mediation analysis on two independent datasets (47,807 interactions). This analysis shows that this paradox is driven by \emph{linguistic certainty}: stronger Workers are more likely to interpret adversarial narratives as legitimate, convey their conclusions assertively, and thereby lead Managers to treat such confident endorsements as justification to execute. In our larger Worker-Only setting ($n_W$=14), certainty mediates 74% of the effect, with 95% confidence intervals (CI) excluding zero under both Monte Carlo and cluster bootstrap; the smaller Full-MAS setting ($n_W$ =6) shows a directionally consistent indirect effect. Worker-side safety prompting does not reliably mitigate this failure. Building on the mediation finding, we propose \emph{heterogeneous ensemble verification}, which pairs Workers of asymmetric domain competence so their complementary vulnerabilities break the certainty-to-execution chain, reducing ASR from 52.8% to 2.0% with negligible benign-task impact. Our results show that upgrading components to stronger models can actively degrade system security, and that effective defenses require exploiting--rather than eliminating--capability asymmetries between agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and analyzes a new attack, semantic hijacking, on multi-agent LLM systems, showing that more capable worker agents can paradoxically increase security risk via overconfident harmful recommendations, and proposes a heterogeneous ensemble verification defense that drastically reduces attack success.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent systems that coordinate multiple LLMs promise better task performance via specialization and decomposition, but their distributed nature opens new, poorly understood attack surfaces. Existing work focuses on classic prompt injection and syntactic attacks, not on subtle, domain-specific manipulations that exploit how agents reason together. The paper aims to systematically characterize such threats, explain why stronger agents can make systems less safe, and design defenses tailored to multi-agent settings rather than single-model prompting.
AI.method: The authors define semantic hijacking, where adversarial intent is embedded within plausible domain narratives that workers process and summarize up to a manager, without obvious injection tokens. They conduct 42,000 adversarial trials across 12 manager models and 7 worker configurations to measure system-level attack success rates as worker capabilities vary. They then run multi-level mediation analyses on two datasets totaling 47,807 interactions to test whether linguistic certainty mediates the relation between worker capability and attack success. They evaluate safety prompting on workers and finally design and test a heterogeneous ensemble verification scheme pairing workers with asymmetric domain strengths to break the certainty-to-execution link, measuring both attack success and benign-task performance.
AI.result: They discover a capability paradox: increasing worker model capability raises the system Attack Success Rate from 18.4% to 63.9%, reaching 94.4% in some conditions. Mediation analysis indicates that linguistic certainty accounts for most of this effect: stronger workers more often misinterpret adversarial narratives as legitimate and express their conclusions with higher confidence, which managers then treat as sufficient justification to execute harmful actions. In a larger worker-only setting (n_W=14), certainty mediates 74% of the capability effect, with 95% CIs excluding zero under Monte Carlo and cluster bootstrap methods; a smaller full multi-agent setting (n_W=6) shows consistent directionality. Safety prompting on workers fails to reliably reduce this vulnerability. The proposed heterogeneous ensemble verification, using workers with complementary competencies and failure modes, reduces attack success from 52.8% to 2.0% while maintaining benign-task performance.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent LLM systems are vulnerable to semantic hijacking attacks that do not rely on traditional prompt injection but instead exploit domain narratives and inter-agent communication. Upgrading worker models to be more capable can unintentionally worsen security because managers over-trust confident, linguistically certain summaries from strong workers, leading to harmful executions. Simple worker-side safety prompts are insufficient. Effective defenses must leverage capability asymmetries between agents: by pairing heterogeneous workers whose differing strengths disrupt the certainty-to-execution pathway, systems can substantially reduce attack success without sacrificing normal-task performance. Designers of multi-agent LLM architectures should therefore treat model upgrades and agent composition as security-critical decisions, not purely performance optimizations.

title: Multi-agent AI systems outperform human teams in creativity
summary: Although artificial intelligence (AI) now matches or exceeds human performance across numerous cognitive tasks, creativity remains a highly contested frontier. As AI systems based on large language models (LLMs) are increasingly adopted in research and innovation, it is essential to understand and augment their creativity. Here we demonstrate that multi-agent LLM teams not only surpass single agents, but also substantially outperform human teams in creativity (Cohen's d=1.50) across 4,541 multi-agent LLM ideas and 341 human-team ideas on six diverse problem-solving tasks. This advantage is driven by novelty while maintaining comparable usefulness. To investigate the generative processes in both groups, we represent conversations as paths through semantic space using neural language model representations. Both LLM and human teams produce more creative ideas when conversations range widely rather than staying centered on a single theme (low global coherence). However, the additional patterns that predict creativity differ: LLM teams benefit from efficient exploration (high semantic spread, shorter paths), while human teams benefit from maintaining smooth conversational flow (high local coherence, frequent pivots). Additionally, we identify model choice and discussion structure as orthogonal design levers that together explain 26.8% of variance in LLM conversational dynamics, paving the way for systematic approaches to developing multi-agent systems with augmented creative capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that multi-agent LLM teams are more creative than both single LLMs and human teams across several tasks, and analyzes how conversational dynamics in semantic space relate to creativity.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in research and innovation, it is important to understand whether and how they can be truly creative, how they compare to humans, and how to systematically design AI systems that enhance creativity rather than just automate routine cognition.
AI.method: The authors compare idea generation by multi-agent LLM teams, single LLM agents, and human teams on six varied problem-solving tasks, scoring ideas on creativity (novelty and usefulness). They quantitatively evaluate performance and then model conversations as trajectories through a neural semantic embedding space to characterize conversational dynamics (e.g., global coherence, local coherence, path length, semantic spread). They also vary LLM models and interaction structures to analyze how design choices shape these dynamics.
AI.result: Multi-agent LLM teams significantly outperform human teams in creativity with a large effect size (Cohen's d=1.50), mainly by producing more novel ideas while preserving usefulness. Both humans and LLMs generate more creative ideas when their discussions traverse a wide semantic range (low global coherence), but additional predictive patterns differ: LLM teams are more creative when they explore efficiently (high semantic spread with shorter paths), whereas human teams are more creative when they maintain smoother local transitions and pivot more frequently. Model selection and discussion protocol independently explain 26.8% of the variance in LLM conversational dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Creativity in multi-agent LLM systems can exceed that of human teams on certain tasks, and can be intentionally shaped by manipulating conversational dynamics, model choice, and discussion structure. Semantic-trajectory analysis offers a principled way to understand and design multi-agent AI systems with enhanced creative capabilities, suggesting that different mechanisms underlie creativity in humans and LLMs.

title: RAG-based EEG-to-Text Translation Using Deep Learning and LLMs
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures. Submitted to the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Systems, Man, and Cybernetics
summary: The decoding of linguistic information from electroencephalography (EEG) signals remains an extremely challenging problem in brain-computer interface (BCI) research. In particular, sentence-level decoding from EEG is difficult due to the low signal-to-noise ratio of these recordings. Previous studies tackling this problem have typically failed to surpass random baseline performance unless teacher forcing is used during the inference phase. In this work, we propose a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG)-based sentence-level EEG-to-text decoding pipeline that combines an EEG encoder aligned with semantic sentence embeddings, a vector retrieval stage, and a large language model (LLM) to refine retrieved sentences into coherent output. Experiments are conducted on the Zurich Cognitive Language Processing Corpus (ZuCo) dataset, which contains single-trial EEG recordings collected during silent reading. To evaluate whether the system extracts meaningful information from these EEG signals, the results are compared with a random baseline. In nine subjects, the proposed pipeline outperforms the random baseline, achieving a mean cosine similarity of 0.181 +- 0.022 compared to 0.139 +- 0.029 for the baseline, corresponding to a relative improvement of 30.45%. Statistical analysis further confirms that this improvement is significant, following a strict evaluation workflow where inference is performed without access to ground-truth labels.

AI.tldr: They propose and evaluate a new RAG-based pipeline to decode full sentences from EEG signals during silent reading, showing statistically significant performance above a random baseline without teacher forcing.
AI.motivation: Sentence-level decoding from EEG for BCIs is very challenging because EEG has low signal-to-noise ratio, and prior work usually cannot beat random baselines unless they use teacher forcing during inference, which weakens claims about true decoding ability. The authors want a method that genuinely extracts linguistic information from EEG and can be evaluated without access to ground-truth labels at inference time.
AI.method: They design a retrieval-augmented generation pipeline that first encodes EEG into a semantic sentence embedding aligned space, then uses vector retrieval to fetch candidate sentences, and finally uses a large language model to refine these retrieved sentences into fluent, coherent text. They train and test on the ZuCo dataset of single-trial EEG collected during silent reading. Performance is measured via cosine similarity between predicted and ground-truth sentence embeddings and compared with a random retrieval baseline, under an evaluation protocol with no teacher forcing or label leakage at inference.
AI.result: On data from nine subjects in the ZuCo corpus, the pipeline achieves a mean cosine similarity of 0.181 ± 0.022, versus 0.139 ± 0.029 for a random baseline, yielding a 30.45% relative improvement. Statistical tests indicate that this performance gain over the random baseline is significant.
AI.conclusion: A RAG-based EEG-to-text system can recover sentence-level semantic information from noisy EEG signals at a level significantly above random, even when evaluated under strict conditions without teacher forcing. This suggests that combining EEG encoders aligned to semantic embeddings, retrieval, and LLM-based refinement is a promising direction for practical sentence-level brain-to-text BCIs.

title: A Pilot Benchmark for NL-to-FOL Translation in Planetary Exploration
summary:
Future planetary exploration envisions autonomous robotic agents operating under severe communication constraints, without global positioning, and with minimal human intervention. In such environments, agents must not only perceive and act, but also reason over mission objectives, operational constraints, and evolving environmental conditions. While prior work has largely focused on perception and control, the translation of high-level mission knowledge into structured, machine-interpretable representations remains underexplored.
  We introduce a pilot benchmark for translating natural language (NL) into First-Order Logic (FOL) within the domain of planetary exploration. The dataset is constructed from real mission documentation sourced from NASA's Planetary Data System (PDS), spanning missions from 2003 to 2013. These documents describe mission phases such as launch, boost, coast, cruise, and orbital operations in rich natural language. We manually annotate these documents with corresponding FOL representations that capture temporal structure, agent roles, and operational dependencies. In addition, we provide structured predicate vocabularies and typed constants to enable controlled experimentation with varying levels of prior knowledge. This pilot benchmark provides a foundation for research at the intersection of language understanding and formal reasoning, grounded in real-world, safety-critical mission data. The dataset is provided at: https://github.com/HaydenMM/planetary-logic-benchmark/blob/main/pilot_benchmark.json
