AI.tldr: Pilot benchmark for translating natural language mission documentation into first-order logic in the planetary exploration domain.
AI.motivation: Autonomous planetary robots must reason about complex mission goals, constraints, and temporal structure using formal representations, but existing work emphasizes perception and control rather than translating high-level natural language mission knowledge into machine-interpretable logic.
AI.method: Collect real mission documents from NASA's Planetary Data System for missions between 2003–2013; identify text describing phases like launch, cruise, and orbital operations; manually annotate this text with corresponding first-order logic formulas encoding temporal relations, agent roles, and operational dependencies; define a predicate vocabulary and typed constants, and package all of this as a benchmark dataset for NL-to-FOL translation with different prior-knowledge settings.
AI.result: A publicly available pilot benchmark dataset that pairs rich natural language descriptions of planetary mission phases with carefully designed FOL representations and a structured predicate/constant schema, enabling controlled experiments on natural language to logic translation in a safety-critical domain.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark establishes an initial, realistic testbed for research on mapping natural language mission documentation into formal logical representations, supporting future work on language understanding and formal reasoning for autonomous space exploration systems.

title: Self-supervised Hierarchical Visual Reasoning with World Model
summary: 3D open-world environments with adversarial opponents remain a core challenge for reinforcement learning due to their vast state spaces. Effective reasoning representations are essential in such settings. While existing self-supervised visual foresight reasoning approaches often suffer from multi-step error accumulation, many recent studies resort to injecting domain-specific knowledge for more stable guidance. Our key insight is that the photorealistic fidelity of visual reasoning representations is secondary; what truly matters is providing informative, task-relevant signals. To this end, we propose ResDreamer, a hierarchical world model in which each higher-level layer is trained to reconstruct the residuals of the layer below. This design enables progressive abstraction of increasingly sophisticated world dynamics and fosters the emergence of richer latent representations. Drawing inspiration from the "Bitter Lesson", ResDreamer trains its reasoning representations in a purely self-supervised manner. The higher-level residual representations are used to modulate lower-level predictions, allowing the world model to scale effectively with only linearly increasing cross-layer communication costs. Experiments show that ResDreamer achieves state-of-the-art sample efficiency and parameter efficiency. This scalable hierarchical visual foresight reasoning architecture paves the way for more capable online RL agents in open-ended, dynamic environments. The code is accessible at \url{https://github.com/XuYuanFei01/ResDreamer}.

AI.tldr: ResDreamer is a hierarchical, self-supervised world model that learns residual-based visual representations to improve sample- and parameter-efficient reinforcement learning in 3D open-world environments.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning in 3D open-world environments is difficult due to large state spaces and complex dynamics. Existing self-supervised visual foresight models suffer from compounding prediction errors over multiple steps, while many recent methods rely on injecting domain-specific knowledge for stability, which limits generality and scalability. The authors aim to find a more general, scalable way to build effective reasoning representations that provide task-relevant signals without requiring photorealistic prediction fidelity or hand-engineered priors.
AI.method: The authors propose ResDreamer, a hierarchical world model where the representation is organized in multiple levels. Each higher-level layer is trained to reconstruct the residual (the prediction error) of the layer below rather than the raw observations. This residual learning induces progressive abstraction of world dynamics and encourages richer latent representations at upper levels. Training is entirely self-supervised, in line with the 'Bitter Lesson', avoiding domain-specific knowledge injection. During inference, higher-level residual representations modulate lower-level predictions, enabling better scaling of the model’s reasoning capacity while keeping cross-layer communication costs growing only linearly with the number of layers.
AI.result: In experiments on 3D open-world environments with adversarial opponents, ResDreamer attains state-of-the-art sample efficiency and parameter efficiency compared to prior world-model and visual foresight baselines. The hierarchical residual structure yields more informative, task-relevant latent signals for reinforcement learning agents, improving performance under limited interaction data and model size constraints.
AI.conclusion: ResDreamer demonstrates that photorealistic visual prediction is not necessary for effective RL in complex environments; instead, hierarchical residual world models that focus on informative, task-relevant signals can deliver superior efficiency and scalability. The proposed residual hierarchy scales with only linear cross-layer communication cost and provides a general, self-supervised framework for visual foresight, paving the way toward more capable online RL agents in open-ended, dynamic 3D environments.

title: Memory-Guided Tree Search with Cross-Branch Knowledge Transfer for LLM Solver Synthesis
summary: Combinatorial optimization (CO) underlies decision-making from logistics to chip design, where infeasible solutions are operationally unusable and small quality gains translate into substantial economic value. Recent work uses large language models (LLMs) to automate solver synthesis: generating executable solver programs from natural-language specifications. However, existing tree-search and evolutionary agents refine candidate trajectories in parallel without explicit knowledge transfer, reintroducing the same constraint violations and converging on similar algorithm families. We introduce MEMOIR, a memory-guided tree-search framework with a two-level memory hierarchy: branch-local memory preserves execution-grounded refinement details within a branch as it iterates on a single algorithmic design, while global memory stores compressed algorithmic and failure-mode summaries across branches. A reflection step at branch termination distills these summaries, enabling cross-branch transfer without polluting future contexts with low-level debugging traces. Across seven CO problems spanning scheduling, routing, packing, and geometric design, MEMOIR achieves 96.7% solution validity (a 9.2 point gap over the strongest baseline) and improves the average normalized score by 7.3 points at matched per-method execution budget. Over three independent runs on four problems, MEMOIR's run-to-run validity standard deviation is more than an order of magnitude below that of every baseline we evaluated in this setting, suggesting that memory-guided exploration yields consistent improvements rather than reflecting sampling variance.

AI.tldr: The paper presents MEMOIR, a memory-guided tree-search framework that uses a two-level memory hierarchy and reflection to transfer algorithmic insights across search branches, significantly improving validity, quality, and stability of LLM‑synthesized combinatorial optimization solvers.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based solver-synthesis agents for combinatorial optimization explore many candidate algorithms in parallel but lack effective knowledge transfer between search branches. As a result, they repeatedly violate the same constraints and converge to similar algorithm designs, limiting solution validity, quality, and robustness. The authors aim to create a framework that can systematically remember, abstract, and reuse both successful strategies and failure modes to guide exploration more intelligently and consistently.
AI.method: The authors propose MEMOIR, a memory-guided tree-search framework with a hierarchical memory design. Each search branch maintains a branch-local memory that accumulates execution-grounded refinement details while iterating on a specific algorithm design. When a branch terminates, a reflection step distills these detailed traces into compact summaries of algorithmic ideas and failure patterns, which are stored in a global memory. Subsequent branches can condition their search on this global memory, enabling cross-branch transfer of high-level insights without cluttering the context with low-level debugging logs. The framework is evaluated on multiple combinatorial optimization problems, using LLMs to generate and refine solver programs within a fixed execution budget.
AI.result: Across seven combinatorial optimization tasks in scheduling, routing, packing, and geometric design, MEMOIR attains 96.7% valid solutions, outperforming the strongest baseline by 9.2 percentage points. It also improves average normalized solution quality by 7.3 points under a matched compute budget. In stability tests with three independent runs across four problems, MEMOIR’s standard deviation of solution validity is more than an order of magnitude lower than all evaluated baselines, indicating much higher run-to-run consistency.
AI.conclusion: By integrating a two-level memory hierarchy and reflective summarization into tree-search for LLM-based solver synthesis, MEMOIR enables effective reuse of algorithmic knowledge and failure patterns across search branches. This yields not only higher solution validity and quality but also significantly more consistent performance across runs, suggesting that structured memory-guided exploration is a powerful direction for advancing automated solver design for combinatorial optimization.

title: Universal Adversarial Triggers
summary: Recent works have illustrated that modern NLP models trained for diverse tasks ranging from sentiment analysis to language generation succumb to universal adversarial attacks, a class of input-agnostic attacks where a common trigger sequence is used to attack the model. Although these attacks are successful, the triggers generated by such attacks are ungrammatical and unnatural. Our work proposes a novel technique combining parts-of-speech filtering and perplexity based loss function to generate sensible triggers that are closer to natural phrases. For the task of sentiment analysis on the SST dataset, the method produces sensible triggers that achieve accuracies as low as 0.04 and 0.12 for flipping positive to negative predictions and vice-versa. To build robust models, we also perform adversarial training using the generated triggers that increases the accuracy of the model from 0.12 to 0.48. We aim to illustrate that adversarial attacks can be made difficult to detect by generating sensible triggers, and to facilitate robust model development through relevant defenses.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to craft natural-sounding universal adversarial triggers for NLP models using POS filtering and a perplexity-based loss, showing they can severely degrade sentiment analysis accuracy while remaining grammatically sensible, and that adversarial training with these triggers improves robustness.
AI.motivation: Existing universal adversarial attacks on NLP models use fixed trigger phrases that, while effective, tend to be ungrammatical and obviously unnatural. This makes them easier to detect and less realistic as threats. The authors are motivated to create adversarial triggers that resemble natural language, both to show that attacks can be stealthier and to support the development of more robust defense strategies.
AI.method: They design a trigger generation procedure that (1) filters candidate trigger tokens based on part-of-speech constraints so they fit better into typical sentence structures, and (2) employs a perplexity-based loss that encourages triggers with low language-model perplexity, i.e., more fluent and natural-sounding sequences. These triggers are universal: the same phrase is appended to many inputs to flip model predictions. They test the approach on sentiment analysis with the SST dataset and then use the generated triggers for adversarial training.
AI.result: For a sentiment analysis model trained on SST, the generated natural triggers achieve very strong attack performance, reducing accuracy to 0.04 for flipping positive to negative predictions and 0.12 for flipping negative to positive. After adversarial training with these triggers, the attacked model’s accuracy under the same attack improves from 0.12 to 0.48, indicating enhanced robustness.
AI.conclusion: Universal adversarial attacks on NLP models can be made substantially more stealthy by enforcing grammaticality and fluency via POS filtering and perplexity-based loss, while retaining high attack strength. Such realistic triggers present a more serious security concern but also provide a useful tool for adversarial training, which can significantly increase model robustness to this class of attacks.

title: BacktestBench: Benchmarking Large Language Models for Automated Quantitative Strategy Backtesting
comment: This paper has been accepted by KDD 2026 (Datasets and Benchmarks Track)
summary: Quantitative backtesting is essential for evaluating trading strategies but remains hampered by high technical barriers and limited scalability. While Large Language Models (LLMs) offer a transformative path to automate this complex, interdisciplinary workflow through advanced code generation, tool usage, and agentic planning, the practical realization is significantly challenged by the current lack of a large-scale benchmark dedicated to automated quantitative backtesting, which hinders progress in this field. To bridge this critical gap, we introduce BacktestBench, the first large-scale benchmark for automated quantitative backtesting. Built from over 6 million real market records, it comprises 18,246 meticulously annotated question-answering pairs across four task categories: metrics calculation, ticker selection, strategy selection, and parameter confirmation. We also propose AutoBacktest, a robust multi-agent baseline that translates natural language strategies into reproducible backtests by coordinating a Summarizer for semantic factor extraction, a Retriever for validated SQL generation, and a Coder for Python backtesting implementation. Our evaluation on 23 mainstream LLMs, complemented by targeted ablations, identifies key factors that influence end-to-end performance and highlights the importance of grounded verification and standardized indicator representations.

AI.tldr: They introduce BacktestBench, a large-scale benchmark and a multi‑agent baseline system (AutoBacktest) for automating quantitative trading backtests with LLMs, and evaluate many LLMs on it.
AI.motivation: Quantitative backtesting of trading strategies is crucial but currently difficult: it requires significant technical expertise, is hard to scale, and existing LLM work lacks a dedicated, realistic benchmark for this end-to-end workflow. This absence of benchmarks slows progress on applying LLMs to automated backtesting and financial research.
AI.method: They build BacktestBench using more than 6 million real market records and construct 18,246 annotated QA pairs spanning four task types: metrics calculation, ticker selection, strategy selection, and parameter confirmation. They also design AutoBacktest, a multi-agent system where: (1) a Summarizer extracts semantic factors from natural language strategy descriptions, (2) a Retriever generates and validates SQL queries over the market data, and (3) a Coder writes Python code to run reproducible backtests. They then systematically evaluate 23 popular LLMs on the benchmark and run ablation studies on the AutoBacktest pipeline.
AI.result: BacktestBench is successfully constructed as a large-scale benchmark dataset, and AutoBacktest is implemented as a strong baseline system. The experiments on 23 LLMs reveal how different models perform across the four task categories, and ablations show which components and design choices (e.g., verification mechanisms, indicator representations) most affect overall performance.
AI.conclusion: A realistic, large-scale benchmark for automated quantitative backtesting is both feasible and valuable. BacktestBench, together with the AutoBacktest baseline, provides a foundation for systematic evaluation and improvement of LLM-based backtesting systems. The findings emphasize that grounded verification and standardized ways of representing financial indicators are critical to achieving reliable, end-to-end automation in quantitative backtesting.

title: Generalization or Memorization? Brittleness Testing for Chess-Trained Language Models
summary: Recent work has fine-tuned language models on chess data and reported high benchmark scores as evidence that the resulting models can understand the rules of chess, play full chess games at a professional level, or generate human-readable explanations grounded in expert knowledge. We train KinGPT, a 25M-parameter character-level language model trained only on (position, best-move) pairs, who exceeds 3B-parameter ChessGPT on a 600-puzzle mate-in-N suite and 4B-parameter C1-4B over a 20-theme puzzle benchmark. We examine several claims made in existing literature regarding chess-trained language models and assert that their impressive benchmark performance is largely explained by pattern-matching. We also demonstrate how LLM-Modulo, a verifier-in-the-loop framework, raises RedPajama 3B's best move accuracy from 1.2% to 21.2% and move generation validity from 19.3% to 95.3% on mate-in-N chess puzzles, comparable to gains achieved from ChessGPT's fine-tuning on chess-specific web corpora at a fraction of the cost. Our results illustrate how pairing a general LLM with an external verifier offers a more flexible alternative to directly training on synthetic data for well-defined domains. We open source all training/evaluation code, datasets, puzzle samples, and KinGPT model checkpoints for reproducibility.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces KinGPT, a small chess-trained language model, and shows that much of prior success in chess LMs comes from pattern matching rather than deep rule understanding. It further demonstrates that combining a general LLM with an external chess verifier can rival specialized chess fine-tuning more cheaply and flexibly.
AI.motivation: Existing chess-trained language models have reported high puzzle and benchmark scores, with claims that these indicate real understanding of chess rules, expert-level play, and grounded explanations. The authors suspect these claims may overstate true chess competence, with performance driven mainly by pattern recognition over large specialized datasets. They aim to rigorously test these claims, quantify how far a small model can go with simple (position, best-move) supervision, and explore whether a verifier-in-the-loop setup with a general LLM can achieve similar or better results at lower cost and higher flexibility.
AI.method: 1) Train KinGPT, a 25M-parameter character-level LM, solely on (position, best-move) pairs. 2) Evaluate it on challenging mate-in-N puzzle suites and thematic puzzle benchmarks, comparing against much larger specialized models like ChessGPT (3B) and C1-4B (4B). 3) Analyze where the performance comes from, arguing that pattern-matching—not full rule understanding—explains much of the success. 4) Implement LLM-Modulo, a verifier-in-the-loop framework where a general-purpose LLM (RedPajama 3B) proposes moves, and an external chess verifier filters and corrects them. 5) Measure how this framework affects best-move accuracy and move-validity on mate-in-N puzzles, and compare these gains to those obtained by chess-specific fine-tuning. 6) Release code, datasets, puzzle samples, and KinGPT checkpoints for reproducibility.
AI.result: KinGPT, despite having only 25M parameters and using simple (position, best-move) supervision, outperforms much larger specialized LMs: it beats 3B-parameter ChessGPT on a 600-puzzle mate-in-N benchmark and surpasses 4B-parameter C1-4B on a 20-theme puzzle suite. Their analysis supports the view that such benchmark performance is largely a consequence of pattern-matching rather than full chess-rule comprehension. Using the LLM-Modulo verifier-in-the-loop system, they boost RedPajama 3B's best-move accuracy from 1.2% to 21.2% and its move validity from 19.3% to 95.3% on mate-in-N puzzles. These improvements are comparable to those achieved via expensive chess-specific fine-tuning, but at significantly lower cost and with greater modularity.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that high benchmark scores of chess-trained LMs do not necessarily imply deep understanding of chess rules or genuine expert-level play; much of the performance can be attributed to pattern recognition from training data. A small, carefully trained model like KinGPT can rival or beat much larger models on key benchmarks, highlighting the efficiency of focused supervision. Moreover, pairing a general-purpose LLM with an external domain-specific verifier (LLM-Modulo) can close much of the gap to specialized fine-tuned models for well-specified tasks like chess move selection, while being cheaper, more flexible, and easier to generalize. Open-sourcing all resources supports reproducibility and further research on these questions.

title: AutoVecCoder: Teaching LLMs to Generate Explicitly Vectorized Code
summary: Vectorization via Single Instruction, Multiple Data (SIMD) architectures is a cornerstone of high-performance computing. To fully exploit hardware potential, developers often resort to explicit vectorization using intrinsics, as compiler-based auto-vectorization frequently yields suboptimal results due to conservative static analysis. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable proficiency in general code generation, they struggle with explicit vectorization due to the scarcity of high-quality corpora and the strict semantic constraints of low-level hardware instructions. In this paper, we propose AutoVecCoder, a novel framework designed to empower LLMs with the capability of automated explicit vectorization. AutoVecCoder integrates two core components: VecPrompt, an automated data synthesis pipeline to inject domain-specific intrinsic knowledge; and VecRL, a reinforcement learning framework that aligns code generation with execution efficiency. AutoVecCoder-8B trained by this framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on the SSE and AVX subsets of SimdBench and, in some cases, generates implementations surpassing standard -O3 optimizations, effectively overcoming the inherent bottlenecks of traditional automated vectorization.

AI.tldr: A framework, AutoVecCoder, uses LLMs plus data synthesis and reinforcement learning to automatically generate explicit SIMD-vectorized code that can outperform compiler -O3 on benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Explicit SIMD vectorization is essential for high-performance computing, but current approaches rely on manual intrinsics because compiler auto-vectorization is conservative and LLMs are weak at this task due to limited training data and strict hardware semantics. The authors want a system that can reliably generate efficient, correct intrinsic-level vectorized code automatically.
AI.method: They build AutoVecCoder with two main components. VecPrompt is an automated data synthesis pipeline that constructs training prompts and examples injecting domain-specific knowledge about SIMD intrinsics. VecRL is a reinforcement learning framework that fine-tunes the LLM to prefer code with higher execution efficiency, using runtime or performance signals as rewards. They train an 8B-parameter model with these components and evaluate it on SIMD benchmarks (SSE and AVX subsets of SimdBench).
AI.result: The resulting AutoVecCoder-8B achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and performance on vectorization tasks in the SSE and AVX subsets of SimdBench. In some cases, the generated intrinsic-based implementations run faster than code produced by standard compiler -O3 optimizations, indicating the system can surpass traditional auto-vectorization.
AI.conclusion: By coupling synthetic intrinsic-focused training data with performance-aware reinforcement learning, LLMs can learn to perform explicit SIMD vectorization effectively. AutoVecCoder demonstrates that such models can overcome limitations of both compiler auto-vectorization and vanilla LLM code generation, delivering competitive or superior performance on real-world SIMD benchmarks.

title: ECG-WM: A Physiology-Informed ECG World Model for Clinical Intervention Simulation
summary: Electrocardiogram (ECG)-based models have achieved strong performance in diagnostic tasks, yet they remain limited in modeling how cardiac dynamics evolve under external interventions. In particular, existing approaches focus primarily on static prediction and lack mechanisms to capture ECG variations under different pharmacological conditions. In this work, we propose an ECG World Model for action-conditioned predictive simulation of cardiac electrophysiology. Moving beyond disjoint pipelines, our framework features a principled integration of physiological ordinary differential equation (ODE) priors into latent diffusion dynamics via energy regularization. This structural constraint enables the synthesis of physiologically plausible post-intervention ECG trajectories while effectively mitigating generative hallucinations. Building on this simulation process, we introduce an uncertainty-aware evaluation strategy that leverages the stochasticity of diffusion sampling to characterize both the expected clinical risk and its variability, allowing a more reliable comparative assessment of candidate interventions. We evaluate our method across diverse settings, including controlled drug-response scenarios and real-world clinical records. Beyond standard waveform metrics, experimental results demonstrate improved risk calibration and strong alignment with expert-informed treatment preferences. These results establish our approach as a robust foundation for safe and intervention-aware clinical decision support.

AI.tldr: An ECG World Model that simulates how ECG signals change under drug or other interventions using ODE-informed latent diffusion, improving physiological plausibility and risk evaluation.
AI.motivation: Most ECG-based machine learning models perform static diagnosis and cannot reliably forecast how a patient’s cardiac dynamics will respond to pharmacological or other external interventions. Clinicians need tools that not only classify current state but also anticipate post-intervention trajectories and associated risks. Existing generative approaches also suffer from physiologically implausible outputs and lack principled ways to quantify uncertainty for treatment comparison.
AI.method: The paper proposes an action-conditioned ECG World Model that integrates physiological knowledge into a generative latent diffusion framework. Cardiac electrophysiology ODEs are embedded as priors via an energy regularization term that shapes the latent diffusion dynamics, constraining generated ECG trajectories to remain consistent with known physiology. The model predicts ECG sequences conditioned on specified interventions (e.g., drugs) and uses the inherent stochastic sampling of diffusion to quantify predictive uncertainty. An uncertainty-aware evaluation strategy then aggregates multiple sampled trajectories to estimate both expected clinical risk and its variability under different candidate interventions.
AI.result: Across controlled drug-response simulations and real-world clinical ECG records, the proposed method generates post-intervention ECG trajectories that better match physiological expectations and expert knowledge than baselines. Quantitatively, beyond standard waveform similarity metrics, the model shows improved risk calibration and alignment with clinician-informed treatment rankings, and it reduces unrealistic or hallucinatory generations compared to unconstrained generative models.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating ODE-based physiological priors into a latent diffusion ECG World Model yields more realistic, intervention-aware simulations of cardiac dynamics and enables uncertainty-aware risk assessment. This framework provides a stronger, safer basis for clinical decision support tools that must not only diagnose from current ECGs but also reason about future cardiac responses to specific treatments.

title: Predictive Prefetching for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
comment: Accepted by Forty-third International Conference on Machine Learning ICML 2026
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) improves factual grounding in large language models but suffers from substantial latency due to synchronous retrieval. While recent work explores asynchronous retrieval, existing approaches rely on heuristic coordination between retrieval and generation and assume stable information demands during decoding that often break in complex, multi-domain settings. In this paper, we propose an advanced asynchronous retrieval framework that enables predictive prefetching aligned with evolving information needs. The framework explicitly predicts when retrieval should be triggered and what information should be retrieved using three components, a retrieval predictor, a context monitor, and a query generator, by exploiting semantic precursors in generation dynamics that emerge several tokens before uncertainty becomes critical. Experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate up to 43.5% end-to-end latency reduction and 62.4% improvement in time-to-first-token, while maintaining answer quality comparable to synchronous RAG baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an advanced asynchronous retrieval framework for RAG that predicts and prefetches needed information during generation, cutting latency significantly while keeping answer quality on par with synchronous RAG.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG reduces hallucinations but is slow because retrieval is done synchronously and repeatedly blocks generation. Existing asynchronous methods use simple heuristics and assume that the model’s information needs don’t change much during decoding, which fails in complex, multi-domain tasks. There is a need for a principled way to coordinate retrieval with generation that adapts to evolving information demands and reduces latency without hurting quality.
AI.method: The authors design a predictive asynchronous retrieval framework composed of three coordinated components: (1) a retrieval predictor that learns to decide when to trigger retrieval based on generation dynamics; (2) a context monitor that tracks how well the current context satisfies emerging information needs; and (3) a query generator that anticipates what information will be needed next and formulates retrieval queries accordingly. The system leverages semantic precursors—patterns in the model’s hidden states and token predictions that appear several tokens before uncertainty becomes critical—to prefetch relevant documents before they are urgently needed.
AI.result: On multiple benchmarks, the framework achieves up to 43.5% reduction in end-to-end latency and up to 62.4% improvement in time-to-first-token compared with standard synchronous RAG, while preserving answer quality (i.e., no significant degradation relative to strong synchronous RAG baselines).
AI.conclusion: Predictive, learning-based coordination of retrieval and generation can overcome the latency bottlenecks of synchronous RAG without sacrificing factual accuracy. By exploiting early semantic signals in the generation process, asynchronous RAG systems can prefetch relevant information in advance, achieving faster response times and better user experience in complex, multi-domain settings.

title: NeuSymMS: A Hybrid Neuro-Symbolic Memory System for Persistent, Self-Curating LLM Agents
summary: We present NeuSymMS, an adaptive memory system that enables large language model (LLM) agents to learn, remember, and reason about users across sessions via a hybrid neuro-symbolic architecture. NeuSymMS couples neural fact extraction from unstructured dialogue with a CLIPS-based expert system that classifies, deduplicates, and reconciles facts under explicit lifecycle rules. The system represents knowledge as subject-relation-value triples stored in relational database management system, supports user/agents/agent-to-agents scoping, and implements a dual-horizon short-term/long-term memory model with access-based promotion and time-based pruning. NeuSymMS maintains continuity of memory while avoiding context-window bloat and cross-entity contamination. We argue that this architecture offers a practical path to trustworthy, auditable memory for production agentic systems and discuss its novelty relative to log retrieval, summarization, and key-value approaches.

AI.tldr: NeuSymMS is a neuro-symbolic memory system for LLM agents that extracts facts from dialogue, stores them as structured triples, and manages them with explicit rules to provide trustworthy, efficient, cross-session user memory.
AI.motivation: LLM agents currently struggle to maintain reliable, long-term, cross-session user memory without bloating context windows, leaking information between different users or agents, or becoming untrustworthy and opaque. Existing methods like log retrieval, summarization, or simple key-value stores are either hard to audit, prone to redundancy and inconsistency, or vulnerable to cross-entity contamination. The paper is motivated by the need for a practical, production-ready memory architecture that is explicit, auditable, and avoids these pitfalls.
AI.method: The paper introduces NeuSymMS, a hybrid neuro-symbolic architecture. Neural components extract structured facts from unstructured dialogue. These facts are passed into a CLIPS-based expert system that applies explicit lifecycle rules to classify, deduplicate, and reconcile them. The knowledge is represented as subject-relation-value triples in a relational database, supporting scoping for users, agents, and inter-agent sharing. The memory model uses dual horizons: short-term and long-term, with rules for promoting frequently accessed items to long-term storage and pruning outdated or unused items over time.
AI.result: NeuSymMS maintains coherent, cross-session memory for LLM agents while controlling context size and preventing cross-entity information leakage. The system can adaptively store and manage user-related facts, ensuring redundancy is minimized and conflicts are reconciled through rule-based reasoning. It demonstrates that combining neural extraction with symbolic rule systems and structured storage yields a more controllable and auditable memory than purely neural or heuristic approaches.
AI.conclusion: The authors conclude that a neuro-symbolic, rule-governed memory system like NeuSymMS provides a viable path to trustworthy, production-grade memory for LLM-based agents. By structuring facts as triples, managing them via an expert system, and separating short- and long-term horizons with explicit promotion and pruning policies, the architecture avoids context bloat and cross-entity contamination. They argue that this approach is novel and more practical than log retrieval, summarization, or simple key-value memory for building robust agentic systems in real-world applications.

title: Bridging the Gap: Converting Read Text to Conversational Dialogue
comment: 11 pages, 4 figures. Published in ICICC 2025, Springer Lecture Notes in Networks and Systems
summary: In recent advancements within speech processing, converting read speech to conversational speech has gained significant attention. The primary challenge in this domain is maintaining naturalness and intelligibility while minimizing computational overhead for real-time applications. Traditional read speech often lacks the nuanced prosodic variation essential for natural conversational interactions, posing challenges for applications in virtual assistants, customer service, and language learning tools. This paper introduces a novel approach, Prosodic Adjustment with Conversational Context (PACC), aimed at converting read speech into natural conversational speech used in various modern applications. PACC utilizes advanced deep neural networks to analyze and modify prosodic features such as intonation, stress, and rhythm. Unlike conventional methods, our approach uses High-Fidelity Generative Adversarial Networks (HiFi-GAN) for speech synthesis. Our experimental results demonstrate significant improvements in speech conversion, enhancing naturalness and achieving better model accuracy with additional training on speech datasets. This research establishes new benchmarks in speech conversion tasks and Mean Opinion Score (MOS) evaluation for testing model accuracy, and we show that our approach can be successfully extended to other speech conversion applications.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PACC, a deep-learning-based method that converts read speech into more natural conversational speech using prosodic adjustment and HiFi-GAN, achieving better naturalness, accuracy, and MOS benchmarks for speech conversion.
AI.motivation: Read speech lacks the prosodic richness (intonation, stress, rhythm) required for natural conversational interactions, limiting the effectiveness of systems like virtual assistants, customer service bots, and language learning tools. There is a need for methods that can transform read speech into natural-sounding conversational speech while remaining computationally efficient for real-time use.
AI.method: The paper introduces Prosodic Adjustment with Conversational Context (PACC), which uses advanced deep neural networks to analyze and modify prosodic features of read speech (intonation, stress, rhythm). For synthesis, it leverages High-Fidelity Generative Adversarial Networks (HiFi-GAN) instead of conventional vocoders, enabling high-quality and efficient speech generation. The model is trained on speech datasets with additional training to refine conversion quality, and performance is evaluated via standard speech conversion metrics and MOS.
AI.result: Experiments show that PACC substantially improves the naturalness and intelligibility of converted speech while maintaining or improving accuracy compared to previous approaches. The method achieves higher Mean Opinion Scores (MOS) and sets new benchmarks on speech conversion tasks, indicating better perceived quality and effectiveness of the conversion.
AI.conclusion: PACC effectively converts read speech into conversational-sounding speech, improving naturalness and model accuracy and setting new benchmarks in speech conversion and MOS evaluation. The approach is scalable and can be extended to other speech conversion scenarios, making it promising for real-world applications requiring natural conversational speech synthesis.

title: Semantic Reranking at Inference Time for Hard Examples in Rhetorical Role Labeling
summary: Rhetorical Role Labeling (RRL) assigns a functional role to each sentence in a document and is widely used in legal, medical, and scientific domains. While language models (LMs) achieve strong average performance, they remain unreliable on hard examples, where prediction confidence is low. Existing approaches typically handle uncertainty implicitly and treat labels as discrete identifiers, overlooking the semantic information encoded in label names. We introduce RISE, an inference-time semantic reranking framework that leverages label semantics to refine predictions on hard instances. RISE automatically identifies low-confidence predictions and reranks model outputs using contrastively learned label representations, without retraining or modifying the underlying model. Experiments on eight domain-specific RRL datasets with seven LMs, including encoder-based and causal architectures, show an average gain of +9.15 macro-F1 points on hard examples. For explainability, we further propose manual hardness annotations to study difficulty from both model and human perspectives, revealing a moderate agreement with Cohen's kappa = 0.40.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RISE, a semantic reranking framework that improves rhetorical role labeling on hard examples by using label semantics and confidence-based selection at inference time, yielding substantial macro-F1 gains without retraining the base language models.
AI.motivation: Rhetorical Role Labeling is important in legal, medical, and scientific texts, but existing language-model-based systems are unreliable on hard cases where their confidence is low. Current methods treat labels as opaque IDs, ignoring the rich semantic information in label names, and they handle uncertainty only implicitly. There is a need for a method that can better handle low-confidence predictions and exploit label semantics without costly model retraining.
AI.method: The authors introduce RISE, an inference-time framework that first detects low-confidence (hard) instances from an LM’s predictions. For these instances, RISE uses contrastively learned vector representations of the labels (based on their semantics) to rerank the model’s candidate outputs. This process uses label-name embeddings and contrastive learning to align sentence and label representations, and operates entirely at inference time, without modifying or fine-tuning the underlying LMs. The method is evaluated across eight domain-specific RRL datasets and seven different LMs, both encoder-based and causal.
AI.result: Across eight RRL datasets and seven LMs, RISE achieves an average improvement of +9.15 macro-F1 on the subset of hard examples identified by low model confidence, indicating a substantial reliability gain in challenging cases. The approach generalizes across legal, medical, and scientific domains and works with diverse model architectures, confirming its robustness and flexibility.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that incorporating label semantics via contrastively learned representations at inference time significantly improves performance on difficult RRL instances, without retraining base models. The manual hardness annotations further show moderate agreement between human and model-defined difficulty (Cohen’s kappa = 0.40), suggesting that model confidence is a meaningful but imperfect proxy for human-perceived difficulty. RISE offers a practical, explainable way to boost reliability of RRL systems in specialized domains.

title: GraphMind: From Operational Traces to Self-Evolving Workflow Automation
summary: Complex operational workflows coordinating personnel, tools, and information are central to enterprise operations, yet end-to-end automation remains challenging due to extensive requirements for human inputs and the inability to adapt over time. We present GraphMind, an end-to-end system that constructs, executes, and evolves action-centric workflow graphs without human effort. The system operates in three phases. First, a scalable offline pipeline extracts structured workflow graphs from large volumes of human resolution traces, capturing problems, actions, and their causal relationships. Second, an online multi-agent traversal engine navigates the graph to dynamically construct and execute workflows, combining graph-guided retrieval with LLM-driven reasoning at each step. Third, Adaptive Traversal Reinforcement (ATR) reinforces successful traversal paths and decays stale elements. This closed-loop mechanism enables the graph to self-optimize and adapt to shifting operational conditions. GraphMind has been deployed across four production cloud database services for incident investigation. Evaluated on production data, the system substantially outperforms a Trace-RAG baseline in mitigation reach, groundedness, and diagnostic throughput, scoring 4.95/5 in blind expert review. The ATR layer provides further gains across all metrics, demonstrating that workflow graphs can learn and improve from execution-derived feedback.

AI.tldr: GraphMind is an end-to-end system that automatically learns, executes, and continually improves action-centric workflow graphs from human operation traces, enabling adaptive, high-quality incident investigation without human-authored workflows.
AI.motivation: Enterprise operational workflows for tasks like incident investigation are complex, involve many steps, people, and tools, and change over time. Hand-coding or manually maintaining end-to-end automation is difficult because it requires extensive human input, does not adapt well, and often breaks under evolving conditions. The authors want a way to automatically capture, reuse, and improve real-world operational know-how at scale, using historical traces instead of manual workflow engineering.
AI.method: GraphMind consists of three main phases. (1) An offline pipeline processes large volumes of human resolution traces to automatically extract structured, action-centric workflow graphs that encode problems, actions, and causal relationships between them. (2) An online multi-agent traversal engine uses these graphs at run time to dynamically construct and execute workflows for new incidents, combining graph-guided retrieval of relevant actions with LLM-based reasoning at each step. (3) An Adaptive Traversal Reinforcement (ATR) mechanism updates the workflow graph based on execution outcomes, reinforcing successful traversal paths and decaying outdated or less effective nodes and edges, thus closing the loop between execution and graph evolution.
AI.result: The system is deployed in four production cloud database services for incident investigation. On real production data, GraphMind significantly outperforms a Trace-RAG baseline (which likely retrieves and reasons over historical traces without structured workflows) on metrics such as mitigation reach (ability to resolve incidents), groundedness (faithfulness to real procedures and data), and diagnostic throughput (speed or volume of handled incidents). Expert evaluators gave it an average score of 4.95/5 in a blind review. Adding the ATR layer further improves all measured metrics, showing that using execution-derived feedback to adapt the workflow graph yields tangible benefits.
AI.conclusion: Automatically induced, action-centric workflow graphs combined with LLM reasoning and closed-loop reinforcement can provide robust, adaptive, end-to-end automation for complex enterprise operations. GraphMind shows that operational workflows can be learned from historical traces, effectively executed by an online traversal engine, and continuously improved using reinforcement from real executions, leading to better performance than trace-based retrieval systems and enabling practical deployment in production cloud services.

title: PROTEA: Offline Evaluation and Iterative Refinement for Multi-Agent LLM Workflows
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures, 1 table. To appear in Proceedings of ACL 2026 System Demonstrations
summary: Multi-agent LLM workflows -- systems composed of multiple role-specific LLM calls -- often outperform single-prompt baselines, but they remain difficult to debug and refine. Failures can originate from subtle errors in intermediate outputs that propagate to downstream nodes, requiring developers to inspect long traces and infer which agent to modify. We present PROTEA, a unified interface for offline, test-driven improvement of multi-agent workflows. PROTEA executes a workflow, scores intermediate node outputs with configurable rubrics, and overlays per-node states and rationales on the workflow graph to localize likely bottlenecks. To support complex systems where final-answer references are the primary supervision, PROTEA performs backward node evaluation: it generates candidate node-level expectations from final-answer references and graph context, then compares them with observed node outputs. For selected nodes, PROTEA presents targeted prompt revisions as editable before/after comparisons, then automatically reruns and re-evaluates the workflow to show output changes and score trajectories within the same interface. In two production-adjacent workflows, PROTEA improved document-inspection accuracy from 64.3% to 83.9% and recommendation Hit@5 from 0.30 to 0.38. In a formative study with six experienced LLM developers, participants valued graph-level localization, per-node rationales, and editable before/after prompt revisions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PROTEA, a system for debugging and improving multi-agent LLM workflows by localizing problematic nodes and supporting targeted prompt edits with test-driven evaluation.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM workflows can surpass single-prompt setups but are hard to debug because failures often stem from subtle intermediate errors that propagate through the workflow graph, forcing developers to manually inspect long traces and guess which prompts to change.
AI.method: The authors build PROTEA, a unified interface that: (1) executes a workflow and scores intermediate node outputs with configurable rubrics; (2) visualizes node states, scores, and rationales on the workflow graph to localize bottlenecks; (3) supports backward node evaluation by inferring node-level expectations from final-answer references and graph context when only end labels are available; and (4) suggests targeted prompt revisions for selected nodes as editable before/after diffs, then automatically reruns and re-evaluates the workflow to show how scores evolve.
AI.result: On two production-adjacent workflows, PROTEA usage raised document-inspection accuracy from 64.3% to 83.9% and recommendation Hit@5 from 0.30 to 0.38. In a user study with six experienced LLM developers, participants reported that graph-level localization, per-node rationales, and editable prompt diffs were particularly useful.
AI.conclusion: PROTEA makes multi-agent LLM workflows more debuggable and optimizable by coupling graph-based visualization, node-level scoring (including backward evaluation from final labels), and iterative prompt editing within a single test-driven interface, yielding measurable accuracy gains and positive developer feedback.

title: Prediction of Challenging Behaviors Associated with Profound Autism in a Classroom Setting Using Wearable Sensors
summary: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) is characterized by challenges with social interaction and communication and by restricted or repetitive patterns of thought and behavior, with significant variability in presentation. Approximately a quarter of children with ASD are classified as having profound autism, who often exhibit challenging behaviors, such as self-injurious behavior, aggression, elopement, or pica, that pose serious safety risks and disrupt learning in educational settings. Prior work has applied wearable sensors and machine learning to detect challenging behaviors, but has been largely confined to controlled laboratory environments. This work demonstrates that predicting challenging behavior episodes is feasible in a real-world special education classroom. We collected approximately 110.7 hours of labeled multimodal wearable data comprising accelerometry, electrodermal activity (EDA), and skin temperature from 9 children and young adults aged 10 to 21 years across standard classroom sessions. We fine-tuned state-of-the-art foundation models for multimodal wearable time-series analysis and show that challenging behavior episodes can be predicted up to 10 minutes in advance with an AUC-ROC of 0.78. These results establish a concrete foundation for developing proactive in-class intervention systems that enable teachers to minimize the safety risks of challenging behaviors in special education classrooms

AI.tldr: The paper shows that wearable-sensor data and fine-tuned multimodal foundation models can predict challenging behavior episodes in autistic students up to 10 minutes in advance in real classrooms, achieving an AUC-ROC of 0.78.
AI.motivation: Challenging behaviors in students with profound autism—such as self-injury, aggression, elopement, and pica—create serious safety risks and interfere with learning, yet existing sensor-based detection methods have mostly been limited to controlled lab settings and focus on detection rather than advance prediction. There is a need for practical, real-world systems that can proactively alert educators before episodes occur so they can intervene earlier and reduce harm.
AI.method: The authors collected 110.7 hours of labeled multimodal wearable data (accelerometry, electrodermal activity, and skin temperature) from 9 students aged 10–21 in a special education classroom during regular sessions. They then fine-tuned state-of-the-art foundation models for multimodal wearable time-series analysis to predict whether a challenging behavior episode would occur within a future time window (up to 10 minutes). Model performance was evaluated using AUC-ROC on this real-world dataset.
AI.result: The fine-tuned multimodal foundation models achieved an AUC-ROC of 0.78 for predicting challenging behavior episodes up to 10 minutes before they occurred, demonstrating that meaningful advance prediction is possible in a noisy, real-world classroom environment using wearable sensor data.
AI.conclusion: The study provides proof-of-concept that multimodal wearable sensing combined with fine-tuned foundation models can predict challenging behaviors in students with profound autism in real classrooms, not just labs, with promising accuracy. This establishes a foundation for building proactive, in-class intervention systems that could alert teachers in advance, potentially reducing safety risks and improving learning conditions in special education settings.

title: PPAI: Enabling Personalized LLM Agent Interoperability for Collaborative Edge Intelligence
summary: Deploying large language model (LLM) on edge device enables personalized LLM agents for various users. The growing availability of diverse personalized agents presents a unique opportunity for peer-to-peer (P2P) collaboration, wherein each user can delegate tasks beyond the local agent's expertise to remote agents more suited for the specific query. This paper introduces PPAI, the first personalized LLM agent interoperability system, which enables users to collaborate with each other based on agent specialization. However, the ever-changing pool of agents and their interchangeable capacity introduce new challenges when it comes to matching queries to agents and balancing loads, compared with existing P2P systems. Therefore, we propose a scalable query-agent pair scoring mechanism based on prototypes to identify suitable agents within a P2P network with churn. Moreover, we propose a multi-agent interoperability Bayesian game to balance local demand and global efficiency, when changes in remote agent load occur too quickly to be observed. Finally, we implement a prototype of PPAI and demonstrate that it substantially broadens the range of tasks that could be carried out while maintaining load balance. On average, it achieves an accuracy improvement of up to 7.96% across multiple tasks, while reducing latency by 16.34% compared to the baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PPAI, a system that lets many personalized LLM agents on edge devices collaborate peer‑to‑peer, so users can route each query to the most suitable remote agent while keeping the system balanced and responsive.
AI.motivation: As LLMs move onto edge devices, many users will own personalized agents tuned to their preferences and data. These agents have complementary strengths, creating an opportunity for peer‑to‑peer sharing: a user could send queries outside their agent’s expertise to another user’s better‑suited agent. Existing P2P systems, however, do not handle the highly dynamic, interchangeable, and task‑specialized nature of such LLM agent pools, especially with respect to matching queries to agents and managing load under churn.
AI.method: The authors design PPAI, a personalized LLM agent interoperability system. They introduce: (1) a scalable query–agent scoring mechanism that uses prototype representations to quickly find suitable agents in a P2P network with frequent node churn, and (2) a multi‑agent interoperability Bayesian game framework that decides when to handle tasks locally vs. delegate them, trading off local demand against global efficiency even when precise, up‑to‑date information about remote agents’ load is unavailable.
AI.result: They implement a prototype of PPAI and evaluate it on multiple tasks. Compared with a baseline system, PPAI increases task accuracy by up to 7.96% on average while also reducing latency by 16.34%, showing that it can expand the range of tasks the network can handle without overloading particular agents.
AI.conclusion: PPAI demonstrates that large numbers of edge‑hosted, personalized LLM agents can interoperate effectively in a P2P manner. With prototype‑based query–agent matching and a Bayesian game‑theoretic load‑balancing mechanism, the system can route tasks to specialized agents, handle dynamic participation (churn), and improve both accuracy and latency versus baseline approaches. This validates PPAI as a viable solution for scalable, collaborative personalized LLM ecosystems.

title: Episodic-Semantic Memory Architecture for Long-Horizon Scientific Agents
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) evolve into persistent scientific collaborators, context window saturation has emerged as a critical bottleneck. Scientific workflows involving iterative data analysis and hypothesis refinement rapidly saturate even extended contexts with dense technical content, while monolithic approaches suffer from quadratic cost scaling and cognitive degradation. We evaluate a Dual Process Memory Architecture that decouples immediate episodic needs (constant 10-message window) from long-term consolidated knowledge (growing at approximately 3 tokens/message). Unlike prior social agent memory systems, our domain-specific consolidation addresses contradictory parameter evolution, multi-hop reasoning across experimental phases, and precise technical fact retention. Through large-scale evaluation spanning 15,000 messages with cross-model validation across six LLMs from three families (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google), totaling 1,440 queries, we establish three key findings. First, while full-context models fail at 10,000 messages due to context overflow, our system maintains 70-85% accuracy with 1-2 second latency using 62% fewer tokens (45,434 vs 120,000+ limit). Second, cross-model validation reveals architecture-level trade-offs independent of specific LLMs: Dual Process excels at numeric/temporal queries (65-90% accuracy) while RAG excels at historical retrieval (60-85%), suggesting complementary deployment strategies. Third, we identify a "Sim-to-Real" gap where synthetic tests maintain constant memory but realistic workflows exhibit linear growth (about 3 tokens/message), with consolidation quality emerging as the primary scalability bottleneck. The architecture successfully manages profiles with 14,000+ scientific facts (125k tokens), demonstrating that domain-specific memory consolidation enables sustained operation beyond full-context limits.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and evaluates a Dual Process Memory Architecture for LLM-based scientific agents that separates short-term episodic context from long-term consolidated memory, overcoming context window saturation and scaling issues while maintaining high accuracy and low latency over very long interactions.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are used as persistent scientific collaborators, their finite context windows quickly saturate in iterative scientific workflows that involve long, technical interactions. Existing approaches that rely on feeding full histories into context are expensive (quadratic scaling), degrade model performance, and cannot handle very long conversations. Prior memory systems for social agents are not tailored to scientific reasoning challenges like evolving experimental parameters, multi-step reasoning over phases, and precise fact retention. There is a need for a scalable memory system that allows LLM agents to operate over tens of thousands of messages without overflowing context while preserving accuracy on complex scientific tasks.
AI.method: The authors design a Dual Process Memory Architecture that separates memory into: (1) a fixed-size episodic buffer (10 most recent messages) for immediate context, and (2) a long-term consolidated memory that grows slowly (≈3 tokens per message) via domain-specific consolidation rules tailored to scientific workflows. They compare this architecture against full-context and RAG-based baselines. They conduct a large-scale evaluation over 15,000-message trajectories with 1,440 queries, using six LLMs from three provider families (OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) to perform cross-model validation. They examine performance on different query types (numeric/temporal, historical retrieval, etc.) and measure accuracy, latency, token usage, and scalability as memory grows to profiles of 14,000+ scientific facts (≈125k tokens).
AI.result: 1) Full-context models fail once histories reach around 10,000 messages because of context overflow and cost, whereas the Dual Process system sustains 70–85% query accuracy with 1–2 second latency while using 62% fewer tokens (45,434 vs. 120,000+). 2) Cross-model evaluation shows that the advantages of the Dual Process vs. RAG are architectural, not model-specific: Dual Process outperforms on numeric and temporal reasoning queries (65–90% accuracy), whereas RAG is better on historical retrieval (60–85%), indicating complementary use-cases. 3) They discover a sim-to-real gap: synthetic benchmarks assume fixed memory size, but real workflows cause linear memory growth (~3 tokens/message), making consolidation quality the key bottleneck. The system successfully handles long-term profiles with over 14,000 scientific facts (≈125k tokens) without hitting full-context limits.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling short-term context from long-term, domain-aware consolidated memory enables LLM-based scientific agents to operate reliably beyond standard context window limits. The Dual Process Memory Architecture offers scalable performance with reduced token usage, particularly enhancing numeric and temporal reasoning in long workflows. However, as realistic usage leads to ever-growing memories, the effectiveness of consolidation becomes the main factor governing scalability, and hybrid strategies with RAG may be optimal for different query types.

title: KVDrive: A Holistic Multi-Tier KV Cache Management System for Long-Context LLM Inference
summary: Supporting long-context LLMs is challenging due to the substantial memory demands of the key-value (KV) cache. Existing offloading systems store the full cache in host memory and selectively fetch critical entries during decoding, but this strategy quickly hits a ceiling: sparsity cannot be pushed further without degrading accuracy. As a result, when context length and batch size grow, the volume of KV transfers rises sharply and becomes the dominant source of decoding latency. We present KVDrive, a holistic multi-tier KV cache management system spanning GPU memory, host DRAM, and SSD. Unlike prior work that pursues greater sparsity through algorithmic refinements, KVDrive tackles the problem from a systems perspective - jointly orchestrating cache placement, pipeline scheduling, and cross-tier coordination to sustain high-throughput inference under tight GPU budgets. KVDrive advances three fundamental capabilities: it adapts cache management to attention behavior to maximize reuse and minimize redundant data movement; it restructures the decoding pipeline to overlap I/O- and CPU/GPU compute-bound stages, eliminating stalls across heterogeneous resources; and it harmonizes data movement across memory tiers to unlock scalable long-context inference far beyond GPU and DRAM limits. We have implemented a fully functional prototype of KVDrive and evaluated it on long-context benchmarks with popular LLMs. The system achieves up to 1.74x higher throughput compared to state-of-the-art works while preserving accuracy.

AI.tldr: KVDrive is a multi-tier KV cache management system for long-context LLMs that coordinates GPU, DRAM, and SSD to reduce KV transfer overhead and increase throughput without hurting accuracy.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLM inference requires large KV caches that do not fit in limited GPU memory. Existing offloading methods keep KV on host memory and selectively fetch entries, but they quickly hit a performance ceiling: as context length and batch size increase, KV transfers dominate latency and further sparsification harms accuracy. A new system-level approach is needed to scale long-context inference under tight GPU memory budgets.
AI.method: KVDrive proposes a holistic, multi-tier cache management system that spans GPU memory, host DRAM, and SSD. It jointly optimizes cache placement, decoding pipeline scheduling, and cross-tier coordination. Specifically, it adapts cache management policies to observed attention patterns to maximize KV reuse and avoid redundant movement; it restructures the decoding pipeline so I/O and CPU/GPU compute stages overlap, reducing stalls; and it coordinates data movement across all memory tiers to efficiently support very long contexts beyond GPU and DRAM capacity.
AI.result: A fully functional KVDrive prototype is implemented and evaluated on long-context benchmarks with popular LLMs. Experiments show that KVDrive achieves up to 1.74× higher throughput than state-of-the-art KV offloading systems while maintaining model accuracy.
AI.conclusion: By treating KV cache handling as a systems and pipeline orchestration problem across GPU, DRAM, and SSD, KVDrive enables scalable, high-throughput long-context LLM inference beyond the limits of GPU and main memory. It improves throughput significantly over prior methods without sacrificing accuracy, demonstrating the value of adaptive, multi-tier KV cache management and pipeline restructuring.

title: WebGameBench: Requirement-to-Application Evaluation for Coding Agents via Browser-Native Games
summary: Coding agents are increasingly used as application builders, yet many evaluations still focus on source code, repository-level tests, or intermediate traces rather than the delivered application. We introduce WebGameBench, a requirement-to-application benchmark that evaluates whether coding agents can turn a frozen Structured WebGame Specification into a browser-accessible game. Browser-native games provide a compact but behavior-dense testbed: even simple games require coordinated input handling, spatial mapping, rule execution, state transitions, terminal conditions, restart behavior, and visible feedback. In WebGameBench, each generated artifact is built, served, and exposed as a browser-accessible application under a unified deployment protocol. A runtime evaluator then interacts with the delivered game in a real browser and assigns a three-way label: EXCELLENT, USABLE, or UNUSABLE. On a human-reviewed subset, the runtime label is broadly aligned with human gameplay review under the Usable-rate criterion. Across 111 tasks, 12 coding agents, and 14 evaluation configurations, WebGameBench separates current systems: the best configuration reaches a 76.9% usable rate but only a 20.2% excellent rate. This gap shows that crossing the minimum playable-delivery threshold is still far from complete requirement satisfaction. To our knowledge, WebGameBench is the first requirement-to-application benchmark for browser-native game delivery that validates delivered-application runtime labels against independent human gameplay review under the Usable-rate criterion.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WebGameBench, a benchmark that evaluates coding agents on their ability to turn structured game specifications into fully working browser games, assessed by runtime interaction rather than just code or tests.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of coding agents largely focus on static artifacts (source code, repository tests, traces) instead of whether the final delivered application actually works as intended. There is a need for an end-to-end, requirement-to-application benchmark that measures agents on delivering usable, correct browser applications and aligns automated evaluation with human perceptions of usability.
AI.method: The authors design WebGameBench, where each task is a ‘frozen’ Structured WebGame Specification describing a browser-native game. Coding agents must generate an artifact that is built and served as a browser-accessible game under a unified deployment protocol. A runtime evaluator then interacts automatically with the game in a real browser and assigns one of three labels—EXCELLENT, USABLE, UNUSABLE—based on observed behavior. The authors also compare these runtime labels with human gameplay reviews on a subset of tasks to validate the evaluation signal. They test 12 coding agents across 111 tasks and 14 evaluation configurations.
AI.result: The automatic runtime labels from WebGameBench broadly agree with human gameplay judgments under the Usable-rate criterion. Benchmark results show a clear spread in performance across agents and configurations: the best-performing configuration achieves a 76.9% rate of usable games but only a 20.2% rate of excellent games, indicating that while many agents can deliver minimally playable games, far fewer can meet all specified requirements with high quality.
AI.conclusion: WebGameBench effectively discriminates between coding agents on an end-to-end requirement-to-application task for browser-native games, and its runtime labels are validated against human gameplay assessments for usability. The observed gap between usable and excellent games demonstrates that achieving minimal playability is not enough for full requirement satisfaction, highlighting remaining challenges for coding agents. The benchmark is claimed to be the first of its kind for requirement-to-application evaluation in browser-native game delivery with validated runtime labels.

title: A Data-Efficient Path to Multilingual LLMs: Language Expansion via Post-training PARAM$Δ$ Integration into Upcycled MoE
summary: Expanding Large Language Models~(LLMs) to new languages is a costly endeavor, demanding extensive Continued Pre-Training~(CPT) and data-intensive alignment. While recent data-free merging techniques attempt to bypass alignment by fusing a multilingual CPT-enhanced model with its instruct counterpart, they are plagued by a critical trade-off: mitigating parameter conflicts to preserve original abilities inevitably dilutes new language acquisition, and vice-versa. To resolve this conflict, we introduce \method, which upcycles a dense model into a Mixture-of-Experts~(MoE) architecture, allocating different experts to different languages. Alignment ability is then transferred by grafting a MoE-expanded parameter delta~($Δ_{\text{post}}$) to the CPT-enhanced base model, bypassing the complex alignment phase. Experiments demonstrate \method's superiority even against baselines with similar FLOPs or number of parameters; it improves performance on expanded languages while effectively preserving original capabilities. We further show our approach is highly applicable across different models and Post-training deltas.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to expand LLMs to new languages by converting a dense model into a Mixture-of-Experts and transferring alignment via a MoE-style parameter delta, avoiding costly data-heavy alignment and outperforming comparable baselines while preserving original capabilities.
AI.motivation: Adapting large language models to new languages typically requires expensive continued pre-training and large-scale alignment data. Existing data-free merging methods that combine a multilingual CPT-enhanced model with an instruction-tuned model suffer from a trade-off: resolving parameter conflicts to maintain original abilities weakens new-language performance, whereas emphasizing new-language learning harms original skills. The authors aim to break this trade-off and make language expansion cheaper and more robust.
AI.method: The authors introduce a method (\method) that transforms a standard dense LLM into a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture, where different experts are allocated to different languages. Instead of jointly re-aligning the expanded model with data, they transfer alignment capabilities by computing a MoE-expanded post-training parameter delta (Δ_post) from an aligned model and grafting this delta onto a multilingual CPT-enhanced base model. This avoids direct, data-intensive alignment while structurally separating parameters responsible for different languages.
AI.result: Across experiments, the proposed method outperforms baselines that have similar FLOPs and parameter counts. It achieves better performance on newly added languages while maintaining the original model’s capabilities more effectively than data-free merging approaches that suffer from parameter conflicts.
AI.conclusion: Converting a dense LLM into a language-specialized MoE and grafting a MoE-style post-training delta provides an effective, data-efficient way to expand models to new languages. The approach alleviates the performance trade-off between new and original languages, generalizes across model architectures and different post-training deltas, and offers a practical alternative to costly, data-heavy alignment procedures.

title: Causal Intervention-Based Memory Selection for Long-Horizon LLM Agents
summary: Long-horizon LLM agents rely on persistent memory to support interactions across sessions, yet existing memory systems often retrieve context using semantic similarity or broad history inclusion, treating retrieved memories as uniformly useful. This assumption is fragile because memories may be topically related while remaining irrelevant, stale, or misleading. We propose Causal Memory Intervention (CMI), a causal memory-selection technique that estimates how candidate memories affect the model's answer under controlled interventions, selecting memories that improve task performance while suppressing unstable, irrelevant, or harmful ones. To evaluate this setting, we introduce Causal-LoCoMo, a causally annotated benchmark derived from long conversational data, where each example contains a user request, a structured memory bank, useful memories, irrelevant distractors, and synthetic harmful memories. We compare CMI against vector, graph, reflection, summary, full-history, and no-memory baselines. Results show that CMI achieves a stronger balance between answer quality and robustness to misleading memory, suggesting that reliable long-term memory requires selecting context based on causal usefulness rather than relevance alone. The full framework, benchmark construction code, and experimental pipeline are available at https://github.com/Saksham4796/causal-memory-intervention.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Causal Memory Intervention (CMI), a method for selecting long-term memories for LLM agents based on their causal impact on answers rather than mere semantic relevance, and provides a new benchmark (Causal-LoCoMo) to evaluate this.
AI.motivation: Existing long-horizon LLM agents depend on persistent memory across sessions, but current memory retrieval methods rely mostly on semantic similarity or including large swaths of history. These approaches assume all retrieved memories are uniformly helpful, which is fragile in practice because topically similar memories can be irrelevant, outdated, or actively misleading. There is a need for a principled way to choose which memories to use that accounts for their actual effect on model behavior and robustness, especially in the presence of distractors and harmful content.
AI.method: The authors propose Causal Memory Intervention (CMI), a causal memory selection mechanism. CMI estimates the effect of each candidate memory on the model’s final answer by performing controlled interventions: it measures how the answer changes when a memory is included or excluded. Using these estimated causal effects, CMI selects memories that improve task performance and suppresses those that introduce instability, irrelevance, or harm. To support evaluation, they construct Causal-LoCoMo, a benchmark built from long conversational data with explicit causal annotations. Each instance contains a user request, a structured memory bank, labeled useful memories, irrelevant distractors, and synthetic harmful memories, enabling systematic testing of robustness and usefulness.
AI.result: Across experiments on the Causal-LoCoMo benchmark, CMI is compared against multiple baselines, including vector-based retrieval, graph-based memory, reflection-based memory, summarized memory, full-history inclusion, and no-memory conditions. The results indicate that CMI better balances answer quality with robustness to misleading or harmful memory: it maintains or improves task performance while being significantly less affected by distractor and harmful memories compared to the baselines.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that effective long-term memory for LLM agents should be based on the causal usefulness of memories—how they change the model’s outputs—rather than on semantic relevance alone. CMI demonstrates that causal memory selection can yield more reliable and robust behavior in long-horizon interactions. The authors release their framework, benchmark construction code, and experimental pipeline to facilitate further research on causal approaches to memory in LLM agents.

title: How Loud Rumbles Hit Newsstands: A Data Analysis of Coverage and Spatial Bias in German News about Landslides Around the World
summary: Landslides often hit newsstands due to their destructive and potentially fatal effects. News are a valuable source of information for creating or enriching disaster databases and for expediting media-based studies of the dynamics of media attention. To accomplish that, news datasets must be filtered, geolocated and validated. This paper focuses on how landslides around the world are reported in German newspapers. We analyse almost 60k news articles about 5.5k news events in a 25-year period, compare it with external measures of countries' susceptibility to landslides and provide insights, e.g.~the overreporting of Southern and Western Europe, to foment further studies on inequalities in media attention to international disasters.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how landslides worldwide are reported in German newspapers, using a large news dataset to understand reporting patterns and inequalities in media attention.
AI.motivation: Landslides cause significant damage and casualties, and news media are an important source of information for disaster databases and for studying media attention dynamics. However, news data must be carefully filtered, geolocated, and validated, and there is limited understanding of how landslide events are unevenly reported across countries in German media.
