AI.method: The authors compile and process nearly 60,000 news articles over 25 years, clustering them into about 5,500 distinct landslide-related news events. They then compare the geographical distribution of reported events with external indicators of countries' susceptibility to landslides, and perform quantitative analyses of spatial reporting biases in German newspapers.
AI.result: The study finds systematic mismatches between where landslides occur (as indicated by susceptibility measures) and where they are reported in German newspapers, notably an overrepresentation of events in Southern and Western Europe relative to their hazard levels. The results quantify biases and inequalities in international disaster coverage.
AI.conclusion: German newspaper coverage of landslides does not proportionally reflect global landslide susceptibility; instead, it overreports some regions (especially Southern and Western Europe) and likely underreports others. These findings highlight geographically biased media attention and provide a basis and dataset for future research on inequalities in disaster reporting and on improving media-based disaster databases.

title: SAPO: Step-Aligned Policy Optimization for Reasoning-Based Generative Recommendation
summary: Generative recommendation treats next-item prediction as autoregressive item-identifier generation. Specifically, items are encoded as semantic identifiers (SIDs), which are short coarse-to-fine token sequences whose early tokens capture broad semantics and later tokens refine them. Recent work augments this paradigm with reasoning traces and optimizes them via reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards, typically outcome-reward algorithm with exact-match feedback on the generated SID. However, in large-catalog recommendation, exact-match feedback on the generated SID only reports whether the final item is correct; when a generated SID mismatches, outcome-reward cannot identify which SID-token prediction caused the mismatch and may penalize matched SID-token positions together with the mismatched position. We identify that the natural unit of credit assignment in this setting is a single reasoning step (one thinking block paired with one SID token). We instantiate this idea in SAPO (Step-Aligned Policy Optimization): rather than broadcasting one advantage to the whole response, SAPO computes a separate group-relative advantage for each reasoning step and applies it only to the corresponding thinking block and SID token. Across three real-world recommendation datasets, SAPO stabilizes reinforcement-learning training and consistently improves over existing generative recommendation baselines, with the largest gains where sparse exact-match feedback makes reasoning-step credit assignment important. Our results suggest that reinforcement-learning objectives for structured generation should mirror the decoder's own decomposition of the output.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SAPO, a reinforcement learning method for generative recommendation that assigns credit at the level of individual reasoning steps instead of the whole generated identifier, improving training stability and recommendation accuracy.
AI.motivation: In generative recommendation, items are represented as semantic identifiers (SIDs) and predicted autoregressively. Existing RL methods use outcome-based rewards with exact-match feedback on the final SID, which only indicates whether the overall item is correct. This coarse feedback cannot localize which specific SID token or reasoning step led to an error, and may wrongly penalize correctly predicted tokens. The authors are motivated to design a finer-grained credit assignment mechanism aligned with the structure of the generation process.
AI.method: The authors introduce SAPO (Step-Aligned Policy Optimization), which aligns reinforcement learning credit assignment with individual reasoning steps. Each reasoning step consists of a thinking block and a corresponding SID token. Instead of broadcasting a single scalar advantage to the entire generated sequence, SAPO computes a separate group-relative advantage for each reasoning step. That per-step advantage is then used to update only the parameters associated with that thinking block and token prediction. This effectively performs structured, step-level policy optimization for generative recommenders that use reasoning traces and semantic identifiers.
AI.result: On three real-world recommendation datasets, SAPO yields more stable reinforcement learning training dynamics and consistently outperforms existing generative recommendation baselines that rely on standard outcome-reward algorithms with exact-match feedback. The performance gains are especially pronounced in large-catalog, sparse-feedback settings, where correctly attributing credit at the reasoning-step level is most critical.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that, for structured generation problems like generative recommendation with semantic identifiers and reasoning traces, reinforcement learning objectives should be decomposed in a way that matches the decoder’s own step-wise structure. Step-aligned credit assignment, as implemented in SAPO, leads to better learning signals, more stable optimization, and improved recommendation performance compared with traditional sequence-level outcome-reward approaches.

title: How Good LLMs Are at Answering Bangla Medical Visual Questions? Dataset and Benchmarking
comment: 14 pages, 7 figures, 5 tables, Proceedings of The Second AAAI Bridge Program on AI for Medicine and Healthcare, PMLR 317:1-14, 2026
summary: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) and Large Vision Language Models (LVLMs) have enabled general-purpose systems to demonstrate promising capabilities in complex reasoning tasks, including those in the medical domain. Medical Visual Question Answering (MedVQA) has particularly benefited from these developments. However, despite Bangla being one of the most widely spoken languages globally, there exists no established MedVQA benchmark for it. To address this gap, we introduce BanglaMedVQA, a dataset comprising clinically validated image-question-answer pairs, along with a comprehensive evaluation of current foundation models on this resource. Consistent with prior findings that report low performance of current models on English MedVQA benchmarks, our analysis reveals that Bangla performance is substantially lower, reflecting the challenges inherent to low-resource languages. Even top-performing models such as Gemini and GPT-4.1 mini fail to accurately answer specialized diagnostic questions, indicating severe limitations in fine-grained medical reasoning. Although certain open-source models, such as Gemma-3, occasionally outperform these models in general categories, they too struggle with clinically complex questions, underscoring the urgent need for top-notch evaluation method.

AI.tldr: Introduces BanglaMedVQA, the first clinically validated Bangla Medical Visual Question Answering benchmark, and shows that current LLMs/LVLMs perform poorly on it, especially for fine-grained diagnostic reasoning.
AI.motivation: While LLMs and LVLMs have advanced medical reasoning and MedVQA in high-resource languages like English, there is no equivalent benchmark for Bangla, despite it being a widely spoken language. This lack of resources prevents fair evaluation and progress on medical multimodal understanding in Bangla and obscures how current models handle low-resource clinical reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Construct a MedVQA dataset in Bangla (BanglaMedVQA) composed of clinically validated image–question–answer triplets. Then systematically evaluate a range of state-of-the-art foundation models—both proprietary (e.g., Gemini, GPT-4.1 mini) and open-source (e.g., Gemma-3)—on this benchmark, analyzing their performance across different question categories, including general and specialized diagnostic questions.
AI.result: Across the evaluated models, performance on BanglaMedVQA is substantially lower than on English MedVQA benchmarks, with especially poor results on specialized diagnostic questions. Even leading models like Gemini and GPT-4.1 mini fail to give accurate answers for fine-grained medical reasoning tasks, and open-source models such as Gemma-3 only occasionally do better on general questions while still struggling with clinically complex ones.
AI.conclusion: BanglaMedVQA exposes significant limitations of current LLMs and LVLMs in Bangla medical visual reasoning, especially for fine-grained diagnostics in a low-resource language setting. The findings highlight both the linguistic and clinical complexity barriers and underscore the urgent need for improved models and rigorous evaluation methods tailored to low-resource medical multimodal tasks.

title: Multimodal Cultural Heritage Knowledge Graph Extension with Language and Vision Models
summary: The preservation and interpretation of cultural heritage increasingly rely on digital technologies, among which Knowledge Graphs (KGs) stand out for their ability to structure vast amounts of data. However, the construction and expansion of these KGs often face challenges due to the diverse and complex nature of cultural heritage information. In this paper, we propose a novel approach for extending KG resources in the domain of cultural heritage, which we applied to French data. First, we introduce a new knowledge graph in the domain of French cultural heritage, WJoconde, which is distinguished by its multimodality as it integrates both textual and image information of the entities. We further introduce three variants of WJoconde to facilitate downstream research, such as Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC). We also built a comprehensive benchmark for KGC methods on our dataset. Second, we propose a new framework for extending cultural heritage KGs using multi-modal approaches leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs), which includes automated data extraction from unstructured resources combined with a special validation pipeline for grounding the output of both models, to further extend WJoconde. Our results show that by integrating the rich text and image information in cultural heritage data, we can efficiently enhance KGs with high reliability. We open-source all code and benchmark datasets with text and images, as well as the original data with an interactive access point

AI.tldr: They build and extend a multimodal French cultural heritage knowledge graph (WJoconde) and a benchmark for knowledge graph completion, using LLMs and vision-language models for automatic KG extension with validation.
AI.motivation: Digital preservation and interpretation of cultural heritage need structured representations of complex, heterogeneous data. Existing cultural heritage knowledge graphs are hard to construct and expand because information is scattered across unstructured text and images, and benchmarks for multimodal knowledge graph completion in this domain are scarce.
AI.method: 1) Construct WJoconde, a multimodal knowledge graph for French cultural heritage that links entities to both textual descriptions and images, and derive three task-oriented variants suitable for downstream research like knowledge graph completion. 2) Build a benchmark suite for evaluating KGC methods on WJoconde. 3) Design a multimodal KG extension framework that uses large language models and vision-language models to automatically extract candidate facts from unstructured resources (text and images) and then validate/ground these candidates via a dedicated pipeline before inserting them into the KG.
AI.result: They successfully instantiated WJoconde and its three variants with linked text and image data, and created a comprehensive KGC benchmark on top of it. Their multimodal LLM+VLM-based extension framework was able to add new, reliable triples to the KG, showing that leveraging both textual and visual cultural heritage information improves the efficiency and trustworthiness of KG expansion.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal knowledge graphs like WJoconde effectively capture the richness of cultural heritage data when they integrate both text and images. Combining large language models and vision-language models in a controlled extraction-and-validation pipeline enables efficient and reliable extension of such KGs. The released datasets, code, and benchmarks provide a reusable resource to advance research in multimodal knowledge graph completion and digital cultural heritage applications.

title: iPOE: Interpretable Prompt Optimization via Explanations
summary: Prompt optimization has often been framed as a discrete search problem to find high-performing and robust instructions for an LLM. However, the search result might not make it transparent why and where specific prompt changes lead to performance gains. This is in contrast to how humans are instructed for annotation tasks. Here, researchers carefully design annotation guidelines, leading to enhanced annotation consistency. Our paper aims at joining these two approaches and introduces iPOE, a novel interpretable prompt optimization strategy via explanations. We guide the prompt optimization process by automatically created guidelines from explanations of annotation decisions (either automatically generated or from humans). This set of guidelines is furthermore optimized by as series of operations, including removing, adding, shuffling, and merging. The resulting prompt includes guidelines that instruct the annotation, making the decision process of the LLM and the optimization transparent. It therefore supports also laypeople in the area of prompt optimization, particularly in challenging domains requiring expertise. In our experiments on four datasets, we find that iPOE can improves over prompts without guidelines and with random selected guidelines by up to $31\%$ and $35\%$, respectively. Moreover, LLM explanations can replace human explanations in the proposed method.

AI.tldr: iPOE is an interpretable prompt optimization method that turns explanation-based guidelines into structured prompt improvements, boosting LLM performance significantly while keeping changes understandable.
AI.motivation: Existing prompt optimization treats prompts as opaque strings searched over a discrete space, which often yields high-performing prompts but does not explain why specific edits help. This contrasts with human annotation practice, where explicit, well-crafted guidelines improve consistency and transparency. The authors want a method that combines the effectiveness of automatic prompt optimization with the interpretability and instructional clarity of human annotation guidelines, especially to support non-expert users and expert-heavy domains.
AI.method: The paper proposes iPOE, an interpretable prompt optimization framework driven by explanations of annotation decisions. First, explanations (from humans or LLMs) for decisions on labeled examples are converted into candidate guidelines that describe how to perform the task. These guidelines are then treated as modular units and systematically optimized through operations such as removing redundant or harmful rules, adding helpful ones, shuffling their order, and merging overlapping guidelines. The final optimized prompt is composed of these curated guidelines plus the base task description, so the optimization acts over a structured guideline space rather than raw token sequences.
AI.result: On four datasets, prompts augmented with iPOE-optimized guidelines outperform both prompts without any guidelines and prompts with randomly selected guidelines, with relative performance gains up to 31% and 35%, respectively. The study further shows that LLM-generated explanations can effectively substitute for human-written explanations within the iPOE pipeline without substantial performance loss, indicating that the approach scales without heavy human annotation overhead.
AI.conclusion: iPOE demonstrates that making prompt optimization guideline-centric and explanation-driven yields both better task performance and higher interpretability. By exposing the intermediate guidelines and the operations applied to them, the method clarifies why prompt modifications help, making the process accessible to non-experts and suitable for complex, expert-dependent annotation tasks. The findings also suggest that automated LLM explanations are a viable, scalable source of guidelines, reducing the need for costly human-crafted instructions.

title: EGI: A Multimodal Emotional AI Framework for Enhancing Scrum Master Real-time Self-Awareness
summary: While increasing research focuses on the emotional well-being of agile team members, a significant gap remains in emotion monitoring studies for Scrum Masters and meeting organizers, whose impact on team dynamics is crucial. This paper proposes a novel application integrating four carefully selected and recommended AI models to monitor the unconsciously expressed emotions of these key roles. This is achieved through: real- time transcription using a speech-to-text model; thresholding for intonation analysis to detect emotional cues in prosody; applying emotion-based vocabulary matching to identify sentiment in spoken content; and providing context-aware suggestions containing emotion keywords using an open-source, multi-module AI API. The system achieved an ASR word error rate WER of 10% in simulated meeting environments. Our evaluation shows that real- time feedback significantly improves emotion awareness during simulated agile meetings, providing Scrum Masters and meeting organizers with real-time and practical suggestions to help them quickly identify and minimize the expression of negative emotions, fostering more positive and effective team interactions.

AI.tldr: An AI-based system monitors and feeds back on the emotions of Scrum Masters and meeting organizers in real time to improve emotional awareness and reduce negative emotions in agile meetings.
AI.motivation: Existing research on emotional well-being in agile teams largely overlooks Scrum Masters and meeting organizers, despite their strong influence on team climate and interactions. There is a lack of tools that can unobtrusively and in real time monitor their emotional expressions and provide actionable feedback during meetings.
AI.method: The paper designs an integrated system combining four AI components: (1) a speech-to-text model for real-time transcription of meeting speech; (2) intonation thresholding to analyze prosody and detect emotional cues from voice patterns; (3) emotion-based vocabulary matching to infer sentiment from the lexical content; and (4) an open-source, multi-module AI API that generates context-aware suggestions containing emotion-related keywords. The system is tested in simulated agile meeting environments, measuring ASR performance and the effect of real-time feedback on emotion awareness.
AI.result: The speech recognition module achieved a word error rate (WER) of 10% under simulated meeting conditions. User evaluations in simulated agile meetings indicate that the real-time feedback provided by the system enhances participants' awareness of their emotional expressions and supports them in recognizing and managing negative emotions during interactions.
AI.conclusion: Integrating multiple AI models for speech transcription, prosody analysis, sentiment detection, and suggestion generation enables effective real-time monitoring and feedback on the emotions of Scrum Masters and meeting organizers. This approach helps reduce the expression of negative emotions and promotes more positive and effective team interactions in agile meetings, suggesting value for both practice and further research into emotion-aware collaboration tools.

title: FOL2NS: Generating Natural Sentences from First-Order Logic
summary: Translating formal language into natural language is a foundational challenge in NLP, driving various downstream applications in semantic parsing, theorem validation, and question answering. In this study, we introduce First-Order Logic to Natural Sentence (FOL2NS), a neurosymbolic framework designed to generate synthetic FOL formulas and convert them into natural human expressions. It handles deeply nested structures with varying quantifier depths (QD), which are rarely captured by existing corpora. By combining rule-driven modules with fine-tuned language models, FOL2NS enhances the diversity and coverage of the generated samples. In our experiments, we systematically evaluate the framework's capabilities through both character-level analysis and overall performance metrics. Experimental results show that FOL2NS can reliably produce well-formed templates and fluent statements, but it faces challenges in achieving precise semantic representations and natural generation as structural complexity increases.

AI.tldr: A neurosymbolic framework (FOL2NS) that generates synthetic first-order logic formulas and translates them into natural language, handling deep quantifier structures but struggling as complexity grows.
AI.motivation: Existing datasets and methods for translating formal representations (like first-order logic) into natural language rarely cover deeply nested structures or high quantifier depths, which are essential for robust semantic parsing, theorem proving, and question answering. There is a need for a system that can systematically generate such complex logical forms and corresponding natural language to improve coverage and evaluation.
AI.method: The authors design FOL2NS, a neurosymbolic framework that combines rule-based modules for generating synthetic first-order logic formulas (with controlled nesting and quantifier depth) and templates, with fine-tuned language models that realize these templates as fluent natural sentences. They then evaluate the framework using character-level analyses and broader performance metrics, assessing the well-formedness of templates and the fluency and semantic correctness of generated statements across increasing structural complexity.
AI.result: FOL2NS successfully generates structurally valid FOL formulas and corresponding natural language outputs that are generally fluent and well-formed. It improves diversity and coverage over existing corpora, especially for deeply nested, high-quantifier-depth structures. However, experimental results indicate that as the structural complexity of formulas increases, the system has difficulty maintaining precise semantic fidelity and fully natural phrasing.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that a neurosymbolic, rule-plus-LM approach is effective for generating complex FOL-to-natural-language pairs, extending beyond what current corpora capture. Nonetheless, the work also reveals persistent challenges in accurately preserving semantics and naturalness for highly complex logical structures, indicating room for future improvements in modeling and data generation strategies.

title: EXG: Self-Evolving Agents with Experience Graphs
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based agents have demonstrated strong capabilities in complex reasoning and problem solving through multi-step interactions, yet most deployed agents remain behaviorally static, with knowledge acquired during execution rarely translating into systematic improvement over time. In response, a growing line of work on self-evolving agents explores how agents can improve through experience during deployment, but most existing approaches either rely on ad hoc reflection limited to single-task correction or adopt unstructured memory that accumulates fragmented experience with delayed usability. To address this limitation, we introduce EXG, an experience graph framework for self-evolving agents that explicitly organizes accumulated successes and failures into a structured, relational representation. EXG is the first experience graph designed for self-evolving agents, supporting both online, real-time graph growth during execution for immediate cross-task experience reuse, and offline reuse of a consolidated experience graph as an external memory module. This design also enables EXG to serve as a plug-and-play component for existing self-evolving agents, organizing prior experience into a unified experience graph and improving both solution quality and resource efficiency as deployment progresses. Extensive experiments across code generation and reasoning benchmarks show that EXG attains more favorable performance-efficiency trade-offs than reflection- and memory-based baselines in both online and offline evaluations. Our results suggest that structuring experience as a graph provides a principled foundation for scalable and transferable self-evolving agent behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes EXG, an experience graph framework that structures LLM agents’ past successes and failures as a graph to enable continual self-improvement with better performance-efficiency trade-offs than prior reflection or memory methods.
AI.motivation: Most LLM-based agents can reason and solve complex tasks via multi-step interactions but remain behaviorally static; knowledge gained in one task rarely improves performance on future tasks. Existing self-evolving methods either use ad hoc single-task reflections or unstructured memory that stores fragmented experiences, which are hard to reuse efficiently. There is a need for a principled, scalable way to organize and exploit accumulated experience so agents can improve over time during deployment.
AI.method: The authors introduce EXG, an experience graph framework that represents an agent’s accumulated successes and failures as a structured, relational graph. EXG supports online, real-time graph construction and updating during agent execution, so that experience from one task can be immediately reused on others, and offline use of a consolidated experience graph as an external memory module. It is designed to be plug-and-play: it can be integrated into existing self-evolving agents to convert their prior experiences into a unified graph structure for retrieval and reuse. The method is evaluated on code generation and reasoning benchmarks against reflection- and memory-based baselines.
AI.result: Across multiple code generation and reasoning benchmarks, EXG achieves better performance-efficiency trade-offs than baselines that rely on reflection or unstructured memory, in both online (during deployment) and offline (post-hoc reuse) settings. This indicates that structuring experience in a graph makes reuse more effective and reduces resource costs (e.g., fewer tokens or steps for similar or better accuracy).
AI.conclusion: Structuring an agent’s experience as a relational graph provides a principled and scalable basis for self-evolving behavior in LLM-based agents. EXG enables immediate and later reuse of past successes and failures, is compatible with existing agents as a plug-and-play component, and leads to improved solution quality and resource efficiency over time. This suggests experience graphs are a promising foundation for building continually improving, transferable LLM agents.

title: Leveraging Graph Structure in Seq2Seq Models for Knowledge Graph Link Prediction
comment: 9 pages, 1 figure, 2 tables. Preprint of a paper accepted at the 5th Workshop on LLM-Integrated Knowledge Graph Generation from Text (TEXT2KG), co-located with ESWC 2026, May 10--14, 2026, Dubrovnik, Croatia
summary: We introduce Graph-Augmented Sequence-to-Sequence (GA-S2S), a novel framework that integrates a T5-small encoder-decoder with a Relational Graph Attention Network (RGAT) to improve link prediction in knowledge graphs. While existing Seq2Seq models rely solely on surface-level textual descriptions of entities and relations and at best, flatten the neighborhoods of a query entity into a single linear sequence, thereby discarding the inherent graph structure, GA-S2S jointly encodes both textual features and the full $k$-hop subgraph topology surrounding the query entity. By integrating raw encoder outputs with RGAT's relation-aware embeddings, our model captures and leverages richer multi-hop relational patterns and textual information. Our preliminary experiments on the CoDEx dataset demonstrate that GA-S2S outperforms competitive Seq2Seq-based baseline models, achieving up to a 19\% relative gain in link prediction accuracy.

AI.tldr: Proposes GA-S2S, a T5-small plus Relational Graph Attention Network framework that jointly encodes text and k-hop graph structure for better link prediction, achieving up to 19% accuracy gain on CoDEx.
AI.motivation: Existing Seq2Seq link prediction models only use textual descriptions and sometimes a flattened neighborhood sequence, losing the true graph topology and limiting their ability to exploit multi-hop relational patterns in knowledge graphs.
AI.method: Integrate a T5-small encoder-decoder with a Relational Graph Attention Network (RGAT). The encoder takes textual descriptions of entities/relations while RGAT encodes the k-hop subgraph around the query entity. Raw encoder outputs are combined with relation-aware RGAT embeddings to form a joint representation that preserves graph structure and text. This is used within a Seq2Seq framework to predict missing links.
AI.result: On the CoDEx knowledge graph dataset, the proposed GA-S2S model outperforms competitive Seq2Seq-based baselines for link prediction, with reported relative improvements of up to 19% in prediction accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling textual descriptions and explicit k-hop subgraph topology via GA-S2S yields richer multi-hop relational representations than text-only or flattened-neighborhood Seq2Seq models, leading to significantly improved link prediction performance in knowledge graphs, as evidenced on CoDEx.

title: Divergence-Suppressing Couplings for Rectified Flow
summary: The promise of Rectified Flow rests on producing self-generated couplings whose trajectories are straight, or nearly so. In practice, trajectories generated by the base flow model can bend and intertwine, and the resulting coupling inherits this distortion. In this paper, we identify that such trajectory entanglement is often associated with regions of nonzero divergence in the learned velocity field, where local expansion or contraction distorts trajectories and steers particles away from their ideal endpoints. We then propose divergence-suppressing couplings for Rectified Flow, an offline correction that attenuate the divergent component of the learned velocity during coupling generation. The correction is paid only once per coupling pair and amortized over training, so deployment runs plain Euler at identical wall-clock cost to standard Rectified Flow. Empirically, this offline modification yields consistent improvements on 2D synthetic benchmarks and on image generation.

AI.tldr: They identify and fix trajectory entanglement in Rectified Flow models by suppressing divergence in the learned velocity field, improving generative performance without extra inference cost.
AI.motivation: Rectified Flow relies on approximately straight transport trajectories, but in practice the learned velocity field often produces bent and intertwined (entangled) trajectories due to regions of nonzero divergence. This entanglement harms the quality and efficiency of the self-generated couplings used for training, motivating a method to regularize or correct the flow without increasing deployment cost.
AI.method: They analyze the learned velocity field of Rectified Flow models and link trajectory entanglement to regions of local expansion/contraction (nonzero divergence). Based on this, they introduce an offline coupling-generation procedure that explicitly suppresses the divergent component of the velocity during coupling construction. The correction is computed once per coupling pair and then reused during training, while inference still uses standard Euler integration of the original Rectified Flow ODE.
AI.result: The proposed divergence-suppressing couplings consistently reduce trajectory entanglement and improve performance on both 2D synthetic benchmarks and real image generation tasks, compared to standard Rectified Flow with uncorrected couplings, without adding runtime overhead at deployment.
AI.conclusion: Controlling divergence in the learned velocity field during coupling generation is an effective way to straighten Rectified Flow trajectories and improve generative quality. An offline divergence-suppressing correction can be amortized over training, preserving inference-time efficiency while delivering empirical gains on synthetic and image benchmarks.

title: Context Memorization for Efficient Long Context Generation
summary: Modern large language model (LLM) applications increasingly rely on long conditioning prefixes to control model behavior at inference time. While prefix-augmented inference is effective, it incurs two structural limitations: i) the prefix's influence fades as generation proceeds, and ii) attention computation over the prefix scales linearly with its length. Existing approaches either keep the prefix in attention while compressing it, or internalize it into model parameters through gradient-based training. The former still attends to the prefix at inference, while the latter is training-intensive and ill-suited to prefix updates. To address these issues, we propose attention-state memory, a training-free approach that externalizes the prefix into a lightweight, lookup-based memory of precomputed attention states between prefix and query tokens. On ManyICLBench with LLaMA-3.1-8B, our method improves accuracy over in-context learning at 1K-8K memory budgets while reducing attention latency by 1.36x at 8K, and surpasses full-attention RAG performance on NBA benchmark using only 20% of its memory footprint.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a training-free, memory-based way to apply long prefixes to LLMs that preserves their effect without paying linear attention cost over the entire prefix.
AI.motivation: Long conditioning prefixes help control LLM behavior, but their influence fades over time and they are expensive because attention scales linearly with prefix length. Existing methods either still require attending over (possibly compressed) prefixes at inference, or need gradient-based retraining to bake prefixes into model weights, which is costly and inflexible. The authors want a more efficient, updatable way to use long prefixes without these drawbacks.
AI.method: They introduce attention-state memory: instead of keeping the whole prefix in the attention context or retraining the model, they precompute and store attention states between prefix tokens and query tokens in a lightweight, lookup-based external memory. At inference, the model consults this stored attention-state memory to inject the effect of the prefix without recomputing full attention over the prefix or keeping it explicitly in the context window. The approach is training-free and works by externalizing prefix information into reusable attention states indexed by queries.
AI.result: On ManyICLBench with LLaMA-3.1-8B, their method outperforms standard in-context learning across memory budgets from 1K to 8K, while also reducing attention latency by 1.36× at the 8K memory setting. On the NBA benchmark, it beats full-attention retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) in performance while using only 20% of RAG’s memory footprint.
AI.conclusion: Attention-state memory can retain the benefits of long conditioning prefixes while substantially reducing attention computation and memory usage, without requiring gradient-based training. It offers a practical, training-free alternative to both prefix compression and retraining approaches, enabling efficient, updatable prefix usage for LLM applications.

title: Harnessing LLM Agents with Skill Programs
summary: Equipping LLM agents with reusable skills derived from past experience has become a popular and successful approach for tackling complex and long-horizon tasks. However, such lessons are often encoded as textual guidance that remains largely advisory, lacking explicit mechanisms for when and how to intervene in the agent loop. To bridge the gap, we introduce HASP(Harnessing LLM Agents with Skill Programs), a new framework that upgrades skills into executable Program Functions (PFs). Rather than offering passive advice, PFs act as executable guardrails that activate on failure-prone states and modify the next action or inject corrective context. HASP is highly modular: it can be applied at inference time for direct agent-loop intervention, during post-training to provide structured supervision, or for self-improvement by evolving validated, teacher-reviewed PFs. Empirically, HASP drives substantial gains compared to both training-free and training-based methods on web-search, math reasoning, and coding tasks. For example, on web-search reasoning, inference-time PFs alone improve the average performance by 25% compared to (multi-loop) ReAct Agent, while post-training and controlled evolution achieve a 30.4% gain over Search-R1. To provide deeper insights into HASP, our mechanism analysis reveals how PFs trigger and intervene, how skills are internalized, and the requirement for stable skill library evolution.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes HASP, a framework that turns textual skills for LLM agents into executable program functions that can actively intervene in the agent loop, leading to large performance gains on web-search, math, and coding tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches give LLM agents textual skills or guidance derived from prior experience, but these are passive: they don’t specify precisely when or how they should affect the agent’s actions, so their impact is limited, especially on complex, long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: The authors introduce HASP, which represents skills as executable Program Functions (PFs) that monitor for failure-prone states and then either modify the agent’s next action or inject corrective context. HASP is modular and can be used (1) at inference time to directly intervene in the agent loop, (2) during post-training as structured supervision, and (3) for self-improvement by evolving PFs that have been validated and reviewed by a teacher. They also perform mechanism analyses to study triggering, intervention, internalization of skills, and how to maintain a stable evolving PF library.
AI.result: On web-search reasoning tasks, using PFs only at inference time yields a 25% average performance improvement over a multi-loop ReAct agent. When combined with post-training and controlled evolution, HASP achieves a 30.4% performance gain over the Search-R1 baseline. Similar substantial gains are observed on math reasoning and coding tasks, outperforming both training-free and training-based baselines.
AI.conclusion: Turning skills into executable program functions that can actively intervene in an LLM agent’s operation significantly improves performance on complex tasks. HASP offers a flexible, modular framework for inference-time control, structured post-training, and systematic self-improvement, and the analysis highlights how and when PFs are triggered, how agents internalize skills, and what is needed for robust evolution of the skill library.

title: SomaliWeb v1: A Quality-Filtered Somali Web Corpus with a Matched Tokenizer and a Public Language-Identification Benchmark
summary: Somali is a Cushitic language of the Horn of Africa with ~25 million speakers, yet no documented dedicated Somali pretraining corpus with a companion tokenizer and language-identification benchmark has been publicly released. Existing Somali text appears either inside multilingual distributions (HPLT v2, CC100, MADLAD-400, OSCAR, mC4) or in small, undocumented Somali-only uploads on Hugging Face. We introduce SomaliWeb v1, a quality-filtered Somali corpus of 819,322 documents (~303M tokens) built from three upstream sources (HPLT v2, CC100, Somali Wikipedia) through a six-stage reproducible pipeline. We release (i) the corpus, (ii) a matched BPE-16K tokenizer, and (iii) the first public side-by-side Somali benchmark of three production language identifiers. Our measurements reveal concrete quality defects in existing distributions: HPLT v2's "cleaned" Somali release retains 17.3% byte-exact duplicates, 56.1% of its documents contain fixable mojibake, and 10.7% of its byte-unique documents are near-duplicates at Jaccard tau=0.80. Our BPE-16K tokenizer emits 40.2% fewer tokens than GPT-4's cl100k_base on FLORES-200 Somali devtest as a tokenizer-level measurement; downstream language-model perplexity comparisons are deferred to a follow-up release.

AI.tldr: They build and release SomaliWeb v1, a cleaned Somali corpus, a tokenizer, and a language-ID benchmark, and diagnose quality problems in existing Somali web data.
AI.motivation: Somali is a major African language but lacks a dedicated, well-documented pretraining corpus, tokenizer, and evaluation resources. Existing Somali data are fragmented, embedded in large multilingual datasets, or poorly documented and noisy. This gap hinders the development and evaluation of language technologies and LLMs for Somali. The authors aim to create a reliable, openly available resource and to quantify quality issues in current Somali datasets and tools.
AI.method: They construct SomaliWeb v1 from three upstream sources (HPLT v2, CC100, Somali Wikipedia) using a six-stage, reproducible pipeline with quality filtering, deduplication (including near-duplicate detection via Jaccard similarity), and mojibake detection/fixing. They then train a 16K BPE tokenizer on this corpus and build a side-by-side benchmark to evaluate three production language-identification systems on Somali. They compare their corpus and tokenizer against existing resources, including GPT-4's cl100k_base tokenizer, using tokenization statistics on FLORES-200 Somali devtest.
AI.result: SomaliWeb v1 contains 819,322 Somali documents (~303M tokens) with documented preprocessing. Analysis exposes serious defects in an existing “cleaned” Somali release (HPLT v2): 17.3% of its data are byte-exact duplicates, 56.1% of documents have fixable mojibake, and 10.7% of byte-unique documents are near-duplicates at Jaccard tau=0.80. Their dedicated BPE-16K tokenizer is substantially more efficient than GPT-4’s cl100k_base for Somali, producing 40.2% fewer tokens on FLORES-200 Somali devtest. They also provide the first public Somali-focused language-ID benchmark, with side-by-side evaluation of three production systems (details not in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: SomaliWeb v1 fills a critical resource gap for Somali NLP by providing a high-quality, reproducible corpus, a tailored tokenizer, and a language-ID benchmark. The work demonstrates that widely used multilingual corpora can contain substantial duplication and encoding noise for low-resource languages, underscoring the need for language-specific cleaning and evaluation. The new tokenizer is markedly more token-efficient for Somali than a popular general-purpose tokenizer, suggesting it can benefit future Somali language modeling; perplexity and downstream modeling results will be presented in subsequent work.

title: Agents for Experiments, Experiments for Agents: A Design Grammar for AI-Enabled Experimental Science
summary: AI systems are becoming active participants in organizational and knowledge work. They increasingly interact with humans, coordinate workflows, and operate in multi-agent arrangements. Understanding their effects therefore requires more than measuring output accuracy; it requires evidence about mechanisms, delegation, feedback, and control. Experiments remain central to this task, but they also face a recursive challenge: we need experiments for agents to study these arrangements, and we may need agents for experiments to help search the expanding space of possible designs. Yet experimental conditions for human-AI and agentic workflows are still largely specified in prose, making them difficult to compare, reuse, or audit. We frame this as a problem of workflow representation, traceability, and governance in AI-enabled knowledge production. We introduce SEED (Structural Encoding for Experimental Discovery), a framework that represents experimental conditions as typed actor-flow graphs. SEED supports three design functions: describing conditions as interaction structures, evaluating structural novelty relative to encoded prior designs, and generating candidate designs under feasibility and governance constraints. We report a lightweight empirical feasibility test that compares graph-blind and SEEDguided generation in a medical-triage design task. In this diagnostic contrast, SEED-guided candidate designs show clearer actor-flow changes, assumptions, and governance checks, supporting the feasibility of the grammar as a design aid. The commentary closes by identifying governance tensions around novelty, replication, validity, diversity of inquiry, and accountability.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes SEED, a structured graph-based framework to represent and design human-AI experimental workflows, improving comparability, traceability, and governance of AI-enabled experiments.
AI.motivation: As AI systems increasingly act as agents that collaborate with humans and other AIs in organizations, simple accuracy metrics and prose descriptions of experiments are insufficient. Researchers need a way to formally represent complex interaction workflows, delegation, and control mechanisms so that experiments can be compared, reused, audited, and systematically designed. There is also a recursive challenge: we need experiments to study agentic workflows and may use agents to help design those experiments, which expands the design space and demands better structure and governance.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize experimental conditions as interaction workflows and encode them as typed actor-flow graphs, forming the SEED (Structural Encoding for Experimental Discovery) framework. SEED is designed to (1) describe experimental conditions as interaction structures among actors and flows, (2) evaluate structural novelty of a candidate design by comparing its graph to prior encoded designs, and (3) generate new candidate experimental designs subject to feasibility and governance constraints. They conduct a lightweight empirical feasibility test in a medical-triage design task, comparing experimental designs produced without access to these graphs (“graph-blind”) versus designs guided by SEED encodings.
AI.result: In the medical-triage design task, SEED-guided generation produced candidate experimental designs with more explicit and clearer changes to actor flows, better-articulated assumptions, and more visible governance checks than graph-blind designs. This indicates that using SEED’s grammar and graph encodings is feasible and can help structure experimental design in human-AI and multi-agent settings.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that SEED offers a promising structural grammar for representing and designing experiments involving human-AI and agentic workflows. By encoding experimental conditions as typed actor-flow graphs, SEED enhances representation, traceability, and governance, and supports systematic exploration of design space. The feasibility test suggests practical benefits as a design aid. The authors note, however, that broader adoption raises governance tensions related to incentivizing novelty vs. replication, maintaining validity, ensuring diversity of inquiry, and clarifying accountability in AI-enabled knowledge production.

title: Multilingual jailbreaking of LLMs using low-resource languages
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to jailbreak attempts that circumvent safety guardrails. We investigate whether multi-turn conversations using low-resource African languages (Afrikaans, Kiswahili, isiXhosa, and isiZulu) can bypass safety mechanisms across commercial LLMs. We translated prompts from existing datasets and evaluated ChatGPT, Claude, DeepSeek, Gemini, and Grok through automated testing and human red-teaming with native speakers. Single-turn translation attacks proved ineffective, while multi-turn conversations achieved English harmful response rates from 52.7% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) to 83.6% (GPT-4o-mini), Afrikaans from 60.0% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) to 78.2% (GPT-4o-mini), and Kiswahili from 41.8% (Claude 3.5 Haiku) to 70.9% (DeepSeek). Human red-teaming increased jailbreak rates compared to automated methods. Over all evaluated languages, the average jailbreak rate increased from 59.8% to 75.8%, with improvements of +20.0% (Afrikaans), +12.7% (isiZulu), +12.3% (isiXhosa), and +1% (Kiswahili), demonstrating that poor translation quality limits jailbreak success. These findings suggest that vulnerabilities in LLMs persist in multilingual contexts and that translation quality is the critical factor determining jailbreak success in low-resource languages.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that multi-turn conversations in low-resource African languages can successfully jailbreak major commercial LLMs, with success heavily dependent on translation quality rather than the language itself.
AI.motivation: Safety guardrails in large language models are mainly studied in high-resource languages like English, leaving open whether low-resource languages can be exploited to bypass protections. The authors aim to test if African low-resource languages can be used for jailbreaks, and how translation quality and interaction style (single vs multi-turn, automated vs human) affect success.
AI.method: The authors select four African languages (Afrikaans, Kiswahili, isiXhosa, isiZulu) and construct jailbreak prompts by translating existing English attack datasets. They evaluate several commercial LLMs (ChatGPT, Claude, DeepSeek, Gemini, Grok) under different settings: single-turn translation attacks vs multi-turn conversations, and automated red-teaming vs human red-teaming by native speakers. They measure harmful response rates (jailbreak rates) across languages and models, and analyze how translation quality correlates with jailbreak success.
AI.result: Single-turn translation-based attacks do not effectively bypass safety mechanisms. However, multi-turn conversations substantially increase jailbreak success, with harmful response rates often above 50% and reaching over 80% in some models and languages. Human red-teaming by native speakers further elevates jailbreak rates compared to automated methods, raising average jailbreak across all languages from 59.8% to 75.8%. Languages with better translation quality (e.g., Afrikaans) show larger increases in jailbreak success, while poorer translation quality limits attack effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: LLM safety vulnerabilities persist in multilingual, low-resource language settings. The main driver of jailbreak success is not the specific low-resource language but the quality of translation and prompt formulation, especially in multi-turn settings. Improving translation quality and considering adversarial, multilingual interactions are essential for designing robust safety mechanisms in commercial LLMs.

title: Surface-Form Neural Sparse Retrieval: Robust Fuzzy Matching for Industrial Music Search
summary: Music search at the scale of Amazon Music presents a unique challenge: queries frequently deviate from indexed metadata due to misspellings, transpositions, and phonetic variations, yet the retrieval system must operate under strict millisecond-level latency constraints. Our existing learning-to-retrieve system, the High Confidence Index (HCI), learns query-entity associations from customer behavior, relying on continual ``exploration'' to choose candidates. Traditional n-gram matching enables this exploration but suffers from poor semantic robustness and high noise, limiting the system's ability to learn from long-tail queries. In this work, we present a \textbf{robust neural sparse retrieval system} designed to maximize exploration efficiency. We adapt a state-of-the-art \textbf{inference-free} sparse retrieval architecture to the music domain, combining it with an effective \textbf{domain-specific granular subword tokenization strategy}. Our approach utilizes short-length token constraints (max 3 chars) to enforce the learning of surface-form robustness over lexical memorization. By pre-computing the neural embeddings and term expansions during the offline indexing phase, online processing is reduced to minimal tokenization and IDF weighting, achieving effectively zero latency overhead for query encoding. Evaluations on a 6M-document production corpus show an aggregate \textbf{91.4\%} recall@10 (vs. \textbf{57.7\%} for trigrams) at comparable throughput. Simulation of the HCI feedback loop demonstrates improved exploration efficiency, with \textbf{+0.8\%} higher stabilized recall than production trigrams. Ablation studies indicate that our sparse training methodology drives the performance gains, while domain-specific pretraining provides a cost-effective alternative to large-scale general-purpose pretraining.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a robust, zero-online-latency neural sparse retrieval system for Amazon-scale music search that dramatically improves recall and exploration efficiency over traditional n‑gram (trigram) matching.
AI.motivation: At Amazon Music scale, queries often differ from indexed metadata because of misspellings, transpositions, and phonetic variations, yet the search system must answer within a few milliseconds. The existing High Confidence Index (HCI) learning-to-retrieve system depends on exploring candidate query-entity mappings via n‑gram matching, which is semantically weak and noisy, especially for long-tail queries. This limits how well HCI can learn from user behavior and hurts search quality. The authors want a retrieval method that is robust to surface-form variation, efficient enough for strict latency budgets, and better at supporting exploration in the HCI feedback loop.
AI.method: They design a robust neural sparse retrieval system tailored to the music domain. They adapt a state-of-the-art inference-free sparse retrieval architecture so that all heavy neural computation (embeddings and term expansions) happens offline during indexing. They introduce a domain-specific granular subword tokenization strategy with very short tokens (maximum length 3 characters) to encourage robustness to misspellings and variations, discouraging simple lexical memorization. At query time, the system only performs lightweight tokenization and inverse document frequency (IDF) weighting, with no neural inference, so latency is effectively unchanged from traditional methods. They evaluate on a 6-million-document production corpus and simulate its use inside the HCI learning-to-retrieve feedback loop. They also run ablations to separate the effects of sparse training design from domain-specific pretraining.
AI.result: On a 6M-document production corpus, their neural sparse retrieval system achieves 91.4% recall@10 versus 57.7% for a strong trigram baseline, at comparable throughput, indicating much higher retrieval quality without sacrificing efficiency. In simulations of the HCI feedback loop, the new system improves exploration efficiency, yielding +0.8% higher stabilized recall than the production trigram system. Ablation studies show that the specialized sparse training procedure is the main driver of the improvements, while domain-specific pretraining still contributes and is more cost-effective than large-scale general-purpose pretraining.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed, inference-free neural sparse retrieval architecture with granular, domain-specific subword tokenization can significantly improve large-scale music search. By pushing neural computation offline and enforcing very short token units, the system gains robustness to noisy, mismatched queries without incurring online latency costs. This leads to substantially higher recall, better long-tail exploration for the HCI learning-to-retrieve system, and offers a practical, cost-effective path beyond traditional n‑gram matching and generic large-scale pretraining for industrial music search applications.

title: Machine Unlearning for Masked Diffusion Language Models
summary: Recent masked diffusion language models (MDLMs), such as LLaDA and Dream, have achieved performance comparable to autoregressive large language models. Unlike autoregressive models, which generate text sequentially, MDLMs generate text by iteratively denoising masked positions in parallel. During fine-tuning, MDLMs learn to recover responses from masked response states conditioned on a prompt, thereby shifting their predictions from a prompt-masked unconditional distribution toward a prompt-conditional distribution. Despite this distinct generative and fine-tuning mechanism, machine unlearning for MDLMs remains largely unexplored. In this paper, we propose Masked Diffusion Unlearning (MDU), the first unlearning framework for MDLMs, by revisiting the process of learning specific knowledge in terms of diffusion. Specifically, MDU minimizes a forward KL divergence from the prompt-conditional prediction to a prompt-masked unconditional anchor at every masked response position, with a temperature scaling parameter to control the privacy-utility trade-off. Our empirical results on standard benchmarks and MDLM backbones show that MDU achieves high unlearning performance compared to existing LLM unlearning methods. Code is available at https://github.com/leegeoru/MDU.

AI.tldr: Introduces Masked Diffusion Unlearning (MDU), the first unlearning framework tailored to masked diffusion language models (MDLMs), achieving strong unlearning performance by pushing model predictions from prompt-conditional back toward a prompt-masked unconditional anchor via KL minimization with temperature control.
AI.motivation: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) like LLaDA and Dream can match autoregressive LLMs, but they differ fundamentally in how they generate and fine-tune text: MDLMs iteratively denoise masked tokens in parallel conditioned on prompts. Existing machine unlearning methods are designed mainly for autoregressive LLMs and do not exploit or respect the diffusion-style training and generation process of MDLMs. There is a gap in understanding and implementing effective unlearning specifically for MDLMs, where we need to remove specific knowledge or responses without retraining from scratch while maintaining utility. The authors aim to conceptualize how MDLMs learn prompt-conditional knowledge in diffusion terms and then invert or counteract that process for unlearning.
AI.method: The authors define unlearning for MDLMs from a diffusion perspective: during fine-tuning, MDLMs learn to map from masked response states (given a prompt) toward the correct prompt-conditional responses. Their Masked Diffusion Unlearning (MDU) reverses this by adjusting model predictions so that, for selected knowledge to be forgotten, the model’s distribution at each masked response position is pulled away from the prompt-conditional distribution and back toward a prompt-masked unconditional anchor distribution. Technically, at each masked position, MDU minimizes a forward KL divergence from the current prompt-conditional prediction to the fixed prompt-masked unconditional anchor. A temperature scaling parameter modulates how strongly the model is moved toward the anchor, giving control over the privacy–utility trade-off. This is applied iteratively across diffusion steps/denoising iterations for the MDLM backbones.
AI.result: Across standard benchmarks and multiple MDLM backbones, MDU yields strong unlearning performance—better than existing LLM unlearning approaches repurposed for MDLMs—while preserving model utility. Experiments demonstrate that the proposed KL-based diffusion-aware objective is more effective at removing targeted knowledge in MDLMs than prior unlearning methods not tailored to diffusion-style masked generation.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes that machine unlearning for masked diffusion language models requires a formulation aligned with their diffusion-style training dynamics. By introducing Masked Diffusion Unlearning, which minimizes forward KL from prompt-conditional predictions toward a prompt-masked unconditional anchor with tunable temperature, the authors provide the first dedicated unlearning framework for MDLMs and show it works well empirically. This opens a direction for privacy and safety techniques specifically designed for diffusion-based language models.

title: Entropy-Gradient Inversion: Moving Toward Internal Mechanism of Large Reasoning Models
summary: The advancement of Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) has catalyzed a paradigm shift from reactive ``fast thinking'' text generation to systematic, step-by-step ``slow thinking'' reasoning, unlocking state-of-the-art performance in complex mathematical and logical tasks. However, the field faces \textit{the fundamental gap between token-level behavioral analysis and internal reasoning mechanisms, and the instability of reinforcement learning (RL) for reasoning optimization relying on costly external verifiers}. We identify and formally define \textbf{Entropy-Gradient Inversion}, a robust negative correlation between token entropy and logit gradients that acts as a definitive geometric fingerprint for LRM reasoning capability. Building on this, we propose \textbf{Correlation-Regularized Group Policy Optimization (CorR-PO)}, which embeds this inversion signature into RL reward regularization. Extensive experiments on various reasoning benchmarks across multiple model scales show CorR-PO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, confirming that stronger inversion directly correlates with superior reasoning performance.

AI.tldr: They identify a geometric “fingerprint” of good reasoning in large reasoning models—an entropy-gradient inversion pattern—and design an RL algorithm (CorR-PO) that regularizes policies to strengthen this pattern, yielding better reasoning performance than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Current large reasoning models excel at slow, stepwise reasoning but we lack a clear, internal indicator of genuine reasoning ability; most analysis is token-level and behavioral. At the same time, reinforcement learning methods for improving reasoning tend to be unstable, expensive, and dependent on external verifiers. The authors want an internally measurable, model-based signal of reasoning quality that can both explain and guide optimization in a stable, scalable way.
AI.method: They empirically discover and then formally define “Entropy-Gradient Inversion”: a negative correlation between per-token entropy and the gradient of logits, which characterizes strong reasoning behavior. They treat this as a geometric property of the model’s representation/optimization landscape. Then they design Correlation-Regularized Group Policy Optimization (CorR-PO), an RL algorithm that adds a regularization term to the reward (or objective) encouraging stronger entropy-gradient inversion during training. They apply this method across different model sizes and reasoning benchmarks and compare to existing RL-based reasoning optimization techniques.
AI.result: Across multiple reasoning benchmarks and model scales, models trained with CorR-PO consistently outperform state-of-the-art baselines. Quantitatively, they show that higher measured entropy-gradient inversion correlates with higher accuracy on complex mathematical and logical reasoning tasks. They also report improved stability of RL training relative to standard methods that rely heavily on external verifiers.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-Gradient Inversion serves as a reliable internal signature of reasoning capability in large reasoning models. By incorporating this signature directly into the RL objective via CorR-PO, they can systematically enhance reasoning performance without relying solely on expensive, unstable external verification. The work narrows the gap between observable token behavior and internal reasoning mechanisms and offers a more principled, geometry-aware route to training better reasoning models.

title: Knowledge-to-Verification: Exploring RLVR for LLMs in Knowledge-Intensive Domains
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has demonstrated promising potential to enhance the reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) in domains such as mathematics and coding. However, its applications on knowledge-intensive domains have not been effectively explored due to the scarcity of high-quality verifiable data. Furthermore, current RLVR focuses solely on the correctness of final answers, leading to the limitations of flawed reasoning and sparse reward signals. In this work, we propose Knowledge-to-Verification (K2V), a framework that extends RLVR to knowledge-intensive domains through automated verifiable data synthesis, while enabling verification of the LLM's reasoning process. Extensive experiments demonstrate that K2V enhances the reasoning of LLM in knowledge-intensive domains without significantly compromising the model's general capabilities. This study also suggests that integrating automated data synthesis with reasoning verification is a promising direction to enhance model capabilities in these broader domains. Code is available at https://github.com/SeedScientist/K2V.

AI.tldr: K2V extends reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) to knowledge-intensive tasks by automatically constructing verifiable datasets and checking not only final answers but also intermediate reasoning, thereby improving LLM reasoning in such domains without harming general abilities.
AI.motivation: Existing RLVR methods work well for domains like math and coding where answers are easily verifiable, but they do not transfer well to knowledge-intensive tasks (e.g., open-domain QA) because high-quality verifiable data is scarce. Moreover, current RLVR mainly verifies final outputs, which can encourage shallow or flawed reasoning and provides sparse feedback signals. The authors aim to address both the data scarcity and the shallow-reasoning issues so that RLVR can be effectively used to strengthen LLM reasoning on knowledge-heavy problems.
AI.method: The authors propose Knowledge-to-Verification (K2V), a framework that (1) automatically synthesizes verifiable data for knowledge-intensive domains and (2) extends the RLVR objective to verify not just final answers but also the intermediate reasoning process produced by LLMs. This transforms knowledge content into verification signals that can supervise and reward step-by-step reasoning, rather than only checking end results.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments, models trained with K2V show improved reasoning performance on knowledge-intensive tasks while maintaining their general-purpose capabilities. Empirical results indicate more accurate and reliable reasoning compared to baselines that rely only on final-answer verification or that lack verifiable supervision in these domains.
AI.conclusion: K2V demonstrates that automated synthesis of verifiable data combined with process-level reasoning verification effectively extends RLVR beyond math and coding into knowledge-intensive domains, improving LLM reasoning without substantial degradation in other skills. The work suggests that jointly designing data-generation pipelines and reasoning-verification mechanisms is a promising path for further enhancing LLM capabilities in broad, knowledge-heavy settings.

title: STRIDE: A Self-Reflective Agent Framework for Reliable Automatic Equation Discovery
summary: LLM-based equation discovery offers a promising route to recovering symbolic laws from data, but many systems still rely on generation-centered loops that propose candidates, fit parameters, score results, and reuse selected examples. Such loops can misjudge useful skeletons under unreliable fitting, discard near-correct equations that require repair, and accumulate redundant memories that provide limited guidance. We propose STRIDE, a self-reflective agent framework that improves reliability by coordinating data-aware generation, mixed-fitting evaluation, critic--executor repair, and diversity-preserving semantic memory. By turning fitted scores and candidate behavior into shared feedback, STRIDE enables equations to be proposed, assessed, refined, and reused within a closed-loop discovery process. Experiments on representative symbolic-regression benchmarks and LSR-Synth suites show that STRIDE improves accuracy, OOD robustness, and structural recovery across multiple LLM backbones, with ablations and analyses confirming the contribution of its core components.

AI.tldr: STRIDE is an LLM-based, self-reflective agent framework for symbolic equation discovery that uses coordinated generation, mixed-fitting evaluation, repair, and diversity-preserving memory to improve accuracy, robustness, and structural recovery over prior generation-centered loops.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based equation discovery systems mainly use a generation-centered loop: they generate candidate equations, fit parameters, score them, and reuse selected examples. This process can fail when parameter fitting is unreliable, can prematurely discard almost-correct equations that just need small structural repairs, and tends to accumulate redundant memories that don’t meaningfully guide future generations. There is therefore a need for a more reliable, feedback-rich discovery process that better leverages data, supports equation repair, and maintains diverse, informative memories.
AI.method: The paper introduces STRIDE, a self-reflective agent framework for symbolic regression. STRIDE coordinates four key components in a closed-loop process: (1) data-aware generation, where LLMs propose equation structures informed by data behavior; (2) mixed-fitting evaluation, combining different fitting strategies to robustly estimate parameters and scores; (3) a critic–executor repair mechanism, where one component critiques candidate equations and another revises them rather than discarding them; and (4) diversity-preserving semantic memory, which stores and reuses past equations in a way that maintains structural and behavioral diversity. Fitted scores and observed candidate behaviors are converted into shared feedback signals that guide subsequent proposal, assessment, and refinement steps across multiple LLM backbones.
AI.result: On symbolic regression benchmarks and the LSR-Synth suites, STRIDE achieves higher accuracy, better out-of-distribution robustness, and improved structural recovery of underlying equations compared to prior LLM-based approaches. The framework is tested with multiple LLM backbones, showing that its benefits are not model-specific. Ablation studies and further analyses demonstrate that each of STRIDE’s core components—mixed-fitting evaluation, critic–executor repair, and diversity-preserving semantic memory—contributes meaningfully to the overall performance gains.
AI.conclusion: STRIDE provides a more reliable, self-reflective closed-loop framework for LLM-based symbolic equation discovery by integrating data-aware generation, robust evaluation, repair mechanisms, and diverse semantic memory. This design reduces the pitfalls of traditional generation-centered loops, such as misjudging good equation skeletons and discarding near-correct candidates, leading to improved accuracy, robustness, and structural recovery across benchmarks and LLM backbones. The results suggest that structured feedback and repair-oriented workflows are key to advancing LLM-driven scientific discovery.

title: From Volume to Value: Preference-Aligned Memory Construction for On-Device RAG
comment: Accepted to ICML 2026. Code and data are available at https://github.com/UbiquitousAILab/EPIC
summary: With the rapid emergence of personal AI agents based on Large Language Models (LLMs), implementing them on-device has become essential for privacy and responsiveness. To handle the inherently personal and context-dependent nature of real-world requests, such agents must ground their generation in device-resident personal context. However, under tight memory budgets, the core bottleneck is what to store so that retrieval remains aligned with the user. We propose EPIC (Efficient Preference-aligned Index Construction), which focuses on user preferences as a compact and stable form of personal context and integrates them throughout the RAG pipeline. EPIC selectively retains preference-relevant information from raw data and aligns retrieval toward preference-aligned contexts. Across four benchmarks covering conversations, debates, explanations, and recommendations, EPIC reduces indexing memory by 2,404 times, improves preference-following accuracy by 20.17 percentage points, and achieves 33.33 times lower retrieval latency over the best-performing baseline. In our on-device experiment, EPIC maintains a memory footprint under 1 MB with 29.35 ms/query latency in streaming updates.

AI.tldr: EPIC is a memory-efficient method to build preference-aligned indexes for on-device LLM agents, drastically cutting storage and latency while improving how well the agent follows user preferences.
AI.motivation: On-device personal AI agents need to use a user’s personal context (e.g., history, data, habits) to answer queries in a personalized way, but device memory and compute are limited. Storing and retrieving all raw personal data is infeasible, and existing retrieval approaches are not explicitly aligned with user preferences, which are the most critical and stable personalization signal. The paper aims to identify what minimal form of personal context should be stored and how to structure retrieval so that it remains tightly aligned with user preferences under strict resource constraints.
AI.method: The authors introduce EPIC (Efficient Preference-aligned Index Construction), a framework that treats user preferences as the core representation of personal context and weaves them into the entire Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline. EPIC selectively filters and retains only preference-relevant information when building the index from raw user data, constructing a compact, preference-centric index. It then uses this index to guide retrieval toward items most aligned with the inferred or explicit user preferences, thereby optimizing both memory usage and preference adherence. The system is evaluated across multiple personalization tasks such as conversations, debates, explanations, and recommendations.
AI.result: On four benchmarks, EPIC dramatically reduces memory required for indexing (by a factor of 2,404×) while simultaneously improving personalization quality, measured as preference-following accuracy, by 20.17 percentage points. It also accelerates retrieval, yielding 33.33× lower latency than the strongest baseline. In an on-device experiment, EPIC operates with under 1 MB memory footprint and supports streaming updates with an average 29.35 ms per query latency, demonstrating its practicality for real-time on-device agents.
AI.conclusion: User preferences can serve as a compact, stable, and effective core for personal context in on-device LLM agents. By centering index construction and retrieval around preferences, EPIC achieves significant gains in memory efficiency, preference alignment, and latency, making it well-suited for resource-constrained devices. This suggests that future on-device RAG systems should prioritize preference-aware indexing and retrieval to deliver both privacy-preserving and highly personalized AI assistance.

title: Accelerating AI-Powered Research: The PuppyChatter Framework for Usable and Flexible Tooling
summary: This research addresses the challenges inherent in developing Artificial Intelligence (AI) applications, particularly those leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs). While AI vendors provide Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) and Software Development Kits (SDKs) to facilitate developer interaction, the former often requires intricate manual request construction, and the latter can lead to significant vendor lock-in. Furthermore, existing model abstraction frameworks, though mitigating vendor dependency, introduce an additional layer of complexity and potential security concerns. To reconcile these conflicting factors, the study introduces PuppyChatter, a novel software framework designed to preserve the intuitive simplicity of vendor-specific SDKs while simultaneously adhering to the vendor-neutrality principles characteristic of model abstraction, thereby offering a more streamlined and flexible development paradigm.

AI.tldr: PuppyChatter is a framework that makes building LLM-based AI apps as simple as using vendor SDKs while remaining vendor-neutral like abstraction layers.
AI.motivation: Developers face trade-offs when building AI apps with LLMs: raw APIs require complex manual request handling; vendor SDKs are easier but create strong vendor lock-in; and existing abstraction frameworks reduce dependency but add complexity and security risks. There is a need for a solution that offers simplicity and usability without sacrificing vendor neutrality.
AI.method: The paper proposes PuppyChatter, a new software framework. Conceptually, it is designed to combine the intuitive, straightforward developer experience of vendor-specific SDKs with the portability and vendor-agnostic design of model abstraction frameworks. It provides an API and abstractions that hide vendor-specific details while keeping the programming model simple.
AI.result: PuppyChatter offers a more streamlined and flexible development approach for AI applications using LLMs, reducing vendor lock-in while avoiding the complexity and security concerns of prior abstraction frameworks. (Specific empirical or quantitative results are not detailed in the abstract.)
AI.conclusion: A framework like PuppyChatter can reconcile the tension between simplicity and vendor neutrality in LLM application development, enabling developers to build AI apps more easily without being tightly bound to a single vendor or burdened by complex abstraction layers.

title: Infini-News: Efficiently Queryable Access to 1.3 Billion Processed Common Crawl News Articles
summary: Large-scale news corpora support a wide range of research in Computational Social Science and NLP, yet access remains constrained: commercial archives impose prohibitive costs and licensing restrictions, while open alternatives like Common Crawl's CC-News require terabyte-scale storage and computationally intensive processing. We present Infini-News, a retrieval toolkit and index for the entire CC-News archive from August 2016 to the latest available snapshot. Our contributions are threefold. First, we extract, clean the text, and parse the structured metadata of over 1.35B articles. Second, we enrich the corpus with language detection using three frontier language classifiers (GlotLID, lingua, and CommonLingua), and with multi-source geographic attribution that resolves a country of origin for 83.4% of articles across 222 countries. Third, we construct Infini-gram indexes: suffix-array structures that let researchers search the full archive for arbitrary text patterns in sub-second time. Together, these resources lower the barrier to longitudinal, cross-national media research.
