Template-based synthetic training data performs nearly as well as data from actual model runs, cutting annotation cost. Max-pooling over token representations yields up to 95% AUROC and stable trajectories, whereas average- and last-token pooling perform near random.
AI.conclusion: Temporal analysis of hidden-state probes—via probe trajectories and derived features—provides a powerful, complementary way to monitor and predict LRM behavior beyond surface CoT text. Properly designed trajectory features and max-pooling make it feasible and effective, and low-cost template-based training data is sufficient, suggesting a practical path for scalable behavior monitoring in large reasoning models.

title: Reconciling Contradictory Views on the Effectiveness of SFT in LLMs: An Interaction Perspective
summary: This paper explores a scientific question in supervised fine-tuning (SFT): why SFT is broadly effective for small-scale deep neural networks, yet can produce inconsistent or even detrimental effects when applied to large language models (LLMs). Recent advances in interaction-based explanations suggest that interactions between words/tokens provide a faithful metric for quantifying the inference patterns encoded by LLMs. We find that the evolution of interactions during SFT can effectively explain the inconsistent effectiveness of SFT for LLMs. Specifically, we find that (1) SFT primarily removes noise-like interactions, while rarely acquiring reliable new interactions. (2) This denoising stage is extremely brief, after which continued fine-tuning tends to introduce overfitted interactions. We validate these findings across multiple LLMs and datasets. Our findings provide new insights into early stopping and offer practical guidance for LLM training.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why supervised fine-tuning (SFT) behaves differently on large language models (LLMs) than on smaller neural nets, and explains this via how token interactions evolve during SFT.
AI.motivation: SFT is a standard way to adapt models, and it works reliably on small deep networks but can be unstable or even harmful on already-strong LLMs. Practitioners observe that SFT sometimes degrades capabilities, but there is limited mechanistic understanding of why this happens and when SFT helps vs hurts. The authors aim to provide a principled, interaction-based explanation of SFT’s effectiveness and failure modes for LLMs, to better guide training and early stopping.
AI.method: The authors use interaction-based explanation techniques that quantify word/token interactions to track how the internal inference patterns of LLMs change during SFT. They examine how SFT modifies these interaction patterns over training time across multiple models and datasets. By analyzing which interactions are removed, preserved, or newly formed, they characterize distinct phases of training: an initial denoising phase and a later overfitting phase.
AI.result: They find that early in SFT, the main effect is to remove noise-like (unreliable or spurious) interactions, with very few genuinely new, reliable interactions being formed. This denoising phase is short. If SFT continues past this phase, the model tends to acquire overfitted interactions that harm generalization, explaining why extended SFT can be detrimental for LLMs. These dynamics are observed consistently across different LLMs and datasets.
AI.conclusion: The effectiveness of SFT on LLMs is largely due to a brief initial denoising of token interactions, after which continued training risks introducing overfitted interaction patterns. This interaction-centric view explains the inconsistent impact of SFT on LLMs and suggests that careful early stopping—aligned with the denoising phase—is crucial. The work offers actionable guidance for designing and scheduling SFT for LLMs, and reframes SFT’s role more as denoising than as learning rich new inference mechanisms.

title: Readers make targeted regressions to plausible errors in reanalysis of "noisy-channel garden-path" sentences
summary: A key question in psycholinguistics is how inferences about the meaning of linguistic input unfold incrementally a comprehender's mind. In this work, we study reading dynamics for ``noisy-channel garden-path'' sentences, which temporarily appear well-formed but feature late-appearing violations of expectation that can be resolved not by inferring an alternative syntactic structure, but by inferring the presence of an error. We find evidence for targeted regressions -- eye movements towards regions that are promising loci of possible errors in light of later-arriving information, showing patterns consistent with the posterior inferences of a model of noisy-channel processing with reanalysis. We discuss the implications of these findings for theories of noisy-channel language comprehension and information-theoretic explanations of reading dynamics.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how people reread earlier parts of a sentence when they later discover an error-like inconsistency, using eye movements and a noisy-channel processing model.
AI.motivation: Psycholinguistics seeks to understand how sentence comprehension proceeds word by word in real time, especially when the input is imperfect or misleading. Traditional garden-path sentences involve syntactic reanalysis, but many naturally occurring sentences are "noisy" and may contain errors. The authors want to understand whether readers treat some late-appearing anomalies as potential errors in earlier parts of the sentence, and how this is reflected in eye-movement behavior during reading. They aim to connect these behaviors to formal noisy-channel models of language comprehension and to broader information-theoretic accounts of reading dynamics.
AI.method: The authors construct and use "noisy-channel garden-path" sentences: sentences that initially appear well-formed but later contain unexpected elements that are best explained by positing an earlier error rather than a different parse. They track readers' eye movements while reading these sentences, focusing on regressions—backward eye movements—to earlier regions in the sentence. They then compare these regression patterns to predictions from a computational model of noisy-channel processing with reanalysis, which computes posterior inferences about possible earlier errors given the full sentence context.
AI.result: Readers exhibit targeted regressions toward sentence regions that are likely locations of potential errors, given the unexpected material they encounter later. The spatial and temporal patterns of these regressions align with the posterior probability patterns predicted by the noisy-channel model with reanalysis, indicating that readers' on-line behavior reflects structured inferential processing about possible noise in the input.
AI.conclusion: The findings support the view that language comprehension is a noisy-channel inference process in which readers rationally consider the possibility of errors in the input and selectively re-examine earlier material when later information conflicts with expectations. The observed eye-movement dynamics are consistent with information-theoretic and Bayesian accounts of comprehension, suggesting that reading involves incremental probabilistic inference over both structures and possible input corruptions, not just syntactic reanalysis. These results refine theories of garden-path effects and reinforce information-theoretic explanations of reading behavior.

title: Unleashing LLMs in Bayesian Optimization: Preference-Guided Framework for Scientific Discovery
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2026. 10 pages main paper, 21 pages appendix, 26 figures
summary: Scientific discovery is increasingly constrained by costly experiments and limited resources, underscoring the need for efficient optimization in AI for science. Bayesian Optimization (BO), though widely adopted for balancing exploration and exploitation, often exhibits slow cold-start performance and poor scalability in high-dimensional settings, limiting its applicability in real-world scientific problems. To overcome these challenges, we propose LLM-Guided Bayesian Optimization (LGBO), the first LLM preference-guided BO framework that continuously integrates the semantic reasoning of large language models (LLMs) into the optimization loop. Unlike prior works that use LLMs only for warm-start initialization or candidate generation, LGBO introduces a region-lifted preference mechanism that embeds LLM-driven preferences into every iteration, shifting the surrogate mean in a stable and controllable way. Theoretically, we prove that LGBO does not perform significantly worse than standard BO in the worst case, while achieving significantly faster convergence when preferences align with the objective. Empirically, LGBO consistently outperforms existing methods across diverse dry benchmarks in physics, chemistry, biology, and materials science. Most notably, in a new wet-lab optimization of Fe-Cr battery electrolytes, LGBO attains \textbf{90\% of the best observed value within 6 iterations}, whereas standard BO and existing LLM-augmented baselines require more than 10. Together, these results suggest that LGBO offers a promising direction for integrating LLMs into scientific optimization workflows.

AI.tldr: They propose LGBO, a Bayesian Optimization framework that uses LLM-guided preferences at every iteration to speed up scientific optimization, with theory and experiments (including a wet lab) showing faster convergence than standard and LLM-augmented BO baselines.
AI.motivation: Scientific discovery often relies on expensive experiments and limited resources, requiring sample-efficient optimization methods. Standard Bayesian Optimization, while strong at balancing exploration and exploitation, can be slow at cold start and scales poorly to high-dimensional problems, which is problematic for real-world scientific tasks. Existing LLM-augmented methods typically only help with initialization or candidate generation and do not fully integrate semantic prior knowledge into the whole optimization loop, leaving performance on the table.
AI.method: They introduce LLM-Guided Bayesian Optimization (LGBO), in which a large language model provides semantic preference information that is incorporated into each BO iteration. LGBO uses a region-lifted preference mechanism that adjusts (shifts) the surrogate model’s mean function in a stable and controllable way according to LLM-provided regional preferences, instead of only using LLMs for warm-starting or generating candidates. They also provide a theoretical analysis comparing LGBO to standard BO, characterizing worst-case performance and conditions under which convergence improves when LLM preferences correlate with the objective.
AI.result: On a variety of benchmark optimization problems drawn from physics, chemistry, biology, and materials science, LGBO consistently outperforms standard BO and existing LLM-augmented approaches, in terms of convergence speed or sample efficiency. In a new real-world wet-lab experiment on optimizing Fe-Cr battery electrolytes, LGBO reaches 90% of the best observed performance in only 6 iterations, whereas standard BO and prior LLM-based baselines need more than 10 iterations to reach the same performance level.
AI.conclusion: Integrating LLM-derived semantic preferences directly and continuously into the Bayesian Optimization loop, via the LGBO framework, can substantially accelerate scientific optimization without sacrificing worst-case performance guarantees. Theoretical and empirical results, including a real wet-lab study, indicate that LGBO is a promising general approach for leveraging LLMs in AI-for-science workflows.

title: LongMINT: Evaluating Memory under Multi-Target Interference in Long-Horizon Agent Systems
comment: Equal contribution; order decided by a coin flip. Code and data: https://github.com/amy-hyunji/LongMINT
summary: Real-world agents operate over long and evolving horizons, where information is repeatedly updated and may interfere across memories, requiring accurate recall and aggregated reasoning over multiple pieces of information. However, existing benchmarks focus on static, independent recall and fail to capture these dynamic interactions between evolving memories. In this paper, we study how current memory-augmented agents perform in realistic, interference-heavy, long-horizon settings across diverse domains and question types. We introduce LongMINT (Long-Horizon Memory under INTerference), a benchmark featuring (1) long, highly interconnected contexts with frequently updated information that induces substantial interference, (2) diverse domains (state tracking, multi-turn dialogue, Wikipedia revisions, and GitHub commits), enabling evaluation of domain generalization, and (3) diverse question types that assess robustness to interference, including (i) single-target recall tasks requiring retrieval of a specific target from long contexts, and (ii) multi-target aggregation tasks requiring reasoning over multiple relevant pieces of information. Overall, LongMINT has 15.6k question-answering pairs over long-horizon contexts averaging 138.8k tokens and extending up to 1.8M tokens per instance. We evaluate 7 representative systems, including vanilla long-context LLMs, RAG, and memory-augmented agent frameworks. Across all systems, we observe consistently low performance (avg. 27.9% accuracy), especially on questions requiring aggregated reasoning over multiple pieces of evidence. Our analysis shows that performance is primarily limited by retrieval and memory construction. Furthermore, current memory systems struggle to recall and reason over earlier facts that are later revised or interfered with by subsequent context, with performance degrading as the number of intervening updates increases.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LongMINT, a benchmark to evaluate how well memory-augmented agents handle long, evolving, and interference-heavy contexts, revealing that current systems perform poorly, especially on multi-step aggregated reasoning and under frequent information updates.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks mostly test static, independent recall, which does not reflect real-world scenarios where information is updated, overwritten, or contradicted over long horizons. There is a need to understand how agents cope with memory interference, revisions, and the requirement to combine multiple pieces of information across time and domains.
AI.method: The authors design LongMINT, a benchmark of long-horizon contexts (up to 1.8M tokens, avg. 138.8k) spanning multiple domains: state tracking, multi-turn dialogue, Wikipedia revisions, and GitHub commits. They define diverse question types focused on: (i) single-target recall from long, interfering contexts; and (ii) multi-target aggregation requiring combining several relevant facts. They then evaluate seven representative systems—standard long-context LLMs, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems, and memory-augmented agent frameworks—measuring accuracy and analyzing failure modes, especially around retrieval, memory construction, and handling of revisions/updates.
AI.result: LongMINT comprises 15.6k QA pairs over very long and highly interconnected contexts. All evaluated systems achieve low accuracy (about 27.9% on average), with particularly poor performance on multi-target aggregation tasks and on questions involving earlier facts that are later revised or interfered with. Performance systematically decreases as the number of intervening updates between the target information and the query grows. The main bottlenecks are retrieval quality and the way memory representations are constructed and updated.
AI.conclusion: Current memory-augmented agents and long-context LLM setups are far from robust in realistic long-horizon, interference-heavy settings. They struggle both to retrieve the right pieces of information from large, evolving contexts and to aggregate multiple relevant facts, especially when information has been updated or contradicted later. LongMINT exposes these limitations and provides a challenging benchmark to drive future research on more reliable memory mechanisms, better retrieval, and improved reasoning over evolving information.

title: Shared Backbone PPO for Multi-UAV Communication Coverage with Connection Preservation
summary: This paper proposes a Shared Backbone Proximal Policy Optimization (Shared Backbone PPO) algorithm. By sharing the base module between the Actor and Critic networks, the algorithm achieves efficient training and improved performance. The algorithm is implemented in a connectivity-preserving multi-UAV swarm communication coverage task and compared with the standard PPO algorithm. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior performance. Furthermore, a graph information aggregation module is incorporated into the model architecture to accommodate the communication conditions among agents. With the integration of this module, the algorithm remains effective, and the trained agent swarm exhibits a higher level of cooperation.

AI.tldr: They propose a Shared Backbone PPO algorithm where actor and critic share a base network, tested on multi-UAV swarm communication coverage, outperforming standard PPO.
AI.motivation: Standard PPO with separate actor and critic networks can be less efficient and may not fully exploit shared representations, particularly in multi-agent UAV swarm tasks requiring communication-aware coordination.
AI.method: Design a PPO variant where actor and critic share a backbone (base module) to learn common features; implement it in a connectivity-preserving multi-UAV swarm communication coverage task; extend the architecture with a graph information aggregation module to encode inter-UAV communication/relations; compare performance with standard PPO.
AI.result: Shared Backbone PPO outperforms standard PPO in the multi-UAV coverage task; when the graph information aggregation module is added, the algorithm remains effective and the trained swarm shows more cooperative behavior.
AI.conclusion: Sharing the backbone between actor and critic improves PPO training efficiency and performance in multi-UAV swarm tasks, and incorporating graph-based communication modeling further enhances cooperative behaviors without degrading learning effectiveness.

title: GUT-IS: A Data-Driven Approach to Integrating Constructs and Their Relations in Information Systems
comment: Accepted at the 34th European Conference on Information Systems (ECIS 2026), Milan, Italy
summary: Structural equation modeling is widely used in IS research. However, inconsistent construct definitions impede the cumulative development of knowledge. In this work, we present an approach that aims at the integration of structural equation models into a unified model: We use a combination of task-adapted text embeddings and clustering to produce a candidate set of construct groupings. Subsequently, we select the optimal solution using a loss function that explicitly trades off semantic purity and parsimony in the number of clusters. By making this trade-off explicit, our approach allows to analyze how construct groupings and their relations change as one shifts the priority from purity to parsimony. Empirically, we evaluate and explore the proposed methodology on two datasets from the IS domain.

AI.tldr: They propose a method to unify many separate structural equation models by clustering constructs using text embeddings, balancing semantic coherence with model simplicity, and test it on two IS datasets.
AI.motivation: In Information Systems (IS) research, structural equation modeling (SEM) is heavily used, but constructs are often defined and labeled inconsistently across studies. This inconsistency hinders the accumulation and integration of knowledge, because it is difficult to see whether different papers are really studying the same underlying constructs or slightly different ones. The authors want a systematic way to integrate separate SEMs into a unified, higher-level model of constructs and their relations.
AI.method: They represent construct definitions and related text using task-adapted text embeddings, then apply clustering to group semantically similar constructs across studies. To choose the best clustering, they define a loss function that explicitly balances semantic purity (how semantically coherent each cluster is) against parsimony (having fewer clusters). By varying this trade-off, they can examine how construct groupings and their interrelations change as one prefers more detailed vs. more aggregated models. They empirically apply and explore this methodology on two real datasets from the IS domain.
AI.result: The method yields candidate groupings of constructs that reflect semantic similarity across SEMs and provides a way to see how constructs merge or split when prioritizing parsimony versus purity. On two IS datasets, they demonstrate that their approach can produce meaningful unified construct structures and visualize or analyze how construct relationships evolve as the trade-off parameter changes.
AI.conclusion: Their approach offers a principled, data-driven way to integrate multiple structural equation models into a unified construct model, making explicit and controllable the trade-off between construct granularity and semantic coherence. This can help IS researchers better align construct definitions across studies and support the cumulative development of theory based on SEM results.

title: TeleCom-Bench: How Far Are Large Language Models from Industrial Telecommunication Applications?
summary: While Large Language Models have achieved remarkable integration in various vertical scenarios, their deployment in the telecommunications domain remains exploratory due to the lack of a standardized evaluation framework. Current telecom benchmarks primarily focus on static, foundational knowledge and isolated atomic skills, neglecting the equipment-specific documentation and end-to-end industrial workflows essential for real-world production systems. To bridge this gap, we present TeleCom-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark comprising 12 evaluation sets with 22,678 curated samples, which evaluates LLMs across a synergistic hierarchy: (1) Multi-dimensional Knowledge Comprehension, which integrates telecommunication fundamentals, 3GPP protocols, and 5G network architecture with proprietary product knowledge across wired, core, and wireless networks via knowledge graph-driven synthesis; and (2)End-to-End Knowledge Application, which formalizes six core tasks on authentic trajectories from live network agent workflows, including intent recognition, entity extraction, event verification, tool invocation, root cause analysis, and solution generation-across network optimization and fault maintenance scenarios. Evaluations of eight state-of-the-art LLMs reveal a universal Execution Wall: while models achieve 90% accuracy in linguistic interface tasks such as intent recognition and entity extraction, performance collapses to approximately 30% in procedural execution tasks like solution generation. This capability gap demonstrates that current LLMs function competently as diagnosticians but fail as field engineers. TeleCom-Bench provides standardized diagnostics to precisely pinpoint this deficit, offering actionable guidance for domain-specific alignment toward production-ready telecom agents. The dataset and evaluation code have been released at https://github.com/ZTE-AICloud/TeleCom-Bench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TeleCom-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark to systematically evaluate LLMs on telecom-specific knowledge and workflows, revealing a major gap between their ability to understand language and to execute end-to-end engineering tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing telecom benchmarks only test static knowledge and isolated skills, ignoring real equipment documentation and complete workflows used in production telecom systems, which prevents a realistic assessment of LLMs for telecom applications.
AI.method: The authors build TeleCom-Bench, a benchmark with 12 evaluation sets and 22,678 samples organized in two layers: (1) multi-dimensional knowledge comprehension combining telecom fundamentals, 3GPP standards, 5G architectures, and proprietary product knowledge via knowledge-graph-based synthesis; and (2) end-to-end knowledge application, capturing six key tasks—intent recognition, entity extraction, event verification, tool invocation, root cause analysis, and solution generation—from real network optimization and fault maintenance workflows.
AI.result: Testing eight state-of-the-art LLMs shows they perform well (>90% accuracy) on linguistic interface tasks like intent recognition and entity extraction but drop to around 30% accuracy on procedural execution tasks such as solution generation, exposing a sharp performance cliff (the “Execution Wall”).
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are suitable as diagnostic assistants in telecom but are not yet capable as autonomous field engineers. TeleCom-Bench offers a standardized way to locate this capability gap and provides a basis for targeted alignment and improvement of production-ready telecom agents; the benchmark and code are publicly released for the community.

title: MA$^{2}$P: A Meta-Cognitive Autonomous Intelligent Agents Framework for Complex Persuasion
comment: 22 pages, 8 figures. Accepted to Findings of ACL 2026
summary: Persuasive dialogue generation plays a vital role in decision-making, negotiation, counseling, and behavior change, yet it remains a challenging problem. In complex persuasion where the persuadee's internal states are not expressed clearly, the persuader must interpret responses, infer the persuadee's latent mental states (e.g., beliefs and desires), and translate them into targeted, strategy-consistent actions; however, current approaches often produce generic or weakly grounded responses even when such cues are identified. Moreover, although large language models (LLMs) can generate persuasive content, their performance varies substantially across domains due to uneven knowledge coverage and limited reasoning generalization. To address these challenges, we propose MA$^{2}$P, a meta-cognitive autonomous intelligent agent framework for complex persuasion. Specifically, we develop an autonomous multi-agent architecture that coordinates perception management, mental-state inference, strategy execution, memory maintenance, and performance evaluation. To mitigate cross-domain performance variation, we further design a meta-cognitive configurator that selects an appropriate meta-strategy from a structured knowledge base at the outset, thereby guiding subsequent reasoning and planning. Experimental results show that our approach achieves a higher persuasion success rate than baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MA^2P, a meta-cognitive, multi-agent framework that improves complex persuasive dialogue generation by inferring latent mental states and using meta-strategies to guide reasoning across domains, achieving higher persuasion success than baselines.
AI.motivation: Persuasive dialogue is crucial in areas like negotiation, counseling, and behavior change, but existing systems struggle in complex scenarios where the persuadee's internal beliefs and desires are not explicitly stated. Current models often generate generic or weakly grounded responses, and even powerful LLMs show inconsistent performance across different domains because of uneven knowledge and limited reasoning transfer. There is a need for a framework that can both infer hidden mental states and maintain strong, domain-robust persuasive performance.
AI.method: The authors propose MA^2P, an autonomous meta-cognitive agent framework composed of multiple coordinated agents/modules: (1) perception management to interpret the persuadee's responses, (2) mental-state inference to estimate latent beliefs and desires, (3) strategy execution to select and implement persuasion actions consistent with a chosen strategy, (4) memory maintenance to track dialogue history and inferred states, and (5) performance evaluation to assess and adapt the persuasion process. Additionally, they design a meta-cognitive configurator that, at the start of an interaction, selects an appropriate high-level meta-strategy from a structured knowledge base. This chosen meta-strategy then constrains and guides subsequent reasoning, planning, and action selection to reduce cross-domain variability.
AI.result: In experiments, MA^2P is empirically evaluated against baseline persuasion systems and shows a higher persuasion success rate. This indicates more effective influence on the persuadee compared to existing methods. The results also suggest better grounding in inferred mental states and improved robustness across domains relative to standard LLM-based approaches.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that a meta-cognitive, multi-agent architecture, combined with a knowledge-based meta-strategy configurator, can significantly enhance complex persuasive dialogue generation. By explicitly modeling and leveraging latent mental states and by tailoring high-level strategies to domains from the outset, MA^2P produces more targeted and effective persuasive interactions than baseline systems.

title: New Insight of Variance reduce in Zero-Order Hard-Thresholding: Mitigating Gradient Error and Expansivity Contradictions
comment: Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2024. 9 pages main paper, 24 pages appendix, 11 figures, 7 tables. Correspondence to Bin Gu and Huan Xiong
summary: Hard-thresholding is an important type of algorithm in machine learning that is used to solve $\ell_0$ constrained optimization problems. However, the true gradient of the objective function can be difficult to access in certain scenarios, which normally can be approximated by zeroth-order (ZO) methods. The SZOHT algorithm is the only algorithm tackling $\ell_0$ sparsity constraints with ZO gradients so far. Unfortunately, SZOHT has a notable limitation on the number of random directions % in ZO gradients due to the inherent conflict between the deviation of ZO gradients and the expansivity of the hard-thresholding operator. This paper approaches this problem by considering the role of variance and provides a new insight into variance reduction: mitigating the unique conflicts between ZO gradients and hard-thresholding. Under this perspective, we propose a generalized variance reduced ZO hard-thresholding algorithm as well as the generalized convergence analysis under standard assumptions. The theoretical results demonstrate the new algorithm eliminates the restrictions on the number of random directions, leading to improved convergence rates and broader applicability compared with SZOHT. Finally, we illustrate the utility of our method on a ridge regression problem as well as black-box adversarial attacks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new variance-reduced zeroth-order hard-thresholding algorithm for solving ℓ0-constrained optimization problems without restrictions on the number of random directions, achieving better convergence and broader applicability than existing SZOHT.
AI.motivation: Existing hard-thresholding methods for ℓ0-constrained optimization usually rely on first-order gradients, which may be unavailable in black-box settings. The only known zeroth-order hard-thresholding method, SZOHT, suffers from strict limits on the number of random directions due to a conflict between noisy zeroth-order gradient estimates and the expansive nature of the hard-thresholding operator. This restricts its convergence and practical use. The authors aim to overcome this limitation.
AI.method: The authors analyze how variance in zeroth-order gradient estimates interacts with the expansivity of the hard-thresholding operator and reinterpret variance reduction as a way to mitigate this conflict. Based on this insight, they design a generalized variance-reduced zeroth-order hard-thresholding (ZOHT) algorithm, and develop a generalized convergence analysis under standard assumptions. Their method allows an arbitrary (or at least less restricted) number of random directions in ZO gradient estimation.
AI.result: The new variance-reduced ZO hard-thresholding algorithm removes the previous restriction on the number of random directions required by SZOHT. The theoretical analysis establishes improved convergence rates and shows that the algorithm functions under broader settings than SZOHT. Empirical experiments on ridge regression and black-box adversarial attack tasks validate that the proposed method is effective and practically useful.
AI.conclusion: By explicitly addressing the variance-induced conflict between zeroth-order gradient estimates and the hard-thresholding operator, the paper introduces a generalized variance-reduced ZO hard-thresholding algorithm with stronger theoretical guarantees and wider applicability than SZOHT. The work advances ℓ0-constrained optimization in gradient-free settings and shows benefits in applications such as ridge regression and black-box adversarial attacks.

title: Forecasting Downstream Performance of LLMs With Proxy Metrics
summary: Progress in language model development is often driven by comparative decisions: which architecture to adopt, which pretraining corpus to use, or which training recipe to apply. Making these decisions well requires reliable performance forecasts, yet the two commonly used signals are fundamentally limited. Cross-entropy loss is poorly aligned with downstream capabilities, and direct downstream evaluation is expensive, sparse, and often uninformative at early training stages. Instead, we propose to construct proxy metrics by aggregating token-level statistics, such as entropy, top-k accuracy, and expert token rank, from a candidate model's next token distribution over expert-written solutions. Across three settings, our proxies consistently outperform loss- and compute-based baselines: 1) For cross-family model selection, they rank a heterogeneous population of reasoning models with mean Spearman Rho = 0.81 (vs. Rho = 0.36 for cross-entropy loss); 2) For pretraining data selection, they reliably rank 25 candidate corpora for a target model at roughly $10{,}000\times$ less compute than direct evaluation, pushing the Pareto frontier beyond existing methods; and 3) for training-time forecasting, they extrapolate downstream accuracy across an $18\times$ compute horizon with roughly half the error of existing alternatives. Together, these results suggest that expert trajectories are a broadly useful source of signal for assessing model capabilities, enabling reliable performance forecasting throughout the model development life cycle.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes new proxy metrics based on token-level statistics over expert-written solutions to more accurately forecast language model performance than traditional loss or direct downstream evaluation.
AI.motivation: Model developers need to make choices about architectures, pretraining data, and training recipes, but existing signals are inadequate: cross-entropy loss correlates poorly with downstream abilities, and direct evaluations are expensive and uninformative early in training. A better, cheaper, and earlier-available proxy for downstream performance is needed.
AI.method: The authors construct proxy metrics using aggregated token-level statistics (e.g., entropy, top-k accuracy, expert token rank) computed from a model’s next-token distributions when run over expert-written solution trajectories. They then test these proxies across three scenarios: cross-family model selection, pretraining corpus selection, and training-time performance forecasting, comparing against loss-based and compute-based baselines.
AI.result: 1) In cross-family model selection, the proxy metrics rank diverse reasoning models with a mean Spearman correlation of 0.81 with true performance, outperforming cross-entropy loss which achieves 0.36. 2) For pretraining data selection, the proxies reliably rank 25 candidate corpora for a target model using about 10,000× less compute than direct downstream evaluation, improving the Pareto frontier compared to previous methods. 3) For training-time forecasting, the proxies extrapolate downstream accuracy over an 18× compute horizon with roughly half the prediction error of existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: Expert solution trajectories, combined with token-level distribution statistics, provide a powerful and general signal for assessing and forecasting language model capabilities. These proxy metrics enable more reliable, compute-efficient performance prediction across different stages and aspects of model development, from model and data selection to training progress forecasting.

title: DocOS: Towards Proactive Document-Guided Actions in GUI Agents
summary: While Graphical User Interface (GUI) agents have shown promising performance in automated device interaction, they primarily depend on static parametric knowledge from pre-training or instruction tuning. This reliance fundamentally limits their ability to handle long-tailed tasks that require explicit procedural knowledge absent from model parameters, often forcing agents to resort to inefficient and brittle trial-and-error exploration. To mitigate this limitation, we introduce \textbf{Proactive Document-Guided Action} for GUI agents in dynamic, open-web environments, a novel paradigm that mirrors human problem-solving by enabling agents to autonomously search for relevant documentation to resolve long-tailed tasks. To evaluate agents' capability in this paradigm, we propose \textbf{DocOS}, a benchmark designed to assess document-guided problem solving in fully interactive environments. DocOS requires agents to autonomously navigate a web browser, locate relevant online documentation, comprehend procedural instructions, and faithfully ground them into executable GUI actions. Extensive experiments reveal that progress is strictly constrained by dual bottlenecks: agents struggle to reliably locate relevant information during proactive search and frequently fail to faithfully ground retrieved instructions into precise actions, pointing toward document-guided interaction as a crucial pathway for enabling self-evolving GUI agents in dynamic environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new paradigm and benchmark where GUI agents proactively search and use online documentation to complete long-tailed tasks in dynamic web environments, revealing key bottlenecks in search and instruction grounding.
AI.motivation: Existing GUI agents rely heavily on static knowledge encoded in model parameters from pre-training or instruction tuning, which limits their ability to solve long-tailed tasks that require explicit, procedural knowledge not contained in those parameters. As a result, agents fall back on inefficient trial-and-error exploration when facing unfamiliar interfaces or workflows. The authors aim to enable more human-like problem solving, where agents can autonomously consult external documentation to handle novel or complex tasks in open-web environments.
AI.method: The authors introduce the paradigm of Proactive Document-Guided Action, where GUI agents are empowered to autonomously search for and leverage external documentation while interacting with dynamic web interfaces. To systematically evaluate this capability, they design DocOS, a benchmark that embeds tasks in fully interactive browser environments. Tasks require agents to: (1) navigate the web to find relevant online documentation, (2) understand procedural instructions in those documents, and (3) translate them into concrete GUI actions. They then run extensive experiments with existing or adapted GUI agents on DocOS to measure performance and identify failure modes across search and grounding stages.
AI.result: Experiments show that current agents are significantly limited in this paradigm. Performance is constrained by two main bottlenecks: agents often fail to reliably locate the appropriate documentation or information during proactive web search, and even when they retrieve relevant instructions, they struggle to accurately map these instructions into the necessary fine-grained GUI actions. These dual weaknesses severely limit task success in DocOS, especially for long-tailed, procedurally complex problems.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that enabling robust, document-guided interaction is critical for the development of self-improving GUI agents that can operate effectively in dynamic, open-web environments. The identified bottlenecks—proactive retrieval of relevant documentation and faithful grounding of instructions into actions—define key challenges and research directions. DocOS provides a benchmark to drive and measure progress on these fronts, positioning document-guided problem solving as a central pathway to more capable, generalizable GUI agents.

title: Language-Switching Triggers Take a Latent Detour Through Language Models
summary: Backdoor attacks on language models pose a growing security concern, yet the internal mechanisms by which a trigger sequence hijacks model computations remain poorly understood. We identify a circuit underlying a language-switching backdoor in an 8B-parameter autoregressive language model, where a three-word Latin trigger (nine tokens) redirects English output to French. We decompose the circuit into three phases: (1) distributed attention heads at early layers compose the trigger tokens into the last sequence position; (2) the resulting signal propagates through mid-layers in a subspace orthogonal to the model's natural language-identity direction; (3) the MLP at the final layer converts this latent signal into French logits. The entire circuit flows through a serial bottleneck at a single position: corrupting that position at any layer entirely mitigate the trigger but also hinder the model's capabilities. The orthogonal latent encoding suggests that defenses that search for language-like signals in intermediate representations would miss this trigger entirely.

AI.tldr: The paper reverse-engineers how a backdoor trigger phrase causes a large language model to switch its output language, uncovering a specific multi-layer circuit and showing why common defenses may fail.
AI.motivation: Backdoor attacks on language models are a serious security risk, but it is unclear mechanistically how short trigger phrases can reliably steer model behavior without affecting normal performance. Understanding the internal circuit-level implementation of such backdoors is needed both for scientific insight and for designing effective defenses that do more than treat the model as a black box.
AI.method: The authors study an 8B-parameter autoregressive language model containing a backdoor where a three-word Latin trigger causes English outputs to switch to French. Using mechanistic interpretability techniques, they trace how information about the trigger flows through the network. They analyze attention head patterns, representational subspaces, and the behavior of MLPs across layers, identifying how the trigger is composed, propagated, and ultimately turned into French-language output logits. They also perform interventions such as corrupting specific positions at different layers to test causal dependence on parts of the circuit.
AI.result: They find a three-phase circuit: (1) early-layer attention heads distributed over positions jointly attend to and compose the trigger tokens into the final sequence position; (2) this composed trigger signal then travels through mid-layers within a latent subspace that is orthogonal to the model’s usual language-identity direction; (3) in the final layer, a particular MLP transforms this latent trigger signal into logits favoring French tokens. They further discover a serial bottleneck at a single sequence position through which the whole backdoor circuit must pass: corrupting that position at any layer completely disables the trigger but simultaneously harms overall model performance. Because the trigger signal is encoded in a direction orthogonal to normal language features, detection methods that look for language-like activations in hidden states would not pick it up.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that this language-switching backdoor is implemented via a specific, interpretable circuit that relies on early trigger composition, orthogonal subspace propagation, and a final-layer MLP readout. The presence of a serial bottleneck highlights both a potential point of vulnerability and the difficulty of neutralizing the backdoor without collateral damage to model capabilities. The orthogonal encoding of the trigger shows that defenses focusing solely on human-interpretable or language-like signals in intermediate representations can fail, underscoring the need for more robust, circuit-level or subspace-aware defense strategies against backdoors in language models.

title: LLM-Guided Communication for Cooperative Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
comment: 9 pages for main, 32 pages for total, Accepted to ICML 2026
summary: Communication is a key component in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) for mitigating partial observability, yet prior approaches often rely on inefficient information exchange or fail to transmit sufficient state information. To address this, we propose LLM-driven Multi-Agent Communication (LMAC), which leverages an LLM's reasoning capability to design a communication protocol that enables all agents to reconstruct the underlying state as accurately and uniformly as possible. LMAC iteratively refines the protocol using an explicit state-awareness criterion, improving state recovery while narrowing differences in agents' knowledge. Experiments on diverse MARL benchmarks show that LMAC improves state reconstruction across agents and yields substantial performance gains over prior communication baselines.

AI.tldr: They propose LMAC, an LLM-designed communication protocol for MARL that lets agents better reconstruct the full environment state, improving coordination and task performance over existing communication methods.
AI.motivation: In partially observable multi-agent environments, each agent only sees part of the state, which harms coordination and performance. Existing MARL communication methods either exchange information inefficiently or still fail to convey enough state information to reconstruct the underlying environment, leaving agents with inconsistent and incomplete views. The authors want a communication framework that systematically maximizes how well and how uniformly each agent can infer the true state from messages.
AI.method: They introduce LLM-driven Multi-Agent Communication (LMAC). An LLM is used as a high-level reasoner to design and iteratively refine a communication protocol. The refinement process is guided by an explicit state-awareness criterion that measures how accurately each agent can reconstruct the underlying state from its local observation plus received messages. The protocol is updated to improve both overall reconstruction accuracy and uniformity of state knowledge across agents, effectively shaping what and how agents communicate to maximize shared situational awareness.
AI.result: Across several MARL benchmarks, LMAC leads to better state reconstruction quality for all agents compared to prior communication schemes, and this improved shared state awareness translates into substantial gains in task performance (e.g., higher returns/episode, better coordination metrics) over baseline communication methods.
AI.conclusion: Using an LLM to reason about and design communication protocols around a state-awareness objective can significantly enhance information sharing in MARL. By enabling agents to reconstruct the underlying state more accurately and uniformly, LMAC improves both their shared understanding and their cooperative performance, suggesting LLM-guided protocol design is a promising direction for multi-agent communication under partial observability.

title: EnvFactory: Scaling Tool-Use Agents via Executable Environments Synthesis and Robust RL
summary: Equipping LLMs with tool-use capabilities via Agentic Reinforcement Learning (Agentic RL) is bottlenecked by two challenges: the lack of scalable, robust execution environments and the scarcity of realistic training data that captures implicit human reasoning. Existing approaches depend on costly real-world APIs, hallucination-prone LLM simulators, or synthetic environments that are often single-turn or depend on pre-collected documents. Moreover, synthetic trajectories are frequently over-specified, resembling instruction sequences rather than natural human intents, reducing their effectiveness for RL training. We introduce EnvFactory, a fully automated framework that addresses both challenges. EnvFactory autonomously explores and verifies stateful, executable tool environments from authentic resources, and synthesizes natural multi-turn trajectories through topology-aware sampling and calibrated refinement, producing grounded queries with implicit intents. Using only 85 verified environments across 7 domains, EnvFactory generates 2,575 SFT and RL trajectories. Despite using significantly fewer environments than prior work, which are often 5 times more, EnvFactory achieves superior training efficiency and downstream performance, improving Qwen3-series models by up to +15% on BFCLv3, +8.6% on MCP-Atlas, and +6% on conversational benchmarks including $τ^2$-Bench and VitaBench. By fully automating both environment construction and trajectory synthesis, EnvFactory provides a scalable, extensible, and robust foundation for Agentic RL.

AI.tldr: EnvFactory is an automated framework that builds executable tool environments and generates realistic multi-turn trajectories to train LLM tool-use via Agentic RL, achieving better performance with fewer environments.
AI.motivation: Training LLMs for tool-use with Agentic RL is limited by the difficulty of building scalable, robust execution environments and by the lack of realistic, human-like multi-turn interaction data. Existing methods rely on expensive real APIs, fragile LLM-based simulators, or oversimplified synthetic setups, and often produce over-specified, instruction-style trajectories that are not representative of natural user intents, which weakens RL training.
AI.method: EnvFactory automatically discovers, explores, and verifies stateful, executable tool environments from authentic resources. It then synthesizes natural, multi-turn trajectories by using topology-aware sampling of environment states and calibrated refinement of prompts and actions, ensuring that the generated queries are grounded in the environment and encode implicit user intents rather than explicit instructions. The framework is fully automated and produces both SFT (supervised fine-tuning) and RL trajectories across multiple domains.
AI.result: Using 85 verified tool environments spanning 7 domains, EnvFactory generates 2,575 trajectories for SFT and RL. Despite using about five times fewer environments than prior work, models trained with EnvFactory’s data (Qwen3 series) show substantial gains: up to +15% on BFCLv3, +8.6% on MCP-Atlas, and around +6% on conversational benchmarks such as τ^2-Bench and VitaBench, indicating higher training efficiency and better downstream performance.
AI.conclusion: EnvFactory effectively removes key bottlenecks in Agentic RL by automating both environment construction and realistic trajectory synthesis. Its ability to discover robust, stateful tool environments and to generate grounded, natural multi-turn interactions makes it a scalable and extensible foundation for training tool-using LLM agents, achieving better performance with fewer resources than prior approaches.

title: Learning to Solve Compositional Geometry Routing Problems
summary: We study the Compositional Geometry Routing Problem (CGRP), a unified superclass of traditional routing problems that covers point-only, line-only, area-only, and arbitrary hybrid task geometries, providing a broad abstraction for real-world routing scenarios. Beyond standard point-based routing, CGRP with non-point tasks can be inherently asymmetric, tightly coupled travel routes with the intrinsic path, and enlarges the action space with numerous feasible yet often irrelevant options, thereby posing significant challenges for both representation learning and decision-making. To address these challenges, we propose DiCon, a differential attention-assisted solver with contrastive learning, as a plug-and-play framework that tackles the problem from two complementary angles. First, we introduce a differential attention mechanism that actively suppresses the probability mass on less competitive candidate actions. Second, we design a double-level contrastive learning objective to promote robust global instance representations and regularize geometry-aware task representations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DiCon achieves strong performance, broad versatility, and superior generalization across diverse CGRP instances with different compositions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Compositional Geometry Routing Problem (CGRP), a unified framework for routing with mixed geometries (points, lines, areas), and proposes DiCon, a differential attention and contrastive-learning-based solver that improves performance and generalization across diverse CGRP instances.
AI.motivation: Traditional routing research mainly focuses on point-based tasks and cannot fully model real-world routing scenarios that involve heterogeneous task geometries such as line segments (roads, inspection paths) and areas (regions to cover). When tasks are non-point, routing becomes asymmetric, tightly coupled with the geometry of the paths, and the action space grows with many irrelevant options, making both representation learning and decision-making difficult. There is a need for a unified formulation (CGRP) and an effective learning-based solver that handles these complexities and generalizes across different compositions of geometric tasks.
AI.method: The authors formalize the Compositional Geometry Routing Problem (CGRP) as a unified superclass of routing problems that includes point-only, line-only, area-only, and hybrid task geometries. To solve CGRP, they propose DiCon, a plug-and-play framework based on a differential attention mechanism and contrastive learning. The differential attention component redistributes probability mass away from weaker candidate actions to emphasize more competitive ones, thus reducing the impact of irrelevant options in the enlarged action space. In parallel, a double-level contrastive learning objective is used: (1) to encourage robust global instance representations and (2) to regularize task representations so that they become geometry-aware. These two aspects jointly improve both representation quality and decision policy.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments across various CGRP instances with different mixtures of point, line, and area tasks, DiCon is shown to achieve strong routing performance, handle a wide variety of problem compositions (broad versatility), and exhibit better generalization compared to baselines. The empirical evidence indicates that DiCon effectively addresses the challenges of asymmetric, geometry-coupled routing with large action spaces.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that CGRP serves as a powerful unified abstraction for diverse real-world routing tasks beyond point-based formulations. The proposed DiCon framework, leveraging differential attention to suppress unpromising actions and double-level contrastive learning to enhance global and geometry-aware task representations, provides an effective, generalizable solver for CGRP. The work suggests that carefully designed attention and representation learning mechanisms are key to tackling complex, compositional geometric routing problems and can serve as a plug-and-play enhancement for routing solvers in heterogeneous environments.

title: Predictable Confabulations: Factual Recall by LLMs Scales with Model Size and Topic Frequency
summary: While scaling laws govern aggregate large language model performance, no scaling law has linked factual recall to both model size and training-data composition. We evaluated 38 models on over 8,900 scholarly references evaluated by an automated reference verification system. Recall quality follows a sigmoid in the log-linear combination of model parameter count and topic representation in training data. These two variables alone explain 60% of the variance across 16 dense models from four families, rising to 74-94% within individual families. The form matches a superposition-inspired account in which recall is gated by a signal-to-noise ratio: signal strength scales with concept frequency and the noise floor with model capacity.

AI.tldr: They find a simple quantitative law describing how well large language models recall scholarly references based on model size and how much each topic appears in training data.
AI.motivation: Although general scaling laws relate model size and data to aggregate performance, there was no law specifically connecting factual recall quality to both model scale and the representation of topics in the training corpus.
AI.method: They tested 38 language models on more than 8,900 scholarly references using an automated system that checks whether generated references are verifiable, then modeled recall performance as a function of model parameter count and each topic’s frequency in the training data.
AI.result: Recall quality across 16 dense models from four families is well-described by a sigmoid function of a log-linear combination of model size and topic representation; these two variables explain about 60% of variance overall and 74–94% within individual model families, consistent with a signal-to-noise interpretation where concept frequency boosts signal and model capacity sets a noise floor.
AI.conclusion: Factual recall in LLMs largely follows a predictable scaling relationship determined by model size and how well-covered a topic is in training data, supporting a superposition-style, signal-to-noise account of knowledge storage and retrieval in neural language models.

title: Safety Geometry Collapse in Multimodal LLMs and Adaptive Drift Correction
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) often fail to transfer safety capabilities learned in the text modality to semantically equivalent non-text inputs, revealing a persistent multimodal safety gap. We study this gap from a representation-geometric perspective by analyzing a text-aligned refusal direction and a modality-induced drift direction. We show that multimodal inputs compress the usable separation along the refusal direction, making it no longer reliable for identifying and refusing harmful inputs. We refer to this failure mode as Safety Geometry Collapse. We quantify it through conditional refusal separability and show that stronger modality-induced drift is consistently associated with weaker refusal separability and higher attack success rates. We then validate the causal role of modality-induced drift through a fixed-strength activation intervention: counteracting the estimated drift restores refusal separability and improves multimodal safety. After drift correction, we further observe self-rectification, where the model recovers its ability to recognize and refuse harmful multimodal inputs during forward dynamics. This effect also provides an internal signal of the model's perceived harmfulness of each input. Motivated by this signal, we propose ReGap, a training-free inference-time method that adaptively corrects modality drift using self-rectification. Experiments across multiple multimodal safety benchmarks and utility benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of ReGap, which significantly improves the safety of MLLMs without compromising general capabilities. Our findings highlight representation-level modality alignment as a crucial direction for real-time safety improvement and for building safer, more reliable MLLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and analyzes a key failure mode in multimodal large language models where safety behaviors learned on text do not reliably transfer to non-text modalities, and proposes ReGap, an inference-time method that corrects representation drift to restore refusals on harmful multimodal inputs without hurting utility.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs are expected to apply the same safety standards to images, audio, or other modalities as they do to text, but in practice they often respond safely to harmful text prompts while failing on equivalent prompts presented partly or wholly in other modalities. This multimodal safety gap is underexplained and undermines trust in MLLMs. The authors aim to understand the problem at the level of internal representations rather than only at the behavioral level, and to leverage that understanding to design a practical, training-free mitigation method that can be applied to existing MLLMs.
AI.method: The authors analyze the internal activation space of MLLMs through a geometric lens. They identify a text-aligned "refusal direction" in the representation space that separates harmful from benign text prompts when the model behaves safely. They then characterize a "modality-induced drift" direction that captures how non-text inputs (e.g., images) systematically shift representations away from the text-only manifold. They introduce the metric of conditional refusal separability to measure how well harmful and benign inputs remain separated along the refusal direction under different modalities. By correlating measured drift with refusal separability and attack success rates, they study the relationship between drift and safety failures. They further perform fixed-strength activation interventions that explicitly counteract the estimated drift and observe the effects on refusals and overall behavior. Building on these insights, they design ReGap, an inference-time procedure that adaptively adjusts representations by exploiting the model’s own self-rectification dynamics as a signal of perceived harmfulness, thereby reducing harmful responses without altering the base model weights.
AI.result: Empirically, the authors find that multimodal inputs compress the separation between harmful and benign examples along the refusal direction, a phenomenon they term Safety Geometry Collapse. Stronger modality-induced drift consistently coincides with weaker refusal separability and higher success rates for multimodal jailbreak attacks. Through controlled activation interventions that cancel out the drift, they restore refusal separability and significantly improve safety behavior on multimodal inputs, confirming the causal role of drift. They also observe a self-rectification effect: after drift correction, the model’s forward dynamics tend to re-establish clear harmfulness signals, which can be harnessed as an internal score. Using this score, the proposed ReGap method adaptively corrects drift at inference time. Across multiple multimodal safety benchmarks, ReGap substantially decreases harmful outputs and attack success rates while maintaining performance on standard utility benchmarks, indicating minimal degradation of general capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that a major source of the multimodal safety gap in MLLMs is representation-level misalignment between text and non-text modalities, which manifests as Safety Geometry Collapse along a safety-related refusal direction. Modality-induced drift both weakens the geometric separability of harmful and benign inputs and drives behavioral safety failures. By explicitly modeling and counteracting this drift via representation-level interventions, it is possible to restore safety behavior without retraining or sacrificing utility. The proposed ReGap method demonstrates that training-free, inference-time corrections leveraging self-rectification signals can provide effective and practical safety improvements for existing MLLMs. The authors argue that future progress on MLLM safety should focus more on internal representation geometry and cross-modal alignment, not just on output-level filtering or fine-tuning.

title: Code as Agent Harness
summary: Recent large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in understanding and generating code, from competitive programming to repository-level software engineering. In emerging agentic systems, code is no longer only a target output. It increasingly serves as an operational substrate for agent reasoning, acting, environment modeling, and execution-based verification. We frame this shift through the lens of agent harnesses and introduce code as agent harness: a unified view that centers code as the basis for agent infrastructure. To systematically study this perspective, we organize the survey around three connected layers. First, we study the harness interface, where code connects agents to reasoning, action, and environment modeling. Second, we examine harness mechanisms: planning, memory, and tool use for long-horizon execution, together with feedback-driven control and optimization that make harness reliable and adaptive. Third, we discuss scaling the harness from single-agent systems to multi-agent settings, where shared code artifacts support multi-agent coordination, review, and verification. Across these layers, we summarize representative methods and practical applications of code as agent harness, spanning coding assistants, GUI/OS automation, embodied agents, scientific discovery, personalization and recommendation, DevOps, and enterprise workflows. We further outline open challenges for harness engineering, including evaluation beyond final task success, verification under incomplete feedback, regression-free harness improvement, consistent shared state across multiple agents, human oversight for safety-critical actions, and extensions to multimodal environments. By centering code as the harness of agentic AI, this survey provides a unified roadmap toward executable, verifiable, and stateful AI agent systems.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys how code is increasingly used not just as output from LLMs but as the central infrastructure (“harness”) that enables AI agents to reason, act, and interact with environments.
AI.motivation: With LLMs becoming strong code generators, many agentic systems now rely on code to control tools, environments, and workflows. However, the field lacks a unified framework to understand how code integrates reasoning, actions, memory, verification, and multi-agent coordination. The authors aim to clarify this emerging paradigm and identify challenges in building reliable, scalable agent systems centered on code.
AI.method: The authors conduct a structured survey organized into three layers: (1) harness interface, explaining how code connects agents to reasoning, actions, and environment models; (2) harness mechanisms, covering planning, memory, tool use, feedback-driven control, and optimization for long-horizon tasks; and (3) scaling to multi-agent systems where shared code artifacts support coordination, review, and verification. They collect representative methods and applications across multiple domains and synthesize open research challenges.
AI.result: The survey systematizes a wide range of agentic AI systems under the concept of “code as agent harness.” It shows that many successful applications—coding assistants, GUI/OS automation, embodied agents, scientific discovery, personalization, DevOps, and enterprise workflows—share a common reliance on executable code to structure agent behavior, maintain state, and enable verification and control.
AI.conclusion: Code should be viewed as the central harness for agentic AI, providing an executable, verifiable, and stateful substrate for reasoning and action. Framing existing work through this lens offers a unified roadmap for building and improving agent systems, and highlights key open problems such as richer evaluation metrics, verification under partial feedback, safe and regression-free harness evolution, robust shared state in multi-agent settings, effective human oversight, and extensions to multimodal environments.

title: TaskGround: Structured Executable Task Inference for Full-Scene Household Reasoning
summary: In real home deployments, household agents must often operate from a complete household scene and a situated household request, rather than from a clean task specification. Such requests require agents to identify task-relevant entities, recover intended task conditions, and resolve ordering constraints from the surrounding scene context. We formalize this capability as full-scene household reasoning: given a complete household scene and a situated household request, an agent must infer executable task structure before producing a grounded skill-level action sequence. This setting is challenging because complete household scenes contain substantial task-irrelevant information, making direct complete-scene prompting inefficient and error-prone. In practical deployment, this challenge is further amplified by privacy and local compute constraints, which favor compact open-weight models with limited long-context reasoning ability. We propose TaskGround, a training-free and model-agnostic Ground-Infer-Execute framework that grounds complete scenes into compact task-relevant scene slices, infers executable task structure, and compiles it into grounded skill-level action sequences. To evaluate this setting, we introduce FullHome, a human-validated evaluation suite of 400 household tasks spanning diverse home-scale environments and both goal-oriented and process-constrained requirements. On FullHome, TaskGround improves task success rates by large margins across both proprietary and open-weight models. Notably, it makes Qwen3.5-9B competitive with GPT-5 under direct complete-scene prompting while reducing total input-token cost by up to 18x. Our results identify executable task-structure inference as a central bottleneck in full-scene household reasoning and show that structured grounding can make compact local models substantially more effective for practical household deployment.

AI.tldr: Introduce TaskGround, a framework for full-scene household reasoning that grounds complex home environments into compact, task-relevant representations to infer executable task structures and generate action sequences.
AI.motivation: Household agents in realistic deployments receive vague, situated requests in cluttered, complete home scenes rather than clean task descriptions. This requires identifying relevant objects, inferring intended task conditions, and resolving action order from noisy context. Existing large-context prompting is inefficient, error-prone, and unsuitable for compact local models constrained by privacy and compute.
AI.method: The authors formalize full-scene household reasoning and propose TaskGround, a training-free, model-agnostic Ground-Infer-Execute framework. It first grounds the full scene into a compact, task-relevant slice, then infers an executable task structure (including ordering and conditions), and finally compiles this into grounded, skill-level action sequences. They also create FullHome, a human-validated benchmark of 400 diverse household tasks in realistic home environments, including both goal-based and process-constrained tasks.
AI.result: On the FullHome benchmark, TaskGround significantly improves task success rates for both proprietary and open-weight language models. It enables the smaller Qwen3.5-9B model to perform competitively with GPT-5 that is directly prompted with complete scenes, while cutting total input-token cost by up to 18x.
AI.conclusion: Executable task-structure inference is a key bottleneck in full-scene household reasoning. By using structured grounding to filter scenes into compact, task-relevant representations, TaskGround substantially boosts the effectiveness of smaller, local models, making them more practical for real-world household deployment under privacy and compute constraints.

title: DashAttention: Differentiable and Adaptive Sparse Hierarchical Attention
summary: Current hierarchical attention methods, such as NSA and InfLLMv2, select the top-k relevant key-value (KV) blocks based on coarse attention scores and subsequently apply fine-grained softmax attention on the selected tokens. However, the top-k operation assumes the number of relevant tokens for any query is fixed and it precludes the gradient flow between the sparse and dense stages. In this work, we propose DashAttention (Differentiable and Adaptive Sparse Hierarchical Attention), which leverages the adaptively sparse $α$-entmax transformation to select a variable number of blocks according to the current query in the first stage. This in turn provides a prior for the second-stage softmax attention, keeping the entire hierarchy fully differentiable. Contrary to other hierarchical attention methods, we show that DashAttention is non-dispersive, translating to better long-context modeling ability. Experiments with large language models (LLMs) show that DashAttention achieves comparable accuracy as full attention with 75% sparsity and a better Pareto frontier than NSA and InfLLMv2, especially in high-sparsity regimes. We also provide an efficient, GPU-aware implementation of DashAttention in Triton, which achieves a speedup of up to over FlashAttention-3 at inference time. Overall, DashAttention offers a cost-effective strategy to model long contexts.

AI.tldr: DashAttention is a differentiable, adaptive sparse hierarchical attention mechanism for LLMs that maintains accuracy close to full attention while being much more efficient for long contexts.
AI.motivation: Existing hierarchical attention methods like NSA and InfLLMv2 rely on a top-k block selection based on coarse scores. This assumes a fixed number of relevant tokens per query, which is unrealistic, and it breaks gradient flow between the sparse (block selection) and dense (token-level attention) stages. There is a need for an attention mechanism that can (1) adapt the sparsity pattern per query, (2) remain fully differentiable, and (3) better handle long-context modeling without dispersing attention mass across many irrelevant positions.
AI.method: The paper introduces DashAttention (Differentiable and Adaptive Sparse Hierarchical Attention). In the first stage, instead of a hard top-k block selection, it uses the α-entmax transformation to obtain adaptively sparse attention over key-value blocks; this yields a variable number of active blocks per query. These sparse, continuous scores act as a prior for the second-stage fine-grained softmax attention over tokens within the selected blocks, preserving full differentiability through the hierarchy. The authors analyze the non-dispersive property of DashAttention in contrast to other hierarchical schemes and implement a GPU-optimized version in Triton tailored for efficient execution, comparing both accuracy and speed to baselines such as full attention, NSA, InfLLMv2, and FlashAttention-3.
AI.result: On large language model benchmarks, DashAttention matches the accuracy of full attention while using about 75% sparsity (i.e., significantly fewer attention computations). It outperforms NSA and InfLLMv2 on the accuracy-efficiency Pareto frontier, with especially strong performance in high-sparsity settings. The Triton-based implementation achieves notable inference-time speedups, in some cases exceeding the speed of FlashAttention-3.
AI.conclusion: DashAttention provides a differentiable, adaptive sparse hierarchical attention mechanism that is better suited for long-context modeling than prior hierarchical methods. Its non-dispersive attention, query-adaptive sparsity, and end-to-end differentiability yield accuracy comparable to full attention at much lower computational cost, and the optimized implementation demonstrates practical speed gains. Thus, DashAttention is presented as a cost-effective and scalable strategy for handling long contexts in LLMs.

title: POST: Prior-Observation Adversarial Learning of Spatio-Temporal Associations for Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection
summary: Existing Multivariate Time Series Anomaly Detection (MTSAD) frameworks increasingly rely on integrating Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) with sequence models to capture complex spatio-temporal dependencies. However, less attention is paid to the spatial over-generalization problem, where unconstrained structural modeling indiscriminately reconstructs anomalies, inevitably degrading detection recall. To tackle this problem, we propose a novel framework that unifies spatio-temporal modeling through a joint prior-observation adversarial learning paradigm. In the spatial dimension, the model alternately learns adjacency matrices as structural prior and models the association discrepancy between prior and data-driven observation in a minimax manner during training. Such adversarial optimization not only improves the model sensitivity for time-wise detection, but also enables the model to localize anomalies to specific channels. To systematically evaluate this anomaly localization capability, we further construct a synthetic benchmark equipped with precise channel-wise annotations. Extensive experiments across public datasets and our dedicated benchmark demonstrate that the proposed framework establishes a new state-of-the-art in both time-wise detection and spatial localization tasks. Our code, pre-trained models, and benchmark are publicly available at https://github.com/anocodetest1/POST.

AI.tldr: They propose a new multivariate time series anomaly detection framework that jointly models spatial and temporal dependencies via adversarial learning between a learned graph prior and data-driven observations, achieving state-of-the-art performance in both detecting when anomalies occur and which channels are anomalous.
AI.motivation: Current MTSAD methods often combine GNNs with sequence models to model complex spatio-temporal dependencies, but they tend to over-generalize spatially. Their graph structures are learned or used in a way that can reconstruct even anomalous patterns, which hurts recall and makes it hard to localize which channels are anomalous. There is also a lack of systematic benchmarks with ground-truth channel-wise anomaly labels to properly assess spatial localization performance.
AI.method: They introduce a joint prior-observation adversarial learning framework. In the spatial dimension, the model alternately: (1) learns adjacency matrices that serve as structural priors, and (2) models the discrepancy between this prior and the observation-derived structure. Training uses a minimax (adversarial) objective between prior and observation, encouraging the model to be sensitive to deviations. This unified spatio-temporal framework simultaneously handles sequence modeling and graph structure learning, and is designed to enhance both time-wise anomaly detection and channel-wise localization.
