AI.result: On multiple public multivariate time series benchmarks and a newly constructed synthetic benchmark (with precise channel-level anomaly annotations), the framework achieves state-of-the-art performance for both when-anomaly-occurs (time-wise detection) and where-anomaly-occurs (spatial/channel-wise localization).
AI.conclusion: Adversarially learning a structural prior against data-driven observations alleviates spatial over-generalization in GNN-based MTSAD models. This improves recall and enables accurate localization of anomalous channels. The authors also contribute a synthetic benchmark to standardize evaluation of channel-wise anomaly localization, along with code and pre-trained models for reproducibility.

title: Generative AI and the Productivity Divide: Human-AI Complementarities in Education
summary: Generative Artificial Intelligence (GenAI) is transforming how firms create, process, and apply knowledge, yet little is known about the heterogeneity of its productivity effects across users. We report results from a randomized controlled experiment in which participants-analogs of early-career knowledge workers-were assigned to self-study a technical domain using either traditional resources or large-language-model (LLM) assistance. On average, GenAI access significantly increased task performance, but the distribution of gains was highly uneven. Improvements were not predicted by GPA or prior knowledge, but by \textit{AI Interaction Competence (AIC)} -- the ability to elicit, filter, and verify model outputs. High-AIC participants realized outsized gains; low-AIC participants saw limited or even negative marginal returns. A scaffolding intervention (conceptual maps) reduced outcome variance, indicating that standardized workflows can mitigate inequality in AI-mediated performance. We interpret these findings through the lens of human-AI complementarities: GenAI raises mean productivity while introducing a new axis of capability inequality. Managerially, firms should pair GenAI access with short AIC micro-training and simple standard operating procedures to capture value consistently and avoid uneven adoption outcomes.

AI.tldr: The paper experimentally studies how access to generative AI affects knowledge-worker-style performance and shows that average productivity rises but gains are highly unequal and depend on users’ AI interaction competence, which firms can partially equalize via simple scaffolding and training.
AI.motivation: Although generative AI is rapidly being deployed in knowledge work, we lack causal evidence on how its productivity effects differ across users and what explains this heterogeneity. Firms need to understand whether GenAI simply boosts everyone, or if it creates new inequalities based on who can use it effectively, and how organizational practices might shape these outcomes.
AI.method: The authors run a randomized controlled experiment with participants representing early-career knowledge workers. Participants must self-study a technical domain and complete tasks either using traditional learning resources or with assistance from a large language model. They measure performance outcomes, collect data on academic indicators (e.g., GPA), prior knowledge, and a new construct called AI Interaction Competence (AIC)—the ability to elicit, filter, and verify AI outputs. They also implement a scaffolding intervention via conceptual maps/standardized workflows to test whether it reduces performance variance.
AI.result: GenAI access significantly improves average task performance, but the gains are very uneven across participants. Traditional predictors like GPA and prior knowledge do not explain who benefits most. Instead, AI Interaction Competence (AIC) strongly predicts the size of productivity gains: high-AIC users obtain large performance improvements, while low-AIC users see modest or even negative marginal returns from GenAI. The scaffolding intervention (conceptual maps) reduces the dispersion of outcomes, suggesting that structured workflows can temper inequality in AI-enabled performance.
AI.conclusion: GenAI increases mean productivity but also introduces a new dimension of capability inequality linked to AI Interaction Competence. Human-AI complementarities mean that value capture depends on users’ skill in prompting, evaluating, and integrating AI outputs. Organizations should not only provide GenAI access but also invest in brief AIC micro-training and simple standard operating procedures to ensure more consistent performance gains and to avoid highly uneven adoption outcomes among workers.

title: Evidence-Grounded Frontier Mapping and Agentic Hypothesis Generation in Nanomedicine
summary: Nanomedicine research spans delivery chemistry, immunology, imaging, biomaterials, and disease-specific translational science, yet its conceptual design space remains fragmented across a large and heterogeneous literature. To date, artificial intelligence in nanomedicine has focused primarily on property prediction and formulation optimization, with much less attention to evidence-grounded discovery support at the level of research direction selection. We introduce pArticleMap, a literature-mapping and research-hypothesis-generation system that combines article embeddings, similarity-graph analysis, sparse frontier extraction, structured evidence-pack retrieval, and an audited large-language-model (LLM) workflow for grounded ideation. Rather than forecasting future concept co-occurrence, pArticleMap targets low-density article-level bridge regions and cluster interfaces, then generates and scores citation-grounded hypotheses with large language models in an agentic setup. We evaluate the system with a retrospective realization benchmark (generate later literature under a historical cutoff) and a blinded human reader assessment layer across cue-conditioned nanomedicine tasks. Across 4 selected retrospective bundles, pArticleMap generated ideas and selected task-retained hypotheses (winner ideas) under the benchmark protocol. For task-level retained hypotheses, a pooled gold recovery rate of 10.8% was obtained, with a recall@10 of 15.9% and a future-neighborhood rate of 61.0%, indicating that the system often reached the correct forward-looking neighborhood (paper ideas) even without exact paper-level recovery. Human-agent agreement is modest overall, indicating that internal scoring is useful as a support signal but does not replace expert judgment. These results position pArticleMap as a conservative, evidence-grounded research assistant for nanomedicine.

AI.tldr: The paper presents pArticleMap, an AI-driven system that maps nanomedicine literature to propose evidence-grounded, forward-looking research hypotheses by targeting underexplored regions in the conceptual space.
AI.motivation: Nanomedicine spans many subfields and its design space is scattered across a heterogeneous literature, making it difficult for researchers to gain a unified view and identify promising research directions. Existing AI tools in nanomedicine mainly focus on predicting material properties or optimizing formulations, not on suggesting new, evidence-based research directions. There is a need for a system that systematically maps the literature and supports hypothesis generation grounded in existing evidence.
AI.method: The authors develop pArticleMap, which uses article embeddings to represent papers, similarity-graph analysis to structure the literature, and sparse frontier extraction to find low-density bridge regions and cluster interfaces in the graph. It then retrieves structured evidence packs related to these regions and uses an audited, agentic large-language-model workflow to generate, score, and refine citation-grounded hypotheses. The system is evaluated via a retrospective benchmark (trying to generate ideas that appear in later literature using only pre-cutoff papers) and a blinded human reader assessment on nanomedicine tasks.
AI.result: In four retrospective test bundles, pArticleMap generated multiple hypotheses and selected a subset of task-retained “winner ideas” that were assessed under the benchmark. For these retained hypotheses, the system achieved a pooled gold recovery rate of 10.8%, a recall@10 of 15.9%, and a future-neighborhood rate of 61.0%, meaning it frequently reached the correct conceptual neighborhood of future papers even when not reproducing them exactly. Human experts showed only modest agreement with the system’s internal scoring, implying that the automatic scores are helpful but insufficient to replace expert evaluation.
AI.conclusion: pArticleMap can function as a conservative, evidence-grounded assistant for guiding nanomedicine research direction selection. By focusing on underexplored bridge regions in the literature graph and grounding LLM-generated hypotheses in citations, it can identify plausible, forward-looking ideas, though expert judgment remains essential to vet and prioritize them.

title: Whispers in the Noise: Surrogate-Guided Concept Awakening via a Multi-Agent Framework
summary: Diffusion models (DMs) are widely used for text-to-image generation, but their strong generative capabilities also raise concerns about unsafe or undesirable content. Concept erasure aims to mitigate these risks by removing specific concepts from pretrained models. However, recent studies show that such methods often suppress rather than fully eliminate target concepts, leaving models vulnerable to awakening attacks. Existing approaches primarily rely on white-box access through optimization or inversion, while concept awakening under black-box constraints remains underexplored. In this work, we revisit the denoising process from a trajectory perspective and show that concept erasure mainly disrupts early-stage text-semantic alignment but does not fully prevent semantic information from propagating along the denoising dynamics. As generation proceeds, the model increasingly depends on the evolving noisy state rather than textual conditions, which creates an opportunity to bypass erased mappings. Motivated by this observation, we propose ConceptAgent, a training-free, black-box, multi-agent framework that awakens erased concepts by initializing the denoising trajectory from surrogate-guided noisy states. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ConceptAgent enables accurate and controllable awakening of erased concepts under black-box settings without access to model parameters, gradients, or internal representations. These results highlight fundamental limitations of current concept erasure methods and provide new insights into the dynamic nature of semantic control in DMs.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why current concept erasure in diffusion models fails and introduces a black-box, training-free method (ConceptAgent) to reliably re-awaken supposedly erased concepts by manipulating the denoising trajectory.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models can generate unsafe or undesirable images. Concept erasure methods aim to remove specific concepts (e.g., harmful ones) from pretrained models, but evidence shows they often only suppress these concepts, which can still be revived via attacks. Existing attacks mostly assume white-box access, leaving a gap in understanding and defending against black-box awakening. The authors want to understand, from a trajectory viewpoint, how and why erasure fails and to demonstrate that erased concepts can still be awakened purely in black-box conditions.
AI.method: The authors study the denoising process of diffusion models as a trajectory, examining how text-semantic alignment evolves over time. They observe that erasure mainly harms early-stage text-conditioning but later stages rely more on the evolving image (noisy state) than text. Building on this, they propose ConceptAgent: a training-free, black-box, multi-agent framework that constructs surrogate-guided initial noisy states for denoising. Multiple agents coordinate to search and refine these starting states so that, even with erased text-to-concept mappings, the denoising trajectory converges to images containing the target (erased) concepts. The method requires only query access to the model, not parameters, gradients, or internal activations.
AI.result: Experiments show that ConceptAgent can systematically and controllably recover concepts that were supposedly erased from diffusion models, under strict black-box access. It produces images that faithfully depict target concepts while respecting desired levels of control. Comparisons suggest that current erasure approaches are vulnerable to such attacks, meaning erased concepts are still embedded in the model and can be exploited through trajectory manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Concept erasure in current diffusion models is fundamentally limited: it primarily disrupts early text-conditioning but does not remove the underlying semantic capacity, which remains accessible via the denoising dynamics. A black-box, training-free attack (ConceptAgent) can reliably re-awaken erased concepts by carefully initializing denoising trajectories. This reveals that robust semantic control in diffusion models must consider the full generative trajectory rather than only static text-to-image mappings.

title: TRACE: Trajectory Correction from Cross-layer Evidence for Hallucination Reduction
summary: Hallucination correction is not a one-direction problem. We show that intermediate layers are neither uniformly more truthful than final layers nor uniformly less trustworthy. Yet hallucination reduction is usually instantiated through one fixed intervention form: contrast one layer against another, steer along a truthfulness direction, or defer to external evidence. This framing is structurally incomplete. Cross-layer factual evidence does not evolve uniformly: in some failures truthful support is present internally and later suppressed, whereas in others candidate competition remains genuinely multi-directional across depth, so no single signed scalar family is generally sufficient. We introduce Trajectory Correction from Cross-layer Evidence for Hallucination Reduction (TRACE), a deterministic, training-free algorithm which corrects hallucinations at inference time by deriving both the corrective layer and the appropriate correction operator from each input's cross-layer candidate trajectory inside the LLM's own forward pass. Under one frozen hyperparameter setting, TRACE selects among scalar reversal, earlier-state recovery, and candidate-space correction using only model-internal evidence. Evaluated as a single universal algorithm across 15 models, 8 model families, and 3 factuality benchmarks, TRACE improves every evaluation cell, yielding mean gains of +12.26 MC1 points and +8.65 MC2-style points with no regressions, with gains reaching +47.20 MC1 and +43.38 MC2-style points. The method uses no labels, retrieval, pretraining, finetuning, or per-model calibration.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a training-free, deterministic inference-time algorithm that reduces hallucinations in LLMs by adaptively choosing how and where to correct answers using cross-layer evidence from the model’s own forward pass.
AI.motivation: Existing hallucination reduction techniques typically assume that some layers (often earlier ones) are uniformly more truthful than others, or rely on a single fixed intervention like steering along a predefined truthfulness direction or comparing with external evidence. However, factual evidence in a model does not evolve monotonically across layers: in some cases, truthful representations appear internally but are suppressed later, while in others, multiple answer candidates compete across depth with no single direction capturing truthfulness. This reveals a structural limitation in current approaches and motivates a more flexible, trajectory-aware method that can adapt intervention type and depth per input.
AI.method: TRACE (Trajectory Correction from Cross-layer Evidence) is a deterministic, training-free algorithm applied at inference time. It analyzes each input’s cross-layer candidate trajectory within the LLM’s forward pass, examining how potential answers evolve across layers. Based on this internal evidence, TRACE automatically selects both (1) which layer to intervene at (the corrective layer) and (2) what form of correction to apply. It chooses among three operator types: scalar reversal (flipping a scalar preference), earlier-state recovery (reverting to an earlier, more truthful representation), and candidate-space correction (adjusting the relative strengths of competing candidates). A single, frozen hyperparameter configuration is used across all models and tasks, and no external labels, retrieval, additional pretraining, finetuning, or per-model tuning is employed.
AI.result: Applied as a single universal algorithm across 15 different models, 8 model families, and 3 factuality benchmarks, TRACE consistently improves factuality metrics in every tested setting. It yields mean improvements of +12.26 MC1 points and +8.65 MC2-style points, with no observed regressions, and in some cases achieves very large gains up to +47.20 MC1 and +43.38 MC2-style points. These results demonstrate broad effectiveness and robustness without task- or model-specific adjustments.
AI.conclusion: Hallucination correction in LLMs cannot be adequately addressed by a single fixed intervention or by assuming a monotonic truthfulness pattern across layers. By exploiting the full cross-layer trajectory of candidate answers and adaptively choosing both where and how to intervene, TRACE provides a universal, label-free, inference-time approach that substantially reduces hallucinations across diverse models and benchmarks, showing that rich internal model dynamics can be harnessed for factuality improvement without additional training or external supervision.

title: Visualizing the Invisible: Generative Visual Grounding Empowers Universal EEG Understanding in MLLMs
summary: Leveraging the universal representations of pre-trained LLMs and MLLMs offers a promising path toward brain foundation models. However, visually-evoked EEG datasets remain scarce, leading existing methods to align neural signals mainly with abstract text, a lossy translation that may discard fine-grained perceptual information encoded in brain activity. We propose Generative Visual Grounding (GVG), a framework that visualizes the invisible by using an EEG-to-image generative model as a visual translator. Instead of forcing EEG into text alone, GVG hallucinates instance-specific proxy images for non-visual EEG, providing structured visual contexts that allow MLLMs to exploit their visual priors for clinical-state interpretation. We validate this idea on two MLLM backbones, GVG-X-Omni and GVG-Janus. Image-only alignment is already competitive: the lightweight GVG-X-Omni matches 1.7B-parameter text-aligned baselines while tuning only 170M parameters on a frozen 7B backbone. We further extend GVG-Janus with trimodal Image+Text alignment, where text supplies categorical semantic anchors and visual proxies enrich neural representations with perceptual details. Experiments show consistent gains in EEG understanding and visual generation, suggesting visual proxy grounding as an effective complement to textual alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Generative Visual Grounding (GVG), which uses an EEG-to-image generator to create visual proxies from brain signals so multimodal large language models (MLLMs) can better interpret EEG than with text-only alignment.
AI.motivation: Existing brain-LLM methods mostly align EEG with abstract text because visually-evoked EEG datasets are scarce, but this text-only mapping is lossy and discards fine-grained perceptual information encoded in neural signals. The authors want a way to exploit the strong visual priors of MLLMs and retain perceptual detail when building brain foundation models, especially for interpreting clinical states from EEG.
AI.method: They design GVG, an EEG-to-image generative framework that transforms non-visual EEG into instance-specific proxy images. These hallucinated images serve as structured visual contexts for MLLMs. Two implementations are proposed: GVG-X-Omni, which performs image-only alignment by training only 170M parameters on top of a frozen 7B MLLM backbone; and GVG-Janus, which performs trimodal alignment (Image+Text+EEG), where text provides categorical semantic anchors and images enrich EEG representations with perceptual details.
AI.result: Image-only GVG-X-Omni achieves performance comparable to or matching text-aligned baselines that rely on a much larger 1.7B-parameter tuning budget, despite using only 170M trainable parameters on a frozen 7B backbone. GVG-Janus with trimodal alignment further improves EEG understanding and visual generation quality over text-only or simpler baselines, showing consistent performance gains across evaluated tasks.
AI.conclusion: Visual proxy grounding—generating EEG-conditioned images and feeding them into MLLMs—is an effective complement to pure text alignment for brain-LLM models. By leveraging MLLMs’ visual priors, GVG preserves more fine-grained perceptual information in EEG signals, enables more parameter-efficient training, and improves both EEG interpretation and brain-conditioned visual synthesis, supporting its potential as a step toward brain foundation models.

title: Scalable Environments Drive Generalizable Agents
summary: Generalizable agents should adapt to diverse tasks and unseen environments beyond their training distribution. This position paper argues that such generalization requires environment scaling: expanding the distribution of executable rule-sets that agents interact with, rather than only increasing trajectories or tasks within fixed benchmarks. Current scaling practices largely focus on collecting more experience or broader task sets under fixed interaction rules, leaving agents brittle when underlying interfaces, dynamics, observations, or feedback signals change. The core challenge is therefore a world-level distribution shift: agents need systematic exposure to environments with meaningfully different executable rule-sets. To clarify this challenge, we propose a unified taxonomy that separates trajectory scaling, task scaling, and environment scaling by their primary deliverables and by what changes in the executable rule-set. Building on this taxonomy, we synthesize construction paradigms for scalable environments, contrasting programmatic generators that prioritize controllability and verifiability with generative world models that offer broader coverage and open-endedness. We further outline how environment scaling can be coupled with stateful learning mechanisms, emphasizing learned update rules for cross-environment adaptation. We conclude by discussing alternative perspectives and argue that scalable environments provide the essential substrate for measurable and controllable progress toward robust general agents.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that achieving robust, generalizable agents requires scaling the diversity of environments (their underlying executable rule-sets), not just the number of trajectories or tasks within fixed benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to building general agents mostly scale data, tasks, or experience within fixed environments. These agents often fail when the underlying rules of interaction—interfaces, dynamics, observations, or feedback—change, highlighting brittleness under world-level distribution shift. The authors aim to clarify what kind of scaling is needed to move toward robust general intelligence.
AI.method: This is a position/conceptual paper. The authors introduce a taxonomy distinguishing trajectory scaling, task scaling, and environment scaling, defined by what changes in the executable rule-set and what each delivers. They then compare two broad paradigms for constructing scalable environments: (1) programmatic generators, which offer controllability and verifiability, and (2) generative world models, which provide broader coverage and open-endedness. They also conceptually link environment scaling to stateful learning mechanisms like learned update rules for cross-environment adaptation.
AI.result: The primary results are conceptual clarifications and frameworks. The paper provides: (1) a unified taxonomy of scaling dimensions for agent training, (2) a synthesis of environment construction approaches in terms of controllability vs coverage, and (3) an articulation of how environment scaling interacts with adaptive learning mechanisms. There are no empirical benchmarks reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: To develop robust general agents, the field must prioritize environment scaling: systematically expanding the distribution of executable rule-sets agents encounter. Scalable environments, combined with stateful learning mechanisms, form a necessary substrate for measurable, controllable progress toward generalization beyond fixed benchmarks and narrow task distributions.

title: Pairwise Preference Reward and Group-Based Diversity Enhancement for Superior Open-Ended Generation
summary: Current reinforcement learning(RL) methods are broadly applicable and powerful in verifiable settings where scalar rewards can be provided. However, in open-ended generation tasks, verifying the correctness of responses remains challenging, and training reward models incurs substantial computational and annotation costs. Moreover, reinforcement learning (RLVR) often leads to diversity collapse and produces stereotypical or rigid outputs, outcomes that are particularly undesirable in open-domain scenarios. We propose Pairwise Preference Reward and Group-based Diversity Enhancement (PPR-GDE), a RL method that is more suitable for open-ended generation. PPR-GDE does not require scalar rewards and incorporates group-level diversity into the reward signal, it preserves the comparative structure of subjective evaluation through a pairwise preference reward, mitigates judge position bias via repeated comparisons with swapped response order, and introduces a group-based diversity reward that explicitly encourages semantic dispersion within a response group, all of these reward signals are integrated into a unified group-relative policy optimization objective. We instantiate PPR-GDE on role-playing task, experiments show that PPR-GDE achieves a better alignment quality as well as expressive diversity than strong RL baselines. Further analysis shows that pairwise preference is critical for preference alignment in subjective perspective, while the diversity metric plays an essential role in achieving superior expressive diversity and broader semantic coverage.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PPR-GDE, a reinforcement learning framework tailored for open-ended generation that uses pairwise preference rewards and group-based diversity rewards instead of scalar rewards, improving both alignment quality and expressive diversity over standard RL baselines.
AI.motivation: Standard RL methods rely on scalar rewards and verifiable correctness, which is problematic for open-ended generation where responses are subjective and hard to score. Training reward models is expensive, and existing RL approaches often cause diversity collapse, leading to rigid, stereotypical outputs that are undesirable in open-domain tasks. There is a need for an RL method that can handle subjective, comparative feedback and explicitly promote diversity.
AI.method: The authors introduce Pairwise Preference Reward and Group-based Diversity Enhancement (PPR-GDE). Instead of scalar rewards, it uses pairwise preference judgments between responses to construct a preference-based reward signal while preserving the comparative structure of human evaluation. To reduce judge position bias, it performs repeated comparisons with swapped response orders. Additionally, it computes a group-based diversity reward that measures and encourages semantic dispersion within a group of responses. These components are combined into a unified group-relative policy optimization objective for RL training.
AI.result: When applied to a role-playing generation task, PPR-GDE outperforms strong RL baselines in both alignment with human preferences and expressive diversity of outputs. Empirical analyses indicate that pairwise preference rewards are crucial for subjective preference alignment, while the diversity metric significantly enhances expressive diversity and semantic coverage across the generated responses.
AI.conclusion: PPR-GDE is a more suitable RL framework for open-ended generation tasks than conventional scalar-reward RL methods. By leveraging pairwise preferences and explicit group-based diversity rewards within a unified optimization objective, it achieves better alignment with subjective human judgments and avoids diversity collapse, leading to richer and more varied open-domain outputs.

title: Beyond the Cartesian Illusion: Testing Two-Stage Multi-Modal Theory of Mind under Perceptual Bottlenecks
summary: While Multi-Modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) demonstrate impressive capabilities in general reasoning, their embodied spatial intelligence remains hampered by a "Cartesian Illusion" - a reliance on text-based probability distributions that lack grounded, 3D topological understanding. This limitation is starkly exposed in multi-agent environments, which demand more than just scene perception; they require second-order Theory of Mind (ToM). Specifically, an Agent A must be able to infer Agent B's belief about the environment, governed strictly by Agent B's physical orientation and sensory limitations. In this paper, we probe the limits of two-stage spatial inference in MLLMs through a novel audio-visual task: requiring Agent A to predict Agent B's estimation of A's relative location. To solve this, we propose an Epistemic Sensory Bottleneck module that abandons rigid, rule-based coordinate transformations. Instead, we introduce an Anchor-Based Embodied Spatial Decomposition Chain-of-Thought (CoT). This guides the MLLM through a "geometric-to-semantic" projection, forcing it to first establish B's local coordinate system and then dynamically weight visual and auditory modalities based on whether A falls within B's visual frustum. Extensive evaluations reveal that while current MLLMs fundamentally struggle with spatial symmetry and out-of-view ambiguities (establishing a rigorous zero-shot baseline of 42% accuracy), our sensory-bounded reasoning chain robustly outperforms pure egocentric and allocentric baselines. By systematically benchmarking these perceptual bottlenecks, our work exposes the current limits of MLLM spatial reasoning and establishes a foundational paradigm for epistemic, modality-aware inference in Embodied AI.

AI.tldr: The paper examines why current multi-modal large language models fail at deep, embodied 3D spatial reasoning in multi-agent settings, and introduces a new reasoning module and benchmark task showing improved performance.
AI.motivation: Multi-modal LLMs are good at general reasoning and perception but perform poorly when spatial understanding must be grounded in physical constraints, especially in multi-agent environments requiring second-order Theory of Mind. Existing models rely on abstract, text-based probability distributions and coordinate tricks rather than genuinely modeling what another agent can or cannot perceive from its situated viewpoint. The authors want to diagnose and quantify these failures and design a method that better respects agents' sensory limitations and local perspectives.
AI.method: The authors design a novel audio-visual task where Agent A must predict Agent B's estimation of A's relative location, strictly based on B's physical orientation and sensing limits. To address this, they propose an Epistemic Sensory Bottleneck module combined with an Anchor-Based Embodied Spatial Decomposition Chain-of-Thought. Instead of using fixed coordinate transforms, the method first builds B's local coordinate system, then performs a geometric-to-semantic projection, and adaptively reweights visual and auditory modalities depending on whether A lies inside B's visual frustum. They compare this to egocentric and allocentric baselines using existing MLLMs in a zero-shot setting.
AI.result: Experiments show that standard MLLMs have fundamental difficulties with spatial symmetry and reasoning about agents or objects that are out of view, achieving only around 42% accuracy on the task in zero-shot conditions. The proposed sensory-bounded reasoning chain substantially outperforms egocentric and allocentric baselines across benchmarks, demonstrating more reliable second-order spatial inference under sensory constraints.
AI.conclusion: Current MLLMs lack robust embodied spatial intelligence and struggle with second-order Theory of Mind in multi-agent, sensory-bounded environments. The proposed Epistemic Sensory Bottleneck and anchor-based CoT framework improves performance by enforcing reasoning within an agent's local geometry and sensory limits. The work both exposes clear perceptual and reasoning bottlenecks in existing models and offers a foundational paradigm for epistemic, modality-aware spatial inference in Embodied AI.

title: DARE-EEG: A Foundation Model for Mining Dual-Aligned Representation of EEG
comment: 22 pages, 10 pages of main text + 12 pages of appendices
summary: Foundation models pre-trained through masked reconstruction on large-scale EEG data have emerged as a promising paradigm for learning generalizable neural representations across diverse brain-computer interface applications. However, a critical yet overlooked challenge is that EEG encoders must learn representations invariant to incomplete observations-when different masked views of the same signal have minimal overlap, existing methods fail to constrain them to a consistent latent subspace, leading to degraded transferability. To address this, we propose DARE-EEG, a self-supervised foundation model that explicitly enforces the mask-invariance property through dual-aligned representation learning during pre-training. Specifically, we introduce mask alignment that constrains representations from multiple masked views of the same EEG sample via contrastive learning, complementing anchor alignment that aligns masked representations to momentum-updated complete features for semantic stability. Additionally, we propose conv-linear-probing, a parameter-efficient strategy that adapts pre-trained representations to heterogeneous electrode configurations and sampling rates through decoupled spectro-spatial projections. Extensive experiments across diverse EEG benchmarks demonstrate that DARE-EEG consistently achieves state-of-the-art in accuracy performance while maintaining relatively low parameter complexity and superior cross-dataset portability compared to existing methods. Furthermore, DARE-EEG contributes to effectively discovering and utilizing the rich potential representations in EEG.

AI.tldr: DARE-EEG is a self-supervised EEG foundation model that enforces mask-invariant representations via dual alignment and introduces a parameter-efficient probing strategy for better transfer and portability across EEG settings.
AI.motivation: Existing EEG foundation models trained with masked reconstruction struggle when different masked views of the same EEG signal have little overlap. Their representations can drift into inconsistent latent subspaces, which harms transferability and robustness under incomplete observations common in real EEG recordings and varied experimental setups. There is a need for pre-training methods that explicitly enforce invariance to masking and support transfer across heterogeneous EEG configurations.
AI.method: DARE-EEG uses a dual-aligned representation learning strategy in a self-supervised pre-training framework. First, it applies mask alignment: multiple differently masked views of the same EEG sample are pushed together in latent space via contrastive learning, making representations mask-invariant. Second, it performs anchor alignment: masked-view representations are aligned to momentum-updated, full-observation features to preserve semantic stability. For downstream adaptation, DARE-EEG introduces conv-linear-probing, which decouples spectral and spatial projections with lightweight convolutional and linear layers to efficiently adapt the pre-trained encoder to varied electrode layouts and sampling rates.
AI.result: Across a broad set of EEG benchmarks, DARE-EEG attains state-of-the-art accuracy while using relatively few parameters. It also shows strong cross-dataset transfer performance, indicating better generalization and portability than prior EEG foundation or self-supervised models.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing mask-invariant representations through dual alignment during self-supervised pre-training substantially improves the robustness and transferability of EEG foundation models. Combined with a parameter-efficient conv-linear probing scheme for handling heterogeneous EEG setups, DARE-EEG provides a strong, portable neural representation backbone and helps better uncover and exploit the rich information embedded in EEG signals.

title: SD-Search: On-Policy Hindsight Self-Distillation for Search-Augmented Reasoning
summary: Search-augmented reasoning agents interleave internal reasoning with calls to an external retriever, and their performance relies on the quality of each issued query. However, under outcome-reward reinforcement learning, every search decision in a rollout shares the same trajectory-level reward, leaving individual queries without step-specific credit. Recent process-supervision approaches address this gap by drawing step-level signals from outside the policy, relying either on a much larger teacher model, or on sub-question annotations produced by a stronger external system. In contrast, we propose SD-Search, which derives step-level supervision from the policy itself through on-policy hindsight self-distillation, requiring neither an external teacher nor additional annotations. In SD-Search, a single model plays two roles that differ only in conditioning: a student that sees only the context available at inference time, and a teacher that additionally conditions on a compact hindsight block summarizing the search queries and final outcomes of a group of rollouts sampled from the same question. Since the teacher knows how each rollout unfolded and which ones succeeded, its query distribution implicitly marks which decisions were worth making, and the student is trained to recover this behavior by minimizing the token-level Jensen--Shannon divergence to the teacher at search-query positions. This layers a dense, step-level signal on top of GRPO's coarse trajectory reward. Crucially, this signal is produced by the policy itself within the standard RL training loop, without external model inference, auxiliary annotation pipeline, or additional training stage.

AI.tldr: Paper proposes SD-Search, a method to add step-level supervision for search-augmented reasoning agents using only the agent’s own rollouts.
AI.motivation: Outcome-based RL assigns the same trajectory reward to all search decisions in a rollout, so individual search queries do not get specific credit or blame. Existing fixes depend on external strong teachers or annotated sub-questions, which are expensive or impractical. The authors want a cheaper, self-contained way to get step-level feedback for search queries in reasoning agents.
AI.method: Introduce SD-Search, where a single model serves as both student and teacher with different conditioning. Multiple rollouts are sampled for each question. The teacher version of the model conditions on the usual input plus a compact hindsight block summarizing all search queries and final outcomes for that question, so it ‘knows’ which rollouts succeeded. The student version only sees the normal inference-time context. The teacher’s query distribution, influenced by hindsight, implicitly encodes which search decisions are good. The student is trained to match this distribution at search-query tokens by minimizing token-level Jensen–Shannon divergence, while standard outcome-based RL (e.g., GRPO) still provides trajectory-level rewards.
AI.result: SD-Search yields a dense, step-level supervision signal for search-query decisions layered on top of standard trajectory rewards. This is done fully on-policy and within the normal RL loop, without external models, extra annotation pipelines, or separate training phases. Empirical results are not in the abstract but are implied to show improved query quality and overall performance of the reasoning agent.
AI.conclusion: Step-level supervision for search-augmented reasoning can be obtained purely from the policy’s own hindsight, avoiding reliance on external teachers or annotations. By conditioning a teacher version of the model on rollout outcomes and distilling its query distribution into a student version, SD-Search provides an efficient, integrated way to guide search decisions during RL training.

title: Causely: A Causal Intelligence Layer for Enterprise AI A Benchmark Study on SRE and Reliability Workflows
summary: AI agents deployed into SRE workflows currently derive their understanding of environment state from raw observability telemetry at query time, paying a semantic-interpretation tax in tokens, latency, and inferential reliability. We propose Causely, a causal intelligence layer that maintains a structured representation of environment topology, attribute dependencies, and causal relationships that are anchroed to a ontological representation of the managed environment. Causely transforms raw telemetry into a live, queryable model providing the semantic and causal foundation AI agents require to diagnose, evaluate impact, and act safely in production. We evaluate this value proposition through a benchmark study conducted in a controlled setting with injected faults in a 24-microservice OpenTelemetry demo application. Our experiments compare four agent configurations (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, HolmesGPT with Sonnet and Gemini backends). Experiments are run with and without access to Causely under two scenarios: an active incident and a healthy baseline. On the active-fault scenario, causal grounding reduces mean time-to-diagnosis by 63\%, mean token consumption by 60\%, and mean tool-call count by 78\%, compressing the investigation footprint by 4.8$\times$ and lowering direct API cost per run by 57\%; root-cause-diagnosis accuracy rises from 75\% to 100\%.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Causely, a causal intelligence layer that converts raw observability telemetry into a structured causal model for SRE workflows, significantly improving AI agents’ speed, cost, and accuracy in incident diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Current AI agents in SRE depend on raw observability data at query time, forcing them to repeatedly infer environment structure, dependencies, and causal relationships from scratch. This causes high token usage, latency, and unreliable reasoning, especially for root cause analysis in complex microservice architectures. The authors are motivated to provide AI agents with persistent, semantically rich, causal knowledge about the environment so they can reason more effectively and safely about incidents and system behavior.
AI.method: The authors design Causely, a causal intelligence layer that maintains an ontologically anchored representation of the environment, including topology, attribute dependencies, and causal relations. Causely ingests raw telemetry (e.g., OpenTelemetry) and transforms it into a live, queryable semantic-causal model that AI agents can use. They run benchmark experiments on a 24-microservice OpenTelemetry demo application with injected faults. Four agent configurations (Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, HolmesGPT with Sonnet and Gemini backends) are evaluated with and without access to Causely under two scenarios: active incident and healthy baseline. Metrics include time-to-diagnosis, token consumption, tool-call count, investigation footprint, API cost, and root cause diagnosis accuracy.
AI.result: In the active incident scenario, using Causely’s causal grounding leads to a 63% reduction in mean time-to-diagnosis, a 60% reduction in mean token consumption, and a 78% reduction in mean tool-call count. The overall investigation footprint is reduced by 4.8×, and direct API cost per run decreases by 57%. Root-cause-diagnosis accuracy improves from 75% to 100%.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that equipping AI agents with a persistent, structured causal model of the production environment substantially improves the efficiency, cost-effectiveness, and reliability of incident diagnosis in SRE workflows. Causely’s causal intelligence layer mitigates the semantic-interpretation overhead of working directly on raw telemetry and provides a robust foundation for safer, more accurate autonomous or semi-autonomous operations in complex microservice systems.

title: QSTRBench: a New Benchmark to Evaluate the Ability of Language Models to Reason with Qualitative Spatial and Temporal Calculi
summary: We introduce an extensive qualitative spatial and temporal reasoning (QSTR) benchmark for evaluating large language models (LLMs). We pose questions concerning compositional reasoning (using composition tables, CT), converse relations, and conceptual neighbourhoods (CN) for QSTR calculi, Point Algebra (PA), Allen's Interval Algebra, Interval and Duration (INDU), Region Connection Calculus (RCC-5, RCC-8, and RCC-22), the nine intersection model, cardinal direction calculus, and STAR. The RCC-22 CN is published here for the first time. An extended benchmark systematically varies question presentation including prefix/infix, words/symbols/nonce terms and schematic descriptions for selected calculi. We report results for contemporary frontier models. All models tested perform better than guessing but none can consistently answer all questions correctly. Performance varies sharply by calculus, with PA being the most straightforward, and RCC-22 the most difficult. We release the benchmark, and our results under an open licence to facilitate further assessment of qualitative spatio/temporal reasoning in LLMs.

AI.tldr: They build and release a benchmark to test how well large language models handle classic qualitative spatial and temporal reasoning, and show that current top models are better than chance but still far from reliable, especially on harder calculi like RCC-22.
AI.motivation: Qualitative spatial and temporal reasoning (QSTR) is central in AI for representing relationships like before/after, overlapping intervals, or regions touching. While there are established calculi and benchmarks in symbolic AI, it is unclear how well large language models can perform such structured, compositional reasoning. Existing evaluations of LLMs often focus on general reasoning or text understanding, not on formal QSTR calculi that require using composition tables, converse relations, and conceptual neighbourhoods. The authors want a systematic, fine-grained way to measure LLM capability gaps here and to provide a shared resource for the community.
AI.method: The authors design a broad benchmark of QSTR tasks built from multiple well-known qualitative calculi: Point Algebra (PA), Allen's Interval Algebra, Interval and Duration (INDU), several variants of the Region Connection Calculus (RCC-5, RCC-8, RCC-22), the nine intersection model, cardinal direction calculus, and STAR. Tasks include compositional reasoning using composition tables (CT), working with converse relations, and navigating conceptual neighbourhoods (CN). They also construct an "extended" benchmark that varies the surface form of questions (prefix vs infix notation, natural language words vs symbols vs arbitrary nonce terms, and schematic descriptions) to test robustness to presentation. They derive and publish the RCC-22 conceptual neighbourhood for the first time. They then run a set of state-of-the-art LLMs on these benchmarks, compare performance to chance, and analyze accuracy by calculus and by task type.
AI.result: All evaluated LLMs perform above chance on the QSTR tasks, indicating that they possess some capacity for structured qualitative reasoning. However, none of the models achieve consistently high accuracy across all calculi and task types. Performance is highly uneven: simpler calculi such as Point Algebra are handled relatively well, whereas more complex ones like RCC-22 show much lower accuracy and present substantial difficulty. Variations in problem presentation also affect model performance, revealing sensitivity to surface form. The paper additionally contributes the first published conceptual neighbourhood structure for RCC-22. All benchmark data and results are released under an open license.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that contemporary frontier LLMs have nontrivial but limited competence in qualitative spatial and temporal reasoning as formalized in classic QSTR calculi. They are better than random guessing but lack robust, generalizable mastery, particularly for complex spatial calculi. This underscores that LLMs do not yet replace dedicated symbolic QSTR systems for tasks demanding reliable formal reasoning. By releasing a comprehensive benchmark, including the newly introduced RCC-22 conceptual neighbourhood, the authors aim to provide a standard tool for tracking progress and driving future research on integrating or enhancing LLMs with strong qualitative spatio-temporal reasoning abilities.

title: When Fireflies Cluster; Enhancing Automatic Clustering via Centroid-Guided Firefly Optimization
summary: This work presents a novel variant of the Firefly Algorithm (FA) for data clustering, addressing limitations of traditional methods like K-Means that struggle with non-uniform cluster shapes, densities, and the need for pre-defining the number of clusters. The proposed algorithm introduces a centroid movement strategy and a multi-objective fitness function that balances compactness, separation, and a novel TSP-based navigation penalty. It automatically estimates the optimal number of clusters and dynamically adjusts cluster boundaries. Application to robotic sensor networks highlights its practical value, with experiments showing improved clustering quality and reduced intra-cluster path distances compared to K-Means. These results confirm the algorithm's robustness in complex spatial clustering tasks, with potential for future extensions to higher-dimensional and adaptive scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proposes a new Firefly Algorithm variant for clustering that automatically finds the number of clusters and performs better than K-Means, especially on complex spatial data.
AI.motivation: Traditional clustering methods like K-Means assume roughly spherical, equally dense clusters and require the number of clusters in advance. These assumptions fail on real-world spatial and robotic sensor data, where cluster shapes and densities are irregular and path cost within clusters matters. There is a need for an algorithm that can both discover the appropriate number of clusters and optimize for spatial navigation efficiency.
AI.method: The authors design a modified Firefly Algorithm tailored for clustering. They introduce: (1) a centroid movement strategy that allows cluster centers to move adaptively; (2) a multi-objective fitness function capturing intra-cluster compactness, inter-cluster separation, and a new traveling salesman problem (TSP)-based navigation penalty that approximates path length through cluster members; and (3) a mechanism to automatically estimate the number of clusters and adjust cluster boundaries dynamically during optimization. The algorithm is then applied and evaluated on robotic sensor network datasets.
AI.result: Experimental comparisons with K-Means show that the proposed FA variant yields clusters with better quality in terms of standard compactness and separation metrics, while also achieving shorter intra-cluster navigation paths (as approximated by the TSP-based penalty). The method robustly handles non-uniform cluster shapes and densities and does not require pre-specifying the number of clusters.
AI.conclusion: The new FA-based clustering algorithm is more robust than K-Means in complex spatial settings and is especially suitable for robotic sensor networks where path efficiency matters. It can automatically determine the number of clusters and refine cluster boundaries, reducing internal path distances. The authors suggest it could be extended to higher-dimensional data and more adaptive or dynamic environments in future work.

title: OCCAM: Open-set Causal Concept explAnation and Ontology induction for black-box vision Models
summary: Interpreting the decisions of deep image classifiers remains challenging, particularly in black-box settings where model internals are inaccessible. We introduce OCCAM, a framework for open-set causal concept explanation and ontology induction in vision models. OCCAM discovers visual concepts in an open-set manner, localizes them via text-guided segmentation, and performs object-level interventions by removing concepts to measure changes in class confidence, estimating each concept's causal contribution. Beyond local explanations, OCCAM aggregates interventional evidence across a dataset to induce a structured concept ontology that captures how classifiers globally organize visual concepts. Reasoning over this ontology reveals consistent dependencies between concepts, exposes latent causal relations, and uncovers systematic model biases. Experiments on Broden and ImageNet-S across multiple classifiers show that OCCAM improves explanation quality in open-set black-box settings while providing richer global insight than per-image attribution methods.

AI.tldr: The paper presents OCCAM, a framework to interpret black-box deep image classifiers by discovering visual concepts, causally testing their influence via interventions, and building a global concept ontology to reveal model reasoning and biases.
AI.motivation: Deep image classifiers are hard to interpret, especially in black-box scenarios where internal representations or gradients are unavailable. Existing explanation methods often focus on per-image attributions, rely on closed vocabularies, or lack causal grounding. There is a need for a method that can discover concepts in an open-set way, provide causally meaningful explanations, and yield global insight into how models organize and depend on visual concepts.
AI.method: OCCAM first discovers visual concepts in an open-set fashion, likely using some vision-language or text-driven component. It then localizes these concepts in images via text-guided segmentation. With localized concepts, it performs object-level interventions—removing or masking out concept regions—to observe changes in the classifier’s output probabilities. These changes are used to estimate each concept’s causal contribution to the model’s decision. Aggregating these causal contributions over a dataset, OCCAM induces a structured ontology that encodes relationships and dependencies between discovered concepts for a given classifier.
AI.result: On benchmarks such as Broden and ImageNet-S and across multiple classifier architectures, OCCAM improves explanation quality in open-set, black-box settings compared to existing attribution methods. It can reveal consistent dependencies between visual concepts, uncover latent causal relationships in the model’s internal decision logic, and highlight systematic biases that are not apparent from per-image attributions alone.
AI.conclusion: OCCAM offers a causal, concept-based and open-set explanation framework for black-box vision models that goes beyond local post-hoc attributions. By performing interventions on discovered concepts and inducing a global ontology, it provides both faithful per-decision explanations and high-level insight into how classifiers structure and rely on visual concepts, enabling the detection of hidden dependencies and biases.

title: A Practical Noise2Noise Denoising Pipeline for High-Throughput Raman Spectroscopy
summary: A lightweight and reproducible denoising pipeline for high-throughput Raman spectroscopy is presented. The approach relies on a one-dimensional convolutional autoencoder trained using a Noise2Noise strategy, requiring neither external spectral libraries nor high signal-to-noise reference spectra for training. From a reduced training subset composed of repeated short-exposure acquisitions, the model learns to reconstruct Raman spectra while efficiently suppressing stochastic noise. The method is evaluated on a heterogeneous mineral sample, using both quantitative spectral fidelity metrics (RMSE, SNR, SSIM) and task-oriented criteria based on unsupervised K-means classification. Results demonstrate that integration times as short as 5 ms per spectrum, which are typically insufficient for reliable interpretation, yield denoised spectra with high fidelity to the reference data while preserving chemically coherent maps. This work provides a practical trade-off between spectral quality and acquisition speed, enabling fast, adaptable Raman workflows compatible with routine laboratory use. It also offers a transferable framework for other one-dimensional spectroscopic modalities.

AI.tldr: They propose a simple, reproducible denoising pipeline for high-throughput Raman spectroscopy based on a 1D convolutional autoencoder trained with a Noise2Noise strategy, enabling much shorter acquisition times while preserving spectral quality and chemical mapping fidelity.
AI.motivation: High-throughput Raman mapping is limited by the trade-off between spectral quality and acquisition time: long integration gives good signal-to-noise but is too slow for large-area or routine measurements, while short exposures yield noisy spectra that are hard to interpret. Existing denoising methods often need clean reference spectra or external libraries, making them less practical and less reproducible. The authors aim to develop a lightweight, self-contained denoising approach that works directly from noisy, repeated short-exposure measurements, improving speed without sacrificing spectral fidelity or chemical interpretability.
AI.method: They design a one-dimensional convolutional autoencoder and train it using a Noise2Noise scheme: instead of clean ground-truth spectra, the network is trained on pairs of independent short-exposure noisy measurements of the same location. From a reduced subset of repeated acquisitions, the model learns to reconstruct underlying Raman spectra while suppressing stochastic noise. Performance is evaluated on a heterogeneous mineral sample using numerical fidelity metrics—RMSE, SNR, SSIM—and task-driven evaluation via unsupervised K-means clustering on the denoised maps to test preservation of chemically meaningful structures.
AI.result: The trained autoencoder effectively denoises very short-exposure Raman spectra (down to 5 ms integration), producing spectra that closely match high-quality reference measurements according to RMSE, SNR, and SSIM. Despite the aggressive reduction in acquisition time, the resulting Raman maps remain chemically coherent when subjected to K-means clustering, indicating that key spectral features and spatial chemical contrasts are preserved. The model is lightweight and trained from a relatively small subset of repeated measurements, without external libraries or high-SNR references.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Noise2Noise-trained 1D convolutional autoencoder offers a practical denoising solution for Raman spectroscopy that substantially relaxes the need for long acquisition times while maintaining spectral fidelity and chemically meaningful imaging. This enables much faster, high-throughput Raman workflows suitable for routine laboratory use. Because the approach is generic for 1D spectral signals and does not rely on external reference datasets, it can be transferred to other spectroscopic modalities that face similar noise–time trade-offs.

title: AMR-SD: Asymmetric Meta-Reflective Self-Distillation for Token-Level Credit Assignment
summary: The alignment of Large Language Models (LLMs) for complex reasoning heavily relies on Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). However, standard algorithms like GRPO apply sequence-level rewards uniformly to all tokens, creating a severe credit-assignment bottleneck. While on-policy self-distillation attempts to resolve this by conditioning a self-teacher on privileged contexts, direct exposure to raw oracle solutions often induces over-conditioned teacher distributions, implicit answer leakage, and late-stage training collapse. To overcome these limitations, we propose Asymmetric Meta-Reflective Self-Distillation (AMR-SD). Instead of conditioning directly on raw reference traces, AMR-SD inserts a reflection bottleneck: it compresses diagnostic signals -- from verifier outcomes, peer rollouts, or reference feedback -- into concise, self-generated Socratic hints and critiques. Furthermore, we introduce Causal Information Gain (CIG) with an asymmetric, ReLU-gated threshold to translate these reflections into sparse, highly precise token-level advantage modulations. Combined with temporal annealing, this mechanism preserves the base environmental reward while filtering out distributional noise. Experiments across scientific, mathematical, and tool-use benchmarks demonstrate that AMR-SD significantly outperforms existing baselines, achieving robust long-horizon stability and successfully preventing late-stage collapse.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AMR-SD, a new reinforcement-learning-based alignment method for LLMs that improves token-level credit assignment using reflective hints and causal information gain, avoiding collapse and boosting complex reasoning performance.
AI.motivation: Existing RL with verifiable rewards methods for aligning LLMs use sequence-level rewards (e.g., GRPO), which give the same reward to all tokens, causing poor credit assignment, especially for complex reasoning. On-policy self-distillation that conditions on oracle solutions tries to fix this, but it often overfits to those solutions, leaks answers, and leads to unstable, collapsing training late in learning. A better method is needed that can exploit rich diagnostic signals without leaking full solutions or destabilizing training.
AI.method: They introduce Asymmetric Meta-Reflective Self-Distillation (AMR-SD). Instead of conditioning the teacher model directly on full reference solutions, they add a reflection bottleneck: the model turns verifier outcomes, peer rollouts, and reference feedback into short, self-generated Socratic hints and critiques. These reflections are then mapped into token-level advantage adjustments using Causal Information Gain (CIG) with an asymmetric ReLU-gated threshold, producing sparse and precise credit signals. Temporal annealing controls how much these modulated advantages influence learning over time, ensuring that the original environment reward remains primary while noise is filtered out.
AI.result: Across benchmarks in scientific reasoning, mathematics, and tool use, AMR-SD consistently outperforms standard RLVR baselines, including GRPO-style approaches and conventional self-distillation. It yields better performance metrics, maintains training stability over long horizons, and shows no late-stage collapse, even where baselines do collapse or plateau.
AI.conclusion: AMR-SD offers a more stable and effective way to align LLMs for complex reasoning: by using reflective, compressed hints plus CIG-based token-level credit assignment, it leverages rich feedback without leaking solutions or destabilizing learning. This approach improves performance, robustness, and long-horizon stability compared to existing RLVR and self-distillation methods, suggesting that reflection bottlenecks and causal information-guided advantages are key design elements for future LLM training algorithms.

title: VISAFF: Speaker-Centered Visual Affective Feature Learning for Emotion Recognition in Conversation
summary: Emotion Recognition in Conversation (ERC) is essential for effective human-machine interaction, aiming to identify speakers' emotional states in multi-turn dialogues. Early text-based methods struggle with complex scenarios like sarcasm because they inherently neglect vital non-verbal information. While recent Vision-Language Models (VLMs) address this by analyzing video directly, they are not inherently tailored for ERC and often focus on emotionally irrelevant background regions or passive listeners rather than the active speaker. Furthermore, fine-tuning these large models incurs prohibitive computational costs. Additionally, isolated visual signals are frequently ambiguous or technically compromised without the context of linguistic content and vocal prosody. To address these challenges, we propose VISAFF, a speaker-centered VISual AFFective feature learning framework for ERC. VISAFF consists of two stages: Speaker-Centered Affective Grounding and Reliability-Guided Affective Complementation. VISAFF utilizes a tuning-free approach to unlock the reasoning capabilities of frozen VLMs, efficiently steering them to focus on the active speaker's emotional visual cues without heavy training overheads. In the second stage, we introduce a reliability-guided affective complementation mechanism that dynamically leverages textual and acoustic modalities to compensate for visual uncertainty. Experiments on two real-world datasets demonstrate that VISAFF achieves highly competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art methods in a tuning-free setting, significantly enhancing computational efficiency by eliminating the need for expensive fine-tuning of large VLMs. The source code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/speaker-2365/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VISAFF, a tuning-free, speaker-centered visual affective feature learning framework that leverages frozen vision-language models plus text and audio to improve emotion recognition in multi-turn conversations efficiently.
AI.motivation: Emotion Recognition in Conversation is crucial for human-machine interaction but text-only methods fail in complex cases like sarcasm, and existing vision-language models are both computationally expensive to fine-tune and often attend to irrelevant visual regions or non-speaking participants. Additionally, visual cues can be ambiguous or degraded without linguistic and acoustic context, motivating a new approach that can harness VLMs effectively without fine-tuning and integrate multimodal information more reliably.
AI.method: The authors propose VISAFF, composed of two main stages. First, Speaker-Centered Affective Grounding uses a tuning-free strategy to guide a frozen vision-language model to focus attention on the active speaker’s emotionally relevant visual cues, avoiding irrelevant background or passive listeners. Second, Reliability-Guided Affective Complementation estimates the reliability of visual affective features and dynamically incorporates textual and acoustic modalities to compensate when visual information is uncertain or ambiguous. The framework uses the reasoning capabilities of frozen VLMs instead of expensive full-model fine-tuning.
AI.result: On two real-world ERC datasets, VISAFF achieves performance that is highly competitive with state-of-the-art methods despite operating in a tuning-free setting, showing that it can match or approach SOTA accuracy while significantly reducing computation, particularly by avoiding fine-tuning large VLMs.
AI.conclusion: VISAFF demonstrates that a speaker-centered, tuning-free use of frozen VLMs, combined with reliability-guided fusion of text and audio, can effectively tackle the challenges of emotion recognition in conversation. The approach focuses visual attention on the active speaker, mitigates visual uncertainty via multimodal complementation, and delivers strong ERC performance with much better computational efficiency than traditional fine-tuned VLM-based methods.

title: GIM: Evaluating models via tasks that integrate multiple cognitive domains
comment: 56 pages, 27 figures, 4 tables. Code: https://github.com/facebookresearch/gim ; Dataset: https://huggingface.co/datasets/facebook/gim
summary: As LLM benchmarks saturate, the evaluation community has pursued two strategies to increase difficulty: escalating knowledge demands (GPQA, HLE) or removing knowledge entirely in favor of abstract reasoning (ARC-AGI). The first conflates memorization with capability; the second divorces reasoning from the practical contexts in which it matters. We take a different approach. The Grounded Integration Measure (GIM) is a benchmark of 820 original problems (615 public, 205 private) where difficulty comes from integration; individual problems require coordinating multiple cognitive operations (constraint satisfaction, state tracking, epistemic vigilance, audience calibration) over broadly accessible knowledge, so that reasoning stays grounded in realistic tasks without being gated on specialized expertise. Each problem is an original expert-authored composition, majority with rubric-decomposed scoring (median 6 independently judged criteria). A balanced public--private split provides built-in contamination diagnostic. We calibrate a continuous response 2-parameter logistic (2PL) IRT model over >200k prompt-response pairs across 28 models, producing robust ability estimates that correctly order test-configurations even when raw accuracy is distorted by errors or missing data, addressing a common challenge in benchmark reporting. Using this framework, we present a comprehensive leaderboard spanning 22 models and 47 test-configurations (unique model, thinking-level pairs), and conduct what is to our knowledge the most extensive published study of how test-time compute trades off against model capability on a fixed benchmark: 11 models swept across 35 test-configurations. We observe that within-family configuration choices, such as thinking budget and quantization, matter as much as model selection. We release the evaluation framework, calibrated IRT parameters, and all public problems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GIM, a new benchmark for large language models focused on realistic, knowledge-grounded integration reasoning, and uses item-response theory over a large dataset of model outputs to build a robust leaderboard and analyze compute–capability trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks are saturating. Current strategies to increase difficulty either (1) escalate obscure or highly specialized knowledge demands, which confounds memorization with genuine reasoning ability, or (2) strip away knowledge entirely and test only abstract reasoning, which disconnects evaluation from practical, real-world tasks where reasoning must be grounded in common knowledge. There is a need for benchmarks that are challenging due to cognitive integration—not esoteric knowledge or artificial abstraction—so we can measure how well models coordinate multiple reasoning skills in realistic contexts.
AI.method: The authors design the Grounded Integration Measure (GIM), a benchmark of 820 original, expert-written problems (615 public, 205 private) that require coordinating several cognitive operations such as constraint satisfaction, state tracking, epistemic vigilance, and audience calibration, all over broadly accessible knowledge. Many problems use rubric-decomposed scoring with a median of 6 independently judged criteria. They maintain a balanced public–private split to detect contamination. For evaluation, they fit a 2-parameter logistic Item Response Theory (2PL IRT) model on over 200k prompt–response pairs from 28 different LLMs, which yields continuous, robust ability estimates that can correctly rank models and configurations even when raw accuracy is noisy, error-prone, or partially missing.
AI.result: Using the calibrated IRT framework, the authors build a comprehensive leaderboard covering 22 models and 47 test configurations (combinations of model and thinking level). They also run 11 models across 35 test configurations to study in detail how test-time compute (e.g., thinking budget) trades off against measured capability. Their analysis reveals that intra-family configuration choices—such as the amount of test-time thinking and quantization settings—can influence performance as much as switching between different model families, highlighting the importance of evaluation at the configuration level rather than only at the base-model level.
AI.conclusion: GIM provides a new, integration-focused, knowledge-grounded benchmark that avoids both over-reliance on obscure knowledge and unrealistic, decontextualized reasoning tests. The IRT-based evaluation framework offers more reliable ability estimates and a principled way to compare diverse models and configurations, even with imperfect accuracy data. The findings underscore that how a model is configured at inference time—thinking budget, quantization, and similar choices—can be as critical as which base model is chosen. The authors release the benchmark problems (public portion), IRT parameters, and evaluation framework to facilitate more robust and nuanced LLM evaluation research.

title: Query-Conditioned Knowledge Alignment for Reliable Cross-System Medical Reasoning
summary:
Cross-domain knowledge alignment is essential for integrating heterogeneous medical systems, yet existing approaches typically treat entity alignment as a static matching problem, ignoring query context and cross-system asymmetry. This limitation is particularly critical in integrative medical settings, where correspondence between concepts is inherently context-dependent, non-bijective, and direction-sensitive.
  In this paper, we propose Query-Conditioned Entity Alignment (QCEA), which reformulates entity alignment as a query-conditioned correspondence problem. Instead of learning a fixed mapping between entity representations, QCEA treats the textual description of a source entity as a query and ranks candidate entities in the target graph, enabling context-dependent alignment. The framework integrates semantic encoding, graph-based representation learning, and a direction-aware transformation module to capture asymmetric and many-to-many correspondence across heterogeneous knowledge systems.
  We evaluate QCEA on TCM--WM knowledge graphs derived from SymMap, covering both symptom alignment and herb--molecule alignment tasks. Experimental results show consistent improvements over representative baselines, particularly on rank-sensitive metrics such as Hit@K and MRR. Furthermore, downstream retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) experiments demonstrate that improved alignment leads to better evidence retrieval, stronger grounding, and higher answer accuracy. These findings highlight that alignment is not merely a data integration step, but a key factor that shapes knowledge accessibility and reliability in cross-system medical reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Query-Conditioned Entity Alignment (QCEA), a method that treats entity alignment between heterogeneous medical knowledge graphs as a context-dependent ranking problem rather than a static one, improving cross-system knowledge integration and downstream medical reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing cross-domain medical knowledge alignment methods treat entity alignment as a static, symmetric, mostly one-to-one mapping between entities, ignoring that in real integrative medicine settings the correspondence between concepts is context-dependent, asymmetric, and often many-to-many. This mismatch harms the integration of Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) and Western Medicine (WM) knowledge graphs and degrades downstream tasks like retrieval-augmented generation and clinical reasoning, where the meaning and usefulness of an alignment depend on the query context and direction.
AI.method: The paper proposes Query-Conditioned Entity Alignment (QCEA), which reformulates alignment as a query-conditioned ranking task: the textual description of a source entity is used as a query to rank candidate entities in the target graph. QCEA combines semantic text encoding with graph-based representation learning to capture structural information, and introduces a direction-aware transformation module to explicitly model asymmetric and many-to-many relations between entities in heterogeneous knowledge systems. The model outputs a ranked list of target entities for each source query, enabling context-sensitive correspondence rather than a fixed bijective mapping.
AI.result: On TCM–WM knowledge graphs constructed from SymMap, evaluated on symptom alignment and herb–molecule alignment tasks, QCEA consistently outperforms strong baselines, especially on ranking-oriented metrics such as Hit@K and Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR). Additional retrieval-augmented generation experiments show that better alignment produced by QCEA improves evidence retrieval quality, enhances factual grounding of generated answers, and increases answer accuracy in cross-system medical reasoning scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Treating entity alignment as a query-conditioned, direction-aware ranking problem more faithfully reflects the asymmetry and context dependence of correspondences between heterogeneous medical knowledge systems. QCEA improves both intrinsic alignment performance and downstream RAG-based reasoning, indicating that alignment should be viewed not just as a preprocessing step for data integration but as a central component that directly shapes knowledge access, grounding, and reliability in integrative medical applications.

title: When Outcome Looks Right But Discipline Fails: Trace-Based Evaluation Under Hidden Competitor State
summary:
Outcome-only evaluation can certify economically unsafe agents: a policy can hit a business KPI while violating deployable behavioral discipline. In hotel pricing with hidden competitor state, a learner can achieve plausible revenue per available room while failing to preserve the rate discipline of a rule-based revenue-management competitor.
  We introduce discipline stability, a trace-based evaluation paradigm: define the benchmark behavior, restrict observations to the deployment regime, induce trace diagnostics from failure, separate mechanisms with ablations, and test transfer and deployment. Across a two-hotel benchmark and a compact hidden-budget bidding task, reward-only PPO variants miss trace alignment; revealing hidden state reduces label uncertainty; deterministic copy collapses uncertainty; and trace-prior or corrected history policies better preserve price or bid distributions. Pure behavior cloning is nearly enough for symmetric imitation, while Trace-Prior RL adds bounded adaptation under capacity asymmetry. The contribution is an evaluation and benchmark paradigm, not a new optimizer or a universal claim about MARL

AI.tldr: The paper argues that evaluating agents only by outcomes (like KPIs) can hide unsafe or undesired behaviors, and proposes a trace-based evaluation paradigm called discipline stability to better align learned policies with desired behavioral rules.
AI.motivation: Current RL and MARL systems are often evaluated only on aggregate performance metrics (e.g., revenue, reward), which can mask economically unsafe or operationally unacceptable behaviors.
