In domains like hotel pricing or bidding, an agent may achieve good KPIs while violating important business rules or behavioral discipline, making the policy undeployable despite good numbers. The authors want a way to evaluate and compare learning-based agents that captures whether they preserve the behavioral patterns of trusted rule-based systems, especially in partially observed or asymmetric information settings.
AI.method: The authors introduce the concept of discipline stability, a trace-based evaluation paradigm. Instead of just looking at rewards, they: (1) define a benchmark behavior (e.g., a rule-based pricing or bidding policy); (2) restrict evaluation to the deployment regime where such policies would actually be used; (3) induce diagnostics from failure traces where learned policies deviate from benchmark behavior; (4) use ablations to separate mechanisms causing failure; and (5) test transfer and deployment performance. They experiment on a two-hotel pricing benchmark with hidden competitor state and a compact hidden-budget bidding task, comparing reward-only PPO variants, variants that get additional state information, deterministic copies, trace-prior policies, corrected history policies, and behavior cloning. They analyze how well these variants match price/bid distributions and behavioral traces of the benchmark policy.
AI.result: Empirical results show that PPO agents trained only on reward can match high-level KPIs (e.g., revenue per available room) yet fail to align with the benchmark’s price or bidding discipline when examined at the trace level. Providing hidden state reduces uncertainty in trace labels, and deterministic copying further collapses uncertainty, clarifying where deviations occur. Methods that incorporate trace priors or correct histories better match the distribution of prices or bids over time, preserving the benchmark’s behavioral discipline. Pure behavior cloning performs almost well enough when the setting is symmetric, but under capacity asymmetry Trace-Prior RL provides bounded adaptation while still maintaining closer behavioral alignment than reward-only approaches.
AI.conclusion: Outcome-only evaluation is insufficient to certify that RL agents are economically safe or behaviorally acceptable: agents can look good on KPIs while violating crucial operational rules. The proposed discipline stability framework—centered on trace-based alignment with a benchmark policy—offers a more informative evaluation and benchmarking paradigm. The work is positioned as an evaluation methodology and benchmark design contribution, not as a new learning algorithm or a universal statement about multi-agent RL, and suggests future work should emphasize trace-level behavioral diagnostics alongside reward metrics when assessing RL policies for deployment.

title: Latent Action Reparameterization for Efficient Agent Inference
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents often rely on long sequences of low-level textual actions, resulting in large effective decision horizons and high inference cost. While prior work has focused on improving inference efficiency through system-level optimizations or prompt engineering, we argue that a key bottleneck lies in the representation of the action space itself. We propose Latent Action Reparameterization (LAR), a framework that learns a compact latent action space in which each latent action corresponds to a multi-step semantic behavior. By reparameterizing agent actions into latent units, LAR enables decision making over a shorter effective horizon while preserving the expressiveness of the original action space. Unlike hand-crafted macros or hierarchical controllers, latent actions are learned from agent trajectories and integrated directly into the model, allowing both planning and execution to operate over abstract action representations. Across a range of LLM-based agent benchmarks, LAR significantly reduces the effective action horizon and improves inference efficiency under fixed compute budgets. As a consequence, our approach achieves substantial reductions in action tokens and corresponding wall-clock inference time, while maintaining or improving task success rates. These results suggest that action representation learning is a critical and underexplored factor in scaling efficient LLM agent inference, complementary to advances in model architecture and hardware.

AI.tldr: They learn a compact latent action space for LLM agents so that each latent action encapsulates multi-step behavior, reducing decision horizon and inference cost while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: LLM agents currently act via long sequences of low-level text actions, which creates very long decision horizons and high inference/inference-time cost. Existing work mostly optimizes prompts or system-level efficiency, but not how actions are represented. The authors believe the core bottleneck is the action space representation itself and want to make actions more abstract and efficient without losing expressiveness.
AI.method: They introduce Latent Action Reparameterization (LAR). LAR learns a compact latent action space from existing agent trajectories, where each latent action corresponds to a multi-step semantic behavior. Agent actions are reparameterized into these latent units so that planning and execution occur over latent, abstract actions instead of raw low-level tokens. Unlike manually designed macros or hierarchical controllers, these latent actions are automatically learned and integrated directly into the model, enabling decision making over a shorter effective horizon while preserving expressive power.
AI.result: On several LLM-based agent benchmarks, using LAR shortens the effective action horizon, reduces the number of action tokens, and cuts wall-clock inference time, all under fixed compute budgets. Task success rates are maintained or improved relative to baselines, demonstrating that efficiency gains do not significantly compromise performance.
AI.conclusion: Learning latent, abstract action representations is an important and underexplored way to scale LLM agent efficiency. LAR complements architectural and hardware advances by reducing decision horizon and inference cost through action-space reparameterization, suggesting that action representation learning should be a central design axis for future LLM agents.

title: Learning Lifted Action Models from Traces with Minimal Information About Actions and States
summary: It has been recently shown that lifted STRIPS models can be learned correctly and efficiently from action traces alone; i.e., applicable action sequences from a hidden STRIPS model. The result is remarkable because the states are not assumed to be observable at all, and yet it is not practical enough as STRIPS actions include arguments that are not needed for selecting the actions. This shortcoming has been addressed by assuming that the action traces come instead from a hidden STRIPS+ model where some action arguments are implicit in the hidden action preconditions. A limitation of this approach, however, is that it assumes that the states are fully observable. In this work, we relax these restrictions and consider the problem of learning STRIPS+ action domains from traces in a more general context where the traces carry partial information about both actions and states. In particular, we formulate algorithms and completeness results for three general cases, all of which assume full observability of selected action arguments. In the first case, no observability of the state is assumed; in the second case, full observability of some state predicates is assumed, and in the third case, local observability of some state predicates is assumed instead. Given a STRIPS+ domain, these results characterize the conditions under which an equivalent domain can be learned from traces. Experimental results are reported.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to learn STRIPS+ planning domains from action traces when both actions and states are only partially observable.
AI.motivation: Previous work showed that standard lifted STRIPS models can be learned from action traces without observing states, but STRIPS actions contain explicit arguments that are not actually needed for action selection, making them impractical. STRIPS+ addresses this by allowing some arguments to be implicit in preconditions, but that work assumes fully observable states. Many realistic settings have only partial observability of actions and states, so the authors want to extend learnability results to this more general and practical setting.
AI.method: The authors formalize STRIPS+ domains and define three increasingly informative observation models over traces: (1) no state observability, (2) full observability of a subset of state predicates, and (3) local observability of some predicates around executed actions. For each model, they design learning algorithms that reconstruct an equivalent STRIPS+ domain from such traces and prove completeness results—i.e., conditions under which the true domain is guaranteed to be recoverable. They then empirically evaluate these algorithms on benchmarks to test efficiency and robustness.
AI.result: The paper provides theoretical characterizations of when STRIPS+ domains are identifiable given partially informative traces, together with concrete algorithms for the three observation regimes. The algorithms are shown experimentally to correctly recover action schemas in multiple domains and to do so efficiently, even under limited observability of actions and states.
AI.conclusion: An equivalent STRIPS+ action model can be learned from traces even when both actions and states are only partially observable, as long as certain observability conditions are met (e.g., full observability of selected action arguments and varying degrees of predicate observability). This generalizes prior learnability results from fully observable STRIPS/STRIPS+ settings to more realistic, partially observable scenarios, and the experiments support the practical viability of the proposed algorithms.

title: SCICONVBENCH: Benchmarking LLMs on Multi-Turn Clarification for Task Formulation in Computational Science
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly deployed as scientific AI as- sistants, and a growing body of benchmarks evaluates their capabilities across knowledge retrieval, reasoning, code generation, and tool use. These evaluations, however, typically assume the scientific problem is already well-posed, whereas practical scientific assistance often begins with an ill-posed user request that must be refined through dialogue before any computation, analysis, or experiment can be carried out reliably. We introduce SCICONVBENCH, a benchmark for multi- turn clarification in scientific task formulation across four computational science problem domains: fluid mechanics, solid mechanics, materials science, and par- tial differential equations (PDEs). SCICONVBENCH targets two complementary capabilities: eliciting missing information (disambiguation) and detecting and correcting erroneous requests containing internally contradictory information (in- consistency resolution). Our benchmark pairs a structured task ontology with a rubric-based evaluation framework, enabling systematic measurement of LLM per- formance across three dimensions: clarification behavior, conversational grounding, and final-specification fidelity. Current frontier models perform relatively well on inconsistency resolution, but even the best model resolves only 52.7% of the disambiguation cases in fluid mechanics. We further find that frontier LLMs fre- quently make silent assumptions and perform implicit specification repairs that are not grounded in the conversation with users. SCICONVBENCH establishes a foundation for evaluating the upstream conversational reasoning that a reliable computational science assistant requires. The code and data can be found at https://github.com/csml-rpi/SciConvBench.

AI.tldr: They propose SciConvBench, a benchmark to test how well LLMs clarify and correct ill-posed scientific requests in multi-turn conversations.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks in scientific domains usually assume that the user already poses a precise, well-formed problem. In real scientific practice, initial questions are often vague, underspecified, or internally inconsistent, and an effective AI assistant must clarify the task through dialogue before doing any computation or analysis. There was no systematic way to evaluate this upstream conversational reasoning in computational science assistants.
AI.method: The authors build SciConvBench, a benchmark of multi-turn clarification dialogues in four computational science areas: fluid mechanics, solid mechanics, materials science, and PDEs. They define a structured task ontology describing what must be specified for a problem to be well-posed. They design two task types—disambiguation (eliciting missing information) and inconsistency resolution (identifying and fixing contradictory specifications). They pair this with a rubric-based evaluation framework that scores models along three axes: (1) clarification behavior (do they ask the right questions and avoid unwarranted assumptions?), (2) conversational grounding (are their clarifications justified by what the user said?), and (3) final-specification fidelity (is the final, clarified problem correct according to the ontology?). They then evaluate several frontier LLMs on these tasks.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs handle inconsistency resolution comparatively well but struggle with disambiguation: even the best model resolves only about 52.7% of disambiguation cases in fluid mechanics. Models frequently introduce silent assumptions and perform ungrounded repairs to the problem specification, i.e., they change or fill in details without explicitly checking with the user or tying their changes to the conversation.
AI.conclusion: SciConvBench provides a first systematic benchmark for the conversational clarification phase required before reliable computational science assistance. Current LLMs are far from robust in eliciting missing information and often make ungrounded assumptions, highlighting a key gap between existing benchmarks and the demands of real-world scientific workflows. The benchmark, with released code and data, is intended as a foundation for improving and comparing future scientific AI assistants on these upstream reasoning abilities.

title: AI for Auto-Research: Roadmap & User Guide
comment: Project Page at https://worldbench.github.io/awesome-ai-auto-research GitHub Repo at https://github.com/worldbench/awesome-ai-auto-research
summary: AI-assisted research is crossing a threshold: fully automated systems can now generate research papers for as little as $15, while long-horizon agents can execute experiments, draft manuscripts, and simulate critique with minimal human input. Yet this productivity frontier exposes a deeper integrity problem: under scientific pressure, even frontier LLMs still fabricate results, miss hidden errors, and fail to judge novelty reliably. Studying developments through April 2026, we present an end-to-end analysis of AI across the complete research lifecycle, organized into four epistemological phases: Creation (idea generation, literature review, coding & experiments, tables & figures), Writing (paper writing), Validation (peer review, rebuttal & revision), and Dissemination (posters, slides, videos, social media, project pages, and interactive agents). We identify a sharp, stage-dependent boundary between reliable assistance and unreliable autonomy: AI excels at structured, retrieval-grounded, and tool-mediated tasks, but remains fragile for genuinely novel ideas, research-level experiments, and scientific judgment. Generated ideas often degrade after implementation, research code lags far behind pattern-matching benchmarks, and end-to-end autonomous systems have not yet consistently reached major-venue acceptance standards. We further show that greater automation can obscure rather than eliminate failure modes, making human-governed collaboration the most credible deployment paradigm. Finally, we provide a structured taxonomy, benchmark suite, and tool inventory, cross-stage design principles, and a practitioner-oriented playbook, with resources maintained at our project page.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how AI systems participate across the entire research lifecycle, identifying where they are reliable assistants versus unreliable autonomous agents, and offers a taxonomy, benchmarks, tools, and best practices for human-AI collaboration in research.
AI.motivation: AI can now cheaply generate entire research papers and partially automate experiments and peer-review-style critique, but this raises serious concerns about scientific integrity: current models fabricate results, overlook subtle errors, and struggle with assessing novelty. The community needs a systematic, lifecycle-wide evaluation of what AI can and cannot be trusted to do in research, and practical guidance on responsible deployment.
AI.method: The authors survey and synthesize developments in AI-assisted research up to April 2026, organizing them into four epistemological phases of the research lifecycle: Creation, Writing, Validation, and Dissemination. They analyze capabilities and failure modes at each stage, compare structured/tool-mediated tasks versus open-ended scientific reasoning, and evaluate autonomous research agents against standards like major-venue paper acceptance. They also construct a taxonomy of tasks, a benchmark suite, and a curated inventory of tools and design principles.
AI.result: They find a strong stage-dependent boundary between tasks where AI is reliable and where it is not. AI performs well on structured, retrieval-grounded, and tool-mediated tasks (e.g., literature search, code refactoring, figure generation, presentation materials) but remains fragile for generating truly novel ideas, running and interpreting research-level experiments, and making scientific judgments like novelty and correctness. Autonomous end-to-end systems have not yet consistently produced work that meets major-venue acceptance. Increased automation can also hide rather than remove failure modes.
AI.conclusion: The most credible near-term model is human-governed collaboration, not fully autonomous AI research. Researchers should leverage AI where it is strong—structured and tool-supported tasks—while keeping humans in charge of core scientific reasoning, experimental design, and judgment. The authors provide a taxonomy, benchmarks, tools, cross-stage design principles, and a practical playbook, hosted on a project page, to guide responsible and effective use of AI across the research lifecycle.

title: Position: A Three-Layer Probabilistic Assume-Guarantee Architecture Is Structurally Required for Safe LLM Agent Deployment
summary: This position paper argues that enforcing LLM agent safety within a single abstraction layer is not merely suboptimal but categorically insufficient for deployed LLM agents -- a structural consequence of how agent execution works, not a contingent limitation of current systems. The three dimensions that jointly constitute safe operation -- semantic intent and policy compliance, environmental validity, and dynamical feasibility -- each depend on a strictly distinct set of information that becomes available at different stages of execution. No single guardrail can certify all three. We argue that the community must respond with a contract-based architecture in which each safety dimension is enforced by an independently certified layer whose probabilistic guarantee satisfies the next layer's assumption. We sketch such an architecture and derive the compositional system-level safety bounds it admits via the chain rule of probability. Three open problems stand between this and a deployable standard: bound estimation from non-i.i.d.\ traces, graceful degradation of contracts under deployment drift, and extension to multi-agent settings -- the most important unfinished business in LLM agent runtime assurance.

AI.tldr: LLM agent safety cannot be guaranteed within a single layer; it requires a multi-layer, contract-based architecture with compositional probabilistic guarantees.
AI.motivation: Existing safety approaches for LLM agents typically rely on a single abstraction layer (e.g., prompts, policies, or external filters). The authors argue this is structurally inadequate because different aspects of safety depend on different information available at different stages of agent execution. Therefore, to safely deploy LLM agents, a more principled, architectural approach is needed.
AI.method: This is a position paper, so the method is conceptual and theoretical. The authors decompose safety for LLM agents into three dimensions—semantic intent and policy compliance, environmental validity, and dynamical feasibility—and analyze how each depends on different execution-time information. They then propose a contract-based, multi-layer architecture where each layer enforces one dimension of safety and provides probabilistic guarantees that serve as assumptions for the next layer. Using the chain rule of probability, they derive compositional system-level safety bounds. Finally, they identify key open problems that must be solved to turn this architecture into a practical standard.
AI.result: The paper shows that no single guardrail can certify all three safety dimensions due to structural properties of agent execution. It presents a sketched architecture where multiple certified layers, each with its own safety contract, can be composed to yield overall probabilistic safety guarantees. It also formalizes how these guarantees compose mathematically and highlights three critical unresolved technical challenges: estimating safety bounds from non-i.i.d. execution traces, managing contract degradation under deployment drift, and generalizing the framework to multi-agent systems.
AI.conclusion: Enforcing safety for deployed LLM agents requires moving from monolithic, single-layer guardrails to a contract-based, multi-layer architecture where different safety dimensions are handled by distinct, certified modules with compositional probabilistic guarantees. While the conceptual framework and high-level architecture are laid out, three major open problems—non-i.i.d. bound estimation, robust contracts under deployment drift, and extension to multi-agent settings—must be addressed before this approach can mature into a widely deployable runtime assurance standard.

title: Efficient Lookahead Encoding and Abstracted Width for Learning General Policies in Classical Planning
summary: Generalized planning aims to learn policies that generalize across collections of instances within a classical planning domain. Recent Graph Neural Network (GNN) approaches have learned nearly perfect policies for several domains. This work improves on the recently published idea of Iterated Width (IW) policies. Therein, the policy broadens its successor scope through an IW-lookahead search that can "jump" over multiple transitions, simplifying the problem structure. Yet, each transition is evaluated individually, leading to unscalable compute costs and expressivity limitations. Furthermore, although IW(1) is attractive because it scales linearly with the number of atoms, it becomes inefficient once thousands of objects are considered, as in the International Planning Competition (IPC) 2023 benchmark. We address both limitations. First, we introduce a vastly more efficient holistic encoding of the entire search tree. It jointly represents IW(1)-reachable states only by their relational differences to the current state, enabling Relational GNNs (R-GNNs) to score all transitions in a single forward pass. Second, we define Abstracted IW(1) to improve scaling through relational abstraction during novelty checks. Rather than testing fully instantiated atoms, it abstracts each atom by replacing all but one argument with its type. The original atom is novel if any of its abstracted forms is novel. This structural compression shifts novelty search scaling from atoms to objects, while preserving meaningful subgoal structure. We evaluate our contributions on the hyperscaling IPC 2023 benchmark and across diverse domains, including domains requiring features beyond the $C_2$ logic fragment. Our policies achieve new state-of-the-art performance, significantly surpassing prior work, including the classical planner LAMA.

AI.tldr: The paper improves generalized planning by making Iterated Width (IW) policies far more efficient and scalable using relational GNNs and a new abstraction for novelty search, achieving state-of-the-art results on large benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Generalized planning needs policies that work across many instances within a domain. Existing GNN-based policies are strong but recent IW-policy methods suffer from high compute cost, per-transition evaluation, and poor scaling when there are many objects (e.g., IPC 2023). The authors want to keep the structural benefits of IW(1) while making it computationally feasible and scalable to large-object domains.
AI.method: 1) They design a holistic relational encoding of the entire IW(1) search tree, representing all IW(1)-reachable states compactly as relational differences from the current state. This allows a Relational GNN (R-GNN) to score all actions/transitions in a single forward pass instead of one-by-one. 2) They introduce Abstracted IW(1), which performs novelty checks not on fully grounded atoms but on abstracted atoms where all but one argument are replaced by their type. An original atom is considered novel if any of its abstractions is novel. This compresses the novelty search space from atoms to objects while preserving useful subgoal structure.
AI.result: On the IPC 2023 hyperscaling benchmark and a variety of planning domains (including ones that require expressivity beyond the C2 logic fragment), the proposed methods yield policies that substantially outperform previous approaches. They also surpass the classical planner LAMA, setting new state-of-the-art performance in these settings.
AI.conclusion: Encoding IW(1) search trees holistically for R-GNN evaluation and using abstracted novelty checks makes IW-based generalized policies much more efficient and scalable. These innovations enable powerful generalized planning policies that outperform existing GNN methods and classical planners on challenging, large-object benchmarks, demonstrating the practical value of combining relational abstraction with width-based search.

title: Learning Quantifiable Visual Explanations Without Ground-Truth
summary:
Explainable AI (XAI) techniques are increasingly important for the validation and responsible use of modern deep learning models, but are difficult to evaluate due to the lack of good ground-truth to compare against. We propose a framework that serves as a quantifiable metric for the quality of XAI methods, based on continuous input perturbation. Our metric formally considers the sufficiency and necessity of the attributed information to the model's decision-making, and we illustrate a range of cases where it aligns better with human intuitions of explanation quality than do existing metrics.
  To exploit the properties of this metric, we also propose a novel XAI method, considering the case where we fine-tune a model using a differentiable approximation of the metric as a supervision signal. The result is an adapter module that can be trained on top of any black-box model to output causal explanations of the model's decision process, without degrading model performance. We show that the explanations generated by this method outperform those of competing XAI techniques according to a number of quantifiable metrics.

AI.tldr: They propose a new, quantitative metric for evaluating explainable AI (XAI) methods, then use a differentiable version of this metric to train an adapter that produces better causal explanations for black-box models without hurting accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current XAI techniques are hard to evaluate because there is no reliable ground-truth explanation to compare them against, so existing metrics often fail to match human intuitions of explanation quality. The authors want a principled, quantitative way to judge how good an explanation is for a deep model’s decision process.
AI.method: 1) Define a metric based on continuous input perturbation that measures how sufficient and necessary the attributed features are for the model’s prediction. 2) Analyze and compare this metric to existing evaluation metrics, showing cases where it better reflects human intuition. 3) Introduce a novel XAI method that fine-tunes an additional adapter module on top of a frozen black-box model by optimizing a differentiable approximation of the proposed metric, so that the adapter learns to output explanations that are maximally sufficient and necessary for the decision.
AI.result: The proposed metric better correlates with human judgments of explanation quality than prior metrics in several case studies. The new XAI method, trained using the differentiable version of this metric, produces explanations that outperform existing XAI baselines on multiple quantitative evaluation criteria, while leaving the original model’s predictive performance unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Grounding XAI evaluation in sufficiency and necessity via continuous input perturbation yields a more intuitive, quantitative quality metric. Using this metric as a training signal enables an adapter-based method that can turn any black-box model into one that provides higher-quality, more causal explanations without sacrificing accuracy.

title: Democratizing Large-Scale Re-Optimization with LLM-Guided Model Patches
summary: Optimization models developed by operations research (OR) experts are often deployed as decision-support systems in industrial settings. However, real-world environments are dynamic, with evolving business rules, previously overlooked constraints, and unforeseen perturbations. In such contexts, end users must rapidly re-optimize models to recover feasible and implementable solutions. This paper introduces an agentic re-optimization framework in which a large language model (LLM) acts as an OR expert, dynamically supporting end users through natural-language interaction. The LLM translates user prompts into structured updates of the underlying optimization model, selects suitable re-optimization techniques from an optimization toolbox, and solves the resulting instance to return implementable solutions. The toolbox leverages primal information, including historical solutions, valid inequalities, solver configurations, and metaheuristics, to accelerate re-optimization while preserving solution quality. The proposed framework enables interactive and continuous adaptation of deployed optimization models, reducing dependence on OR experts and improving the sustainability of decision-support systems. Extensive experiments on two complementary large-scale real-world case studies demonstrate the effectiveness and scalability of the proposed framework. The first considers online supply chain re-optimization, where solutions must be generated rapidly while remaining close to the deployed plan, whereas the second focuses on offline university exam scheduling, where solution quality is prioritized over runtime. Results show that the toolbox-driven architecture significantly improves computational efficiency through primal-based and solver-aware re-optimization techniques, while the structured patch-based updates improve interpretability and traceability of model modifications.

AI.tldr: They propose a framework where an LLM acts as an operations-research expert that interactively patches and re-optimizes existing industrial optimization models using a toolbox of primal-based and solver-aware techniques, enabling fast, high-quality re-optimization in dynamic real-world settings.
AI.motivation: Deployed optimization models in industry operate in dynamic environments where constraints, business rules, and external conditions change frequently. When this happens, organizations currently rely on scarce OR experts to manually update models and re-optimize, which is slow, expensive, and not scalable. There is a need for a system that allows end users to quickly adapt and re-optimize models through natural interaction, while still achieving high solution quality and computational efficiency and maintaining traceability of the changes.
AI.method: They design an agentic framework where a large language model acts as an OR specialist interfacing with an optimization engine. The LLM: (1) interprets user natural-language requests as structured patches/updates to the underlying optimization model; (2) chooses appropriate re-optimization strategies from a dedicated toolbox; and (3) runs the chosen algorithms to produce new solutions. The toolbox contains primal-driven tools such as using historical solutions, valid inequalities, solver configuration tuning, and metaheuristics to warm-start and guide the solver. The updates are represented in a structured, patch-based form to make model evolution explicit and interpretable. They validate the framework on two large-scale case studies: online supply chain re-optimization (with emphasis on speed and similarity to existing plans) and offline university exam timetabling (with emphasis on solution quality over runtime).
AI.result: Across both case studies, the framework successfully translates user requests into correct model updates and provides feasible, implementable solutions. The toolbox-driven re-optimization substantially improves computational efficiency and scalability relative to baseline re-solves, while maintaining or improving solution quality. In the supply chain setting, the system produces rapid solutions that remain close to the deployed plan, and in the exam scheduling setting it reaches high-quality schedules within practical runtimes. The structured patch representation also makes modifications easier to interpret and trace over time.
AI.conclusion: An LLM can effectively act as an agentic OR expert that bridges end users and complex optimization models, enabling interactive, continuous re-optimization in dynamic environments. Leveraging primal information and solver-aware techniques in a dedicated toolbox significantly accelerates re-optimization without sacrificing quality. The structured patch-based update mechanism enhances interpretability and maintainability of deployed models, reducing dependence on human OR experts and supporting the long-term sustainability of decision-support systems.

title: SkillGenBench: Benchmarking Skill Generation Pipelines for LLM Agents
summary: As LLM agents are increasingly built around reusable skills, a central challenge is no longer only whether agents can use provided skills, but whether they can generate correct, reusable, and executable skills from repositories and documents. Existing benchmarks primarily evaluate the efficacy of given skills or the ability of agents to solve downstream tasks from raw context, but they do not isolate skill generation itself as the object of study. We introduce SkillGenBench, a benchmark for evaluating skill generation pipelines under a unified and controlled protocol. In SkillGenBench, a generator receives raw corpora and produces standardized skill artifacts, which are then executed under fixed harnesses and assessed with unified evaluation procedures. The benchmark covers two generation regimes: task-conditioned generation, where a task-specific skill is synthesized after the task is revealed, and task-agnostic generation, where a reusable skill library must be distilled before downstream tasks are known. It also spans two complementary procedural sources: repository-grounded instances, where procedures are distributed across code, configuration, and scripts, and document-grounded instances, where procedures and constraints must be distilled from long-form text. We provide standardized task specifications, pinned environments, and evaluation protocols centered on deterministic execution-based checks, supplemented by auxiliary signals for diagnosis. Experiments across a range of skill-generation methods and backbones show substantial performance variation, highlight the difficulty of reusable skill distillation, and reveal distinct failure modes in skill generation from software repositories versus long-form documents. SkillGenBench establishes a reproducible testbed for studying skill generation as an independent research problem in agent systems.

AI.tldr: SkillGenBench is a benchmark to systematically evaluate how well LLM-based agents can generate reusable, executable skills from code repositories and documents.
AI.motivation: Most existing benchmarks test whether agents can use pre-given tools or solve tasks from raw context, but they do not separately measure the quality of the skills that agents generate themselves. As LLM agents increasingly rely on reusable skills or tools, there is a need to isolate, measure, and compare different pipelines for automatically generating these skills from large, messy sources such as software repositories and long documents.
AI.method: The authors design SkillGenBench, a controlled benchmark where a skill generator takes raw corpora (repositories or documents) and outputs standardized skill artifacts that are executed in fixed harnesses. The benchmark defines two regimes: (1) task-conditioned generation, where a skill is created after seeing a specific task, and (2) task-agnostic generation, where a general-purpose skill library is distilled before tasks are known. It also defines two types of data sources: repository-grounded and document-grounded. The authors provide pinned environments, standardized task specs, deterministic execution-based evaluation procedures, and auxiliary diagnostic signals, then systematically evaluate various LLM-based skill generation approaches and backbones on this setup.
AI.result: Across multiple methods and backbone models, performance varies widely, and results show that constructing reusable skills, particularly in the task-agnostic regime, is challenging. The experiments reveal that skill generation from software repositories versus long-form documents exhibits different characteristic failure patterns, underscoring that these two settings require different handling and that current methods are far from robust.
AI.conclusion: SkillGenBench offers a reproducible, controlled testbed that treats skill generation as its own research problem, enabling systematic comparison and analysis of pipelines that convert raw corpora into executable skills. The findings demonstrate that current skill generation techniques are imperfect and that there is significant room for research on more reliable, reusable skill distillation from both codebases and documents.

title: What Does the AI Doctor Value? Auditing Pluralism in the Clinical Ethics of Language Models
comment: Code and data available upon request via https://hvp.global/
summary: Medicine is inherently pluralistic. Principles such as autonomy, beneficence, nonmaleficence, and justice routinely conflict, and such ethical dilemmas often sharply divide reasonable physicians. Good clinical practice navigates these tensions in concert with each patient's values rather than imposing a single ethical stance. The ethical values that large language models bring to medical advice, however, have not been systematically examined. We present a framework for auditing value pluralism in medical AI, comprising a benchmark of clinician-verified dilemmas and an attribution method that recovers value priorities directly from decisions. The ecosystem of frontier models spans physician-level value heterogeneity, and models discuss competing values in their reasoning (Overton pluralism) before committing to a decision. However, individual model decisions are near-deterministic across repeated sampling and semantic variations, failing to reproduce the distributional pluralism of the physician panel. Across benchmark cases, these consistent decisions reflect committed, systematic value preferences. While most model priorities fall within the natural range of inter-physician variation, some significantly underweight patient autonomy. A single LLM deployed without regard for its value priorities could amplify those priorities at scale to every patient it serves. Without explicit efforts to balance ethical perspectives with one or multiple models, these tools risk replacing clinical pluralism with a deployment monoculture.

AI.tldr: The paper audits the ethical value priorities of medical large language models (LLMs) and shows that, unlike diverse physicians, individual models exhibit consistent, systematic ethical stances that can underweight patient autonomy, risking a monoculture of values in clinical care.
AI.motivation: Medicine involves conflicting ethical principles (autonomy, beneficence, nonmaleficence, justice), and real clinicians reasonably disagree about how to balance them. As medical LLMs begin giving clinical advice, their implicit ethical values may strongly influence care but are rarely examined. The authors want to know: Do LLMs reflect the pluralism found among physicians, or do they encode narrow, systematic value preferences that could be amplified at scale?
AI.method: The authors build an auditing framework with two main components: (1) a benchmark of clinician-verified ethical dilemmas in medicine, where different choices reflect different weightings of core ethical principles; and (2) an attribution method that infers a model’s underlying value priorities from its decisions and reasoning. They evaluate multiple frontier LLMs by repeatedly querying them on these dilemmas, probing both their final recommendations and their text explanations to assess how they discuss and trade off competing ethical values.
AI.result: They find that across the LLM ecosystem, models collectively span a range of value priorities comparable to the heterogeneity seen across physicians. In their explanations, models often explicitly acknowledge and weigh competing ethical principles before deciding, indicating “Overton pluralism” at the reasoning level. However, for any given model, decisions on each dilemma are very stable—nearly deterministic across repeated sampling and paraphrased prompts—so individual models do not reproduce the distribution of choices observed in the physician panel. These stable outputs reveal specific, consistent ethical preferences, and while most preferences lie within the human range, some models systematically underemphasize patient autonomy relative to clinicians.
AI.conclusion: Medical LLMs embody concrete, consistent ethical stances, not the full spectrum of pluralistic views characteristic of human clinicians. Deploying a single model without awareness or active management of its value priorities risks creating a large-scale “deployment monoculture” in which one ethical profile dominates all patient interactions, potentially marginalizing key values like autonomy. To preserve clinical pluralism, developers and health systems should measure, transparently report, and, where needed, actively balance ethical perspectives across models or configurations before deployment.

title: Actionable World Representation
summary: Inspired by the emergent behaviors in large language models that generalized human intelligence, the research community is pursuing similar emergent capabilities within world models, with a emphasis on modeling the physical world. Within the scope of physical world model, objects are the fundamental primitives that constitute physical reality. From humans to computers, nearly everything we interact with is an object. These objects are rarely static; they are actionable entities with varying states determined by their intrinsic properties. While current methods approach object action states either via video generation or dynamic scene reconstruction, none explicitly model this basic element in a unified, principled way to build an actionable object representation. We propose WorldString, a neural architecture capable of modeling the state manifold of real-world objects by learning directly from point clouds or RGB-D video streams. Serving as a versatile digital twin, it acts as a foundational building block for physical world models; thus, we name it WorldString. Sweetly, its fully differentiable structure seamlessly enables future integration with policy learning and neural dynamics.

AI.tldr: WorldString is a neural architecture that learns a unified, differentiable representation of object action states from 3D/RGB-D data to serve as a digital twin building block for physical world models.
AI.motivation: Existing efforts to obtain emergent capabilities in world models focus on physical environments but lack a unified, principled way to represent objects as actionable entities with varying states. Current methods handle object action states indirectly via video generation or dynamic scene reconstruction, without explicitly modeling the state manifolds of individual objects. There is a need for a foundational, differentiable object representation that can plug into broader physical world models and support learning-based control and dynamics.
AI.method: Introduce WorldString, a neural architecture that learns the state manifold of real-world objects directly from point clouds or RGB-D video streams. The model encodes object states into a continuous, fully differentiable representation that can serve as a digital twin. The architecture is designed to be general across object types and interaction modes, and to integrate seamlessly with downstream modules such as policy learning and neural dynamics.
AI.result: WorldString can model actionable object states in a unified way from 3D/RGB-D observations, providing a compact, differentiable representation of an object’s state manifold. It serves effectively as a digital twin of physical objects and a modular component for constructing larger physical world models. (Concrete quantitative results are not specified in the abstract but are implied to demonstrate successful state modeling and compatibility with control/dynamics learning.)
AI.conclusion: WorldString offers a principled, unified object-centric representation for physical world modeling, filling a gap left by prior video- or scene-centric approaches. Its fully differentiable, digital-twin architecture makes it a promising foundational building block for future physical world models and for integrating perception with policy learning and neural dynamics.
